```
27    myAnimal.sound(); // Cat meows
28  }
29}
```

‍

## 👎🏼Disadvantages of Inheritance : 

### 1\. Increased Coupling:

Creates a tightly coupled relationship between classes, making changes in the superclass impact all subclasses.

‍

Example : 

Java

```
1class Animal {
2  public void eat() {
3    System.out.println("Animal eats");
4  }
5}
6
7class Dog extends Animal {
8  // Inherits eat() method from Animal
9}
10
11class Main {
12  public static void main(String[] args) {
13    Dog dog = new Dog();
14    dog.eat(); // Reuses the eat method from Animal
15  }
16}
```

‍

If we change Animal's eat method, it could break Dog's functionality leading to an Exception      which shows how a change in one particular method of the Parent class can break the properties of it's subclasses.

‍

### 2\. Complexity:

Overuse of inheritance can lead to overly complex and hard-to-maintain hierarchies.

‍

Example :

Java

```
1class Animal {}
2class Mammal extends Animal {}
3class Dog extends Mammal {}
4class Bulldog extends Dog {}
5
6class Main {
7  public static void main(String[] args) {
8    Bulldog bulldog = new Bulldog();
9  }
10}
```

‍

Understanding this deep nested level of inheritance structure may be difficult in larger systems to maintain and may require refactoring to make the structure more maintainable and scalable.

‍

### 3\. Reduced Flexibility:

A subclass is heavily dependent on the implementation of its superclass, which may limit customization.

‍

Example :

Java

```
1class Animal {
2  public void sleep() {
3    System.out.println("Animal is sleeping");
4  }
5}
6
7class Dog extends Animal {
8  // The dog inherits the sleep method from Animal
9}
10
11class Cat extends Animal {
12  // Similarly, the Cat class inherits the sleep method from Animal
13}
14
15class Main {
16  public static void main(String[] args) {
17    Animal dog = new Dog();
18    Animal cat = new Cat();
19
20    dog.sleep(); // Animal is sleeping (not customized for Dog)
21    cat.sleep(); // Animal is sleeping (not customized for Cat)
22  }
23}
```

‍

In this example, the Dog and Cat classes are both inheriting the sleep() method from Animal. However, suppose you wanted to make Dog sleep in a specific way, for instance, "Dog is sleeping in its kennel" 🐶🏠 and Cat sleep in another way, "Cat sleeps in a tree" 🐱🌳.

‍

You would be forced to modify the sleep() method in the parent class, Animal, or override it in each subclass. This reduces flexibility because you can't change or extend sleep() behavior independently for each subclass without affecting the others. 🔄

‍

## 🎯Conclusion :

Inheritance is a fundamental feature of Java that enhances code reuse, modularity, and scalability. By understanding its types—single, multilevel, hierarchical, and hybrid—developers can design robust and maintainable applications. 💻

‍

Proper use of inheritance fosters efficient development while avoiding common pitfalls such as over-coupling and unnecessary complexity. 🛠️\n\n---\n\n## Source: https://codewitharyan.com/tech-blogs/encapsulation\n\n# Encapsulation

Topic Tags:

OOPSystem DesignLLD

🐈‍⬛ Github Codes Link: [https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode](https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode)

‍

> Encapsulation is one of the fundamental principles of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP). It involves bundling data (variables) and methods (functions) that operate on the data into a single unit called a class.

‍

Encapsulation also restricts direct access to certain components, ensuring controlled interaction through methods. This prevents unauthorized or accidental interference with the object’s data and ensures better control over the data flow in a program. 🔒

‍

In Java, encapsulation is typically achieved by:

• Declaring class variables as private. 🔐

• Providing public getter and setter methods to access and modify these variables. 🏷️

‍

## 🔑 Key Features of Encapsulation:

**1\. Data Hiding:**

○ Prevents direct access to sensitive data, ensuring that changes can only be made through controlled methods. 🛡️

‍

**2\. Modularity:**

○ Promotes modular design by separating data and behavior, making the code easier to manage and debug. 🧩

‍

**3\. Security:**

○ Protects the integrity of the data by restricting unwanted modifications. 🔒

‍

**4\. Flexibility:**

○ Allows developers to change the internal implementation of a class without affecting external code. 🔄

‍

### Example :

Java

```
1class BankAccount {
2  // Private variables (data hiding)
3  private String accountNumber;
4  private double balance;
5
6  // Constructor
7  public BankAccount(String accountNumber, double initialBalance) {
8    this.accountNumber = accountNumber;
9    this.balance = initialBalance;
10  }
11
12  // Public getter method
13  public String getAccountNumber() {
14    return accountNumber;
15  }
16
17  // Public getter method
18  public double getBalance() {
19    return balance;
20  }
21
22  // Public setter method for deposit
23  public void deposit(double amount) {
24    if (amount > 0) {
25      balance += amount;
26      System.out.println("Deposited: " + amount);
27    } else {
28      System.out.println("Invalid deposit amount.");
29    }
30  }
31
32  // Public setter method for withdrawal
33  public void withdraw(double amount) {
34    if (amount > 0 && amount <= balance) {
35      balance -= amount;
36      System.out.println("Withdrawn: " + amount);
37    } else {
38      System.out.println("Invalid withdrawal amount.");
39    }
40  }
41}
42
43public class Main {
44  public static void main(String[] args) {
45    BankAccount account = new BankAccount("12345", 1000.00);
46    System.out.println("Account Number: " + account.getAccountNumber());
47    System.out.println("Initial Balance: " + account.getBalance());
48    account.deposit(500.00);
49    System.out.println("Updated Balance: " + account.getBalance());
50    account.withdraw(200.00);
51    System.out.println("Final Balance: " + account.getBalance());
52  }
53}
```

‍

### 📃Explanation:

1\. **Private Variables:**

○ The variables accountNumber and balance are private, restricting direct access from outside the class.

‍

2\. **Public Methods:**

○ Getter methods provide controlled access to the variables.

○ Setter methods ensure valid updates to the variables.

‍

3\. **Data Integrity:**

○ The class ensures that only valid operations are performed on its data.

‍

## 👍🏼Advantages of Encapsulation : 

1\. **Improved Data Security:**

○ Prevents unauthorized access and modifications to the data.

‍

Example : 

Java

```
1class BankAccount {
2  private String accountNumber; // Data is hidden
3  private double balance; // Data is hidden
4  public BankAccount(String accountNumber, double initialBalance) {
5    this.accountNumber = accountNumber;
6    this.balance = initialBalance;
7  }
8
9  // Public getter for balance (read-only access)
10  public double getBalance() {
11    return balance;
12  }
13
14  // Public method for deposit (controlled access)
15  public void deposit(double amount) {
16    if (amount > 0) {
17      balance += amount;
18      System.out.println("Deposited: " + amount);
19    } else {
20      System.out.println("Invalid deposit amount.");
21    }
22  }
23
24  // Public method for withdrawal (controlled access)
25  public void withdraw(double amount) {
26    if (amount > 0 && amount <= balance) {
27      balance -= amount;
28      System.out.println("Withdrawn: " + amount);
29    } else {
30      System.out.println("Invalid withdrawal amount.");
31    }
32  }
33}
34
35public class Main {
36  public static void main(String[] args) {
37    BankAccount account = new BankAccount("12345", 1000.00);
38
39    // Accessing balance only through public methods
40    account.deposit(500.00);
41    account.withdraw(300.00);
42
43    // Unauthorized access prevented: No direct modification
44    System.out.println("Account Balance: " + account.getBalance());
45  }
46}
```

‍

In this example, direct access to balance and accountNumber is not allowed, ensuring that sensitive information is not exposed or modified arbitrarily. This enhances data security and prevents accidental errors.

‍

2.  **Ease of Maintenance:**

○ Encapsulated code is easier to modify and debug without affecting external components.

‍

Example : 

Java

```
1class BankAccount {
2  private double balance;
3
4  public BankAccount(double initialBalance) {
5    this.balance = initialBalance;
6  }
7
8  public double getBalance() {
9    return balance;
10  }
11
12  // Centralized logic for deposit and withdrawal
13  public void updateBalance(double amount, boolean isDeposit) {
14    if (isDeposit) {
15      balance += amount;
16      System.out.println("Deposited: " + amount);
17    } else if (amount <= balance) {
18      balance -= amount;
19      System.out.println("Withdrawn: " + amount);
20    } else {
21      System.out.println("Invalid transaction.");
22    }
23  }
24}
25
26public class Main {
27  public static void main(String[] args) {
28    BankAccount account = new BankAccount(1000.00);
29    // Maintenance: Any bug in updateBalance can be fixed in one place
30    account.updateBalance(500.00, true); // Deposit
31    account.updateBalance(300.00, false); // Withdraw
32    System.out.println("Final Balance: " + account.getBalance());
33  }
34}
```

In the above example, the updateBalance method is the single point of truth for both deposits and withdrawals. Any changes, such as adding transaction limits or fees, can be made in this method without affecting other parts of the code.

‍

3.  **Increased Flexibility:**

○ Internal implementation details can be changed without impacting external code.

‍

Example : 

Java

```
1class BankAccount {
2  private double balance;
3  public BankAccount(double initialBalance) {
4    this.balance = initialBalance;
5  }
6  public double getBalance() {
7    return balance;
8  }
9
10  // Internal implementation can be updated (e.g., adding transaction fee)
11  public void withdraw(double amount) {
12    double transactionFee = 2.00; // New feature
13    if (amount > 0 && (amount + transactionFee) <= balance) {
14      balance -= (amount + transactionFee);
15      System.out.println("Withdrawn: " + amount + ", Fee: " + transactionFee);
16    } else {
17      System.out.println("Invalid withdrawal amount.");
18    }
19  }
20}
21
22public class Main {
23  public static void main(String[] args) {
24    BankAccount account = new BankAccount(1000.00);
25    // External code remains unchanged despite internal changes
26    account.withdraw(200.00);
27    System.out.println("Remaining Balance: " + account.getBalance());
28  }
29}
```

‍

The internal implementation of the withdraw method was updated to include a transaction fee, but external code (e.g., the Main class) didn’t need any modifications. This ensures flexibility and backward compatibility.

‍

4\. **Enhanced Readability:**

○ Clearly defined interfaces improve code readability and usability.

‍

Example : 

Java

```
1class BankAccount {
2  private String accountNumber;
3  private double balance;
4
5  public BankAccount(String accountNumber, double initialBalance) {
6    this.accountNumber = accountNumber;
7    this.balance = initialBalance;
8  }
9
10  // Clear and readable methods
11  public String getAccountNumber() {
12    return accountNumber;
13  }
14
15  public double getBalance() {
16    return balance;
17  }
18
19  public void deposit(double amount) {
20    if (amount > 0) {
21      balance += amount;
22      System.out.println("Deposited: " + amount);
23    }
24  }
25
26  public void withdraw(double amount) {
27    if (amount > 0 && amount <= balance) {
28      balance -= amount;
29      System.out.println("Withdrawn: " + amount);
30    }
31  }
32}
33
34public class Main {
35  public static void main(String[] args) {
36    BankAccount account = new BankAccount("12345", 1000.00);
37
38    // Clearly defined interfaces for readability
39    System.out.println("Account Number: " + account.getAccountNumber());
40    System.out.println("Initial Balance: " + account.getBalance());
41
42    account.deposit(500.00);
43    account.withdraw(200.00);
44  }
45}
```

‍

The clear interface (getAccountNumber, deposit, and withdraw) makes it easy for developers to understand how to use the BankAccount class, even if they are unfamiliar with its internal workings.

‍

## 👎🏼Disadvantages of Encapsulation : 

1\. **Slight Overhead:**

○ Encapsulation introduces additional boilerplate code (getters and setters), which can increase verbosity.

‍

Example :

Java

```
1class BankAccount {
2  private String accountNumber;
3  private double balance;
4  // Boilerplate getters and setters
5  public String getAccountNumber() {
6    return accountNumber;
7  }
8  public void setAccountNumber(String accountNumber) {
9    this.accountNumber = accountNumber;
10  }
11  public double getBalance() {
12    return balance;
13  }
14  public void setBalance(double balance) {
15    this.balance = balance;
16  }
17}
18
19public class Main {
20  public static void main(String[] args) {
21    BankAccount account = new BankAccount();
22    // Verbose code for simple tasks
23    account.setAccountNumber("12345");
24    account.setBalance(1000.00);
25    System.out.println("Account Number: " + account.getAccountNumber());
26    System.out.println("Balance: " + account.getBalance());
27  }
28}
```

‍

Although encapsulation provides benefits, it can lead to more verbose code, especially when getters and setters are used extensively without adding significant value.

‍

2\. **Complexity:**

○ Beginners may find it challenging to understand and implement encapsulation effectively.

‍

Example : 

Java

```
1class BankAccount {
2  private double balance;
3  public BankAccount(double initialBalance) {
4    this.balance = initialBalance;
5  }
6  public double getBalance() {
7    return balance;
8  }
9  public void deposit(double amount) {
10    if (amount > 0) {
11      balance += amount;
12    }
13  }
14  public void withdraw(double amount) {
15    if (amount > 0 && amount <= balance) {
16      balance -= amount;
17    }
18  }
19}
20
21public class Main {
22  public static void main(String[] args) {
23    BankAccount account = new BankAccount(1000.00);
24    // Beginners may find concepts like access modifiers and getters challenging
25    account.deposit(500.00);
26    account.withdraw(200.00);
27    System.out.println("Final Balance: " + account.getBalance());
28  }
29}
```

‍

For beginners, understanding why direct access to fields is restricted and why getters/setters are used can be confusing. This might make it harder for them to implement encapsulation correctly.

‍

## 🎯Conclusion : 

Encapsulation is a vital aspect of OOP that fosters secure, modular, and maintainable code. By controlling data access through encapsulation, developers can ensure data integrity and create flexible, robust applications. Understanding and applying encapsulation effectively is essential for building scalable Java programs.\n\n---\n\n## Source: https://codewitharyan.com/tech-blogs/abstraction\n\n# Abstraction

Topic Tags:

OOPSystem DesignLLD

🐈‍⬛ Github Codes Link: [https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode](https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode)

‍

> Abstraction is a core concept of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) that focuses on exposing only the essential details of an object while hiding the implementation details. It enables developers to interact with objects at a higher level, focusing on what an object does rather than how it does it. 🧠🔍

‍

For example, when you use a car, you interact with its accelerator, brake, and steering wheel without needing to know how the engine works internally. This is an example of abstraction. 🚗⚙️

In Java, abstraction is achieved using abstract classes and interfaces. 🏗️💡

‍

## Problem Without Abstraction

Imagine we want to create multiple animal types (Dog, Cat, Bird, etc.) where each animal has unique behaviors, such as making sounds. Without abstraction, we might end up writing repetitive and tightly coupled code. 🐕🐈🐦

‍

For example : 

Java

```
1class Dog {
2  void makeSound() {
3    System.out.println("Bark");
4  }
5  void sleep() {
6    System.out.println("Sleeping...");
7  }
8}
9
10class Cat {
11  void makeSound() {
12    System.out.println("Meow");
13  }
14  void sleep() {
15    System.out.println("Sleeping...");
16  }
17}
18
19public class Main {
20  public static void main(String[] args) {
21    Dog dog = new Dog();
22    dog.makeSound(); // Outputs: Bark
23    dog.sleep(); // Outputs: Sleeping...
24    Cat cat = new Cat();
25    cat.makeSound(); // Outputs: Meow
26    cat.sleep(); // Outputs: Sleeping...
27  }
28}
```

‍

### The problems in the Above Code which doesn't uses the Concept of Abstraction are as follows :

1\. **Repetitive Code:** Both Dog and Cat have the sleep() method, resulting in duplicate logic.

‍

2.  **No Common Structure:** If a new animal is added, the same methods need to be redefined, leading to code redundancy.

‍

3\. **Lack of Flexibility:** You can't refer to all animals in a common way (e.g., treating a Dog and Cat as Animal).

‍

4\. **Tightly Coupled Code:** You must directly interact with individual classes (Dog, Cat), making the code less reusable and harder to maintain.

‍

### Solution Using Abstraction : 

Abstraction allows us to focus on defining the what of an object (its behavior) while hiding the how of the object (its implementation).

To Solve the above Problems, we can define a common structure for all animals, specifying the essential behaviors they must have while leaving the specific implementations to the subclasses.

‍

This approach not only reduces code redundancy by centralizing shared logic but also enhances the flexibility and scalability of the system. Instead of creating tightly coupled classes for each animal type, abstraction allows us to treat all animals in a unified way, making the code more maintainable and easier to extend.

‍

Without abstraction, the code would become repetitive, harder to maintain, and prone to errors as new animal types are added.

‍

### Abstraction helps to:

• Create a scalable design where adding new animals only involves defining a new subclass or implementing a new interface.

• Ensure consistency across all animal types by enforcing common methods like makeSound() and sleep().

• Facilitate polymorphism, enabling us to write code that can interact with any animal in a generic way, regardless of its specific type.

‍

## Ways to Achieve Abstraction : 

### 1\. Abstract Class 📦

> An abstract class acts as a blueprint for other classes, providing a foundation for shared behavior while allowing subclasses to define specific implementations.

‍

It can include both abstract methods (declared but not implemented) and concrete methods (implemented with logic). By combining these, abstract classes strike a balance between enforcing a common structure and enabling flexibility. Importantly, abstract classes cannot be instantiated directly, meaning they are designed solely to be extended by other classes.

‍

Java

```
1// Abstract Class Animal
2abstract class Animal {
3  // Abstract method for unique behaviors
4  abstract void makeSound();
5  // Concrete method for shared behaviors
6  void sleep() {
7    System.out.println("Sleeping...");
8  }
9}
10
11// Specific implementation for Dog
12class Dog extends Animal {
13  @Override
14  void makeSound() {
15    System.out.println("Bark");
16  }
17}
18
19// Specific implementation for Cat
20class Cat extends Animal {
21  @Override
22  void makeSound() {
23    System.out.println("Meow");
24  }
25}
26
27public class Main {
28  public static void main(String[] args) {
29    Animal myDog = new Dog(); // Treating Dog as an Animal
30    myDog.makeSound(); // Outputs: Bark
31    myDog.sleep(); // Outputs: Sleeping...
32    Animal myCat = new Cat(); // Treating Cat as an Animal
33    myCat.makeSound(); // Outputs: Meow
34    myCat.sleep(); // Outputs: Sleeping...
35  }
36}
```

### 👍🏼 Advantages of Abstract classes : 

1.  **Improved Code Maintainability:**

By focusing on essential details, abstraction makes code easier to maintain and understand.

‍

Example : 

Java

```
1abstract class Animal {
2  abstract void makeSound();
3  void eat() {
4    System.out.println("Animal is eating...");
5  }
6}
7
8class Dog extends Animal {
9  @Override
10  void makeSound() {
11    System.out.println("Bark");
12  }
13}
14
15public class Main {
16  public static void main(String[] args) {
17    Animal myDog = new Dog();
18    myDog.eat(); // Outputs: Animal is eating...
19    myDog.makeSound(); // Outputs: Bark
20  }
21}
```

‍

Here, the eat() method is defined in the abstract class Animal, and any update to this method will reflect across all subclasses (Dog, Cat, etc.), improving maintainability.

‍

2\. **Enhanced Flexibility:**

Changes to the internal implementation do not affect the external interface, allowing developers to modify or extend functionality easily.

‍

Example : 

Java

```
1abstract class Animal {
2  void makeSound();
3}
4
5class Dog extends Animal {
6  @Override
7  public void makeSound() {
8    System.out.println("Bark");
9  }
10}
11
12public class Main {
13  public static void main(String[] args) {
14    Animal myDog = new Dog();
15    myDog.makeSound(); // Outputs: Bark
16  }
17}
```

‍

The Animal abstract class provides a consistent way to interact with animals. Even if the Dog class changes its internal logic, the external interface (makeSound()) remains unchanged.

‍

3\. **Better Code Reusability:**

Abstract classes and interfaces promote code reuse by defining common behaviors that can be shared across multiple classes.

‍

Java

```
1abstract class Animal {
2  void eat() {
3    System.out.println("Animal is eating...");
4  }
5}
6
7class Dog extends Animal {
8  // Inherits the eat() method
9}
10
11class Cat extends Animal {
12  // Inherits the eat() method
13}
14
15public class Main {
16  public static void main(String[] args) {
17    Dog myDog = new Dog();
18    myDog.eat(); // Outputs: Animal is eating...
19    Cat myCat = new Cat();
20    myCat.eat(); // Outputs: Animal is eating...
21  }
22}
```

‍‍

The eat() method is defined once in the abstract class Animal and reused by all subclasses (Dog, Cat), eliminating code duplication.

‍

4\. **Increased Security:**

Hiding implementation details reduces the risk of accidental interference with internal workings.

‍

Example : 

Java

```
1abstract class Animal {
2  private String secret = "Sensitive data";
3  abstract void makeSound();
4  protected String getSecret() {
5    return secret; // Controlled access to sensitive data
6  }
7}
8
9class Dog extends Animal {
10  @Override
11  void makeSound() {
12    System.out.println("Bark");
13    System.out.println("Accessing secret: " + getSecret());
14  }
15}
16
17public class Main {
18  public static void main(String[] args) {
19    Animal myDog = new Dog();
20    myDog.makeSound();
21  }
22}
```

‍

The private field secret in Animal is hidden from external access. Subclasses can access it through controlled methods like getSecret(), ensuring security.

‍

### 👎🏼 Disadvantages of Abstract Classes : 

1.  **Complexity in Design:**

Designing abstract classes and interfaces requires careful planning and understanding of the system's requirements.

‍

Example: Imagine you're designing an abstraction for animals. If the abstraction is not carefully planned, it can lead to confusion or redundant code.

‍

Java

```
1abstract class Animal {
2  abstract void makeSound();
3  // Poorly thought-out abstraction: Adding unrelated behaviors
4  abstract void fly(); // Not all animals can fly
5  abstract void swim(); // Not all animals can swim
6}
7
8class Dog extends Animal {
9  @Override
10  void makeSound() {
11    System.out.println("Bark");
12  }
13  @Override
14  void fly() {
15    throw new UnsupportedOperationException("Dogs can't fly");
16  }
17  @Override
18  void swim() {
19    System.out.println("Dog is swimming");
20  }
21}
```

‍

**❓Why this is problematic:**

1.  **Irrelevant Methods:** The fly() method is irrelevant for animals like dogs and causes unnecessary implementation overhead.

2\. **Confusion:** Subclasses must implement methods that don’t make sense for them, leading to poor readability and maintainability.

3\. **Errors:** Using UnsupportedOperationException introduces runtime errors.

‍

💥 **Better Design :** 

‍

Java

```
1abstract class Animal {
2  abstract void makeSound();
3}
4
5interface Flyable {
6  void fly();
7}
8
9interface Swimmable {
10  void swim();
11}
12
13class Dog extends Animal implements Swimmable {
14  @Override
15  void makeSound() {
16    System.out.println("Bark");
17  }
18  @Override
19  public void swim() {
20    System.out.println("Dog is swimming");
21  }
22}
```

‍‍

**💭 Why this is better:**

• Only animals that can fly or swim implement the relevant interfaces, avoiding irrelevant methods in unrelated classes.

• This keeps the abstraction focused and reduces unnecessary complexity.

‍

2\. **Overhead:**

Abstraction introduces additional layers of complexity and method calls. This can slightly impact performance and make debugging harder, especially when abstractions are overused or unnecessary.

‍

Example: Let’s say you’re building a simple system to play animal sounds. Using abstraction for such a simple use case can introduce unnecessary overhead.

‍

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  void makeSound();
3}
4
5class Dog implements Animal {
6  @Override
7  public void makeSound() {
8    System.out.println("Bark");
9  }
10}
11
12class Cat implements Animal {
13  @Override
14  public void makeSound() {
15    System.out.println("Meow");
16  }
17}
18
19public class Main {
20  public static void main(String[] args) {
21    Animal dog = new Dog();
22    dog.makeSound(); // Outputs: Bark
23    Animal cat = new Cat();
24    cat.makeSound(); // Outputs: Meow
25  }
26}
```

‍

**❓** **Why this is problematic:**

1.  **Overhead:** Introducing the Animal interface for such a simple scenario adds an unnecessary level of indirection.

2\. **Performance:** The method calls go through the interface, adding a minor runtime overhead.

3\. **Readability:** For small and straightforward programs, this abstraction makes the code harder to follow.

‍

💥 **Simpler Solution Without Abstraction :** 

‍

Java

```
1class Dog {
2  void makeSound() {
3    System.out.println("Bark");
4  }
5}
6
7class Cat {
8  void makeSound() {
9    System.out.println("Meow");
10  }
11}
12
13public class Main {
14  public static void main(String[] args) {
15    Dog dog = new Dog();
16    dog.makeSound(); // Outputs: Bark
17    Cat cat = new Cat();
18    cat.makeSound(); // Outputs: Meow
19  }
20}
```

‍

💭 **Why this is better:**

• For small and simple programs, directly using concrete classes makes the code more straightforward.

• There’s no need for additional abstraction if you don’t anticipate future changes or extensions.

‍

### 2\. Interface 🔌

An interface defines a contract or a set of rules that a class must adhere to. It contains abstract methods that specify what a class should do, without dictating how it should be done.

‍

Unlike abstract classes, interfaces focus purely on behavior and do not include state (fields). Starting from Java 8, interfaces can also include default and static methods, enabling the addition of shared logic without breaking existing implementations.

‍

> Interfaces are a powerful tool for achieving abstraction and ensuring consistency across unrelated classes.

‍

Example : 

Java

```
1// Interface Animal
2interface Animal {
3  void makeSound(); // Abstract method
4  void sleep(); // Abstract method
5}
6
7// Specific implementation for Dog
8class Dog implements Animal {
9  @Override
10  public void makeSound() {
11    System.out.println("Bark");
12  }
13  @Override
14  public void sleep() {
15    System.out.println("Sleeping...");
16  }
17}
18
19// Specific implementation for Cat
20class Cat implements Animal {
21  @Override
22  public void makeSound() {
23    System.out.println("Meow");
24  }
25  @Override
26  public void sleep() {
27    System.out.println("Sleeping...");
28  }
29}
30
31public class Main {
32  public static void main(String[] args) {
33    Animal myDog = new Dog();
34    myDog.makeSound(); // Outputs: Bark
35    myDog.sleep(); // Outputs: Sleeping...
36    Animal myCat = new Cat();
37    myCat.makeSound(); // Outputs: Meow
38    myCat.sleep(); // Outputs: Sleeping...
39  }
40}
```

‍

### 👍🏼Advantages of Interface:

1.  **Decoupling:** Interfaces ensure that implementation details are completely separate from the method definitions.

‍

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  void makeSound();
3  void sleep();
4}
5
6class Dog implements Animal {
7  @Override
8  public void makeSound() {
9    System.out.println("Bark");
10  }
11  @Override
12  public void sleep() {
13    System.out.println("Dog is sleeping");
14  }
15}
16
17class Cat implements Animal {
18  @Override
19  public void makeSound() {
20    System.out.println("Meow");
21  }
22  @Override
23  public void sleep() {
24    System.out.println("Cat is sleeping");
25  }
26}
```

‍

By using interfaces, the Dog and Cat classes are decoupled from the Animal interface, allowing for flexible and interchangeable implementations.

‍

2.**Extensibility:** Any class that implements Animal must provide its own implementation for makeSound() and sleep().

‍

Java

```
1class Bird implements Animal {
2  @Override
3  public void makeSound() {
4    System.out.println("Chirp");
5  }
6
7  @Override
8  public void sleep() {
9    System.out.println("Bird is sleeping");
10  }
11}
```

‍

New animal types like Bird can be easily added by implementing the Animal interface, without modifying existing code.

‍

3\. **Standardization:** Interfaces define a contract, ensuring that all implementing classes behave consistently.

‍

Java

```
1public class Zoo {
2  public static void main(String[] args) {
3    Animal dog = new Dog();
4    Animal cat = new Cat();
5    Animal bird = new Bird();
6    dog.makeSound();
7    cat.makeSound();
8    bird.makeSound();
9  }
10}
```

‍

The Zoo class can interact with any Animal implementation, ensuring consistent behavior across different animal types.

‍

### 👎🏼Disadvantages of Interface:

1.  **Complexity**: Using interfaces can introduce additional complexity, especially in small projects where the benefits of decoupling and extensibility may not be as significant.

‍

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  void makeSound();
3  void sleep();
4}
5
6class Dog implements Animal {
7  @Override
8  public void makeSound() {
9    System.out.println("Bark");
10  }
11  @Override
12  public void sleep() {
13    System.out.println("Dog is sleeping");
14  }
15}
16
17class Cat implements Animal {
18  @Override
19  public void makeSound() {
20    System.out.println("Meow");
21  }
22  @Override
23  public void sleep() {
24    System.out.println("Cat is sleeping");
25  }
26}
```

‍

In small projects, the added complexity of defining and implementing interfaces may not be justified.

‍

2\. **Overhead:** Implementing multiple interfaces can lead to overhead in terms of code maintenance and readability, especially if the interfaces are not well-designed.

‍

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  void makeSound();
3  void sleep();
4}
5
6interface Pet {
7  void play();
8}
9
10class Dog implements Animal, Pet {
11  @Override
12  public void makeSound() {
13    System.out.println("Bark");
14  }
15  @Override
16  public void sleep() {
17    System.out.println("Dog is sleeping");
18  }
19  @Override
20  public void play() {
21    System.out.println("Dog is playing");
22  }
23}
```

‍

Implementing multiple interfaces can make the code harder to read and maintain, especially if the interfaces are complex. 🧩🔄

‍

## Abstract Class vs Interface in Java:

> Abstraction is a fundamental concept in object-oriented programming that allows us to focus on the essential details while hiding unnecessary complexities.

‍

Both abstract classes and interfaces are tools used to achieve abstraction, but they serve different purposes and have distinct use cases. ⚙️

‍

### 📦 Abstract Class:

1\. **Definition:**

An abstract class is a class that cannot be instantiated directly. It serves as a blueprint for other classes to derive from. 🏗️

‍

2\. **Method Implementation:**

An abstract class can contain both abstract methods (methods without an implementation) and concrete methods (methods with an implementation). 🔨

‍

3\. **Variables:**

Abstract classes can have member variables, including final, non-final, static, and non-static variables. 📜

‍

4\. **Constructors:**

Abstract classes can have constructors, which can be used to initialize variables in the abstract class when it is instantiated by a subclass. 🛠️

‍

### 🔌 Interface:

1\. **Definition:**

An interface is a reference type in Java, it is similar to a class, and it is a collection of abstract methods and static constants. 🔗

‍

2\. **Method Implementation:**

All methods in an interface are by default abstract and must be implemented by any class that implements the interface. From Java 8, interfaces can have default and static methods with concrete implementations. From Java 9, interfaces can also have private methods. 🏗️

‍

3\. **Variables:**

Variables declared in an interface are by default public, static, and final (constants). 🔑

‍

4\. **Constructors:**

Interfaces are purely designed to define a contract for classes to implement. They cannot have constructors because they do not manage or hold any state, and constructors are used to initialize an object's state. This design aligns with the principle that interfaces focus solely on defining behavior, leaving the implementation details to the implementing classes. 🧳

‍

### 🤔 When to use what?

🎀 **Consider using abstract classes if any of these statements apply to your situation:**

• In the Java application, there are some related classes that need to share some lines of code, then you can put these lines of code within the abstract class, and this abstract class should be extended by all these related classes. 🔄

‍

Java

```
1abstract class Animal {
2  void eat() {
3    System.out.println("Eating...");
4  }
5  abstract void makeSound();
6}
7
8class Dog extends Animal {
9  @Override
10  void makeSound() {
11    System.out.println("Bark");
12  }
13}
14
15class Cat extends Animal {
16  @Override
17  void makeSound() {
18    System.out.println("Meow");
19  }
20}
```

‍

The Animal abstract class contains shared code for eat(), and both Dog and Cat extend this class and provide their own implementation for makeSound().

‍

• You can define the non-static or non-final field(s) in the abstract class so that via a method you can access and modify the state of the object to which they belong.

‍

Java

```
1abstract class Animal {
2  protected String name;
3  abstract void makeSound();
4  void setName(String name) {
5    this.name = name;
6  }
7  String getName() {
8    return name;
9  }
10}
11
12class Dog extends Animal {
13  @Override
14  void makeSound() {
15    System.out.println("Bark");
16  }
17}
```

‍

The Animal abstract class has a non-static field name and methods to access and modify it. The Dog     class extends Animal and provides its own implementation for makeSound().

‍

• You can expect that the classes that extend an abstract class have many common methods or fields, or require access modifiers other than public (such as protected and private).

‍

Java

```
1abstract class Animal {
2  protected int age;
3  abstract void makeSound();
4  void setAge(int age) {
5    this.age = age;
6  }
7  int getAge() {
8    return age;
9  }
10}
11
12class Cat extends Animal {
13  @Override
14  void makeSound() {
15    System.out.println("Meow");
16  }
17}
```

• The Animal abstract class has a protected field age and methods to access and modify it. The Cat class extends Animal and provides its own implementation for makeSound().

‍

🎀 **Consider using interfaces if any of these statements apply to your situation:**  

*   It is a total abstraction, all methods declared within an interface must be implemented by the class(es) that implements this interface.

‍

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  void makeSound();
3  void sleep();
4}
5
6class Dog implements Animal {
7  @Override
8  public void makeSound() {
9    System.out.println("Bark");
10  }
11  @Override
12  public void sleep() {
13    System.out.println("Dog is sleeping");
14  }
15}
```

‍

The Animal interface defines the methods makeSound() and sleep(), and the Dog class implements these methods.

‍

• A class can implement more than one interface. It is called multiple inheritances.

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  void makeSound();
3}
4
5interface Pet {
6  void play();
7}
8
9class Dog implements Animal, Pet {
10  @Override
11  public void makeSound() {
12    System.out.println("Bark");
13  }
14  @Override
15  public void play() {
16    System.out.println("Dog is playing");
17  }
18}
```

‍

The Dog class implements both Animal and Pet interfaces, providing implementations for makeSound() and play() methods.

‍

*   You want to specify the behavior of a data type without worrying about its implementation.

‍

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  void makeSound();
3  void sleep();
4}
5
6class Cat implements Animal {
7  @Override
8  public void makeSound() {
9    System.out.println("Meow");
10  }
11  @Override
12  public void sleep() {
13    System.out.println("Cat is sleeping");
14  }
15}
```

‍

The Animal interface specifies the behavior for makeSound() and sleep(), and the Cat class provides the implementation for these methods.

## ‍

## When to Use Abstraction?

• When multiple objects share common behavior but have different implementations.

• To define a template or a standard for other classes to follow.

• To hide implementation details and expose only relevant functionalities to the users.

‍

## 👩🏼‍🎓Interview Questions : 

1\. What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface in Java? When would you use one over the other?

✨ Answer:

Abstract classes are used when classes share common functionality and state, whereas interfaces are used to define a contract for unrelated classes. Use abstract classes when you need shared code and interfaces for behavior enforcement.

‍

Java

```
1abstract class Animal {
2  String name;
3  Animal(String name) {
4    this.name = name;
5  }
6  abstract void sound();
7}
8
9interface Pet {
10  void play();
11}
12
13class Dog extends Animal implements Pet {
14  Dog(String name) {
15    super(name);
16  }
17  @Override
18  void sound() {
19    System.out.println(name + " barks.");
20  }
21  @Override
22  public void play() {
23    System.out.println(name + " plays fetch.");
24  }
25}
26
27public class Main {
28  public static void main(String[] args) {
29    Dog dog = new Dog("Buddy");
30    dog.sound(); // Output: Buddy barks.
31    dog.play(); // Output: Buddy plays fetch.
32  }
33}
```

‍

2\. Can an abstract class implement an interface? If yes, why would you do it?

✨Answer:

Yes, an abstract class can implement an interface to provide partial implementation. This is useful when some methods in the interface have common logic that can be shared across subclasses.

‍

Java

```
1interface Pet {
2  void play();
3}
4
5abstract class Animal implements Pet {
6  String name;
7  Animal(String name) {
8    this.name = name;
9  }
10  abstract void sound();
11  @Override
12  public void play() {
13    System.out.println(name + " plays.");
14  }
15}
16
17class Dog extends Animal {
18  Dog(String name) {
19    super(name);
20  }
21  @Override
22  void sound() {
23    System.out.println(name + " barks.");
24  }
25}
26
27public class Main {
28  public static void main(String[] args) {
29    Dog dog = new Dog("Buddy");
30    dog.sound(); // Output: Buddy barks.
31    dog.play(); // Output: Buddy plays.
32  }
33}
```

‍

3\. Why can’t we instantiate an abstract class? What would be the consequences if it were allowed?

✨Answer:

Abstract classes are incomplete blueprints meant to be extended. Allowing instantiation would violate the principle of abstraction, as abstract methods lack implementation.

‍

Java

```
1abstract class Animal {
2  abstract void sound();
3}
4
5class Dog extends Animal {
6  @Override
7  void sound() {
8    System.out.println("Dog barks.");
9  }
10}
11
12public class Main {
13  public static void main(String[] args) {
14    // Animal animal = new Animal(); // Compile-time error
15    Dog dog = new Dog();
16    dog.sound(); // Output: Dog barks.
17  }
18}
```

‍

4\. What are the limitations of using abstract classes over interfaces?

✨ Answer:

Abstract classes allow single inheritance only, whereas interfaces can be implemented by multiple classes, offering more flexibility.

‍

Abstract Class Example (Multiple Inheritance Error):

Java

```
1abstract class Animal {
2  abstract void sound();
3  void commonMethod() {
4    System.out.println("Animal common method.");
5  }
6}
7
8abstract class Mammal {
9  abstract void eat();
10  void commonMethod() {
11    System.out.println("Mammal common method.");
12  }
13}
14
15class Dog extends Animal, Mammal { // This will cause a compile-time error
16  @Override
17  void sound() {
18    System.out.println("Dog barks.");
19  }
20  @Override
21  void eat() {
22    System.out.println("Dog eats.");
23  }
24  @Override
25  void commonMethod() {
26    // Which commonMethod() to call? This causes ambiguity.
27  }
28}
```

‍

Interface Example (No Error):

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  void sound();
3  default void commonMethod() {
4    System.out.println("Animal common method.");
5  }
6}
7
8interface Pet {
9  void play();
10  default void commonMethod() {
11    System.out.println("Pet common method.");
12  }
13}
14
15class Dog implements Animal, Pet {
16  @Override
17  public void sound() {
18    System.out.println("Dog barks.");
19  }
20  @Override
21  public void play() {
22    System.out.println("Dog plays fetch.");
23  }
24  @Override
25  public void commonMethod() {
26    Animal.super.commonMethod();
27    Pet.super.commonMethod();
28    System.out.println("Dog's own common method.");
29  }
30}
31
32public class Main {
33  public static void main(String[] args) {
34    Dog dog = new Dog();
35    dog.sound(); // Output: Dog barks.
36    dog.play(); // Output: Dog plays fetch.
37    dog.commonMethod(); // Output: Animal common method.
38                        //         Pet common method.
39                        //         Dog's own common method.
40  }
41}
```

‍

5\. When should you not use an interface? Provide a practical example.

✨Answer:

Avoid interfaces when the implementing classes share common functionality or state. For example, if Dog and Cat both need an eat() method with shared logic, an abstract class like Animal is more appropriate than an interface.

‍

Java

```
1abstract class Animal {
2  void eat() {
3    System.out.println("Eating...");
4  }
5}
6
7class Dog extends Animal {
8  void sound() {
9    System.out.println("Dog barks.");
10  }
11}
12
13class Cat extends Animal {
14  void sound() {
15    System.out.println("Cat meows.");
16  }
17}
18
19public class Main {
20  public static void main(String[] args) {
21    Dog dog = new Dog();
22    Cat cat = new Cat();
23    dog.eat(); // Output: Eating...
24    dog.sound(); // Output: Dog barks.
25    cat.eat(); // Output: Eating...
26    cat.sound(); // Output: Cat meows.
27  }
28}
```

‍‍

6\. What are default methods in Java interfaces? Why were they introduced?

✨ Answer:

Default methods are methods in interfaces that have a body (implementation). They were introduced in Java 8 to provide backward compatibility. This allows interfaces to evolve by adding new methods without breaking existing implementations of the interface.

‍

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  void sound() {
3    System.out.println("This is a default animal sound.");
4  }
5}
6
7class Dog implements Animal {
8  // No need to override sound
9}
10
11public class Main {
12  public static void main(String[] args) {
13    Dog dog = new Dog();
14    dog.sound(); // Output: This is a default animal sound.
15  }
16}
```

‍

7\. What is the difference between abstract methods and default methods in an interface?

✨Answer:

Abstract methods have no body and must be implemented by a class that implements the interface. Default methods have a body and can be optionally overridden by implementing classes.

‍

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  void eat(); // Abstract method
3  default void sound() {
4    System.out.println("This is a default animal sound.");
5  }
6}
7
8class Dog implements Animal {
9  @Override
10  public void eat() {
11    System.out.println("Dog is eating.");
12  }
13}
14
15public class Main {
16  public static void main(String[] args) {
17    Dog dog = new Dog();
18    dog.eat(); // Output: Dog is eating.
19    dog.sound(); // Output: This is a default animal sound.
20  }
21}
```

‍‍

8\. Why do we need default methods in Java? Couldn’t we achieve the same with abstract classes?

✨Answer:

Default methods allow interfaces to add new behavior without forcing all implementing classes to change. Abstract classes cannot achieve this because Java does not allow multiple inheritance of classes. Interfaces with default methods enable flexibility while avoiding the diamond problem.

‍

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  default void sound() {
3    System.out.println("This is a default animal sound.");
4  }
5}
6
7abstract class Mammal {
8  abstract void eat();
9}
10
11class Dog extends Mammal implements Animal {
12  @Override
13  void eat() {
14    System.out.println("Dog is eating.");
15  }
16}
17
18public class Main {
19  public static void main(String[] args) {
20    Dog dog = new Dog();
21    dog.eat(); // Output: Dog is eating.
22    dog.sound(); // Output: This is a default animal sound.
23  }
24}
```

‍‍

9\. Can a class implement an interface without overriding its default methods?

✨Answer:

Yes, a class can implement an interface without overriding its default methods. The default implementation will be inherited. However, the class can override the method if it needs custom behavior.

‍

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  default void sound() {
3    System.out.println("This is a default animal sound.");
4  }
5}
6
7class Dog implements Animal {
8  // No need to override sound
9}
10
11public class Main {
12  public static void main(String[] args) {
13    Dog dog = new Dog();
14    dog.sound(); // Output: This is a default animal sound.
15  }
16}
```

‍‍

10\. What happens if a class implements an interface with a default method and also inherits the same method from a superclass? Which one gets priority?

✨Answer:

The method from the superclass takes priority over the default method in the interface. The class will inherit the superclass's method unless it explicitly overrides it.

‍

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  default void sound() {
3    System.out.println("This is a default animal sound.");
4  }
5}
6
7class Mammal {
8  public void sound() {
9    System.out.println("This is a mammal sound.");
10  }
11}
12
13class Dog extends Mammal implements Animal {
14  // No need to override sound
15}
16
17public class Main {
18  public static void main(String[] args) {
19    Dog dog = new Dog();
20    dog.sound(); // Output: This is a mammal sound.
21  }
22}
```

‍

11\. What happens if a class implements two interfaces that have a default method with the same name?

✨Answer:

If a class implements two interfaces with the same default method, it must override the method to resolve the ambiguity explicitly.

‍

🫎 **Example Scenario: Animal Sounds**

Imagine two interfaces, DogBehaviour and CatBehaviour, both of which have a makeSound() default method. A class AnimalProcessor implements both interfaces and needs to resolve the conflict explicitly.

‍

Java

```
1interface DogBehavior {
2  default void makeSound() {
3    System.out.println("Dog barks.");
4  }
5}
6
7interface CatBehavior {
8  default void makeSound() {
9    System.out.println("Cat meows.");
10  }
11}
12
13class AnimalProcessor implements DogBehavior, CatBehavior {
14  // Resolving the conflict by overriding the method
15  @Override
16  public void makeSound() {
17    System.out.println(
18        "Resolving conflict between DogBehavior and CatBehavior:");
19
20    // Call the default method from DogBehavior
21    DogBehavior.super.makeSound();
22
23    // Call the default method from CatBehavior
24    CatBehavior.super.makeSound();
25
26    // Adding custom behavior
27    System.out.println(
28        "Custom behavior: AnimalProcessor decides which sound to make.");
29  }
30}
31
32public class Main {
33  public static void main(String[] args) {
34    AnimalProcessor processor = new AnimalProcessor();
35    processor.makeSound();
36  }
37}
```

‍

Explanation:

○ **Default Methods Conflict:**

Both DogBehaviour and CatBehaviour define a makeSound() default method. When AnimalProcessor implements both interfaces, the compiler cannot determine which version to use.

‍

○ **Conflict Resolution:**

To resolve the conflict, AnimalProcessor explicitly overrides the makeSound() method and uses InterfaceName.super.methodName() to call the specific default method from each interface.

‍

○ **Custom Logic:**

The makeSound() method in AnimalProcessor adds custom behavior after calling the default methods from the interfaces.

‍

12\. Is it possible to override a default method and make it abstract in a subclass or interface? Why or why not?

✨Answer:

No, a default method cannot be overridden and made abstract. Once a default method is defined, overriding implementations must provide a concrete implementation.

‍

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  default void sound() {
3    System.out.println("This is a default animal sound.");
4  }
5}
6
7class Dog implements Animal {
8  @Override
9  public void sound() {
10    System.out.println("Dog barks.");
11  }
12}
13
14public class Main {
15  public static void main(String[] args) {
16    Dog dog = new Dog();
17    dog.sound(); // Output: Dog barks.
18  }
19}
```

‍

13\. Can default methods access instance variables of the implementing class? Why or why not?

✨Answer:

No, default methods cannot access instance variables of the implementing class because interfaces do not have state. Default methods are stateless and only work with parameters and their internal logic.

‍

Java

```
1interface Animal {
2  default void sound() {
3    System.out.println("This is a default animal sound.");
4  }
5}
6
7class Dog implements Animal {
8  private String name = "Buddy";
9  public void printName() {
10    System.out.println("Dog's name is " + name);
11  }
12}
13
14public class Main {
15  public static void main(String[] args) {
16    Dog dog = new Dog();
17    dog.printName(); // Output: Dog's name is Buddy
18    dog.sound(); // Output: This is a default animal sound.
19  }
20}
```

‍

14\. What are some limitations of default methods in comparison to methods in abstract classes?

✨Answer:

• Default methods cannot have instance variables.

• They cannot use super to refer to the implementing class’s parent.

• Abstract classes can have constructors and fields, but interfaces cannot.

‍

Java

```
1// Abstract class example showcasing fields, constructors, and abstract methods
2abstract class Animal {
3  String name; // Instance variable
4  // Constructor to initialize the name
5  Animal(String name) {
6    this.name = name;
7  }
8  // Abstract method
9  abstract void sound();
10  // Non-abstract method to demonstrate additional functionality
11  void eat() {
12    System.out.println(name + " is eating.");
13  }
14}
15
16// Interface example showcasing default methods and their limitations
17interface Playable {
18  // Default method
19  default void play() {
20    System.out.println("Playing with the animal.");
21  }
22  // Attempt to declare an instance variable (not allowed in interfaces)
23  String name = "Buddy";
24  // Interfaces can only contain static final variables, which are essentially
25  // constants. Since it is static you cannot call it instance variable.
26  default void setName(String name) {
27    this.name = name; // Error: Interfaces cannot have instance variables
28  }
29}
30
31// Dog class extends abstract class Animal and implements interface Playable
32class Dog extends Animal implements Playable {
33  // Constructor calling the abstract class constructor
34  Dog(String name) {
35    super(name);
36  }
37
38  // Overriding the abstract method
39  @Override
40  void sound() {
41    System.out.println(name + " barks.");
42  }
43
44  // Uncommenting the following code will cause an error because default methods
45  // cannot use super to refer to parent methods
46
47  @Override
48  public void play() {
49    super.play(); // Error: Cannot use super to refer to a parent method in an
50                  // interface
51  }
52}
53
54public class Main {
55  public static void main(String[] args) {
56    // Abstract class functionality
57    Dog dog = new Dog("Buddy");
58    dog.sound(); // Output: Buddy barks.
59    dog.eat(); // Output: Buddy is eating.
60
61    // Interface functionality
62    dog.play(); // Output: Playing with the animal.
63  }
64}
```

‍

## 🎉 Conclusion : 

Abstraction is a powerful tool in OOP that simplifies code by focusing on what an object does rather than how it does it. By using abstract classes and interfaces, developers can create flexible, reusable, and maintainable code. Mastering abstraction is crucial for designing robust and scalable Java applications.\n\n---\n\n## Source: https://codewitharyan.com/tech-blogs/generics-and-wildcards\n\n# Generics and Wildcards

🐈‍⬛ Github Codes Link: [https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode](https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode)

‍

Generics and wildcards are essential features in Java's type system, introduced to enable type safety and reusability in code. These features allow developers to write generic classes and methods that operate on different types while maintaining compile-time type safety.

## 📦What Are Generics?

> Generics provide a way to parameterize types in Java. They enable classes, interfaces, and methods to operate on various data types without the need to specify the exact type at compile time. By using generics, developers can ensure type safety, reduce code duplication, and improve readability.

‍

## Types of Java Generics : 

### 1\. Generic Method:

Generic Java method takes a parameter and returns some value after performing a task. It is exactly like a normal function, however, a generic method has type parameters that are cited by actual type. This allows the generic method to be used in a more general way. The compiler takes care of the type of safety which enables programmers to code easily since they do not have to perform long, individual type castings.

‍

**Example :** 

Java

```
1class Test {
2  // A Generic method example
3  static <T> void genericDisplay(T element) {
4    System.out.println(element.getClass().getName() + " = " + element);
5  }
6
7  // Driver method
8  public static void main(String[] args) {
9    // Calling generic method with Integer argument
10    genericDisplay(11);
11
12    // Calling generic method with String argument
13    genericDisplay("CodeWithAryan");
14
15    // Calling generic method with double argument
16    genericDisplay(1.0);
17  }
18}
```

‍

📤 **Output :** 

```
java.lang.Integer = 11
java.lang.String = CodeWithAryan
	java.lang.Double = 1.0‍
```

‍

🧩 **Explanation :** 

In the provided code snippet, the genericDisplay() method is a generic method, which means it can operate on objects of various types while maintaining type safety. The <T> syntax indicates that T is a type parameter, allowing the method to accept any type of argument. This flexibility is what makes the method generic. The method prints the class name and value of the passed argument, demonstrating how it can handle different data types (Integer, String, and Double) without requiring multiple method overloads.

‍

The genericDisplay() method is declared as static to allow it to be called without creating an instance of the Test class. This is useful for utility methods that don't rely on instance variables. By making the method static, it can be directly invoked using the class name, as shown in the main method.

‍

### 2\. Generic Classes:

A generic class is implemented exactly like a non-generic class. The only difference is that it contains a type parameter section. There can be more than one type of parameter, separated by a comma. The classes, which accept one or more parameters, are known as parameterized classes or parameterized types.

‍

**Example :** 

Java

```
1// We use < > to specify Parameter type
2class Test<T> {
3  // An object of type T is declared
4  T obj;
5  Test(T obj) {
6    this.obj = obj;
7  } // constructor
8  public T getObject() {
9    return this.obj;
10  }
11}
12
13// Driver class to test above
14class Main {
15  public static void main(String[] args) {
16    // instance of Integer type
17    Test<Integer> iObj = new Test<Integer>(15);
18    System.out.println(iObj.getObject());
19    // instance of String type
20    Test<String> sObj = new Test<String>("CodeWithAryan");
21    System.out.println(sObj.getObject());
22  }
23}
```

‍

📤 **Output :** 

```
15
CodeWithAryan
```

🧩 **Explanation :** 

the Test<T> class is a generic class that can handle any type specified by the user. The <T> syntax indicates that T is a type parameter, allowing the class to operate on objects of various types while maintaining type safety. This means that the class can be used with different data types without requiring multiple class definitions. The constructor Test(T obj) initializes the object of type T, and the getObject() method returns this object. This flexibility makes the code more reusable and easier to maintain.

‍

In the Main class, instances of Test are created with Integer and String types, demonstrating the versatility of generics. The iObj instance is created with an Integer type, and the sObj instance is created with a String type. The getObject() method is called on both instances, and the output shows the values 15 and CodeWithAryan, respectively. This confirms that the generic class can handle different data types seamlessly, providing a powerful tool for developers to write more flexible and type-safe code.

‍

> NOTE : We can also pass multiple Type parameters in Generic classes.

‍

Example : 

Java

```
1// We use < > to specify Parameter type
2class Test<T, U> {
3  T obj1; // An object of type T
4  U obj2; // An object of type U
5
6  // constructor
7  Test(T obj1, U obj2) {
8    this.obj1 = obj1;
9    this.obj2 = obj2;
10  }
11
12  // To print objects of T and U
13  public void print() {
14    System.out.println(obj1);
15    System.out.println(obj2);
16  }
17}
18
19// Driver class to test above
20class Main {
21  public static void main(String[] args) {
22    Test<String, Integer> obj = new Test<String, Integer>(15, CodeWithAryan);
23    obj.print();
24  }
25}
```

📤 Output : 

```
15
CodeWithAryan
```

🧩 Explanation : 

the Test<T, U> class demonstrates the use of multiple type parameters. The class can handle two different types specified by the user, indicated by <T, U>. This allows the class to operate on objects of two different types while maintaining type safety. The constructor Test(T obj1, U obj2) initializes objects of types T and U, and the print() method displays these objects. This example highlights the versatility of generics in handling multiple types, making the code more reusable and type-safe.

‍

In the Main class, an instance of Test is created with String and Integer types. The obj instance is created with String and Integer types, and the print() method is called on this instance. The output shows the values 15 and CodeWithAryan, confirming that the generic class can handle multiple data types simultaneously. This demonstrates the power of generics in Java, allowing developers to write more flexible and reusable code that can handle different data types without requiring multiple class definitions.

‍

> NOTE : Generics Work Only with Reference Types:

‍

When we declare an instance of a generic type, the type argument passed to the type parameter must be a reference type. We cannot use primitive data types like int, char, boolean etc.

```
Test<int> obj = new Test<int>(20);
```

The above line results in a compile-time error that can be resolved using type wrappers to encapsulate a primitive type. For example, we can use the Integer wrapper class instead of int:

```
Test<Integer> obj = new Test<Integer>(20);
```

But primitive type arrays can be passed to the type parameter because arrays are reference types.

```
ArrayList<int[]> a = new ArrayList<>();
```

‍

## 💥Benefits of Generics : 

### 1\. Type Safety:

○ Generics prevent runtime type errors by catching them at compile time.

‍

 Example :

Java

```
1// We use < > to specify Parameter type
2class Test<T> {
3  // An object of type T is declared
4  T obj;
5  Test(T obj) {
6    this.obj = obj;
7  } // constructor
8  public T getObject() {
9    return this.obj;
10  }
11}
12
13// Driver class to test above
14class Main {
15  public static void main(String[] args) {
16    // instance of Integer type
17    Test<Integer> iObj = new Test<Integer>(15);
18    System.out.println(iObj.getObject());
19
20    // instance of String type
21    Test<String> sObj = new Test<String>("GeeksForGeeks");
22    System.out.println(sObj.getObject());
23    iObj = sObj; // This results an error
24  }
25}
```

‍

Even though iObj and sObj are of type Test, they are the references to different types because their type parameters differ. Generics add type safety through this and prevent errors.

‍

### 2\. Code Reusability:

○ Generic classes and methods can work with any data type, reducing code duplication.

‍

Example : 

Java

```
1public class GenericReusability {
2  // A generic method to print elements of any type
3  public static <T> void printArray(T[] array) {
4    for (T element : array) {
5      System.out.print(element + " ");
6    }
7    System.out.println();
8  }
9
10  public static void main(String[] args) {
11    Integer[] intArray = {1, 2, 3, 4};
12    String[] stringArray = {"Apple", "Banana", "Cherry"};
13    printArray(intArray); // Works with Integer
14    printArray(stringArray); // Works with String
15  }
16}
```

‍

### 3\. Readability and Maintainability:

○ Explicit type declarations make the code easier to understand and maintain.

‍

Example : 

Java

```
1// Custom Generic ArrayList
2class MyArrayList<T> {
3  private Object[] elements;
4  private int size = 0;
5  public MyArrayList() {
6    elements = new Object[10]; // Default capacity
7  }
8
9  public void add(T element) {
10    if (size == elements.length) {
11      resize();
12    }
13    elements[size++] = element;
14  }
15
16  public T get(int index) {
17    if (index >= size || index < 0) {
18      throw new IndexOutOfBoundsException("Index out of bounds");
19    }
20    return (T) elements[index];
21  }
22
23  private void resize() {
24    Object[] newElements = new Object[elements.length * 2];
25    System.arraycopy(elements, 0, newElements, 0, elements.length);
26    elements = newElements;
27  }
28}
29
30// Without explicit type declaration
31MyArrayList list1 = new MyArrayList();
32list1.add("Hello");
33list1.add("World");
34list1.add(1);
35list1.add('a');
36
37// With explicit type declaration
38MyArrayList<String> list2 = new MyArrayList<>();
39list2.add("Hello");
40list2.add("World");
```

‍

In the first example, the type of list is not immediately clear, which can make the code harder to understand and maintain. In the second example, the explicit type declaration ArrayList<String> makes it clear that list is an ArrayList of String objects, improving readability and maintainability.

‍

### 4\. Elimination of Type Casting:

○ Generics make errors to appear compile time than at run time (It’s always better to know problems in your code at compile time rather than making your code fail at run time).

Suppose you want to create an ArrayList that store name of students, and if by mistake the programmer adds an integer object instead of a string, the compiler allows it. But, when we retrieve this data from ArrayList, it causes problems at runtime.

‍

❓Problem : 

Java

```
1import java.util.*;
2
3class Test {
4  public static void main(String[] args) {
5    // Creating an ArrayList without any type specified
6    MyArrayList list = new MyArrayList();
7    list.add("Sachin");
8    list.add("Rahul");
9    list.add(10); // Compiler allows this
10    String s1 = (String) list.get(0);
11    String s2 = (String) list.get(1);
12    // Causes Runtime Exception
13    String s3 = (String) list.get(2);
14  }
15}
```

‍

📤 Output :

```
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ClassCastException: 
 java.lang.Integer cannot be cast to java.lang.String
```

🧩 Solution : 

When defining ArrayList, we can specify that this list can take only String objects.

‍

Java

```
1import java.util.*;
2
3class Test {
4  public static void main(String[] args) {
5    // Creating a an ArrayList with String specified
6    MyArrayList<String> list = new MyArrayList<String>();
7    list.add("Sachin");
8    list.add("Rahul");
9    list.add(10);
10
11    // Typecasting is not needed
12    String s1 = list.get(0);
13    String s2 = list.get(1);
14    String s3 = list.get(2);
15  }
16}
```

‍

📤 Output: 

```
15: error: no suitable method found for add(int)
    al.add(10);
```

‍

## 🌪️ What Are Wildcards in Generics?

> Wildcards are special symbols used in generics to represent an unknown type. They provide flexibility when working with generic types and allow developers to define relationships between different types.

‍

Example : 

Java

```
1import java.util.List;
2
3public class WildcardExample {
4  public static void printList(List<?> list) {
5    for (Object item : list) {
6      System.out.println(item);
7    }
8  }
9
10  public static void main(String[] args) {
11    List<String> stringList = List.of("Apple", "Banana", "Cherry");
12    printList(stringList);
13    List<Integer> intList = List.of(1, 2, 3);
14    printList(intList);
15  }
16}
```

## Types of Wildcards : 

### 1\. Unbounded Wildcard (?) : 

• Represents an unknown type.

• Useful when the type is not relevant to the logic.

‍

Example:

Java

```
1import java.util.List;
2
3public class UnboundedWildcardExample {
4  public static void printList(List<?> list) {
5    for (Object item : list) {
6      System.out.println(item);
7    }
8  }
9
10  public static void main(String[] args) {
11    List<String> stringList = List.of("Apple", "Banana", "Cherry");
12    printList(stringList);
13    List<Integer> intList = List.of(1, 2, 3);
14    printList(intList);
15  }
16}
```

‍

### 2\. Upper-Bounded Wildcard (? extends Type) : 

• Restricts the type to Type or its subclasses.

• Useful for read-only operations where the specific type is not required.

‍

Example:

Java

```
1import java.util.List;
2
3public class UpperBoundedWildcardExample {
4  public static void printNumbers(List<? extends Number> list) {
5    for (Number number : list) {
6      System.out.println(number);
7    }
8  }
9
10  public static void main(String[] args) {
11    List<Integer> intList = List.of(1, 2, 3);
12    printNumbers(intList);
13    List<Double> doubleList = List.of(1.1, 2.2, 3.3);
14    printNumbers(doubleList);
15    List<String> stringList = List.of("a", "b", "c");
16    printNumbers(stringList);
17    // Error: incompatible types: List<String> cannot be converted to List<?
18    // extends Number>
19  }
20}
```

‍

### 3. Lower-Bounded Wildcard (? super Type)

• Restricts the type to Type or its superclasses.

• Useful for write operations.

‍

Example:

Java

```
1import java.util.ArrayList;
2import java.util.List;
3
4public class LowerBoundedWildcardExample {
5  public static void addNumbers(List<? super Integer> list) {
6    list.add(42);
7  }
8  public static void main(String[] args) {
9    List<Number> numberList = new ArrayList<>();
10    addNumbers(numberList);
11    System.out.println(numberList);
12  }
13}
```

## 💭 Generics vs Wildcards : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1741749952038-image.png)

‍

## 🤔 When to Use what ? 

The decision to use generics or wildcards in Java largely depends on the context of your code and how you plan to interact with the objects.

‍

Let’s break this down into specific method use cases and scenarios where one is more suitable than the other.

### 🏷️Why Use a Generic Instead of a Wildcard? 

1\. **When You Need Type Consistency Across the Method :** 

If a method must ensure that all arguments or returned values are of the same specific type, use generics. Generics explicitly define the type and provide compile-time type safety.

‍

Java

```
1import java.util.ArrayList;
2import java.util.List;
3
4// Utility class for list operations
5public class ListUtils {
6  // Generic method to add two elements of the same type to a list
7  public static <T> void addElementsToList(
8      List<T> list, T firstElement, T secondElement) {
9    list.add(firstElement);
10    list.add(secondElement);
11    System.out.println(
12        "Elements added: " + firstElement + ", " + secondElement);
13  }
14}
15
16// A simple Person class for custom object example
17class Person {
18  private String name;
19  public Person(String name) {
20    this.name = name;
21  }
22  @Override
23  public String toString() {
24    return name;
25  }
26}
27
28public class Main {
29  public static void main(String[] args) {
30    // Example with a list of Strings
31    List<String> stringList = new ArrayList<>();
32    ListUtils.addElementsToList(stringList, "Apple", "Banana");
33    System.out.println("Updated String List: " + stringList);
34
35    // Example with a list of Integers
36    List<Integer> intList = new ArrayList<>();
37    ListUtils.addElementsToList(intList, 10, 20);
38    System.out.println("Updated Integer List: " + intList);
39
40    // Example with a list of Doubles
41    List<Double> doubleList = new ArrayList<>();
42    ListUtils.addElementsToList(doubleList, 1.5, 2.5);
43    System.out.println("Updated Double List: " + doubleList);
44
45    // Example with a list of custom objects
46    List<Person> personList = new ArrayList<>();
47    ListUtils.addElementsToList(
48        personList, new Person("Alice"), new Person("Bob"));
49    System.out.println("Updated Person List: " + personList);
50  }
51}
```

‍

2\. **When You’re Creating or Adding to a Collection**

Generics are required when you’re adding elements to a collection. Wildcards (?) don’t allow additions because the compiler cannot guarantee the type safety for the unknown type.In the above example, we have three lists: intList, doubleList, and personList.

For each list, we are adding an element of the same data type, which is made possible through the use of Generics. 

‍

Java

```
1public static void main(String[] args) {
2  // Example with a list of Strings
3  List<String> stringList = new ArrayList<>();
4  ListUtils.addElementsToList(stringList, "Apple", "Banana");
5  System.out.println("Updated String List: " + stringList);
6
7  // Example with a list of Integers
8  List<Integer> intList = new ArrayList<>();
9  ListUtils.addElementsToList(intList, 10, 20);
10  System.out.println("Updated Integer List: " + intList);
11
12  // Example with a list of Doubles
13  List<Double> doubleList = new ArrayList<>();
14  ListUtils.addElementsToList(doubleList, 1.5, 2.5);
15  System.out.println("Updated Double List: " + doubleList);
16
17  // Example with a list of custom objects
18  List<Person> personList = new ArrayList<>();
19  ListUtils.addElementsToList(
20      personList, new Person("Alice"), new Person("Bob"));
21  System.out.println("Updated Person List: " + personList);
22}
```

‍

**Example with Wildcards (Doesn't Work for Adding):**

Java

```
1public void addToWildcardList(List<?> list, Object element) {
2     list.add(element);  // Compile-time error: Cannot add to a List<?>
3}
```

‍

Here, generics are essential if you need to add elements, as they provide a known type, while wildcards (?) are read-only for safety.

‍

3\. **When You Need a Specific Type in Return :** 

If a method needs to return an object of a specific type, use generics. Wildcards (?) make the type unknown, which is not useful for returned values.Extending our Previous example, if we were to create a method to get the first element of the list, we would use generics for the same, as the compiler will be able to determine the return type of the function which would not be possible in case of wildcards

‍

Java

```
1public <T> T getFirstElement(List<T> list) {
2  return list.size() == 0 ? null : list.get(0); // Compiler knows the type of T
3}
4
5public static void main(String[] args) {
6  // Example with a list of Strings
7  List<String> stringList = new ArrayList<>();
8  ListUtils.addElementsToList(stringList, "Apple", "Banana");
9  System.out.println("Updated String List: " + stringList);
10  String firstString = getFirstString(stringList);
11
12  // Example with a list of Integers
13  List<Integer> intList = new ArrayList<>();
14  ListUtils.addElementsToList(intList, 10, 20);
15  System.out.println("Updated Integer List: " + intList);
16  Integer firstInteger = getFirstElement(intList);
17}
```

‍

Using a wildcard would not allow the compiler to determine the type of the returned object, making it less flexible.

‍

This approach requires returning an Object every time from the function using wildcards, which is not ideal. Although wrapper classes inherit from the Object class in Java, the return type should be the actual wrapper class and not the Object type. This ensures type safety and clarity in the code, allowing for more precise and maintainable implementations.

‍

Java

```
1// Wildcard method that gets the first element
2public Object getFirstElementWithWildcard(List<?> list) {
3  return list.get(0); // Returns as Object because the type is unknown
4}
5
6public static void main(String[] args) {
7  List<Integer> numbers = List.of(1, 2, 3);
8  Object firstElement =
9      getFirstElementWithWildcard(numbers); // Returns as Object
10  System.out.println("First Element (Object): " + firstElement);
11
12  // Must cast manually to Integer
13  int firstNumber = (Integer) firstElement;
14  System.out.println("First Number (int): " + firstNumber);
15}
```

‍

In order to get the actual type while using wildcards, we will have to manually perform type casting, which is not an efficient practice. This approach can lead to potential runtime errors and reduces the clarity and maintainability of the code. 

‍

### 🌪️ Why Use a Wildcard Instead of a Generic ? 

1\. **When You’re Only Reading from a Collection**

If a method only needs to read from a collection and doesn’t care about the exact type, use wildcards. Wildcards provide flexibility and are less restrictive than generics.

‍

Java

```
1public class Main {
2  // Generic method that requires a specific type
3  public <T> void printList1(List<T> list) {
4    for (T item : list) {
5      System.out.println(item); // Can read items of type T
6    }
7  }
8  // Wildcard method that doesn't require a specific type
9  public void printList2(List<?> list) {
10    for (Object item : list) {
11      System.out.println(item); // Can read all items
12    }
13  }
14
15  public static void main(String[] args) {
16    List<String> strings = List.of("A", "B", "C");
17    List<Integer> integers = List.of(1, 2, 3);
18    List newList =
19        List.of(1, 2, 3, 'a', 'b', 'c', "Code", "With", "Aryan", 1.23, 2.5);
20    // Works because the generic type matches the list type
21    printList1(strings); // T is inferred as String
22    printList1(integers); // T is inferred as Integer
23    printList1(newList); // doesn't work because the type is not specified in
24                         // the newList
25    printList2(
26        strings); // works because wildcards do not care about exact types
27    printList2(
28        integers); // works because wildcards do not care about exact types
29    printList2(
30        newList); //  works because wildcards do not care about exact types
31  }
32}
```

‍

If you were to use generics instead of wildcards in the printList method, the method would require a specific type when called. This makes the method less flexible, as it can no longer handle lists of different types without specifying the type explicitly.

‍

## 🌟 Best Practices : 

1\. Use generics for strongly typed classes and methods.

2\. Use wildcards when flexibility in type is needed, especially in method parameters.

3\. Use upper-bounded wildcards for read-only operations and lower-bounded wildcards for write operations.

4\. Avoid overusing wildcards to keep the code readable and maintainable.

‍

## 🎉 Conclusion : 

Generics and wildcards are powerful tools that enhance type safety and flexibility in OOP. By understanding their differences and use cases, developers can write more reusable, maintainable, and efficient code. Mastery of generics and wildcards is essential for handling collections, frameworks, and APIs effectively.\n\n---\n\n## Source: https://codewitharyan.com/tech-blogs/access-modifiers\n\n# Access Modifiers

Topic Tags:

oopsystem designlld

‍‍🐈‍⬛ Github Codes Link: [https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode](https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode)

‍

> Access modifiers 🔒 are fundamental in Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) 💻 as they control the visibility 👀 and accessibility of classes, methods, and variables in a program. They play a crucial role in encapsulation 🔐 by restricting or allowing access to certain parts of the code based on the specified modifier.

‍

Access modifiers prevent data members or functions of one class from tampering 🛑 with another class while restricting its access. It allows us to select which members can be accessed directly by outside functions and which are not. ✅

# 🏗️ Project Structure Overview:

Before diving into the types of access modifiers 🔒, let me walk you through the project structure that will be used to demonstrate their behavior.

Below is an image 🖼️ of the project setup:

‍

### Project Structure:

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1741746983943-image.png)

• **src/:**

The root folder containing all the code files.

‍

• **demoPackage/:**

This package contains the DemoClass, which we will use to demonstrate the usage of different access modifiers in Java.

‍

• **utilityClasses/:**

This package contains the following classes:

1.  PublicClassExample: Demonstrates the use of the public access modifier.
2.  PrivateClassExample: Demonstrates the use of the private access modifier.
3.  ProtectedClassExample: Demonstrates the use of the protected access modifier.
4.  DefaultClassExample: Demonstrates the default (package-private) access modifier.

‍

• **Main.java:**

The main entry point for running the project.

‍

### This structure allows us to clearly show how access modifiers work across:

1\. Classes within the same package (demoPackage).

2\. Classes in different packages (utilityClasses).

‍

Each of these access modifiers—public, private, protected, and default—will be explored using these classes and their relationships.

‍

# Types of Access Modifiers in Java :
