System DesignLLD

### 🐈‍⬛ Github Codes Link: [https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode](https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode)

## ‍

## Low-Level Design: Snake & Food Game 🐍🍏

The Snake & Food game is a classic arcade game where the player controls a snake moving on a grid, attempting to eat food items while avoiding collisions. The game is simple in concept but requires strategic movement to maximize the snake's growth without running into walls or itself.

## Rules of the Game : 

### Setup : 

• The game is played on an N x N grid.

The snake starts at an initial position with a small length.

• The player controls the movement of the snake using directional inputs (Up, Down, Left, Right).

‍

### Game Mechanics : 

• The snake moves one step at a time in the chosen direction.

• When the snake eats a food item, it grows in length, and a new food item spawns at a random position on the grid.

• The game continues until the snake collides with the wall or itself, at which point the game ends.

‍‍

### Game Over Conditions : 

• The game ends if the snake collides with the boundary of the grid.

• The game also ends if the snake collides with itself (i.e., its own body).

‍

## Interview Setting 🤝

**Point 1: Introduction and Vague Problem Statement :** 

Interviewer: Let's start with a basic problem statement. Design a Snake & Food game system.

‍

Candidate: Sure! Based on my understanding, the game follows these key mechanics:

• A snake moves in a grid-based environment.

• The player controls the snake's movement direction.

• The snake grows when it eats food.

• The game ends if the snake collides with a wall or itself.

Is this the kind of game flow you had in mind?

‍

🧑‍💼Interviewer: Yes, you're aligned with the requirements. Please continue.

‍

**Point 2: Clarifying Requirements :** 

🧑‍💼Interviewer: We want a simple system that:

• Supports a standard grid-based snake game with width × height dimensions.

• Allows a player to control the snake's movement.

• Supports food placement and consumption mechanics.

• Detects collisions (self-collision and wall collision).

• Ensures proper movement and growth.

• Tracks the game score.

‍

Candidate: To clarify, the key requirements are:

• A game grid of specific width and height.

• A single snake entity, controlled by the player.

• Food objects that appear at predefined positions on the grid.

• Movement mechanics, updating the game state with each move.

• Collision detection for both walls and snake's body.

• Scoring mechanism based on food consumption.

‍

🧑‍💼Interviewer: That sounds perfect. Let's proceed.

‍

**Point 3: Identifying Key Components :** 

Candidate: Now that we have the requirements clarified, let's identify the key components of our Snake Game system.

Game Board:

• Class: GameBoard

• Description: Represents the game area with specified width and height where the snake moves and food appears.

Java

```
1public class GameBoard {
2	    private int width;
3	    private int height;
4	    
5	    public GameBoard(int width, int height) {
6	        this.width = width;
7	        this.height = height;
8	    }
9	    
10	    public int getWidth() { return width; }
11	    public int getHeight() { return height; }
12	}
```

‍

Snake:

• Class: Snake

Description: Represents the snake, which consists of a list of body segments and moves in a given direction.

‍

Java

```
1public class Snake {
2	    private Deque<Pair> body; // Snake body as a deque of positions
3	    private Map<Pair, Boolean> positionMap; // For O(1) collision check	    
4	    public Snake() {
5	        this.body = new LinkedList<>();
6	        this.positionMap = new HashMap<>();
7	        // Initialize snake at position [0,0]
8	        Pair initialPos = new Pair<>(0, 0);
9	        this.body.offerFirst(initialPos);
10	        this.positionMap.put(initialPos, true);
11	    }
12	    
13	    // Methods for movement, growth, collision detection
14}
```

‍

Food : 

• Class: Food

Description: Represents the food items that the snake consumes.

‍

Java

```
1public class Food {
2	    private int[][] foodPositions;
3	    private int currentFoodIndex;	    
4	    public Food(int[][] foodPositions) {
5	        this.foodPositions = foodPositions;
6	        this.currentFoodIndex = 0;
7	    }	    
8	    // Methods for food management
9	}
```

‍

Game Controller:

• Class: SnakeGame

Description: Controls the game flow, processes moves, and tracks the score.

‍

Java

```
1public class SnakeGame {
2	    private GameBoard board;
3	    private Snake snake;
4	    private Food food;
5	    private int score;	    
6	    // Game initialization and movement logic
7}
```

‍

Pair:

• Class: Pair

Description: Represents a coordinate on the game board with row and column values. It is used for positions of the snake and food, enabling clearer and reusable code for 2D coordinates.

Java

```
1public class Pair {
2	    int row;
3	    int col;
4	    public Pair(int row , int col){
5	        this.row = row;
6	        this.col = col;
7	    }
8	    public int getRow(){
9	        return row;
10	    }
11	    public int getCol(){
12	        return col;
13	    }
14}
```

‍

**Point 4: Design Challenges :** 

🧑‍💼Interviewer: What design challenges do you anticipate?

‍

Candidate: The key challenges in designing this game include:

1\. Efficient Snake Movement: Managing the snake's body segments during movement.

2\. Collision Detection: Efficiently detecting when the snake collides with walls or itself.

3\. Food Management: Handling the predefined food positions and consumption.

4\. Game State Management: Tracking score and detecting game over conditions.

5\. Extensibility: Allowing for different movement strategies and game configurations.

‍

**Point 5: Design Patterns Approach :** 

🧑‍💼Interviewer: How would you approach these challenges to ensure the game functions smoothly?

‍

Candidate: To handle these design challenges, I propose the following design patterns:

1\. Strategy Pattern for Snake Movement : 

• This pattern allows us to define a family of algorithms for snake movement, encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable.

‍

2\. Factory Pattern for Food Placement : 

• Ensures Different food types (normal, special, poisonous) should be dynamically generated without modifying existing code.

‍

‍ 3. Singleton Pattern for Board : 

• Ensures that only one instance of the game board exists:

4\. Observer Design Pattern for Score Updates:

• Can be used to notify UI components of score changes or game over events:

‍

**Point 6: Implementation :** 

‍

🧑‍💼Interviewer: Ready to discuss implementation?

Candidate: Yes. I’ll focus on a modular and scalable design that meets the core Snake & Food game requirements.

‍

## Snake and Food Game Design with Design Patterns 🎲: 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743136421742-Frame-241.png)

‍

### 1\. Strategy Pattern for Snake Movement :

○ Movement Strategy Interface : 

This interface defines a strategy for determining the next position of a snake's head based on the current position and movement direction.

‍

Java

```
1// Movement strategy interface
2	interface MovementStrategy {
3	  Pair getNextPosition(Pair currentHead, String direction);
4	}
```

‍

○ Human Player Movement Strategy : 

This class implements the MovementStrategy interface and provides movement logic for a human-controlled snake. The movement is determined by the input direction (U for Up, D for Down, L for Left, and R for Right).

‍

Java

```
1// Human player movement strategy
2	class HumanMovementStrategy implements MovementStrategy {
3	    @Override
4	    public Pair getNextPosition(Pair currentHead, String direction) {
5	        int row = currentHead.getKey();
6	        int col = currentHead.getValue();	        
7	        switch (direction) {
8	            case "U": return new Pair<>(row - 1, col);
9	            case "D": return new Pair<>(row + 1, col);
10	            case "L": return new Pair<>(row, col - 1);
11	            case "R": return new Pair<>(row, col + 1);
12	            default: return currentHead;
13	        }
14	    }
15}
```

‍

○ AI Movement Strategy : 

This class implements the MovementStrategy interface and is responsible for controlling the snake's movement in an AI-driven manner. The AI logic will determine the optimal move based on food position, obstacles, and pathfinding techniques.

‍

Java

```
1// AI movement strategy (for autonomous snake movement)
2	class AIMovementStrategy implements MovementStrategy {
3	    @Override
4	    public Pair getNextPosition(Pair currentHead, String direction) {
5	        // AI logic to determine next best move based on food position and obstacles
6	        // For simplicity, this could just implement a basic pathfinding algorithm
7	        // or even random movement that avoids obstacles
8	        return currentHead; // Placeholder - actual implementation would be more complex
9	    }
10}
```

‍

### 2\. Factory Pattern for Food Placement : 

○ FoodItem Abstract Class : 

This abstract class represents a food item placed on the game board. It defines the common properties such as position (row, column) and the points awarded when consumed.

‍

Java

```
1// Abstract class representing a food item in the game
2	abstract class FoodItem {
3	    protected int row, column; // Position of the food item
4	    protected int points; // Points awarded when consumed
5	    // Constructor to initialize food item position
6	    public FoodItem(int row, int column) {
7	        this.row = row;
8	        this.column = column;
9	    }
10	    // Getter methods to retrieve food item properties
11	    public int getRow() { return row; }
12	    public int getColumn() { return column; }
13	    public int getPoints() { return points; }
14	}
```

‍

Concrete Food Item Classes : 

These are specific implementations of FoodItem, defining different types of food with varying point values.

‍

**NormalFood Class :**

Represents a standard food item that awards 1 point when consumed

‍

Java

```
1// Concrete class representing normal food with 1 point
2	class NormalFood extends FoodItem {
3	    public NormalFood(int row, int column) {
4	        super(row, column); // Call superclass constructor
5	        this.points = 1; // Assign point value
6	    }
7}
```

‍

**BonusFood Class :**

Represents a special bonus food item that awards 3 points when consumed.

‍

Java

```
1// Concrete class representing bonus food with 3 points
2	class BonusFood extends FoodItem {
3	    public BonusFood(int row, int column) {
4	        super(row, column); // Call superclass constructor
5	        this.points = 3; // Assign higher point value
6	    }
7}
```

‍

**FoodFactory Class :** 

The Factory Pattern is implemented here to create FoodItem objects dynamically based on the given type.

‍

Java

```
1// Factory class for creating food items based on type
2	class FoodFactory {
3	    // Static method to create a food item at a given position and type
4	    public static FoodItem createFood(int[] position, String type) {
5	        if ("bonus".equals(type)) {
6	            return new BonusFood(position[0], position[1]); // Create bonus food
7	        }
8	        return new NormalFood(position[0], position[1]); // Default to normal food
9	    }
10}
```

‍

This implementation makes the code flexible and easily extendable for adding more food types in the future.

‍

3.) Singleton Pattern for Game Board:

Ensures that only one instance of the game board exists, preventing the creation of multiple boards.

‍

Java

```
1// Singleton class representing the game board
2public class GameBoard {
3    private static GameBoard instance; // Single instance of the game board
4    private int width;  // Width of the game board
5    private int height; // Height of the game board
6    // Private constructor to prevent direct instantiation
7    private GameBoard(int width, int height) {
8        this.width = width;
9        this.height = height;
10    }
11    // Method to get the singleton instance of the game board
12    public static GameBoard getInstance(int width, int height) {
13        if (instance == null) {
14            instance = new GameBoard(width, height); // Create instance if not already created
15        }
16        return instance; // Return the existing instance
17    }
18    // Getter methods to retrieve board dimensions
19    public int getWidth() { return width; }
20    public int getHeight() { return height; }
21}
```

‍

4.) Defining the GameController Class (SnakeGame.java) :

‍

Java

```
1class SnakeGame {
2    private GameBoard board;
3    private Deque<Pair> snake;
4    private Map<Pair, Boolean> snakeMap;
5    private int[][] food;
6    private int foodIndex;
7    private MovementStrategy movementStrategy;
8    
9    // Initialize the game with specified dimensions and food positions.
10    public SnakeGame(int width, int height, int[][] food) {
11        this.board = GameBoard.getInstance(width, height);
12        this.food = food;
13        this.foodIndex = 0;
14        
15        // Initialize snake
16        this.snake = new LinkedList<>();
17        this.snakeMap = new HashMap<>();
18        Pair initialPos = new Pair<>(0, 0);
19        this.snake.offerFirst(initialPos);
20        this.snakeMap.put(initialPos, true);
21        
22        // Set default movement strategy
23        this.movementStrategy = new HumanMovementStrategy();
24     }
25    
26    // Set the movement strategy (Human or AI)
27    public void setMovementStrategy(MovementStrategy strategy) {
28        this.movementStrategy = strategy;
29    }
30    
31    // Returns the new score or -1 if game over.
32    public int move(String direction) {
33        // Get current head
34        Pair currentHead = this.snake.peekFirst();
35        
36        // Get next position using strategy pattern
37        Pair newHead = this.movementStrategy.getNextPosition(currentHead, direction);
38        int newHeadRow = newHead.getKey();
39        int newHeadColumn = newHead.getValue();        
40        // Check boundary conditions
41        boolean crossesBoundary = newHeadRow < 0 || newHeadRow >= this.board.getHeight() ||
42                                  newHeadColumn < 0 || newHeadColumn >= this.board.getWidth();
43        
44        // Get current tail for collision check
45        Pair currentTail = this.snake.peekLast();
46        
47        // Check if snake bites itself (excluding tail which will move away)
48        boolean bitesItself = this.snakeMap.containsKey(newHead) && 
49                             !(newHead.getKey() == currentTail.getKey() && 
50                               newHead.getValue() == currentTail.getValue());
51        
52        // Game over conditions
53        if (crossesBoundary || bitesItself) {
54            return -1;
55        }   
56        // Check if snake eats food
57        boolean ateFood = (this.foodIndex < this.food.length) &&
58                         (this.food[this.foodIndex][0] == newHeadRow) &&
59                         (this.food[this.foodIndex][1] == newHeadColumn);    
60        if (ateFood) {
61            // Increment food index to move to next food
62            this.foodIndex++;
63        } else {
64            // If no food eaten, remove tail
65            this.snake.pollLast();
66            this.snakeMap.remove(currentTail);
67        }        
68        // Add new head
69        this.snake.addFirst(newHead);
70        this.snakeMap.put(newHead, true);       
71        // Calculate ans return score
72        int score = this.snake.size() - 1;
73        return score;
74    }
75}
```

‍

 Main Method to Run the game : 

‍

Java

```
1public class SnakeGameMain {
2  public static void main(String[] args) {
3    // Define game configuration
4    // can be taken as user input as well
5    int width = 20;
6    int height = 15;
7    // Define some food positions (more can be generated during gameplay)
8    int[][] foodPositions = {
9        {5, 5}, // Initial food
10        {10, 8}, // Second food
11        {3, 12}, // Third food
12        {8, 17}, // Fourth food
13        {12, 3} // Fifth food
14    };
15    // Initialize the game
16    SnakeGame game = new SnakeGame(width, height, foodPositions);
17    // Display game instructions
18    System.out.println("===== SNAKE GAME =====");
19    System.out.println(
20        "Controls: W (Up), S (Down), A (Left), D (Right), Q (Quit)");
21    System.out.println("Eat food to grow your snake and increase your score.");
22    System.out.println("Don't hit the walls or bite yourself!");
23    System.out.println("=======================");
24    // Create scanner for user input
25    Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
26    boolean gameRunning = true;
27    int score = 0;
28    // Main game loop
29    while (gameRunning) {
30      // Display current game state (in a real implementation, you would
31      // have a graphical representation of the board)
32      displayGameState(game);
33      // Get user input
34      System.out.print("Enter move (W/A/S/D) or Q to quit: ");
35      String input = scanner.nextLine().toUpperCase();
36      // Handle quit command
37      if (input.equals("Q")) {
38        System.out.println("Game ended by player. Final score: " + score);
39        gameRunning = false;
40        continue;
41      }
42      // Convert WASD input to UDLR for game processing
43      String direction = convertInput(input);
44      // Skip invalid inputs
45      if (direction.isEmpty()) {
46        System.out.println("Invalid input! Use W/A/S/D to move or Q to quit.");
47        continue;
48      }
49      // Make the move and get the new score
50      score = game.move(direction);
51      // Check for game over
52      if (score == -1) {
53        System.out.println("GAME OVER! You hit a wall or bit yourself.");
54        System.out.println("Final score: " + (game.snake.size() - 1));
55        gameRunning = false;
56      } else {
57        System.out.println("Score: " + score);
58      }
59    }
60    scanner.close();
61    System.out.println("Thanks for playing!");
62  }
63  // Convert user-friendly WASD input to UDLR for the game engine
64  private static String convertInput(String input) {
65    switch (input) {
66      case "W":
67        return "U"; // Up
68      case "S":
69        return "D"; // Down
70      case "A":
71        return "L"; // Left
72      case "D":
73        return "R"; // Right
74      default:
75        return ""; // Invalid input
76    }
77  }
78  // A simple method to display the game state in the console
79  // In a real implementation, this would be replaced with graphics
80  private static void displayGameState(SnakeGame game) {
81    // This is a placeholder - in a real implementation, you would
82    // access the game's state and render it appropriately
83    System.out.println("
84Current snake length: " + game.snake.size());
85    // In a complete implementation, you would render the board with the
86    // snake, food, and boundaries visually
87  }
88}
```

‍

🧑‍💼Interviewer: Sounds good. What makes your approach effective?

‍

Candidate: Here are the key strengths of my approach:

1\. Simplicity: The design is kept minimal and straightforward, avoiding unnecessary complexity.

2\. Clarity: It's easy to understand, which makes it accessible for developers to implement and maintain.

3\. Efficiency: The implementation is direct and logical, ensuring smooth gameplay.

4\. Separation of Concerns: Each component has a clear responsibility, enhancing modularity and ease of updates.

‍

Extensibility : 

1.) Extensible Board Size : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743136950398-Frame-241-\(2\).png)

‍

The Singleton GameBoard implementation supports variable grid dimensions, making the game adaptable to different screen sizes and difficulty levels:

‍

Example :

1.) GameBoard.java :  

Java

```
1public class GameBoard {
2    private static GameBoard instance;
3    private int width;
4    private int height;    
5    private GameBoard(int width, int height) {
6        this.width = width;
7        this.height = height;
8    }    
9    public static GameBoard getInstance(int width, int height) {
10        if (instance == null) {
11            instance = new GameBoard(width, height);
12        }
13        return instance;
14    }   
15    public int getWidth() { return width; }
16    public int getHeight() { return height; }
17}
```

‍

Usage in Main Class : 

‍

Java

```
1public class SnakeGameMain {
2// Create a small 8x8 board for mobile
3SnakeGame smallGame = new SnakeGame(8, 8, foodPositions);
4// Create a larger 20x15 board for desktop
5SnakeGame largeGame = new SnakeGame(20, 15, foodPositions);
6// rest code remains the same 
7}
```

‍

2.) Observer Pattern for Game Event Tracking : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743137436921-Frame-241-\(3\).png)

‍

The Observer Pattern can be used to notify users about each move, the current state of the game, and other events, allowing users to stay updated on the game's progress. This involves implementing the GameObserver interface, creating a concrete listener class, and modifying the GameController class to include observer functionality.

‍

### Step 1: Implement the GameObserver Interface : 

‍

Java

```
1public interface GameObserver {
2    void onMoveMade(Pair newHeadPosition);
3    void onFoodEaten(int foodIndex, int newScore);
4    void onGameOver(int finalScore);
5}
```

‍

### Step 2: Create a Concrete Observer Classe : 

‍

Java

```
1// Console-based observer for debugging and logging
2public class ConsoleGameObserver implements GameObserver {
3    @Override
4    public void onMoveMade(Pair newHeadPosition) {
5        System.out.println("Snake moved to position: [" + 
6                           newHeadPosition.getKey() + ", " + 
7                           newHeadPosition.getValue() + "]");
8    }
9    
10    @Override
11    public void onFoodEaten(int foodIndex, int newScore) {
12        System.out.println("Food eaten! Current score: " + newScore);
13    }
14    
15    @Override
16    public void onGameOver(int finalScore) {
17        System.out.println("Game Over! Final score: " + finalScore);
18    }
19}
```

‍

### Step 3: Modify the GameController (SnakeGame.java) to Include Observer Functionality : 

‍

Java

```
1class SnakeGame {
2  private GameBoard board;
3  private Deque<Pair>> snake;
4  private Map<Pair, Boolean> snakeMap;
5  private int[][] food;
6  private int foodIndex;
7  private MovementStrategy movementStrategy;
8  private List<GameObserver> observers;
9  // Initialize the game with specified dimensions and food positions.
10  public SnakeGame(int width, int height, int[][] food) {
11    this.board = GameBoard.getInstance(width, height);
12    this.food = food;
13    this.foodIndex = 0;
14    // Initialize snake
15    this.snake = new LinkedList<>();
16    this.snakeMap = new HashMap<>();
17    Pair initialPos = new Pair(0, 0);
18    this.snake.offerFirst(initialPos);
19    this.snakeMap.put(initialPos, true);
20    // Set default movement strategy
21    this.movementStrategy = new HumanMovementStrategy();
22    // Initialize observers list
23    this.observers = new ArrayList<>();
24  }
25  // Set the movement strategy (Human or AI)
26  public void setMovementStrategy(MovementStrategy strategy) {
27    this.movementStrategy = strategy;
28  }
29  // Add observer for game events
30  public void addObserver(GameObserver observer) {
31    observers.add(observer);
32  }
33  // Notify observers of score update
34  private void notifyScoreUpdate(int score) {
35    for (GameObserver observer : observers) {
36      observer.onScoreUpdate(score);
37    }
38  }
39  // Notify observers of game over
40  private void notifyGameOver() {
41    for (GameObserver observer : observers) {
42      observer.onGameOver();
43    }
44  }
45  // Process a move in the specified direction.
46  // Returns the new score or -1 if game over.
47  public int move(String direction) {
48    // Get current head
49    Pair currentHead = this.snake.peekFirst();
50    // Get next position using strategy pattern
51    Pair newHead =
52        this.movementStrategy.getNextPosition(currentHead, direction);
53    int newHeadRow = newHead.getKey();
54    int newHeadColumn = newHead.getValue();
55    // Check boundary conditions
56    boolean crossesBoundary = newHeadRow < 0
57        || newHeadRow >= this.board.getHeight() || newHeadColumn < 0
58        || newHeadColumn >= this.board.getWidth();
59    // Get current tail for collision check
60    Pair currentTail = this.snake.peekLast();
61    // Check if snake bites itself (excluding tail which will move away)
62    boolean bitesItself = this.snakeMap.containsKey(newHead)
63        && !(newHead.getKey() == currentTail.getKey()
64            && newHead.getValue() == currentTail.getValue());
65    // Game over conditions
66    if (crossesBoundary || bitesItself) {
67      notifyGameOver();
68      return -1;
69    }
70    // Check if snake eats food
71    boolean ateFood = (this.foodIndex < this.food.length)
72        && (this.food[this.foodIndex][0] == newHeadRow)
73        && (this.food[this.foodIndex][1] == newHeadColumn);
74    if (ateFood) {
75      // Increment food index to move to next food
76      this.foodIndex++;
77    } else {
78      // If no food eaten, remove tail
79      this.snake.pollLast();
80      this.snakeMap.remove(currentTail);
81    }
82    // Add new head
83    this.snake.addFirst(newHead);
84    this.snakeMap.put(newHead, true);
85    // Calculate and notify score
86    int score = this.snake.size() - 1;
87    notifyScoreUpdate(score);
88    return score;
89  }
90}
```

‍

3\. When The game has No walls : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743137731476-Frame-241-\(5\).png)

To enhance our Snake game, we can implement an infinite height and width feature. In this scenario, there are no walls, and the snake can only lose if it collides with itself. Here's how we can achieve this:

‍

Modify SnakeGame Class To incorporate the logic of walls and no walls: 

‍

Java

```
1// Modified SnakeGame class with no walls - wrapping around edges
2class SnakeGame {
3  private GameBoard board;
4  private Deque<Pair> snake;
5  private Map<Pair, Boolean> snakeMap;
6  private int[][] food;
7  private int foodIndex;
8  private MovementStrategy movementStrategy;
9  private List<GameObserver> observers;
10  private boolean hasWalls; // Flag to determine if walls exist
11  // Initialize the game with specified dimensions, food positions, and wall
12  // settings
13  public SnakeGame(int width, int height, int[][] food, boolean hasWalls) {
14    this.board = GameBoard.getInstance(width, height);
15    this.food = food;
16    this.foodIndex = 0;
17    this.hasWalls = hasWalls;
18    // Initialize snake
19    this.snake = new LinkedList<>();
20    this.snakeMap = new HashMap<>();
21    Pair initialPos = new Pair(0, 0);
22    this.snake.offerFirst(initialPos);
23    this.snakeMap.put(initialPos, true);
24    // Set default movement strategy
25    this.movementStrategy = new HumanMovementStrategy();
26    // Initialize observers list
27    this.observers = new ArrayList<>();
28  }
29  // Constructor with default walls setting (has walls)
30  public SnakeGame(int width, int height, int[][] food) {
31    this(width, height, food, true);
32  }
33  // Set the movement strategy (Human or AI)
34  public void setMovementStrategy(MovementStrategy strategy) {
35    this.movementStrategy = strategy;
36  }
37  // Add observer for game events
38  public void addObserver(GameObserver observer) {
39    observers.add(observer);
40  }
41  // Notify observers of move made
42  private void notifyMoveMade(Pair newHead) {
43    for (GameObserver observer : observers) {
44      observer.onMoveMade(newHead);
45    }
46  }
47  // Notify observers of food eaten
48  private void notifyFoodEaten(int foodIndex, int newScore) {
49    for (GameObserver observer : observers) {
50      observer.onFoodEaten(foodIndex, newScore);
51    }
52  }
53  // Notify observers of game over
54  private void notifyGameOver(int finalScore) {
55    for (GameObserver observer : observers) {
56      observer.onGameOver(finalScore);
57    }
58  }
59  // Wrap position around board edges (for no-walls mode)
60  private Pair wrapPosition(int row, int col) {
61    int height = this.board.getHeight();
62    int width = this.board.getWidth();
63    // Wrap around height
64    if (row < 0) {
65      row = height - 1;
66    } else if (row >= height) {
67      row = 0;
68    }
69    // Wrap around width
70    if (col < 0) {
71      col = width - 1;
72    } else if (col >= width) {
73      col = 0;
74    }
75    return new Pair<>(row, col);
76  }
77  // Process a move in the specified direction.
78  // Returns the new score or -1 if game over.
79  public int move(String direction) {
80    // Get current head
81    Pair currentHead = this.snake.peekFirst();
82    // Get next position using strategy pattern
83    Pair newHead =
84        this.movementStrategy.getNextPosition(currentHead, direction);
85    int newHeadRow = newHead.getKey();
86    int newHeadColumn = newHead.getValue();
87    // Check boundary conditions if walls are enabled
88    boolean crossesBoundary = false;
89    if (hasWalls) {
90      // Traditional boundary checking with walls
91      crossesBoundary = newHeadRow < 0 || newHeadRow >= this.board.getHeight()
92          || newHeadColumn < 0 || newHeadColumn >= this.board.getWidth();
93    } else {
94      // No walls - wrap around the edges
95      newHead = wrapPosition(newHeadRow, newHeadColumn);
96      newHeadRow = newHead.getKey();
97      newHeadColumn = newHead.getValue();
98    }
99    // Get current tail for collision check
100    Pair currentTail = this.snake.peekLast();
101    // Check if snake bites itself (excluding tail which will move away)
102    boolean bitesItself = this.snakeMap.containsKey(newHead)
103        && !(newHead.getKey() == currentTail.getKey()
104            && newHead.getValue() == currentTail.getValue());
105    // Game over conditions
106    if (crossesBoundary || bitesItself) {
107      notifyGameOver(this.snake.size() - 1);
108      return -1;
109    }
110    // Check if snake eats food
111    boolean ateFood = (this.foodIndex < this.food.length)
112        && (this.food[this.foodIndex][0] == newHeadRow)
113        && (this.food[this.foodIndex][1] == newHeadColumn);
114    if (ateFood) {
115      // Increment food index to move to next food
116      this.foodIndex++;
117      // Notify observers
118      int newScore = this.snake.size();
119      notifyFoodEaten(this.foodIndex - 1, newScore);
120    } else {
121      // If no food eaten, remove tail
122      this.snake.pollLast();
123      this.snakeMap.remove(currentTail);
124    }
125    // Add new head
126    this.snake.addFirst(newHead);
127    this.snakeMap.put(newHead, true);
128    // Notify observers
129    notifyMoveMade(newHead);
130    // Calculate and return score
131    int score = this.snake.size() - 1;
132    return score;
133  }
134}
```

‍

Modify Main Class to handle No Walls Logic : 

‍

Java

```
1public class SnakeGameMain {
2  public static void main(String[] args) {
3    // Define game configuration
4    int width = 20;
5    int height = 15;
6    // Define some food positions
7    int[][] foodPositions = {
8        {5, 5}, // Initial food
9        {10, 8}, // Second food
10        {3, 12}, // Third food
11        {8, 17}, // Fourth food
12        {12, 3} // Fifth food
13    };
14    // Initialize the game with NO walls - passing 'false' for the hasWalls
15    // parameter
16    SnakeGame game = new SnakeGame(width, height, foodPositions, false);
17    // Add a console observer to track game events
18    game.addObserver(new ConsoleGameObserver());
19    // Display game instructions
20    System.out.println("===== SNAKE GAME (NO WALLS MODE) =====");
21    System.out.println(
22        "Controls: W (Up), S (Down), A (Left), D (Right), Q (Quit)");
23    System.out.println("Eat food to grow your snake and increase your score.");
24    System.out.println(
25        "You can move through edges and appear on the opposite side!");
26    System.out.println("Don't bite yourself!");
27    System.out.println("=======================================");
28    // Create scanner for user input
29    Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
30    boolean gameRunning = true;
31    int score = 0;
32    // Main game loop
33    while (gameRunning) {
34      // Display current game state
35      displayGameState(game);
36      // Get user input
37      System.out.print("Enter move (W/A/S/D) or Q to quit: ");
38      String input = scanner.nextLine().toUpperCase();
39      // Handle quit command
40      if (input.equals("Q")) {
41        System.out.println("Game ended by player. Final score: " + score);
42        gameRunning = false;
43        continue;
44      }
45      // Convert WASD input to UDLR for game processing
46      String direction = convertInput(input);
47      // Skip invalid inputs
48      if (direction.isEmpty()) {
49        System.out.println("Invalid input! Use W/A/S/D to move or Q to quit.");
50        continue;
51      }
52      // Make the move and get the new score
53      score = game.move(direction);
54      // Check for game over
55      if (score == -1) {
56        System.out.println("GAME OVER! You bit yourself.");
57        System.out.println("Final score: " + (game.snake.size() - 1));
58        gameRunning = false;
59      } else {
60        System.out.println("Score: " + score);
61      }
62    }
63    scanner.close();
64    System.out.println("Thanks for playing!");
65  }
66
67  // Convert user-friendly WASD input to UDLR for the game engine
68  private static String convertInput(String input) {
69    switch (input) {
70      case "W":
71        return "U"; // Up
72      case "S":
73        return "D"; // Down
74      case "A":
75        return "L"; // Left
76      case "D":
77        return "R"; // Right
78      default:
79        return ""; // Invalid input
80    }
81  }
82
83  // A simple method to display the game state in the console
84  private static void displayGameState(SnakeGame game) {
85    System.out.println("
86Current snake length: " + game.snake.size());
87    // In a real implementation, you would render the board with the
88    // snake, food, and boundaries visually
89    // For demonstration purposes, we'll display the head position
90    Pair head = game.snake.peekFirst();
91    System.out.println(
92        "Head position: [" + head.getKey() + ", " + head.getValue() + "]");
93    // Show next food location if available
94    if (game.foodIndex < game.food.length) {
95      System.out.println("Next food at: [" + game.food[game.foodIndex][0] + ", "
96          + game.food[game.foodIndex][1] + "]");
97    }
98  }
99}
```

‍

By implementing these changes, we can create a more challenging and dynamic version of the Snake game that offers a unique gaming experience.

‍

## Conclusion : 

This low-level design for the Snake and Food game showcases a well-structured and scalable architecture, emphasizing modularity and extensibility. By supporting various enhancements such as custom board sizes, different game states, and event tracking, this design ensures maintainability and flexibility. The use of design patterns like Strategy, Factory, Singleton, State, and Observer highlights the robustness and adaptability of the solution.

‍

In an interview setting, presenting this design would demonstrate your ability to create comprehensive and well-organized solutions, showcasing your proficiency in applying design patterns and best practices. This approach ensures that the game can be easily expanded and maintained, providing a solid foundation for future enhancements and features.\n\n---\n\n## Source: https://codewitharyan.com/tech-blogs/design-parking-lot\n\n# Design Parking Lot

Topic Tags:

system designlld

### 🐈‍⬛ Github Codes Link: [https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode](https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode)

# ‍

# Low-Level Design: Parking Lot System 🏧

A parking lot management system is designed to handle the operations of parking vehicles, collecting payments, and managing available space efficiently. The system should be able to accommodate different types of vehicles, provide payment options, and ensure a smooth user experience.

‍

## Rules of the System : 

Setup:

• The parking lot has multiple slots available for parking.

• Different types of vehicles (bike, car, truck) can occupy different slot sizes.

• Each vehicle is issued a parking ticket upon entry.

• The system calculates the parking fee based on the duration of stay and vehicle type.

‍

Exit and Payment:

• A vehicle needs to make a payment before exiting.

• Multiple payment methods (Cash, Card, UPI) should be supported.

• Once payment is successful, the vehicle is allowed to exit, and the parking slot is freed.

‍

Illegal Actions:

• A vehicle cannot park in an already occupied slot.

• Vehicles cannot vacate without completing the payment process.

## Interview Setting 🤝

### Point 1: Introduction and Vague Problem Statement : 

Interviewer: Let's start with a basic problem statement. Design a Parking Lot system.

‍

Candidate: Certainly! Here’s my understanding of the Parking Lot system:

• The system will manage different types of vehicles.

• Vehicles will enter and exit after making a payment.

• A parking slot is assigned upon entry and freed upon exit.

• Payment must be completed before leaving.

• The system should handle different vehicle sizes and slot allocations efficiently.

Is this the expected flow?

‍

Interviewer: Yes, you are aligned with the flow. Please continue ahead.

‍

Candidate: Great! Before diving into the design, I’d like to clarify a few requirements:

• Will we have multiple types of parking slots based on vehicle size?

• Are we supporting multiple payment methods?

• Should the system track the duration of each parked vehicle?

• Can there be multiple floors parking lots ?

‍

### Point 2: Clarifying Requirements : 

Interviewer: We want a system that:

• Supports different vehicle types (Bike, Car, Truck).

• Has different slot sizes based on vehicle type.

• Allows multiple payment methods.

• Ensures smooth entry and exit with parking fee calculation.

‍

Candidate: To summarize, the key requirements are:

• A parking lot with multiple slot types.

• Support for bikes, cars, and trucks.

• Dynamic slot allocation based on vehicle size.

• Payment processing with multiple methods.

• Entry ticket issuance and exit validation.

‍

Interviewer: Perfect, let’s proceed.

‍

### Point 3: Identifying Key Components : 

Candidate: Now that we have the requirements, let’s identify the key components of our Parking Lot system:

1\. Vehicle: Represents different types of vehicles.

Class: Vehicle

Description: This class represents vehicles entering the parking lot.

‍

Java

```
1public abstract class Vehicle {
2	     protected String licensePlate; 
3	     protected VehicleType type; 
4}
```

‍

2\. Parking Lot: Manages parking slots and vehicle allocations.

Class: ParkingLot

Description: This class manages the allocation and release of parking slots.

‍

Java

```
1public class ParkingLot {
2	     private List<ParkingSlot> slots; 
3}
```

‍

3\. Parking Slot: Represents an individual parking space.

Class: ParkingSlot

Description: A slot has a type (Bike, Car, Truck) and an availability status.

‍

Java

```
1public class ParkingSlot {
2	     private VehicleType slotType;
3	     private boolean isOccupied; 
4}
```

‍

4\. Payment System: Handles different payment methods like Credit Card, Cash, UPI etc

Class: Payment

Description: This class processes payments before exit.

‍

Java

```
1public interface PaymentStrategy { 
2	    boolean processPayment(double amount);
3 }
```

‍

Interviewer: That sounds good. Let’s proceed with the design details.

‍

### Point 4: Design Challenges : 

Interviewer: What design challenges do you anticipate?

‍

Candidate: The key challenges for the Parking Lot system include:

1\. Efficient Slot Allocation: Ensuring vehicles are assigned to the correct slot type.

2\. Tracking Vehicle Duration: Keeping a record of entry times for payment calculations.

3\. Handling Payments: Supporting multiple payment methods dynamically.

4\. Managing Concurrency: Ensuring simultaneous vehicle entries and exits are handled properly.

‍

### Point 5: Approach :

Interviewer: How would you approach these challenges?

Candidate: I propose using design patterns effectively. Here are my strategies:

1\. Factory for Vehicle Creation : 

• Allows easy extension for new vehicle types.

• Ensures consistent object creation.

2\. Strategy Pattern for Payments and Parking Fares : 

• Enables flexible payment methods and Parking fares based on the vehicle type and duration for the parking.

• Easily extendable for future payment integrations and new fare strategy additions.

3\. Singleton Pattern for Parking Lot Management : 

• Ensures only one instance of the parking lot exists.

4\. Observer Pattern for Exit Notifications : 

• Notifies the system when a vehicle exits.

• Can be extended for alerts or logging.

‍

Interviewer: That sounds like a solid approach. Let’s delve into the implementation details.

‍

### Point 6: Implementation : 

Interviewer: Ready to discuss implementation?

Candidate: Yes. I'll focus on a modular and scalable design that meets the core Parking Lot requirements.

‍

## Parking Lot Implementation with Design Patterns 🚗: 

‍

1. Strategy Pattern for Parking Fare : 

• ParkingFeeStrategy Interface : 

Defines a common interface for different parking fee calculation strategies.

‍

Java

```
1// Interface for Parking Fee Calculation Strategy
2	public interface ParkingFeeStrategy {
3	    /**
4	     - Calculate parking fee based on vehicle type and duration
5	     - 
6	     - @param vehicleType Type of vehicle being parked
7	     - @param duration Duration of parking (in hours or days)
8	     - @param durationType Type of duration (HOURS or DAYS)
9	     - @return Calculated parking fee
10	     */
11	    double calculateFee(String vehicleType, int duration, DurationType durationType);
12	}
```

‍

• DurationType Enum : 

Specifies the type of duration for parking (HOURS or DAYS).

‍

Java

```
1// Enum to specify duration type
2	public enum DurationType {
3	    HOURS, 
4	    DAYS
5}
```

‍

• Define Concrete Strategies : 

a. BasicHourlyRateStrategy Class : 

Concrete strategy for basic hourly rates. Rates vary based on vehicle type.

‍

Java

```
1// Concrete Strategy for Basic Hourly Rates
2	public class BasicHourlyRateStrategy implements ParkingFeeStrategy {
3	    @Override
4	    public double calculateFee(String vehicleType, int duration, DurationType durationType) {
5	        // Different rates for different vehicle types
6	        switch (vehicleType.toLowerCase()) {
7	            case "car":
8	                return durationType == DurationType.HOURS 
9	                    ? duration * 10.0   // $10 per hour for cars
10	                    : duration * 10.0 * 24;  // Daily rate
11	            
12	            case "bike":
13	                return durationType == DurationType.HOURS 
14	                    ? duration * 5.0    // $5 per hour for bikes
15	                    : duration * 5.0 * 24;  // Daily rate
16	            
17	            case "auto":
18	                return durationType == DurationType.HOURS 
19	                    ? duration * 8.0    // $8 per hour for autos
20	                    : duration * 8.0 * 24;  // Daily rate
21	            
22	            default:
23	                return durationType == DurationType.HOURS 
24	                    ? duration * 15.0   // $15 per hour for other vehicles
25	                    : duration * 15.0 * 24;  // Daily rate
26	        }
27	    }
28	}
```

‍

‍ b. PremiumRateStrategy Class : 

Concrete strategy for premium rates. Premium rates have higher multipliers.

‍

Java

```
1// Concrete Strategy for Premium Rates
2	public class PremiumRateStrategy implements ParkingFeeStrategy {
3	    @Override
4	    public double calculateFee(String vehicleType, int duration, DurationType durationType) {
5	        // Premium rates with higher multipliers
6	        switch (vehicleType.toLowerCase()) {
7	            case "car":
8	                return durationType == DurationType.HOURS 
9	                    ? duration * 15.0   // $15 per hour for cars
10	                    : duration * 15.0 * 24;  // Daily rate
11	            
12	            case "bike":
13	                return durationType == DurationType.HOURS 
14	                    ? duration * 8.0    // $8 per hour for bikes
15	                    : duration * 8.0 * 24;  // Daily rate
16	            
17	            case "auto":
18	                return durationType == DurationType.HOURS 
19	                    ? duration * 12.0   // $12 per hour for autos
20	                    : duration * 12.0 * 24;  // Daily rate
21	            
22	            default:
23	                return durationType == DurationType.HOURS 
24	                    ? duration * 20.0   // $20 per hour for other vehicles
25	                    : duration * 20.0 * 24;  // Daily rate
26	        }
27	    }
28	}
```

‍

2\. Factory Pattern for Vehicle Creation : (FYI - Not necessarily a CORE PART OF THE PROBLEM !!)

     ‍

To efficiently manage families of related objects such as cars, bikes, and scooters, we will utilize the Factory pattern for Vehicle Creation. First, let's define the Factory interfaces and their corresponding concrete factories.

‍

Vehicle.java: Common Abstract Class for vehicles : 

‍

Java

```
1// Abstract class representing a vehicle
2public abstract class Vehicle {  
3    private String licensePlate; // Stores the vehicle's license plate number
4    private String vehicleType; // Stores the type of vehicle (e.g., car, bike, truck)
5    private ParkingFeeStrategy feeStrategy; // Strategy for calculating parking fees
6    // Constructor to initialize a vehicle with its license plate, type, and fee strategy
7    public Vehicle(String licensePlate, String vehicleType, ParkingFeeStrategy feeStrategy) {  
8        this.licensePlate = licensePlate;  
9        this.vehicleType = vehicleType;  
10        this.feeStrategy = feeStrategy;  
11    }  
12    // Getter method to retrieve the vehicle type
13    public String getVehicleType() {  
14        return vehicleType;  
15    }  
16    // Getter method to retrieve the vehicle's license plate number
17    public String getLicensePlate() {  
18        return licensePlate;  
19    }  
20    // Method to calculate parking fee based on duration and duration type
21    public double calculateFee(int duration, DurationType durationType) {  
22        return feeStrategy.calculateFee(vehicleType, duration, durationType);
23    }  
24}
```

‍

• Concrete Classes For Vehicle Types :

CarVehicle.java : 

‍

Java

```
1package VehicleFactoryPattern.ConcreteVehicles;
2import FareStrategyPattern.ParkingFeeStrategy;
3import VehicleFactoryPattern.Vehicle;
4
5public class CarVehicle extends Vehicle {
6    public CarVehicle(String licensePlate, String vehicleType, ParkingFeeStrategy feeStrategy) {
7        super(licensePlate, vehicleType, feeStrategy);
8    }
9}
```

‍

2\. BikeVehicle.java : 

‍

Java

```
1package VehicleFactoryPattern.ConcreteVehicles;
2import FareStrategyPattern.ParkingFeeStrategy;
3import VehicleFactoryPattern.Vehicle;
4
5public class BikeVehicle extends Vehicle {
6    public BikeVehicle(String licensePlate, String vehicleType, ParkingFeeStrategy feeStrategy) {
7        super(licensePlate, vehicleType, feeStrategy);
8    }
9}
```

‍

3\. OtherVehicles.java : 

‍

> NOTE : We can add multiple types of vehicles similarly like we have added for Car and Bikes, OtherVehicles.java is just a common example to generalize all other types of vehicles .

‍

Java

```
1package VehicleFactoryPattern.ConcreteVehicles;
2import FareStrategyPattern.ParkingFeeStrategy;
3import VehicleFactoryPattern.Vehicle;
4
5public class OtherVehicle extends Vehicle {
6    public OtherVehicle(String licensePlate, String vehicleType, ParkingFeeStrategy feeStrategy) {
7        super(licensePlate, vehicleType, feeStrategy);
8    }
9}
```

‍

• VehicleFactory.java Class : 

‍

Java

```
1package VehicleFactoryPattern;
2
3import FareStrategyPattern.ParkingFeeStrategy;
4import VehicleFactoryPattern.ConcreteVehicles.BikeVehicle;
5import VehicleFactoryPattern.ConcreteVehicles.CarVehicle;
6import VehicleFactoryPattern.ConcreteVehicles.OtherVehicle;
7
8public class VehicleFactory {
9    public static Vehicle createVehicle(String vehicleType, String licensePlate, ParkingFeeStrategy feeStrategy) {
10        if (vehicleType.equalsIgnoreCase("Car")) {
11            return new CarVehicle(licensePlate, vehicleType, feeStrategy);
12        } else if (vehicleType.equalsIgnoreCase("Bike")) {
13            return new BikeVehicle(licensePlate, vehicleType, feeStrategy);
14        }
15        return new OtherVehicle(licensePlate, vehicleType, feeStrategy); // For unsupported vehicle types
16    }
17}
```

‍

3\. Strategy Pattern for Payments : 

To efficiently manage parking payments for different Vehicles, we will utilize the Strategy pattern for Vehicle Payments. let's define the Strategy interfaces and their corresponding concrete strategies.

‍

PaymentStrategy.java, Common Interface for Different Payment Strategies : 

‍

Java

```
1public interface PaymentStrategy {
2	    void processPayment(double amount);
3}
```

‍

• Concrete Payment Strategies : 

CashPayment.java : 

‍

Java

```
1package PaymentStrategyPattern.ConcretePaymentStrategies;
2import PaymentStrategyPattern.PaymentStrategy;
3
4public class CashPayment implements PaymentStrategy {
5    public CashPayment(double fee) {
6    }
7    @Override
8    public void processPayment(double amount) {
9            System.out.println("Processing cash payment of $" + amount);
10            // Logic for cash payment processing
11    }
12}
```

‍

○ CreditCardPayment.java : 

‍

Java

```
1package PaymentStrategyPattern.ConcretePaymentStrategies;
2import PaymentStrategyPattern.PaymentStrategy;
3
4public class CreditCardPayment implements PaymentStrategy {
5    public CreditCardPayment(double fee) {
6    }
7    @Override
8    public void processPayment(double amount) {
9        System.out.println("Processing credit card payment of $" + amount);
10    }
11}
```

‍

○ Payment.java Client class for handling Payments : 

‍

Java

```
1public class Payment {
2	    private double amount;
3	    private PaymentStrategy paymentStrategy; // Payment strategy interface
4    // Constructor to initialize the payment amount and payment strategy
5    public Payment(double amount, PaymentStrategy paymentStrategy) {
6        this.amount = amount;
7        this.paymentStrategy = paymentStrategy;
8    }
9    // Process the payment using the assigned strategy
10    public void processPayment() {
11        if (amount > 0) {
12            paymentStrategy.processPayment(amount);  // Delegating to strategy
13        } else {
14            System.out.println("Invalid payment amount.");
15        }
16    }
17}
```

‍

4\. Parking Lot Logic and Implementation : 

• In a parking lot, we can have multiple parking spots for different types of vehicles, such as cars, bikes, autos, scooters, etc. To efficiently manage these spots, we will start by defining a common abstract class for parking spots and then create concrete implementations for each type of vehicle.

‍

• Here's how we can approach this:‍

Abstract Parking Spot Class: This class will serve as the base class for all types of parking spots and will define the common properties and methods shared by all spots.

‍

Java

```
1public abstract class ParkingSpot {
2  private int spotNumber;
3  private boolean isOccupied;
4  private Vehicle vehicle;
5  private String spotType;
6  // Constructor to initialize parking spot with spot number and type
7  public ParkingSpot(int spotNumber, String spotType) {
8    this.spotNumber = spotNumber;
9    this.isOccupied = false;
10    this.spotType = spotType;
11  }
12  // Method to check if the spot is occupied
13  public boolean isOccupied() {
14    return isOccupied;
15  }
16  // Abstract method to check if a vehicle can park in this spot
17  public abstract boolean canParkVehicle(Vehicle vehicle);
18  // Method to park a vehicle in the spot
19  public void parkVehicle(Vehicle vehicle) {
20    // Check if the spot is already occupied
21    if (isOccupied) {
22      throw new IllegalStateException("Spot is already occupied.");
23    }
24    // Check if the vehicle can be parked in this spot
25    if (!canParkVehicle(vehicle)) {
26      throw new IllegalArgumentException(
27          "This spot is not suitable for" + vehicle.getVehicleType());
28    }
29    this.vehicle = vehicle;
30    this.isOccupied = true;
31  }
32  // Method to vacate the parking spot
33  public void vacate() {
34    // Check if the spot is already vacant
35    if (!isOccupied) {
36      throw new IllegalStateException("Spot is already vacant.");
37    }
38    this.vehicle = null;
39    this.isOccupied = false;
40  }
41  // Getter for spot number
42  public int getSpotNumber() {
43    return spotNumber;
44  }
45  // Getter for the vehicle parked in the spot
46  public Vehicle getVehicle() {
47    return vehicle;
48  }
49  // Getter for spot type
50  public String getSpotType() {
51    return spotType;
52  }
53}
```

‍

○ Concrete Parking Spot Classes: These classes will extend the abstract class and will represent specific types of parking spots for different vehicles (e.g., car, bike, scooter).

‍

CarParkingSpot.java : 

Java

```
1package ParkingSpots.ConcreteParkingSpots;
2import ParkingSpots.ParkingSpot;
3import VehicleFactoryPattern.Vehicle;
4
5public class CarParkingSpot extends ParkingSpot {
6    public CarParkingSpot(int spotNumber, String spotType) {
7        super(spotNumber, spotType);
8    }
9    @Override
10    public boolean canParkVehicle(Vehicle vehicle) {
11        return "Car".equalsIgnoreCase(vehicle.getVehicleType());
12    }
13}
```

‍

  b.   BikeParkingSpot.java : 

Java

```
1package ParkingSpots.ConcreteParkingSpots;
2import ParkingSpots.ParkingSpot;
3import VehicleFactoryPattern.Vehicle;
4
5public class BikeParkingSpot extends ParkingSpot {
6    public BikeParkingSpot(int spotNumber, String spotType) {
7        super(spotNumber, spotType);
8    }
9    @Override
10    public boolean canParkVehicle(Vehicle vehicle) {
11        return "Bike".equalsIgnoreCase(vehicle.getVehicleType());
12    }
13}
```

‍

> NOTE : Similarly, We can add for Other types of Vehicles as well.

‍

Having defined our parking spots, let's proceed with implementing the ParkingLot class, which will handle the logic for managing parking spots and vehicles.

‍

ParkingLot.java : 

‍

Java

```
1public class ParkingLot {
2  private List<ParkingSpot> parkingSpots;
3  // Constructor to initialize the parking lot with parking spots
4  public ParkingLot(List<ParkingSpot> parkingSpots) {
5    this.parkingSpots = parkingSpots;
6  }
7  // Method to find an available spot based on vehicle type
8  public ParkingSpot findAvailableSpot(String vehicleType) {
9    for (ParkingSpot spot : parkingSpots) {
10      if (!spot.isOccupied() && spot.getSpotType().equals(vehicleType)) {
11        return spot; // Found an available spot for the vehicle type
12      }
13    }
14    return null; // No available spot found for the given vehicle type
15  }
16  // Method to park a vehicle
17  public ParkingSpot parkVehicle(Vehicle vehicle) {
18    ParkingSpot spot = findAvailableSpot(vehicle.getVehicleType());
19    if (spot != null) {
20      spot.parkVehicle(vehicle); // Mark the spot as occupied
21      System.out.println(
22          "Vehicle parked successfully in spot: " + spot.getSpotNumber());
23      return spot;
24    }
25    System.out.println(
26        "No parking spots available for " + vehicle.getVehicleType() + "!");
27    return null;
28  }
29  // Method to vacate a parking spot
30  public void vacateSpot(ParkingSpot spot, Vehicle vehicle) {
31    if (spot != null && spot.isOccupied()
32        && spot.getVehicle().equals(vehicle)) {
33      spot.vacate(); // Free the spot
34      System.out.println(vehicle.getVehicleType()
35          + " vacated the spot: " + spot.getSpotNumber());
36    } else {
37      System.out.println("Invalid operation! Either the spot is already vacant "
38                         + "or the vehicle does not match.");
39    }
40  }
41  // Method to find a spot by its number
42  public ParkingSpot getSpotByNumber(int spotNumber) {
43    for (ParkingSpot spot : parkingSpots) {
44      if (spot.getSpotNumber() == spotNumber) {
45        return spot;
46      }
47    }
48    return null; // Spot not found
49  }
50  // Getter for parking spots
51  public List<ParkingSpot> getParkingSpots() {
52    return parkingSpots;
53  }
54}
```

‍

4\. Main Method to Run the Parking Lot : 

‍

Java

```
1public class ParkingLotMain {
2  public static void main(String[] args) {
3    // Initialize parking spots
4    List<ParkingSpot> parkingSpots = new ArrayList<>();
5    parkingSpots.add(new CarParkingSpot(1));
6    parkingSpots.add(new CarParkingSpot(2));
7    parkingSpots.add(new BikeParkingSpot(3));
8    parkingSpots.add(new BikeParkingSpot(4));
9    // Initialize parking lot
10    ParkingLot parkingLot = new ParkingLot(parkingSpots);
11    // Create fee strategies
12    ParkingFeeStrategy basicHourlyRateStrategy = new BasicHourlyRateStrategy();
13    ParkingFeeStrategy premiumRateStrategy = new PremiumRateStrategy();
14    // Create vehicles using Factory Pattern with fee strategies
15     Vehicle car1 = VehicleFactory.createVehicle("Car", "CAR123", basicHourlyRateStrategy);
16        Vehicle car2 = VehicleFactory.createVehicle("Car", "CAR345", basicHourlyRateStrategy);
17
18        Vehicle bike1 = VehicleFactory.createVehicle("Bike", "BIKE456", premiumRateStrategy);
19        Vehicle bike2 = VehicleFactory.createVehicle("Bike", "BIKE123", premiumRateStrategy);
20
21    // Park vehicles
22    ParkingSpot carSpot = parkingLot.parkVehicle(car1);
23    ParkingSpot bikeSpot = parkingLot.parkVehicle(bike1);
24    Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
25    System.out.println("Select payment method for your vehicle:");
26    System.out.println("1. Credit Card");
27    System.out.println("2. Cash");
28    int paymentMethod = scanner.nextInt();
29    // Process payments using Strategy Patterns
30    if (carSpot != null) {
31      // Calculate fee using the specific strategy for the vehicle
32      double carFee = car1.calculateFee(2, DurationType.HOURS);
33      PaymentStrategy carPaymentStrategy =
34          getPaymentStrategy(paymentMethod, carFee);
35      carPaymentStrategy.processPayment(carFee);
36      parkingLot.vacateSpot(carSpot, car1);
37    }
38    if (bikeSpot != null) {
39      // Calculate fee using the specific strategy for the vehicle
40      double bikeFee = bike1.calculateFee(3, DurationType.HOURS);
41      PaymentStrategy bikePaymentStrategy =
42          getPaymentStrategy(paymentMethod, bikeFee);
43      bikePaymentStrategy.processPayment(bikeFee);
44      parkingLot.vacateSpot(bikeSpot, bike1);
45    }
46    scanner.close();
47  }
48  private static PaymentStrategy getPaymentStrategy(
49      int paymentMethod, double fee) {
50    switch (paymentMethod) {
51      case 1:
52        return new CreditCardPayment(fee);
53      case 2:
54        return new CashPayment(fee);
55      default:
56        System.out.println("Invalid choice! Default to Credit card payment.");
57        return new CreditCardPayment(fee);
58    }
59  }
60}
```

‍

Interviewer: Looks good. What makes your approach effective?

‍

Candidate: Here are the key strengths of my approach:

• Scalability:

The design allows easy expansion to accommodate more vehicle types, parking spot categories, and payment methods.

• Modularity:

Each component, such as vehicle creation, parking management, and payment processing, is handled separately, ensuring a clean and maintainable structure.

• Flexibility:

The use of design patterns like Factory and Strategy allows seamless modifications and enhancements without affecting existing code.

• Clarity:

The architecture is intuitive, making it easy for developers to understand, implement, and extend when needed.

‍

Extensibility : ‍

1.) Implementing Multi-Floor Parking Lot : 

Currently, our Parking Lot implementation is based on a single floor. However, if an interviewer asks how you would extend your solution to accommodate a multi-floor Parking Lot.

‍

Here’s how you should respond:

‍

To extend the solution for a multi-floor Parking Lot, we can create a concrete ParkingFloor class and encapsulate the logic of handling parking spots within this class. The ParkingLot class would then be responsible for managing multiple parking floors, while each floor would handle its own parking spots. This approach adheres to the Single Responsibility Principle, ensuring that each class has a clear and distinct responsibility.

‍

**Implementation :** 

1\. Concrete Parking Floor class : 

ParkingFloor.java : 

‍

Java

```
1public class ParkingFloor {
2  // List of parking spots on this floor
3  private List<ParkingSpot> spots;
4  // Unique identifier for the floor
5  private int floorNumber;
6  public ParkingFloor(int floorNumber) {
7    this.floorNumber = floorNumber;
8    this.spots = new ArrayList<>();
9  }
10  // Adds a parking spot to this floor.
11  public void addParkingSpot(ParkingSpot spot) {
12    this.spots.add(spot);
13  }
14  // Finds an available parking spot for a specific vehicle type.
15  public ParkingSpot findAvailableSpot(String vehicleType) {
16    // Iterate through all spots to find an available spot matching the vehicle
17    // type
18    for (ParkingSpot spot : spots) {
19      // Check if the spot is not occupied and matches the vehicle type
20      if (!spot.isOccupied()
21          && spot.getSpotType().equalsIgnoreCase(vehicleType)) {
22        return spot;
23      }
24    }
25    return null; // No available spot found
26  }
27  // Retrieves all parking spots on this floor.
28  public List<ParkingSpot> getParkingSpots() {
29    return spots;
30  }
31  public int getFloorNumber() {
32    return floorNumber;
33  }
34}
```

‍

2\. Create a New Builder Class for Parking Lot : 

‍

Java

```
1/**
2 - Builder class for creating a flexible and extensible parking lot.
3 - Provides a fluent interface for constructing multi-floor parking lots.
4 */
5public class ParkingLotBuilder {
6  // List of floors to be added to the parking lot
7  private List<ParkingFloor> floors;
8  // Constructor initializes the list of floors.
9  public ParkingLotBuilder() {
10    this.floors = new ArrayList<>();
11  }
12  // Adds a pre-configured parking floor to the parking lot.
13  public ParkingLotBuilder addFloor(ParkingFloor floor) {
14    floors.add(floor);
15    return this;
16  }
17  // Creates a floor with specified numbers of different vehicle parking spots.
18  public ParkingLotBuilder createFloor(int floorNumber, int numOfCarSpots,
19      int numOfBikeSpots, int... otherSpotCounts) {
20    // Create a new parking floor
21    ParkingFloor floor = new ParkingFloor(floorNumber);
22    // Add car spots
23    for (int i = 0; i < numOfCarSpots; i++) {
24      floor.addParkingSpot(new CarParkingSpot(i + 1));
25    }
26    // Add bike spots
27    for (int i = 0; i < numOfBikeSpots; i++) {
28      floor.addParkingSpot(new BikeParkingSpot(numOfCarSpots + i + 1));
29    }
30    // Add other types of spots if provided
31    int spotOffset = numOfCarSpots + numOfBikeSpots;
32    for (int i = 0; i < otherSpotCounts.length; i++) {
33      for (int j = 0; j < otherSpotCounts[i]; j++) {
34        // Dynamically add other vehicle type spots
35        // Note: This uses OtherVehicle as a placeholder. In a real system,
36        // you might want a more robust way to handle different vehicle types
37        floor.addParkingSpot(new OtherVehicle(
38            spotOffset + j + 1, new BasicHourlyRateStrategy()));
39      }
40      // Update the spot offset for the next type of vehicle
41      spotOffset += otherSpotCounts[i];
42    }
43    // Add the configured floor to the list of floors
44    floors.add(floor);
45    return this;
46  }
47  // Builds and returns the fully configured parking lot.
48  public ParkingLot build() {
49    return new ParkingLot(floors);
50  }
51}
```

‍

3\. Modify The parkingLot class to Accommodate the Multiple Floors logic : 

‍

Java

```
1import java.util.List;
2
3// Class representing a parking lot with multiple floors
4public class ParkingLot {
5  private List<ParkingFloor>
6      floors; // List of parking floors in the parking lot
7  // Constructor to initialize the parking lot with given floors
8  public ParkingLot(List<ParkingFloor> floors) {
9    this.floors = floors;
10  }
11  // Method to find the first available parking spot for a given vehicle type
12  public ParkingSpot findAvailableSpot(String vehicleType) {
13    for (ParkingFloor floor : floors) {
14      ParkingSpot spot = floor.findAvailableSpot(vehicleType);
15      if (spot != null) {
16        return spot; // Return the first available spot found
17      }
18    }
19    return null; // Return null if no spot is available
20  }
21  // Method to park a vehicle in an available spot
22  public ParkingSpot parkVehicle(Vehicle vehicle) {
23    ParkingSpot spot = findAvailableSpot(vehicle.getVehicleType());
24    if (spot != null) {
25      spot.parkVehicle(vehicle); // Park the vehicle in the found spot
26      System.out.println(
27          "Vehicle parked successfully in spot: " + spot.getSpotNumber());
28      return spot;
29    }
30    // If no spot is available, notify the user
31    System.out.println(
32        "No parking spots available for " + vehicle.getVehicleType() + "!");
33    return null;
34  }
35  // Method to vacate a parking spot
36  public void vacateSpot(ParkingSpot spot, Vehicle vehicle) {
37    // Ensure the spot is occupied and the vehicle matches before vacating
38    if (spot != null && spot.isOccupied()
39        && spot.getVehicle().equals(vehicle)) {
40      spot.vacate(); // Free up the parking spot
41      System.out.println(vehicle.getVehicleType()
42          + " vacated the spot: " + spot.getSpotNumber());
43    } else {
44      System.out.println("Invalid operation! Either the spot is already vacant "
45                         + "or the vehicle does not match.");
46    }
47  }
48  // Method to retrieve a parking spot by its spot number
49  public ParkingSpot getSpotByNumber(int spotNumber) {
50    for (ParkingFloor floor : floors) {
51      for (ParkingSpot spot : floor.getParkingSpots()) {
52        if (spot.getSpotNumber() == spotNumber) {
53          return spot; // Return the parking spot if found
54        }
55      }
56    }
57    return null; // Return null if no spot with the given number exists
58  }
59  // Getter method to retrieve the list of parking floors
60  public List<ParkingFloor> getFloors() {
61    return floors;
62  }
63}
```

‍

4\. Modify the parking lot Main class to Accommodate the Multiple floors logic : 

‍

Java

```
1// Demonstrates the usage of the flexible parking lot builder.
2public class ParkingLotMain {
3  public static void main(String[] args) {
4    // Create a parking lot with multiple floors and varied spot configurations
5    ParkingLot parkingLot =
6        new ParkingLotBuilder()
7            // First floor: 2 car spots, 2 bike spots
8            .createFloor(1, 2, 2)
9            // Second floor: 3 car spots, 1 bike spot, 1 other vehicle spot
10            .createFloor(2, 3, 1, 1)
11            .build();

13    // Example: parking vehicles, calculating fees, etc.
14  }
15}
```

‍

## Conclusion : 

This low-level design for Parking Lot showcases a well-structured and scalable architecture, emphasizing modularity and extensibility. By supporting various enhancements such as new Vehicles and new payment types addition and multiple floors feature, this design ensures maintainability and flexibility. In an interview setting, presenting this design would demonstrate your ability to create robust and adaptable solutions, highlighting your proficiency in applying design patterns and best practices.\n\n---\n\n## Source: https://codewitharyan.com/tech-blogs/design-elevator-system\n\n# Design Elevator System
