### 🐈‍⬛ Github Codes Link: [https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode](https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode)

## ‍

## Low-Level Design: Elevator System 🏗️

An elevator system is designed to efficiently transport people between floors in a building. The system needs to handle multiple requests, optimize elevator movement, and ensure safety. The system should be able to accommodate different priorities, handle emergency situations, and provide a smooth user experience.

‍

## Rules of the System: 

 Setup:

• The building has multiple floors and can have multiple elevators.

• Elevators can be called from any floor and can travel to any floor.

• Each elevator has a maximum capacity and weight limit.

• The system schedules elevator movement based on requests and optimizes for efficiency.

‍

Operation:

• Users can request an elevator from any floor by pressing up/down buttons.

• Inside the elevator, users can select destination floors. (There is one more type, where users can choose destination floors outside only, but we are not covering those here !!)

• The system prioritizes and schedules these requests efficiently.

• Elevators have indicators showing current floor and direction.

 ‍

Safety Features:

• Emergency stop functionality must be available.

• Overload detection prevents elevator movement if capacity is exceeded.

• Door sensors prevent doors from closing if obstructed.

## ‍

## Interview Setting 🤝

### Point 1: Introduction and Vague Problem Statement:

Interviewer: Let's start with a basic problem statement. Design an Elevator system.

‍

Candidate: Certainly! Here's my understanding of the Elevator system:

• The system will manage multiple elevators within a building.

• Users can request elevators from different floors.

• The system optimizes elevator assignments to minimize wait times.

• Safety features must be implemented to handle emergencies.

• The system should be scalable to handle buildings of different sizes.

Is this the expected flow?

‍

Interviewer: Yes, you are aligned with the flow. Please continue ahead.

‍

Candidate: Great! Before diving into the design, I'd like to clarify a few requirements:

• How many elevators should the system support?

• Are there special considerations like express elevators or service elevators?

• How should the system optimize elevator assignment?

‍

### Point 2: Clarifying Requirements:

Interviewer: We want a system that:

• Supports multiple elevators in a building with any number of floors.

• Handles both external requests (from floors) and internal requests (from inside elevators).

• Efficiently schedules elevator movement to minimize wait times. 

‍

Candidate: To summarize, the key requirements are:

• A building with multiple floors and elevators.

• External and internal request handling.

• Intelligent scheduling algorithm for elevator assignment.

• Ability to handle edge cases like power outages or maintenance.

 ‍

Interviewer: Perfect, let's proceed.

 ‍

### Point 3: Identifying Key Components:

Candidate: Now that we have the requirements, let's identify the key components of our Elevator system:

1\. Elevator: Represents individual elevator cars.

     Class: Elevator

     Description: This class represents individual elevators with their current state.

‍‍

Java

```
1// Core Elevator class with a simple queue for managing requests
2class Elevator {
3  private int id; // Unique identifier for the elevator
4  private int currentFloor; // The floor where the elevator is currently located
5  private Direction
6      direction; // The current direction of the elevator (UP, DOWN, or IDLE)
7  private ElevatorState state; // The current operational state of the elevator
8                               // (e.g., MOVING, IDLE, etc.)
9  private List<ElevatorObserver>
10      observers; // A list of observers (listeners) that monitor the elevator's
11                 // status
12  private Queue<ElevatorRequest>
13      requests; // A simple queue to manage floor requests in the order they are
14                // received
15
16  // Get the elevator's ID
17  public int getId() {
18    return id;
19  }
20
21  // Get the elevator's current floor
22  public int getCurrentFloor() {
23    return currentFloor;
24  }
25
26  // Get the elevator's current direction
27  public Direction getDirection() {
28    return direction;
29  }
30
31  // Get the elevator's current state
32  public ElevatorState getState() {
33    return state;
34  }
35
36  // Get a copy of the current requests queue to prevent external modification
37  public Queue<ElevatorRequest> getRequestsQueue() {
38    return new LinkedList<>(requests);
39  }
40
41  // Get a list of all destination floors for display purposes
42  public List<ElevatorRequest> getDestinationFloors() {
43    return new ArrayList<>(requests);
44  }
45}
```

‍

2\. Building: Contains floors and elevators.

     Class: Building

     Description: This class represents the building structure.

‍

Java

```
1class Building {
2  private String name; // Name of the building
3  private int numberOfFloors; // Total number of floors in the building
4  private ElevatorController
5      elevatorController; // Controller to manage all elevators in the building
6}
```

‍

3\. Floor : Contains a Single Floor : 

Class : Floor 

Description : This class represents a Single Floor

‍

Java

```
1// Represents a floor in a building
2	public class Floor {
3	    private int floorNumber;
4	    
5	    public Floor(int floorNumber) {
6	        this.floorNumber = floorNumber;
7	    }
8	    
9	    public int getFloorNumber() {
10	        return floorNumber;
11	    }
12	}
```

‍

4\. ElevatorController: Manages elevator operations and scheduling.

     Class: ElevatorController

     Description: This class handles request processing and elevator assignment.

‍

Java

```
1// Manages the operations and coordination of all elevators in a building
2class ElevatorController {
3    private List<Elevator> elevators; // List of all elevators controlled by this system
4    private List<Floor> floors; // List of floors in the building
5    private SchedulingStrategy schedulingStrategy; // Strategy to decide which elevator should handle a request
6    private int currentElevatorId; // Keeps track of the current elevator's ID for handling internal requests
7}
```

‍

5\. State Management with Enums:

To effectively manage elevator states, we'll use enums to achieve the functionality.

‍

Java

```
1// Enum to represent the direction of the elevator
2	enum Direction {
3	    UP,      // The elevator is moving upward
4	    DOWN,    // The elevator is moving downward
5	    IDLE     // The elevator is stationary, not moving
6	}
7
8	// Enum to represent the state of the elevator
9	enum ElevatorState {
10	    IDLE,         // The elevator is not moving, waiting for requests
11	    MOVING,       // The elevator is in motion (either up or down)
12	    STOPPED,      // The elevator has temporarily stopped (e.g., at a floor)
13	    MAINTENANCE   // The elevator is out of service and undergoing maintenance
14}
```

‍

Interviewer: That sounds good. Let's proceed with the design details.

 ‍

Point 4: Design Challenges:

Interviewer: What design challenges do you anticipate?

 ‍

Candidate: The key challenges for the Elevator system include:

• Efficient Scheduling Algorithm: Minimizing wait times and optimizing elevator assignments.

• Handling Concurrent Requests: Managing multiple simultaneous requests. (Can be ignored also)

• Fault Tolerance: Ensuring system functionality if an elevator fails. (Edge Case)

• Load Balancing: Distributing requests across multiple elevators.

• Handling emergency stops, power outages, and maintenance modes. (Edge Cases)

 ‍

Point 5: Approach:

Interviewer: How would you approach these challenges?

‍

Candidate: I propose using design patterns effectively. Here are my strategies:

1\. Observer Pattern for Event Handling:

  - Notifies system components of state changes.

  - Enables real-time updates for display panels and monitoring systems.

 ‍

2\. Command Pattern for Request Processing:

  - Encapsulates each request as an object.

  - Allows for request queuing, prioritization, and cancellation.

 ‍

3\. Strategy Pattern for Scheduling Algorithms:

  - Enables different scheduling strategies.

  - Can switch between algorithms based on time of day or building needs.

 ‍‍

Interviewer: That sounds like a solid approach. Let's delve into the implementation details.

 ‍

Point 6: Implementation:

Interviewer: Ready to discuss implementation?

Candidate: Yes. I'll focus on a modular and scalable design that meets the core Elevator system requirements.

‍

Elevator System Implementation With Design Patterns : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743478554267-Frame-245-\(4\).png)

‍

1.) Observer Pattern for Event Handling:

The Observer pattern will help notify relevant components about elevator state changes and events.

ElevatorObserver Interface : 

‍

Java

```
1// Observer interface for handling elevator events
2interface ElevatorObserver {
3  // Called when an elevator's state changes
4  void onElevatorStateChange(Elevator elevator, ElevatorState state);
5
6
7  // Called when an elevator changes its current floor
8  void onElevatorFloorChange(Elevator elevator, int floor);
9}
```

‍

• Elevator Display Concrete Observer Class :

‍

Java

```
1// Concrete implementation of the Observer interface
2	class ElevatorDisplay implements ElevatorObserver {
3	    @Override
4	    public void onElevatorStateChange(Elevator elevator, ElevatorState state) {
5	        // Display the new state of the elevator
6	        System.out.println("Elevator " + elevator.getId() + " state changed to " + state);
7	    }
8
9	    @Override
10	    public void onElevatorFloorChange(Elevator elevator, int floor) {
11	        // Display the elevator's movement to a new floor
12	        System.out.println("Elevator " + elevator.getId() + " moved to floor " + floor);
13	    }
14	}
```

‍

2.) Command Pattern for Request Processing : 

‍

Command Interface : 

Java

```
1// Command Pattern for Request Processing
2	interface ElevatorCommand {
3	    // Method to execute the command
4	    void execute();
5	}
```

‍‍

‍ • Elevator Request for all the elevator requests.

‍

Java

```
1package CommandPatternIMP.ConcreteClasses;
2import CommandPatternIMP.ElevatorCommand;
3import CommonEnums.Direction;
4import UtilityClasses.ElevatorController;
5
6public class ElevatorRequest implements ElevatorCommand {
7    private int elevatorId; // ID of the elevator involved in the request
8    private int floor; // Floor where the request is made
9    private Direction requestDirection; // The direction of the elevator request
10    private ElevatorController controller; // Reference to the ElevatorController to handle the request
11    private boolean isInternalRequest; // Distinguishes internal vs external requests
12    // Constructor to initialize the elevator request
13    public ElevatorRequest(int elevatorId, int floor, boolean isInternalRequest,
14                           Direction direction) {
15        this.elevatorId = elevatorId;
16        this.floor = floor;
17        this.isInternalRequest = isInternalRequest;
18        this.requestDirection = direction;
19        this.controller = new ElevatorController();
20    }
21
22    // Execute method to process the request via the controller
23    @Override
24    public void execute() {
25        if (isInternalRequest)
26            controller.requestFloor(elevatorId, floor);
27        else
28            controller.requestElevator(elevatorId, floor, requestDirection);
29    }
30
31    // Getters and Setters for the ElevatorRequest
32    public Direction getDirection() {
33        return requestDirection;
34    }
35
36    public int getFloor() {
37        return floor;
38    }
39
40    public boolean checkIsInternalRequest(){
41        return isInternalRequest;
42    }
43}
```

‍‍

2.) Strategy Pattern for Scheduling Algorithms :

• SchedulingStrategy.java, Common Interface for Different Scheduling Strategies : 

This interface defines the common method selectElevator for different scheduling strategies. It takes a list of elevators, the requested floor, and the direction as parameters and returns the selected elevator.

‍‍

Java

```
1// Strategy Pattern for Scheduling
2	interface SchedulingStrategy {
3	    // Determines the next stop for the given elevator
4	    int getNextStop(Elevator elevator);
5	}
```

‍

Concrete Strategies : 

• First Come first Serve Scheduling Strategy:

This class implements the FCFS algorithm, where the elevator completes the requests in the same order as they are received.

‍‍

Java

```
1// First-Come-First-Served Algorithm
2class FCFSSchedulingStrategy implements SchedulingStrategy {
3  @Override
4  public int getNextStop(Elevator elevator) {
5    // Get the elevator's current direction and floor
6    Direction elevatorDirection = elevator.getDirection();
7    int currentFloor = elevator.getCurrentFloor();
8
9    // Retrieve the FIFO queue of floor requests
10    Queue<ElevatorRequest> requestQueue = elevator.getRequestsQueue();
11
12    // If the request queue is empty, stay on the current floor
13    if (requestQueue.isEmpty())
14      return currentFloor;
15
16    // Fetch the next requested floor
17    int nextRequestedFloor = requestQueue.poll().getFloor();
18
19    // If the next floor is the current floor, return it
20    if (nextRequestedFloor == currentFloor)
21      return currentFloor;
22    // Set elevator's direction based on its current state and next floor
23    if (elevatorDirection == Direction.IDLE) {
24      elevator.setDirection(
25          nextRequestedFloor > currentFloor ? Direction.UP : Direction.DOWN);
26    } else if (elevatorDirection == Direction.UP
27        && nextRequestedFloor < currentFloor) {
28      elevator.setDirection(Direction.DOWN);
29    } else if (nextRequestedFloor > currentFloor) {
30      elevator.setDirection(Direction.UP);
31    }
32
33    // Return the next requested floor
34    return nextRequestedFloor;
35  }
36}
```

‍

• Scan Scheduling Strategy : 

‍

Java

```
1/* SIMULATION SCENARIO : Down Request While Elevator is Moving Down
2	 - 
3	 - Setup:
4	 - - Elevator is at floor 10
5	 - - Elevator is moving DOWN
6	 - - Elevator has destinations at floors 7, 5, and 2
7	 - - External DOWN request arrives from floor 8
8	 - 
9	 - System Reaction: 
10	 - The elevator controller will assign Elevator to serve this request since it's already 
11	 - moving DOWN and will pass floor 8. The external request will be inserted into 
12	 - Elevator's request queue in proper order (between floors 10 and 7).
13	 - The elevator will stop at floor 8 to pick up the passenger without changing
14	 - its overall downward journey pattern, continuing to floors 7, 5, and 2 afterward.
15	 */
```

‍

This class implements the SCAN algorithm, where the elevator scans in one direction until the elevator has reached the last floor of the 

Current direction it is moving in.

‍

Java

```
1// Scan Scheduling Strategy for handling elevator requests
2class ScanSchedulingStrategy implements SchedulingStrategy {
3  @Override
4  public int getNextStop(Elevator elevator) {
5    // Retrieve elevator's current direction and floor
6    Direction elevatorDirection = elevator.getDirection();
7    int currentFloor = elevator.getCurrentFloor();
8    Queue<ElevatorRequest> requests = elevator.getRequestsQueue();
9
10
11    // If there are no requests, stay on the current floor
12    if (requests.isEmpty())
13      return currentFloor;
14
15
16    // Priority queues to handle requests in up and down directions
17    PriorityQueue<ElevatorRequest> upQueue =
18        new PriorityQueue<>(); // Min-heap for upward requests
19    PriorityQueue<ElevatorRequest> downQueue =
20        new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> b.getFloor() - a.getFloor()); // Max-heap for downward requests
21
22
23    // Categorize requests based on their relative position to the current floor
24    while (!requests.isEmpty()) {
25      ElevatorRequest elevatorRequest = requests.poll();
26      int floor = elevatorRequest.getFloor();
27      if (floor > currentFloor)
28        upQueue.add(elevatorRequest);
29      else
30        downQueue.add(elevatorRequest);
31    }
32
33
34    // Handle the case when the elevator is IDLE
35    if (elevatorDirection == Direction.IDLE) {
36      // Determine the nearest request and set direction accordingly
37      int nearestUpwardRequest =
38          upQueue.isEmpty() ? -1 : upQueue.peek().getFloor();
39      int nearestDownwardRequest =
40          downQueue.isEmpty() ? -1 : downQueue.peek().getFloor();
41
42
43      if (nearestUpwardRequest == -1) {
44        elevator.setDirection(Direction.DOWN);
45        return downQueue.poll().getFloor();
46      } else if (nearestDownwardRequest == -1) {
47        elevator.setDirection(Direction.UP);
48        return upQueue.poll().getFloor();
49      } else {
50        // Choose the closest request
51        if (Math.abs(nearestUpwardRequest - currentFloor)
52            < Math.abs(nearestDownwardRequest - currentFloor)) {
53          elevator.setDirection(Direction.UP);
54          return upQueue.poll().getFloor();
55        } else {
56          elevator.setDirection(Direction.DOWN);
57          return downQueue.poll().getFloor();
58        }
59      }
60    }
61
62
63    // Handle movement in the UP direction
64    if (elevatorDirection == Direction.UP) {
65      return !upQueue.isEmpty() ? UpQueue.poll().getFloor()
66                                : switchDirection(elevator, downQueue);
67    }
68    // Handle movement in the DOWN direction
69    else {
70      return !downQueue.isEmpty() ? DownQueue.poll().getFloor()
71                                  : switchDirection(elevator, upQueue);
72    }
73  }
74
75
76  // Helper method to switch the elevator's direction when no further requests
77  // exist in the current direction
78  private int switchDirection(
79      Elevator elevator, PriorityQueue<Integer> requestsQueue) {
80    elevator.setDirection(elevator.getDirection() == Direction.UP
81            ? Direction.DOWN
82            : Direction.UP);
83    return requestsQueue.isEmpty() ? elevator.getCurrentFloor()
84                                   : requestsQueue.poll().getFloor();
85  }
86}
87
88
89/* SIMULATION SCENARIO : Up Request While Elevator is Moving Down
90 -  - Setup:
91 - - Elevator is at floor 10
92 - - Elevator is moving DOWN
93 - - Elevator has destinations at floors 6 and 2
94 - - External UP request arrives from floor 4
95 -  - System Reaction:
96 - The Scan Scheduling Strategy would not assign Elevator to this request
97 - because its current direction doesn't match the request direction. Instead,
98 - this request would be queued. the Elevator will complete its current DOWN
99 - journey to floor 2, then reverse direction and fulfill the UP request from
100 - floor 4.
101 */
```

‍

### • Difference Between Scan and Look Strategies Algorithms : 

• **Scan Algorithm :** 

The Scan algorithm serves requests in the elevator's current direction until the end, the elevator moves till the last floor for a particular direction and serves requests as they come, then switches direction. It completely ignores requests that require the elevator to reverse its current direction of travel.

‍

• **Look Algorithm :** 

The Look algorithm also prioritizes requests in the current direction. The elevator does not goes till the end in one direction, it only goes till The floor where the last request was received for a particular direction and it keeps track of requests that would require changing direction. It stores these requests in a queue and serves them when it changes direction, before taking new requests.

‍

• Example Explained : 

Imagine an elevator at the 4th floor moving upward:

In the Scan algorithm:

• It will serve all requests from floors 5, 6, 7, etc.

• If someone on floor 2 presses the "up" button, the Scan algorithm ignores this request until it has finished all upward requests and reversed direction.

‍

In the Look algorithm:

• It will also serve all requests from floors 5, 6, 7, etc. first

• If someone on floor 2 presses the "up" button, the Look algorithm remembers this request

• After completing all upward requests and changing to downward direction, it will store all these pending requests in a pending queue

• When it switches back to upward direction, it will first serve the pending requests present int the queue from floor 2 (if it hasn't been handled by another elevator), then take any new upward requests

‍

The key difference is that Look is more efficient by remembering and prioritizing previously skipped requests when it changes direction.

‍

### • Look Scheduling Strategy : (Explained better in Video, please watch video)

Example to understand flow:

• Starting Floor: 5

• Internal Requests (in order):

1\. -1(first request, so the elevator initially plans to go down)

2\. 8

3\. 2

4\. 6

5\. -5

6\. 10

• External Requests (with direction):

• Floor 3with direction DOWN

• Floor 4with direction UP

• Floor -8with direction DOWN

‍

How the Code Works

1\. Determine the Primary Target & Direction:

• The method first inspects the first request in the queue.

• In our example, the first request is for floor -1.

• Since -1is below 5, the travel direction is set to DOWN.

‍

2\. Scan for Intermediate Stops:

• The algorithm then iterates through all requests in the queue to find any floor that lies between the current floor (5) and the primary target (-1).

• For Internal Requests:

• Every internal request is eligible if its floor lies between 5 and -1.

• In our example, floor 2qualifies because 5 > 2 > -1.

• For External Requests:

• Only those external requests that are also in the DOWNdirection are considered.

• Among the external requests, only floor 3with direction DOWN qualifies if it lies between 5 and -1.

• However, notice that even though both floor 2 (internal) and floor 3 (external) are in range, the method chooses the one that appears first along the journey from floor 5 toward -1 (i.e. the candidate that is closest to 5).

‍

3\. Choosing the Next Stop:

• The algorithm compares all candidates found in the scan.

• In our example:

• Although the primary target is -1, the intermediate candidate floor 2(or 3if the external request is encountered before 2) is considered first based on the order in the journey.

• According to your example details, the elevator should stop at 2because that is the first floor encountered on the way from 5 to -1 that meets the criteria.

• Once an intermediate stop is found, it is returned as the next stop, and the elevator will later continue toward the primary target after handling the intermediate stop.

‍

4\. Handling Mixed Requests:

• Internal requestsare always accepted if they lie in the travel range.

• External requestsare only accepted if they:

• Are in the same travel direction (in this case, DOWN).

• Lie within the range between the current floor and the primary target.

• This ensures that if, for instance, an external request from floor 4(UP) is present, it will be ignored since it does not match the DOWN direction.

‍

In Summary

• Step 1:The first request (-1) sets the direction to DOWN.

• Step 2:As the elevator starts moving from 5toward -1, it scans the queue for any stops that fall between these floors.

• Step 3:Internal requests (like floor 2) and matching external requests (like floor 3with DOWN direction) are candidates.

• Step 4:The method returns the first encountered candidate (e.g., floor 2) as the next stop.

‍

This design ensures that the elevator optimizes its journey by stopping at all intermediate requests that lie along the planned path, and it only honors external requests that match the travel direction.

‍

Java

```
1public class LookSchedulingStrategy implements SchedulingStrategy {
2    @Override
3    public int getNextStop(Elevator elevator) {
4        int currentFloor = elevator.getCurrentFloor();
5        Queue<ElevatorRequest> requests = elevator.getRequestsQueue();
6        // If there are no pending requests, remain on the current floor.
7        if (requests == null || requests.isEmpty()) {
8            return currentFloor;
9        }
10        // Determine the primary target from the first request in the queue.
11        ElevatorRequest primaryRequest = requests.peek();
12        int primaryFloor = primaryRequest.getFloor();
13        // Determine the travel direction based on the primary target.
14        Direction travelDirection;
15        if (primaryFloor > currentFloor) {
16            travelDirection = Direction.UP;
17        } else if (primaryFloor < currentFloor) {
18            travelDirection = Direction.DOWN;
19        } else {
20            return currentFloor; // Already at the requested floor.
21        }
22        // Look for any request along the journey from currentFloor to primaryFloor.
23        // For upward movement, we need the smallest floor greater than currentFloor and <=
24        // primaryFloor. For downward movement, we need the largest floor less than currentFloor and >=
25        // primaryFloor.
26        Integer candidate = null;
27
28
29        for (ElevatorRequest req : requests) {
30            int reqFloor = req.getFloor();
31            // Check if the request is within the range between currentFloor and primaryFloor.
32            if (travelDirection == Direction.UP && reqFloor > currentFloor && reqFloor <= primaryFloor) {
33                // For internal requests we always consider; for external requests, only if they are going
34                // UP.
35                if (req.checkIsInternalRequest()
36                        || (!req.checkIsInternalRequest() && req.getDirection() == Direction.UP)) {
37                    // Choose the candidate that is closest to the current floor (i.e. the smallest floor
38                    // greater than currentFloor).
39                    if (candidate == null || reqFloor < candidate) {
40                        candidate = reqFloor;
41                    }
42                }
43            } else if (travelDirection == Direction.DOWN && reqFloor < currentFloor
44                    && reqFloor >= primaryFloor) {
45                // For downward movement, consider the request if internal or if external with direction
46                // DOWN.
47                if (req.checkIsInternalRequest()
48                        || (!req.checkIsInternalRequest() && req.getDirection() == Direction.DOWN)) {
49                    // For a downward journey, we choose the candidate that is closest to the current floor
50                    // (i.e. the largest floor less than currentFloor).
51                    if (candidate == null || reqFloor > candidate) {
52                        candidate = reqFloor;
53                    }
54                }
55            }
56        }
57        // If a candidate was found in the path, return that as the next stop;
58        // otherwise, fall back to the primary target.
59        return (candidate != null) ? candidate : primaryFloor;
60    }
61}
```

‍

4.) Core Classes Implementation:

To begin, let's create the core classes for our elevator system design, which will be responsible for managing the elevator operations.

‍

• Elevator Class : 

• The Elevator class manages the core functionalities and state of an elevator system. It also implements the Observer Pattern to notify observers about state and floor changes.

‍

Java

```
1// Core Elevator class with simple queue for managing requests
2class Elevator {
3  // Unique ID for the elevator
4  private int id;
5  // Current floor where the elevator is located
6  private int currentFloor;
7  // Current direction of the elevator (UP, DOWN, or IDLE)
8  private Direction direction;
9  // Current operational state of the elevator (IDLE, MOVING, etc.)
10  private ElevatorState state;
11  // List of observers to monitor elevator events
12  private List<ElevatorObserver> observers;
13  // Queue to manage all requests (both internal and external)
14  private Queue<ElevatorRequest> requests;
15  // Constructor to initialize the elevator
16  public Elevator(int id) {
17    this.id = id;
18    this.currentFloor = 1; // Default initial floor
19    this.direction = Direction.IDLE;
20    this.state = ElevatorState.IDLE;
21    this.observers = new ArrayList<>();
22    this.requests = new LinkedList<>();
23  }
24  // Add an observer to monitor elevator events
25  public void addObserver(ElevatorObserver observer) {
26    observers.add(observer);
27  }
28
29  // Remove an observer
30  public void removeObserver(ElevatorObserver observer) {
31    observers.remove(observer);
32  }
33
34  // Notify all observers about a state change
35  private void notifyStateChange(ElevatorState state) {
36    for (ElevatorObserver observer : observers) {
37      observer.onElevatorStateChange(this, state);
38    }
39  }
40
41  // Notify all observers about a floor change
42  private void notifyFloorChange(int floor) {
43    for (ElevatorObserver observer : observers) {
44      observer.onElevatorFloorChange(this, floor);
45    }
46  }
47
48  // Set a new state for the elevator and notify observers
49  public void setState(ElevatorState newState) {
50    this.state = newState;
51    notifyStateChange(newState);
52  }
53
54  // Set the direction of the elevator
55  public void setDirection(Direction newDirection) {
56    this.direction = newDirection;
57  }
58
59  // Add a new floor request to the queue
60  public void addRequest(ElevatorRequest elevatorRequest) {
61    // Avoid duplicate requests
62    if (!requests.contains(elevatorRequest)) {
63      requests.add(elevatorRequest);
64    }
65
66    int requestedFloor = elevatorRequest.getFloor();
67    // If elevator is idle, determine direction and start moving
68    if (state == ElevatorState.IDLE && !requests.isEmpty()) {
69      if (requestedFloor > currentFloor) {
70        direction = Direction.UP;
71      } else if (requestedFloor < currentFloor) {
72        direction = Direction.DOWN;
73      }
74      setState(ElevatorState.MOVING);
75    }
76  }
77
78  /* SIMULATION SCENARIO : Down Request from Above Current Position
79   -    - Setup:
80   - - Elevator is at floor 5
81   - - Elevator is IDLE
82   - - External DOWN request arrives from floor 11
83   -    - System Reaction:
84   - The elevator controller would send Elevator UP to floor 11 to service the
85   - DOWN request. After reaching floor 11, Elevator would change its direction
86   - to DOWN and wait for the passenger to select their destination floor. This
87   - demonstrates how the system correctly handles serving a request that
88   - initially requires moving in the opposite direction of the requested
89   - travel.
90   */
91
92
93  // Move the elevator to the next stop as decided by the scheduling strategy
94  public void moveToNextStop(int nextStop) {
95    // Only move if the elevator is currently in the MOVING state
96    if (state != ElevatorState.MOVING)
97      return;
98    while (currentFloor != nextStop) {
99      // Update floor based on direction
100      if (direction == Direction.UP) {
101        currentFloor++;
102      } else {
103        currentFloor--;
104      }
105      // Notify observers about the floor change
106      notifyFloorChange(currentFloor);
107      // Complete arrival once the target floor is reached
108      if (currentFloor == nextStop) {
109        completeArrival();
110        return;
111      }
112    }
113  }
114  // Handle the elevator's arrival at a destination floor
115  private void completeArrival() {
116    // Stop the elevator and notify observers
117    setState(ElevatorState.STOPPED);
118    // Remove the current floor from the requests queue
119    requests.removeIf(request = > request.getFloor() == currentFloor);
120    // If no more requests, set state to IDLE
121    if (requests.isEmpty()) {
122      direction = Direction.IDLE;
123      setState(ElevatorState.IDLE);
124    } else {
125      // Otherwise, continue moving after a brief stop
126      setState(ElevatorState.MOVING);
127    }
128  }
129  // Get the elevator's ID
130  public int getId() {
131    return id;
132  }
133  // Get the elevator's current floor
134  public int getCurrentFloor() {
135    return currentFloor;
136  }
137  // Get the elevator's current direction
138  public Direction getDirection() {
139    return direction;
140  }
141  // Get the elevator's current state
142  public ElevatorState getState() {
143    return state;
144  }
145  // Get a copy of the current requests queue to prevent external modification
146  public Queue<ElevatorRequest> getRequestsQueue() {
147    return new LinkedList<>(requests);
148  }
149  // Get a list of all destination floors for display purposes
150  public List<ElevatorRequest> getDestinationFloors() {
151    return new ArrayList<>(requests);
152  }
153}
```

‍

• Elevator Controller class : 

The Elevator Controller class manages the operation of multiple elevators within a building. It initializes elevators, handles external and internal requests, sets scheduling strategies, and simulates elevator movement.

‍

Java

```
1// Elevator Controller class to manage elevators and floor requests
2class ElevatorController {
3  // List of all elevators in the system
4  private List<Elevator> elevators;
5  // List of all floors in the building
6  private List<Floor> floors;
7  // Strategy to determine the scheduling of elevators
8  private SchedulingStrategy schedulingStrategy;
9  // ID of the current elevator (used for internal operations)
10  private int currentElevatorId;
11  // Constructor to initialize elevators and floors
12  public ElevatorController(int numberOfElevators, int numberOfFloors) {
13    this.elevators = new ArrayList<>();
14    this.floors = new ArrayList<>();
15    this.schedulingStrategy = new ScanSchedulingStrategy(); // Default strategy
16    // Initialize elevators with unique IDs
17    for (int i = 1; i <= numberOfElevators; i++) {
18      elevators.add(new Elevator(i));
19    }
20    // Initialize floors
21    for (int i = 1; i <= numberOfFloors; i++) {
22      floors.add(new Floor(i));
23    }
24  }
25
26  // Set the scheduling strategy dynamically
27  public void setSchedulingStrategy(SchedulingStrategy strategy) {
28    this.schedulingStrategy = strategy;
29  }
30
31  // Handle external elevator requests from a specific floor
32  public void requestElevator(
33      int elevatorId, int floorNumber, Direction direction) {
34    System.out.println(
35        "External request: Floor " + floorNumber + ", Direction " + direction);
36    // Find the elevator by its ID
37    Elevator selectedElevator = getElevatorById(elevatorId);
38    if (selectedElevator != null) {
39      // Add the request to the selected elevator
40      selectedElevator.addRequest(
41          new ElevatorRequest(elevatorId, floorNumber, false, direction));
42      System.out.println("Assigned elevator " + selectedElevator.getId()
43          + " to floor " + floorNumber);
44    } else {
45      // If no suitable elevator is found
46      System.out.println("No elevator available for floor " + floorNumber);
47    }
48  }
49
50  // Handle internal elevator requests to a specific floor
51  public void requestFloor(int elevatorId, int floorNumber) {
52    // Find the elevator by its ID
53    Elevator elevator = getElevatorById(elevatorId);
54    System.out.println("Internal request: Elevator " + elevator.getId()
55        + " to floor " + floorNumber);
56    // Determine the direction of the request
57    Direction direction = floorNumber > elevator.getCurrentFloor()
58        ? Direction.UP
59        : Direction.DOWN;
60    // Add the request to the elevator
61    elevator.addRequest(
62        new ElevatorRequest(elevatorId, floorNumber, true, direction));
63  }
64
65  // Find an elevator by its ID
66  private Elevator getElevatorById(int elevatorId) {
67    for (Elevator elevator : elevators) {
68      if (elevator.getId() == elevatorId)
69        return elevator;
70    }
71    return null; // Return null if no matching elevator is found
72  }
73
74  // Perform a simulation step by moving all elevators
75  public void step() {
76    // Iterate through all elevators
77    for (Elevator elevator : elevators) {
78      // Only process elevators with pending requests
79      if (!elevator.getRequestsQueue().isEmpty()) {
80        // Use the scheduling strategy to find the next stop
81        int nextStop = schedulingStrategy.getNextStop(elevator);
82
83
84        // Move the elevator to the next stop if needed
85        if (elevator.getCurrentFloor() != nextStop)
86          elevator.moveToNextStop(nextStop);
87      }
88    }
89  }
90
91  // Get the list of all elevators
92  public List<Elevator> getElevators() {
93    return elevators;
94  }
95
96  // Get the list of all floors
97  public List<Floor> getFloors() {
98    return floors;
99  }
100
101  // Set the ID of the current elevator
102  public void setCurrentElevator(int elevatorId) {
103    this.currentElevatorId = elevatorId;
104  }
105}
```

‍

• Building Class : 

‍

Java

```
1// Represents a building with elevators and multiple floors
2class Building {
3  private String name; // Name of the building
4  private int numberOfFloors; // Total number of floors in the building
5  private ElevatorController
6      elevatorController; // Controller to manage all elevators in the building
7  // Constructor to initialize the building's details and its elevator system
8  public Building(String name, int numberOfFloors, int numberOfElevators) {
9    this.name = name; // Assign the building's name
10    this.numberOfFloors = numberOfFloors; // Set the total number of floors
11    // Initialize the elevator controller with the specified number of elevators
12    // and floors
13    this.elevatorController =
14        new ElevatorController(numberOfElevators, numberOfFloors);
15  }
16
17  // Getters and Setters for the Building
18  public String getName() {
19    return name;
20  }
21
22  public int getNumberOfFloors() {
23    return numberOfFloors;
24  }
25
26  public ElevatorController getElevatorController() {
27    return elevatorController;
28  }
29}
```

‍

5.) Main Method to Run the Code : 

‍

Java

```
1public class Main {
2    public static void main(String[] args) {
3        // Initialize a building with 10 floors and 3 elevators
4        Building building = new Building("Office Tower", 10, 3);
5        ElevatorController controller = building.getElevatorController();
6        // Create an ElevatorDisplay to observe and display elevator events
7        ElevatorDisplay display = new ElevatorDisplay();
8        for (Elevator elevator : controller.getElevators()) {
9            elevator.addObserver(display); // Add the display as an observer for all elevators
10        }
11        // Simulate elevator requests using a command-line interface
12        Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
13        boolean running = true;
14        // Display simulation details and options
15        System.out.println("Elevator System Simulation");
16        System.out.println("Building: " + building.getName());
17        System.out.println("Floors: " + building.getNumberOfFloors());
18        System.out.println("Elevators: " + controller.getElevators().size());
19        // Main loop for user interactions
20        while (running) {
21            System.out.println("nSelect an option:");
22            System.out.println("1. Request elevator (external)");
23            System.out.println("2. Request floor (internal)");
24            System.out.println("3. Simulate next step");
25            System.out.println("4. Change scheduling strategy");
26            System.out.println("5. Exit simulation");
27            int choice = scanner.nextInt(); // Read user's menu choice
28            switch (choice) {
29                case 1:
30                    // Handle external elevator request
31                    // Handle internal elevator floor request
32                    System.out.print("Enter elevator ID: ");
33                    int externalElevatorId = scanner.nextInt();
34                    controller.setCurrentElevator(externalElevatorId); // Set the selected elevator
35                    System.out.print("Enter floor number: ");
36                    int floorNum = scanner.nextInt();
37                    System.out.print("Direction (1 for UP, 2 for DOWN): ");
38                    int dirChoice = scanner.nextInt();
39                    Direction dir = dirChoice == 1 ? Direction.UP : Direction.DOWN;
40                    controller.requestElevator(externalElevatorId, floorNum, dir);
41                    break;
42                case 2:
43                    // Handle internal elevator floor request
44                    System.out.print("Enter elevator ID: ");
45                    int elevatorId = scanner.nextInt();
46                    controller.setCurrentElevator(elevatorId); // Set the selected elevator
47                    System.out.print("Enter destination floor: ");
48                    int destFloor = scanner.nextInt();
49                    controller.requestFloor(elevatorId, destFloor);
50                    break;
51                case 3:
52                    // Simulate the next step in the system
53                    System.out.println("Simulating next step...");
54                    controller.step(); // Perform the simulation step
55                    displayElevatorStatus(
56                            controller.getElevators()); // Display elevator statuses
57                    break;
58                case 4:
59                    // Change the scheduling strategy
60                    System.out.println("Select strategy:");
61                    System.out.println("1. SCAN Algorithm");
62                    System.out.println("2. FCFS Algorithm");
63                    System.out.println("3. Look Algorithm");
64                    int strategyChoice = scanner.nextInt();
65                    if (strategyChoice == 1) {
66                        controller.setSchedulingStrategy(new ScanSchedulingStrategy());
67                        System.out.println("Strategy set to SCAN Algorithm");
68                    } else {
69                        controller.setSchedulingStrategy(new FCFSSchedulingStrategy());
70                        System.out.println("Strategy set to Nearest Elevator First");
71                    }
72                    break;
73                case 5:
74                    // Exit the simulation
75                    running = false;
76                    break;
77                default:
78                    // Handle invalid choices
79                    System.out.println("Invalid choice!");
80            }
81        }
82        scanner.close(); // Close the scanner to release resources
83        System.out.println("Simulation ended"); // End of simulation
84    }
85
86
87    // Display the status of all elevators in the system
88    private static void displayElevatorStatus(List<Elevator> elevators) {
89        System.out.println("nElevator Status:");
90        for (Elevator elevator : elevators) {
91            // Print details of each elevator, including current floor, direction, and
92            // state
93            System.out.println("Elevator " + elevator.getId() + ": Floor "
94                    + elevator.getCurrentFloor() + ", Direction "
95                    + elevator.getDirection() + ", State " + elevator.getState()
96                    + ", Destinations " + elevator.getDestinationFloors());
97        }
98    }
99 }
```

‍

Interviewer: Looks good. What makes your approach effective?

‍

Candidate: Here are the key strengths of my approach:

• Scalability: The design accommodates easy expansion to include additional elevators, floors, and scheduling algorithms. This ensures that the system can grow with the needs of the building without major redesigns.

‍

• Modularity: Each component, such as elevator control, scheduling, and state management, is handled separately. This separation ensures a clean and maintainable structure, making it easy to update or replace individual components without affecting the overall system.

‍

• Flexibility: The use of design patterns like Strategy and Observer allows for seamless modifications and enhancements. New scheduling strategies or state changes can be introduced without disrupting existing functionality, providing a robust and adaptable system.

‍

• Clarity: The architecture is intuitive, making it straightforward for developers to understand, implement, and extend. Clear responsibilities and interactions between classes and components enhance the overall readability and maintainability of the codebase.

‍

‍

Extensibility : 

1\. Adding New Elevator Types : 

‍

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743482171576-Frame-245-\(5\).png)

‍

The modular design allows for easy integration of different elevator types (e.g., express elevators, freight elevators, or glass elevators) by extending the base Elevator class:

‍

Example : Adding a new Express Elevator

‍

Java

```
1// Extend the base Elevator class for specialized elevator types
2class ExpressElevator extends Elevator {
3  private static final int SPEED_MULTIPLIER = 2;
4  public ExpressElevator(int id) {
5    super(id); // Call the parent constructor
6  }
7
8  @Override
9  public void moveToNextStop(int nextStop) {
10    // Only move if the elevator is currently in the MOVING state
11    if (getState() != ElevatorState.MOVING)
12      return;
13    while (getCurrentFloor() != nextStop) {
14      // Express elevators move twice as fast (simplified)
15      if (getDirection() == Direction.UP) {
16        // Move up but ensure we don't overshoot
17        int newFloor = Math.min(getCurrentFloor() + SPEED_MULTIPLIER, nextStop);
18        int currentFloor = getCurrentFloor();
19        // Update the current floor
20        while (currentFloor < newFloor) {
21          currentFloor++;
22          // Notify observers about the floor change
23          notifyFloorChange(currentFloor);
24        }
25      } else {
26        // Move down but ensure we don't overshoot
27        int newFloor = Math.max(getCurrentFloor() - SPEED_MULTIPLIER, nextStop);
28        int currentFloor = getCurrentFloor();
29        // Update the current floor
30        while (currentFloor > newFloor) {
31          currentFloor--;
32          // Notify observers about the floor change
33          notifyFloorChange(currentFloor);
34        }
35      }
36      // Complete arrival once the target floor is reached
37      if (getCurrentFloor() == nextStop) {
38        completeArrival();
39        return;
40      }
41    }
42  }
43}
```

‍

Similarly, to accommodate new elevator types, we can utilize the Factory Design Pattern. This approach ensures that our code remains clean, modular, and maintainable when adding new elevators. By defining a common interface for all elevator types and creating a factory class to instantiate the appropriate elevator objects, we can easily integrate new elevator types without affecting existing code.

‍

Example : 

 ElevatorFactory.java : 

‍

Java

```
1// Factory for creating different elevator types
2	class ElevatorFactory {
3	    public static Elevator createElevator(String type, int id) {
4	        switch (type.toLowerCase()) {
5	            case "standard":
6	                return new Elevator(id);
7	            case "express":
8	                return new ExpressElevator(id);
9	            default:
10	                throw new IllegalArgumentException("Unknown elevator type: " + type);
11	        }
12	    }
13}
```

‍

• Modify The Elevator Controller class to accommodate ElevatorFactory Class : 

‍

Java

```
1class ElevatorController {
2  // Existing fields...
3
4  // Constructor that allows specifying elevator types
5  public ElevatorController(
6      int floors, Map<String, Integer> elevatorTypeQuantities) {
7    this.elevators = new ArrayList<>();
8    this.floors = new ArrayList<>();
9    this.schedulingStrategy = new ScanSchedulingStrategy(); // Default strategy
10    // Initialize floors
11    for (int i = 1; i <= floors; i++) {
12      this.floors.add(new Floor(i));
13    }
14    // Initialize elevators with different types
15    int idCounter = 1;
16    for (Map.Entry<String, Integer> entry : elevatorTypeQuantities.entrySet()) {
17      String type = entry.getKey();
18      int quantity = entry.getValue();
19      for (int i = 0; i < quantity; i++) {
20        Elevator elevator = ElevatorFactory.createElevator(type, idCounter++);
21        elevators.add(elevator);
22      }
23    }
24  }
25
26  // Original constructor now calls the new constructor
27  public ElevatorController(int numberOfElevators, int numberOfFloors) {
28    // Create a map with standard elevators
29    Map<String, Integer> elevatorTypes = new HashMap<>();
30    elevatorTypes.put("standard", numberOfElevators);
31    // Initialize floors
32    this.floors = new ArrayList<>();
33    for (int i = 1; i <= numberOfFloors; i++) {
34      this.floors.add(new Floor(i));
35    }
36    // Initialize elevators
37    this.elevators = new ArrayList<>();
38    for (int i = 1; i <= numberOfElevators; i++) {
39      this.elevators.add(new Elevator(i));
40    }
41    // Set default strategy
42    this.schedulingStrategy = new ScanSchedulingStrategy();
43  }
44
45  // Add methods to manage elevator types
46  public void addElevator(String type) {
47    int nextId = elevators.size() + 1;
48    elevators.add(ElevatorFactory.createElevator(type, nextId));
49  }
50
51  public boolean replaceElevator(int elevatorId, String newType) {
52    Elevator oldElevator = getElevatorById(elevatorId);
53    if (oldElevator != null) {
54      int index = elevators.indexOf(oldElevator);
55      Elevator newElevator =
56          ElevatorFactory.createElevator(newType, elevatorId);
57      elevators.set(index, newElevator);
58      return true;
59    }
60    return false;
61  }
62
63  // Rest of the ElevatorController code remains the same...
64}
```

‍

2.) Implementing Additional Scheduling Strategies : 

‍

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743482491450-Frame-245-\(6\).png)

‍

Since we are using the Strategy Pattern for our scheduling algorithms, we can easily extend our scheduling capabilities to incorporate new algorithms. This can be achieved by creating a new class that implements the new strategy and extends the Scheduling Strategy interface. 

‍

Example : 

Round Robin Scheduling Strategy : 

‍

Java

```
1// Round Robin Scheduling Strategy
2class RoundRobinSchedulingStrategy implements SchedulingStrategy {
3  private int lastAssignedIndex = -1;
4
5  @Override
6  public int getNextStop(Elevator elevator) {
7    // Current implementation focuses on determining the next stop
8    // for a specific elevator rather than selecting an elevator
9    // Get current floor and direction
10    int currentFloor = elevator.getCurrentFloor();
11    Direction currentDirection = elevator.getDirection();
12    // Get the requests queue
13    Queue<ElevatorRequest> requests = elevator.getRequestsQueue();
14    // If no requests, stay on current floor
15    if (requests.isEmpty()) {
16      return currentFloor;
17    }
18    // Get the next request in a round-robin fashion
19    ElevatorRequest nextRequest = requests.poll();
20    int nextFloor = nextRequest.getFloor();
21    // Update direction if needed
22    if (nextFloor > currentFloor) {
23      elevator.setDirection(Direction.UP);
24    } else if (nextFloor < currentFloor) {
25      elevator.setDirection(Direction.DOWN);
26    }
27    return nextFloor;
28  }
29}
```

‍

• Energy Efficient Scheduling Algorithm : 

‍

Java

```
1// Energy Efficient Scheduling Strategy
2class EnergyEfficientSchedulingStrategy implements SchedulingStrategy {
3  @Override
4  public int getNextStop(Elevator elevator) {
5    // Get current floor and direction
6    int currentFloor = elevator.getCurrentFloor();
7    Direction currentDirection = elevator.getDirection();
8    // Retrieve the elevator's requests
9    Queue<ElevatorRequest> requests = elevator.getRequestsQueue();
10    // If no requests, stay on current floor
11    if (requests.isEmpty()) {
12      return currentFloor;
13    }
14    // Sort requests by energy efficiency (minimize direction changes)
15    // This is a simplified implementation - in a real system, you'd
16    // consider factors like motor power, acceleration, etc.
17    // Get requests in the current direction first
18    List<ElevatorRequest> sameDirectionRequests = new ArrayList<>();
19    List<ElevatorRequest> oppositeDirectionRequests = new ArrayList<>();
20    for (ElevatorRequest request : requests) {
21      int requestFloor = request.getFloor();
22      if ((currentDirection == Direction.UP && requestFloor > currentFloor)
23          || (currentDirection == Direction.DOWN
24              && requestFloor < currentFloor)) {
25        sameDirectionRequests.add(request);
26      } else {
27        oppositeDirectionRequests.add(request);
28      }
29    }
30    // Process same direction requests first to minimize direction changes
31    if (!sameDirectionRequests.isEmpty()) {
32      // Find the closest request in the same direction
33      ElevatorRequest closestRequest = sameDirectionRequests.get(0);
34      int closestDistance = Math.abs(closestRequest.getFloor() - currentFloor);
35      for (ElevatorRequest request : sameDirectionRequests) {
36        int distance = Math.abs(request.getFloor() - currentFloor);
37        if (distance < closestDistance) {
38          closestDistance = distance;
39          closestRequest = request;
40        }
41      }
42      return closestRequest.getFloor();
43    } else if (!oppositeDirectionRequests.isEmpty()) {
44      // Need to change direction, find the closest request
45      ElevatorRequest closestRequest = oppositeDirectionRequests.get(0);
46      int closestDistance = Math.abs(closestRequest.getFloor() - currentFloor);
47      for (ElevatorRequest request : oppositeDirectionRequests) {
48        int distance = Math.abs(request.getFloor() - currentFloor);
49        if (distance < closestDistance) {
50          closestDistance = distance;
51          closestRequest = request;
52        }
53      }
54      // Update direction based on the selected request
55      if (closestRequest.getFloor() > currentFloor) {
56        elevator.setDirection(Direction.UP);
57      } else {
58        elevator.setDirection(Direction.DOWN);
59      }
60
61
62      return closestRequest.getFloor();
63    }
64    // Fallback to current floor if no valid requests (should not happen)
65    return currentFloor;
66  }
67}
```

‍

## ✨Conclusion : 

This low-level design for Elevator System showcases a well-structured and scalable architecture, emphasizing modularity and extensibility. By supporting various enhancements such as new Elevator Types and new scheduling strategies addition, this design ensures maintainability and flexibility. In an interview setting, presenting this design would demonstrate your ability to create robust and adaptable solutions, highlighting your proficiency in applying design patterns and best practices.\n\n---\n\n## Source: https://codewitharyan.com/tech-blogs/design-inventory-management-system\n\n# Design Inventory Management System

Topic Tags:

System DesignLLDInterview Questions

### 🐈‍⬛ Github Codes Link: [https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode](https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode)

# Low-Level Design: Inventory Management System 🏗️

An inventory management system is designed to efficiently track, manage, and optimize product inventory within a business. The system needs to handle multiple products, track stock levels, process orders, and ensure timely replenishment. The system should be able to accommodate different product types, handle various inventory operations, and provide accurate reporting.

‍

## Rules of the System:

### Setup:

• The business has multiple warehouses and can store multiple product types.

• Products can be added, removed, or transferred between warehouses.

• Each product has attributes like SKU(Stock Keeping Unit - Unique Id of a Product), name, price, and quantity.

• The system tracks inventory levels and triggers alerts for low stock.

‍

Operation:

• Users can add new products to the inventory.

• Stock levels are updated when products are received or shipped.

• The system generates reports on inventory status and movement.

• Inventory can be searched and filtered based on various criteria.

‍

Safety Features:

• Threshold alerts prevent stockouts.

• Audit trails track all inventory changes.

• Access control limits who can perform different inventory operations.

‍

Interview Setting 🤝

Point 1: Introduction and Vague Problem Statement:

Interviewer: Let's start with a basic problem statement. Design an Inventory Management System.

Candidate: Certainly! Here's my understanding of the Inventory Management System:

• The system will manage multiple products across different warehouses.

• Users can add, remove, and transfer inventory items.

• The system tracks stock levels and alerts on low inventory.

• Reports can be generated for inventory analysis.

• The system should be scalable to handle businesses of different sizes.

Is this the expected flow?

Interviewer: Yes, you are aligned with the flow. Please continue ahead.

Candidate: Great! Before diving into the design, I'd like to clarify a few requirements:

• What types of products should the system support?

• Are there specific inventory replenishment (restoration of stock) strategies to implement?

• How should the system handle product categorization?

‍

Point 2: Clarifying Requirements :

Interviewer: We want a system that:

• Supports multiple product types in multiple warehouses.

• Handles both inventory additions and removals.

• Efficiently notifies when stock levels are low.

‍

Candidate: To summarize, the key requirements are:

• A system with multiple warehouses and product categories.

• Stock level tracking and management.

• Intelligent replenishment strategy implementation.

• Ability to handle edge cases like damaged inventory or returns.

Interviewer: Perfect, let's proceed.

‍

Point 3: Identifying Key Components/Entities:

Candidate: Now that we have the requirements, let's identify the key components of our Inventory Management System:

Product: Represents individual products in inventory.

• Class: Product

• Description: This class represents individual products with their attributes.

‍

Java

```
1public class Product {
2	    private String sku;
3	    private String name;
4	    private double price;
5	    private int quantity;
6	    private int threshold;
7	    private ProductCategory category;
8	}
```

‍

Warehouse: Contains products and manages storage.

• Class: Warehouse

Description: This class represents physical storage locations.

‍

Java

```
1public class Warehouse {
2	    private int id;
3	    private String name;
4	    private String location;
5	    private Map<String, Product> products; // SKU -> Product	    
6	    public void addProduct(Product product, int quantity);
7	    public void removeProduct(String sku, int quantity);
8	    public int getAvailableQuantity(String sku);
9}
```

‍

InventoryManager: Manages inventory operations and tracking.

• Class: InventoryManager

Description: This class handles inventory operations and uses the Singleton pattern.

‍

Java

```
1public class InventoryManager {
2	    private static InventoryManager instance;
3	    private List<Warehouse> warehouses;
4	    private ProductFactory productFactory;
5	    private List<InventoryObserver> observers;
6	    private ReplenishmentStrategy replenishmentStrategy;
7}
```

‍

Interviewer: That sounds good. Let's proceed with the design details.

‍

### Point 4: Design Challenges:

Interviewer: What design challenges do you anticipate?

‍

Candidate: The key challenges for the Inventory Management System include:

1\. Scalability: Handling large numbers of products and warehouses.

2\. Concurrency: Managing multiple simultaneous inventory operations.

3\. Data Consistency: Ensuring accurate inventory counts across the system.

4\. Customizable Replenishment: Supporting different replenishment strategies.

5\. Edge Cases: Handling product returns, damaged inventory, and seasonal demand fluctuations.

### Point 5: Approach:

Interviewer: How would you approach these challenges?

‍

Candidate: I propose using design patterns effectively. Here are my strategies:

1\. Singleton Pattern for Inventory Manager: 

• Ensures a single point of control for inventory operations.

• Maintains consistency across the system.

2\. Observer Pattern for Inventory Alerts: 

• Notifies relevant stakeholders when stock falls below thresholds.

• Enables real-time inventory monitoring.

‍

3\. Factory Pattern for Product Creation:

• Encapsulates product creation logic.

• Allows for easy addition of new product types.

‍

4\. Strategy Pattern for Replenishment: 

• Enables different replenishment strategies.

• Can switch between algorithms based on product type or season.

‍

5\. State Management with Enums: 

• To effectively manage product categories and operations, we'll use enums.

‍

Java

```
1public enum ProductCategory { 
2       ELECTRONICS, CLOTHING, GROCERY, FURNITURE, OTHER 
3}
4
5public enum InventoryOperation { 
6       ADD, REMOVE, TRANSFER, ADJUST 
7}
```

‍

Interviewer: That sounds like a solid approach. Let's delve into the implementation details.

‍

### Point 6: Implementation:

Interviewer: Ready to discuss implementation?

‍

Candidate: Yes. I'll focus on implementing the design patterns we discussed and show how they work together in the Inventory Management System:

‍

## Inventory Management System Implementation with Design Patterns 🏗: 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743315295359-Frame-245.png)

‍

1\. Factory Pattern For Product Creation : 

‍

• Product.java : 

This is an abstract base class representing a general product with common attributes like SKU, name, price, quantity and category. It serves as the parent class for specific product types.

‍

Java

```
1// Abstract Product class
2public abstract class Product {
3  private String sku;
4  private String name;
5  private double price;
6  private int quantity;
7  private int threshold;
8  private ProductCategory category;
9
10  // Getters and setters
11  public String getSku() {
12    return sku;
13  }
14
15  public void setSku(String sku) {
16    this.sku = sku;
17  }
18
19  public String getName() {
20    return name;
21  }
22
23  public void setName(String name) {
24    this.name = name;
25  }
26
27  public double getPrice() {
28    return price;
29  }
30
31  public void setPrice(double price) {
32    this.price = price;
33  }
34
35  public int getQuantity() {
36    return quantity;
37  }
38
39  public void setQuantity(int quantity) {
40    this.quantity = quantity;
41  }
42
43  public ProductCategory getCategory() {
44    return category;
45  }
46
47  public void setCategory(ProductCategory category) {
48    this.category = category;
49  }
50}
```

‍

• Product Categories Enum : 

An enumeration defining the various categories of products, such as electronics, clothing, grocery, furniture, and other miscellaneous categories. This helps in categorizing the products.

‍

Java

```
1// Enum for product categories
2	public enum ProductCategory {
3	    ELECTRONICS, CLOTHING, GROCERY, FURNITURE, OTHER
4}
```

‍

• Concrete Products :

Concrete Products are the specific product implementations that extend the abstract Product class. Each concrete product class (like ElectronicsProduct, ClothingProduct, and GroceryProduct) represents a distinct type of product with attributes and behaviors specific to that type. This allows for the creation of varied products while maintaining a common interface and shared attributes.

‍

1.) ElectronicsProduct.java : 

‍

Java

```
1package ProductsFactory.ConcreteProducts;
2
3import CommonEnum.ProductCategory;
4import ProductsFactory.Product;
5
6public class ElectronicsProduct extends Product {
7    private String brand;
8    private int warrantyPeriod; // in months
9
10    public ElectronicsProduct(String sku, String name, double price, int quantity, int threshold) {
11        super();
12        setSku(sku);
13        setName(name);
14        setPrice(price);
15        setQuantity(quantity);
16        setCategory(ProductCategory.ELECTRONICS);
17        setThreshold(threshold);
18    }
19
20    // Getters and setters for electronics-specific attributes
21    public String getBrand() {
22        return brand;
23    }
24
25    public void setBrand(String brand) {
26        this.brand = brand;
27    }
28}
```

‍

2.) ClothingProduct.java : 

‍

Java

```

26    }
```
