3.) GroceryProduct.java : 

‍

Java

```
1package ProductsFactory.ConcreteProducts;
2
3import CommonEnum.ProductCategory;
4import ProductsFactory.Product;
5import java.util.Date;
6
7public class GroceryProduct extends Product {
8    private Date expiryDate;
9    private boolean refrigerated;
10
11    public GroceryProduct(String sku, String name, double price, int quantity, int threshold) {
12        super();
13        setSku(sku);
14        setName(name);
15        setPrice(price);
16        setQuantity(quantity);
17        setCategory(ProductCategory.GROCERY);
18        setThreshold(threshold);
19    }
20
21    // Getters and setters for grocery-specific attributes
22    public Date getExpiryDate() {
23        return expiryDate;
24    }
25
26    public void setExpiryDate(Date expiryDate) {
27        this.expiryDate = expiryDate;
28    }
29
30    public boolean isRefrigerated() {
31        return refrigerated;
32    }
33
34    public void setRefrigerated(boolean refrigerated) {
35        this.refrigerated = refrigerated;
36    }
37}
```

‍

• ProductFactory.java : 

A factory class implementing the Factory Pattern. It has a method to create product instances based on the given product category. It switches through the categories and returns the appropriate product instance. If an unsupported category is provided, it throws an IllegalArgumentException.

‍

Java

```
1package ProductsFactory;
2
3import CommonEnum.ProductCategory;
4import ProductsFactory.ConcreteProducts.ClothingProduct;
5import ProductsFactory.ConcreteProducts.ElectronicsProduct;
6import ProductsFactory.ConcreteProducts.GroceryProduct;
7
8// Product Factory class implementing Factory Pattern
9public class ProductFactory {
10    public Product createProduct(ProductCategory category, String sku, String name, double price, int quantity, int threshold) {
11        switch (category) {
12            case ELECTRONICS:
13                return new ElectronicsProduct(sku, name, price, quantity,threshold);
14            case CLOTHING:
15                return new ClothingProduct(sku, name, price, quantity,threshold);
16            case GROCERY:
17                return new GroceryProduct(sku, name, price, quantity,threshold);
18            default:
19                throw new IllegalArgumentException(
20                        "Unsupported product category: " + category);
21        }
22    }
23}
```

‍

2\. Strategy Pattern for Product Replenishment : 

‍

• ReplenishmentStrategy.java : 

This is an interface for different replenishment strategies. It declares a method replenish(Product product) that will be implemented by concrete strategies to provide various ways to replenish stock for a given product.

‍

Java

```
1// Interface for different replenishment strategies
2	public interface ReplenishmentStrategy {
3	    // Method to replenish stock for a given product
4	    void replenish(Product product);
5}
```

‍

• Concrete Strategies : 

Concrete strategies implement the ReplenishmentStrategy interface. These are specific replenishment algorithms that can be used based on the needs of the product inventory management.

‍

JustInTimeStrategy.java : 

Java

```
1// Just-In-Time replenishment strategy
2	public class JustInTimeStrategy implements ReplenishmentStrategy {
3	    @Override
4	    public void replenish(Product product) {
5	        // Implement Just-In-Time replenishment logic
6	        System.out.println("Applying Just-In-Time replenishment for " + product.getName());
7	        // Calculate optimal order quantity based on demand rate
8	    }
9	}
```

‍

2\. BulkOrderStrategy.java : 

‍

Java

```
1// Bulk Order replenishment strategy
2	public class BulkOrderStrategy implements ReplenishmentStrategy {
3	    @Override
4	    public void replenish(Product product) {
5	        // Implement Bulk Order replenishment logic
6	        System.out.println("Applying Bulk Order replenishment for " + product.getName());
7	        // Order in large quantities to minimize order costs
8	    }
9	}
```

‍

3\. Singleton Pattern for Inventory Manager : 

The InventoryManager class implements the Singleton pattern, which ensures that only one instance of the inventory management system exists throughout the application lifecycle. This is crucial for maintaining data consistency and providing a single point of control for all inventory operations.

‍

InventoryManager.java : 

Java

```
1public class InventoryManager {
2  // Singleton instance
3  private static InventoryManager instance;
4
5  // System components
6  private List<Warehouse> warehouses;
7  private ProductFactory productFactory;
8  private ReplenishmentStrategy replenishmentStrategy;
9
10  // Private constructor to prevent instantiation from outside
11  private InventoryManager() {
12    // Initialize collections and dependencies
13    warehouses = new ArrayList<>();
14    productFactory = new ProductFactory();
15  }
16
17  // Static method to get the singleton instance with thread safety
18  public static synchronized InventoryManager getInstance() {
19    if (instance == null) {
20      instance = new InventoryManager();
21    }
22    return instance;
23  }
24}
```

‍

4\. Core Classes Implementation : 

Warehouse.java : 

‍

Java

```
1public class Warehouse {
2  private int id;
3  private String name;
4  private String location;
5  private Map<String, Product> products; // SKU -> Product
6  public Warehouse(int id, String name, String location) {
7    this.id = id;
8    this.name = name;
9    this.location = location;
10    this.products = new HashMap<>();
11  }
12
13  public void setLocation(String location) {
14    this.location = location;
15  }
16
17  // Add a product to the warehouse
18  public void addProduct(Product product, int quantity) {
19    String sku = product.getSku();
20    if (products.containsKey(sku)) {
21      // Product exists, update quantity
22      Product existingProduct = products.get(sku);
23      existingProduct.addStock(quantity);
24    } else {
25      // New product, add to inventory
26      product.setQuantity(quantity);
27      products.put(sku, product);
28    }
29    System.out.println(quantity + " units of " + product.getName()
30        + " (SKU: " + sku + ") added to " + name
31        + ". New quantity: " + getAvailableQuantity(sku));
32  }
33
34  // Remove a product from the warehouse
35  public boolean removeProduct(String sku, int quantity) {
36    if (products.containsKey(sku)) {
37      Product product = products.get(sku);
38      int currentQuantity = product.getQuantity();
39      if (currentQuantity >= quantity) {
40        // Sufficient inventory to remove
41        product.removeStock(quantity);
42        System.out.println(quantity + " units of " + product.getName()
43            + " (SKU: " + sku + ") removed from " + name
44            + ". Remaining quantity: " + product.getQuantity());
45        // If quantity becomes zero, consider removing the product entirely
46        if (product.getQuantity() == 0) {
47          // Remove products with zero quantity
48          products.remove(sku);
49          System.out.println("Product " + product.getName()
50              + " removed from inventory as quantity is now zero.");
51        }
52        return true;
53      } else {
54        System.out.println("Error: Insufficient inventory. Requested: "
55            + quantity + ", Available: " + currentQuantity);
56        return false;
57      }
58    } else {
59      System.out.println(
60          "Error: Product with SKU " + sku + " not found in " + name);
61      return false;
62    }
63  }
64
65  // Get available quantity of a product
66  public int getAvailableQuantity(String sku) {
67    if (products.containsKey(sku)) {
68      return products.get(sku).getQuantity();
69    }
70    return 0; // Product not found
71  }
72
73  // Get a product by SKU
74  public Product getProductBySku(String sku) {
75    return products.get(sku);
76  }
77
78  // Get all products in this warehouse
79  public Collection<Product> getAllProducts() {
80    return products.values();
81  }
82}
```

‍

• InventoryManager.java : 

‍

Java

```
1public class InventoryManager {
2  // Singleton instance
3  private static InventoryManager instance;
4
5  // System components
6  private List<Warehouse> warehouses;
7  private ProductFactory productFactory;
8  private ReplenishmentStrategy replenishmentStrategy;
9
10  // Private constructor to prevent instantiation from outside
11  private InventoryManager() {
12    // Initialize collections and dependencies
13    warehouses = new ArrayList<>();
14    productFactory = new ProductFactory();
15  }
16
17  // Static method to get the singleton instance with thread safety
18  public static synchronized InventoryManager getInstance() {
19    if (instance == null) {
20      instance = new InventoryManager();
21    }
22    return instance;
23  }
24
25  // Strategy pattern method
26  public void setReplenishmentStrategy(ReplenishmentStrategy strategy) {
27    this.replenishmentStrategy = strategy;
28  }
29
30  // Warehouse management
31  public void addWarehouse(Warehouse warehouse) {
32    warehouses.add(warehouse);
33  }
34
35  public void removeWarehouse(Warehouse warehouse) {
36    warehouses.remove(warehouse);
37  }
38
39  // Product inventory operations
40  public Product getProductBySku(String sku) {
41    for (Warehouse warehouse : warehouses) {
42      Product product = warehouse.getProductBySku(sku);
43      if (product != null) {
44        return product;
45      }
46    }
47    return null;
48  }
49
50  // Check stock levels and apply replenishment strategy if needed
51  public void checkAndReplenish(String sku) {
52    Product product = getProductBySku(sku);
53    if (product != null) {
54      // If product is below threshold, notify observers
55      if (product.getQuantity() < product.getThreshold()) {
56        notifyObservers(product);
57        // Apply current replenishment strategy
58        if (replenishmentStrategy != null) {
59          replenishmentStrategy.replenish(product);
60        }
61      }
62    }
63  }
64
65  // Global inventory check
66  public void performInventoryCheck() {
67    for (Warehouse warehouse : warehouses) {
68      for (Product product : warehouse.getAllProducts()) {
69        if (product.getQuantity() < product.getThreshold()) {
70          notifyObservers(product);
71          if (replenishmentStrategy != null) {
72            replenishmentStrategy.replenish(product);
73          }
74        }
75      }
76    }
77  }
78}
```

‍

5\. Main Method to run the System : 

‍

Java

```
1public class Main {
2  public static void main(String[] args) {
3    // Get the singleton instance of InventoryManager
4    InventoryManager inventoryManager = InventoryManager.getInstance();
5
6    // Create and add warehouses
7    Warehouse warehouse1 = new Warehouse("Warehouse 1");
8    Warehouse warehouse2 = new Warehouse("Warehouse 2");
9    inventoryManager.addWarehouse(warehouse1);
10    inventoryManager.addWarehouse(warehouse2);
11
12    // Create products using ProductFactory
13    ProductFactory productFactory = new ProductFactory();
14    Product laptop = productFactory.createProduct(
15        ProductCategory.ELECTRONICS, "SKU123", "Laptop", 1000.0, 50, 25);
16    Product tShirt = productFactory.createProduct(
17        ProductCategory.CLOTHING, "SKU456", "T-Shirt", 20.0, 200, 100);
18    Product apple = productFactory.createProduct(
19        ProductCategory.GROCERY, "SKU789", "Apple", 1.0, 100, 200);
20
21    // Add products to warehouses
22    warehouse1.addProduct(laptop, 15);
23    warehouse1.addProduct(tShirt, 20);
24    warehouse2.addProduct(apple, 50);
25
26    // Set replenishment strategy to Just-In-Time
27    inventoryManager.setReplenishmentStrategy(new JustInTimeStrategy());
28
29    // Perform inventory check and replenish if needed
30    inventoryManager.performInventoryCheck();
31
32    // Switch replenishment strategy to Bulk Order
33    inventoryManager.setReplenishmentStrategy(new BulkOrderStrategy());
34
35    // Replenish a specific product if needed
36    inventoryManager.checkAndReplenish("SKU123");
37  }
38}
```

‍

Interviewer: Looks good. What makes your approach effective? 

‍

Candidate: Here are the key strengths of my approach for the Inventory Management System:

• Scalability: The design allows easy addition of new product categories, replenishment strategies, and warehouses, ensuring that the system can grow with the business.

‍

• Modularity: Each component, such as product creation, inventory management, and replenishment strategies, is handled separately, ensuring a clean and maintainable structure.

‍

• Flexibility: The use of design patterns like Factory and Strategy allows seamless modifications and enhancements without affecting existing code. This enables the easy introduction of new features and strategies.

‍

• Clarity: The architecture is intuitive, making it easy for developers to understand, implement, and extend when needed. This ensures that the system remains maintainable and adaptable over time.

‍

Extensibility :

1\. Observer Pattern For Inventory Alerts : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743316444350-Frame-245-\(1\).png)

‍

• InventoryObserver.java : 

‍

This interface represents the observer in the Observer Pattern. It declares a method update(Product product) that will be implemented by concrete observers to react to changes in product inventory.

‍

Java

```
1public interface InventoryObserver {
2	    void update(Product product);
3}
```

‍

• Concrete Listeners : 

Concrete listeners are the specific implementations of the InventoryObserver interface. These listeners will perform actions when notified about changes in product inventory.

‍

SupplierNotifier.java : (Used to notify the suppliers regarding inventory changes)

‍

Java

```
1public class SupplierNotifier implements InventoryObserver {
2  private String supplierName;
3  private String contactEmail;
4
5  public SupplierNotifier(String supplierName, String contactEmail) {
6    this.supplierName = supplierName;
7    this.contactEmail = contactEmail;
8  }
9
10  @Override
11  public void update(Product product) {
12    if (product.getQuantity() < product.getThreshold()) {
13      // Send email notification to supplier
14      System.out.println("Notification sent to " + supplierName
15          + " for low stock of " + product.getName());
16    }
17  }
18}
```

‍

• DashboardAlertSystem.java : 

‍

Java

```
1// Second concrete observer - Dashboard alert system
2public class DashboardAlertSystem implements InventoryObserver {
3  private String alertLevel;
4  private List<String> adminUsers;
5
6  public DashboardAlertSystem(String alertLevel, List<String> adminUsers) {
7    this.alertLevel = alertLevel;
8    this.adminUsers = adminUsers;
9  }
10
11  @Override
12  public void update(Product product) {
13    double stockPercentage =
14        ((double) product.getQuantity() / product.getThreshold()) * 100;
15
16    if (stockPercentage <= 25) {
17      // Critical alert - red notification
18      System.out.println("CRITICAL ALERT: " + product.getName()
19          + " stock critically low at " + product.getQuantity() + " units ("
20          + String.format("%.1f", stockPercentage) + "% of threshold)");
21      notifyAdmins(product, "CRITICAL");
22    } else if (stockPercentage <= 50) {
23      // Warning alert - yellow notification
24      System.out.println("WARNING ALERT: " + product.getName()
25          + " stock low at " + product.getQuantity() + " units ("
26          + String.format("%.1f", stockPercentage) + "% of threshold)");
27      notifyAdmins(product, "WARNING");
28    }
29  }
30
31  private void notifyAdmins(Product product, String level) {
32    for (String admin : adminUsers) {
33      System.out.println("Dashboard notification sent to admin: " + admin
34          + " - " + level + " level alert for " + product.getName());
35      // Actual implementation would update dashboard UI and push notifications
36    }
37  }
38}
```

‍

• Modify the InventoryManager Class to accommodate the observers: 

‍

the InventoryManager class is modified to include observer-related functionalities. It maintains a list of InventoryObserver instances and provides methods to add, remove, and notify these observers when there are changes in product inventory.

‍

Java

```
1public class InventoryManager {
2  // Singleton instance
3  private static InventoryManager instance;
4
5  // System components
6  private List<Warehouse> warehouses;
7  private ProductFactory productFactory;
8  private List<InventoryObserver> observers;
9  private ReplenishmentStrategy replenishmentStrategy;
10
11  // Private constructor to prevent instantiation from outside
12  private InventoryManager() {
13    // Initialize collections and dependencies
14    warehouses = new ArrayList<>();
15    observers = new ArrayList<>();
16    productFactory = new ProductFactory();
17  }
18
19  // Static method to get the singleton instance with thread safety
20  public static synchronized InventoryManager getInstance() {
21    if (instance == null) {
22      instance = new InventoryManager();
23    }
24    return instance;
25  }
26
27  // Observer pattern methods
28  public void addObserver(InventoryObserver observer) {
29    observers.add(observer);
30  }
31
32  public void removeObserver(InventoryObserver observer) {
33    observers.remove(observer);
34  }
35
36  public void notifyObservers(Product product) {
37    for (InventoryObserver observer : observers) {
38      observer.update(product);
39    }
40  }
41}
```

‍

2\. Addition of New Product Types : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743316682189-Frame-245-\(2\).png)

‍

New product types can be easily integrated by creating a concrete implementation for the new product type and making the necessary modifications to the Product Factory class to handle the newly added product.

‍

Example: Suppose we want to add a new product type, Furniture. Here's how we would extend our codebase to accommodate Furniture:

‍

Create a Concrete Implementation for Furniture:

‍

Java

```
1public class FurnituresProduct extends Product {
2		    private String brand;
3		    private int warrantyPeriod;
4		    
5		    public FurnituresProduct(String sku, String name, double price, int quantity, int threshold) {
6		        super();
7		        setSku(sku);
8		        setName(name);
9		        setPrice(price);
10		        setQuantity(quantity);
11		        setThreshold(threshold);
12		        setCategory(ProductCategory.ELECTRONICS);
13		    }
14		    
15		    // Getters and setters for furniture-specific attributes
16		}
```

‍

• Modify the Product Factory Class : 

‍

Java

```
1public class ProductFactory { 
2		    public Product createProduct(ProductCategory category, String sku, String name, double price, int quantity) { 
3		        switch (category) { 
4		            case ELECTRONICS: 
5		                return new ElectronicsProduct(sku, name, price, quantity); 
6		            case CLOTHING: 
7		                return new ClothingProduct(sku, name, price, quantity); 
8		            case GROCERY: 
9		                return new GroceryProduct(sku, name, price, quantity); 
10		            case FURNITURE: 
11		                return new FurnituresProduct(sku, name, price, quantity);
12		            default: 
13		                throw new IllegalArgumentException("Unsupported product category: " + category); 
14		        } 
15		    } 
16}
```

‍

By following these steps, we ensure that our codebase remains flexible and adaptable, allowing for the seamless addition of new vehicle types while maintaining a clean and maintainable architecture.

‍

Builder Design Pattern for Complex Products : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743317220088-Frame-245-\(3\).png)

‍

Builder pattern would be a valuable addition to the Inventory Management System design. It would work particularly well for creating complex product objects with many optional parameters.

• Update the Product Class to accommodate for the Builder Design Pattern : 

‍

Product.java : 

The Product class can been modified to work with the Builder pattern. This includes a protected constructor that takes a Builder object and initializes the product's attributes. The Builder class is an abstract nested class within Product, providing methods to set optional parameters and build the final product object.

‍

Java

```
1// Product class modified to work with Builder pattern
2public abstract class Product {
3  // Core attributes
4  private final String sku;
5  private final String name;
6  private final double price;
7  private int quantity; // optional
8  private final int threshold; // optional
9  private final ProductCategory category;
10
11  // Protected constructor to be used by concrete builders
12  protected Product(Builder<?> builder) {
13    this.sku = builder.sku;
14    this.name = builder.name;
15    this.price = builder.price;
16    this.quantity = builder.quantity;
17    this.threshold = builder.threshold;
18    this.category = builder.category;
19  }
20
21  // Getters
22  public String getSku() {
23    return sku;
24  }
25  public String getName() {
26    return name;
27  }
28  public double getPrice() {
29    return price;
30  }
31  public int getQuantity() {
32    return quantity;
33  }
34  public int getThreshold() {
35    return threshold;
36  }
37  public ProductCategory getCategory() {
38    return category;
39  }
40
41  // Setters only for mutable properties
42  public void setQuantity(int quantity) {
43    this.quantity = quantity;
44  }
45
46  // Abstract Builder class
47  public static abstract class Builder<T extends Builder<T>> {
48    // Required parameters
49    private final String sku;
50    private final String name;
51    private final double price;
52    private final ProductCategory category;
53
54    // Optional parameters with default values
55    private int quantity = 0;
56    private int threshold = 10;
57
58    // Constructor with required parameters
59    public Builder(
60        String sku, String name, double price, ProductCategory category) {
61      this.sku = sku;
62      this.name = name;
63      this.price = price;
64      this.category = category;
65    }
66
67    // Methods to set optional parameters
68    public T quantity(int quantity) {
69      this.quantity = quantity;
70      return self();
71    }
72
73    public T threshold(int threshold) {
74      this.threshold = threshold;
75      return self();
76    }
77
78    // Method to be overridden by subclasses to return this (the current object)
79    protected abstract T self();
80
81    // Build method to be implemented by concrete builders
82    public abstract Product build();
83  }
84}
```

‍

• Update Concrete Product Classes Implementation : 

‍

ElectronicsProduct.java : 

The ElectronicsProduct class has been updated to incorporate the Builder pattern. The ElectronicsBuilder class is a nested static class within ElectronicsProduct and extends the Builder class from Product. It includes methods to set electronics-specific optional parameters and builds the final ElectronicsProduct object.

‍

Java

```
1// Electronics Product with Builder
2public class ElectronicsProduct extends Product {
3  // Electronics-specific attributes
4  private final String brand;
5  private final int warrantyPeriod;
6  private final String modelNumber;
7  private final boolean wirelessConnectivity;
8  private final int powerConsumption;
9
10  private ElectronicsProduct(ElectronicsBuilder builder) {
11    super(builder);
12    this.brand = builder.brand;
13    this.warrantyPeriod = builder.warrantyPeriod;
14    this.modelNumber = builder.modelNumber;
15    this.wirelessConnectivity = builder.wirelessConnectivity;
16    this.powerConsumption = builder.powerConsumption;
17  }
18
19  // Getters for electronics-specific attributes
20  public String getBrand() {
21    return brand;
22  }
23  public int getWarrantyPeriod() {
24    return warrantyPeriod;
25  }
26  public String getModelNumber() {
27    return modelNumber;
28  }
29  public boolean hasWirelessConnectivity() {
30    return wirelessConnectivity;
31  }
32  public int getPowerConsumption() {
33    return powerConsumption;
34  }
35
36  // Concrete Builder for ElectronicsProduct
37  public static class ElectronicsBuilder extends Builder<ElectronicsBuilder> {
38    // Required electronics parameters
39    private final String brand;
40
41    // Optional electronics parameters with default values
42    private int warrantyPeriod = 12; // 12 months default
43    private String modelNumber = "";
44    private boolean wirelessConnectivity = false;
45    private int powerConsumption = 0;
46
47    public ElectronicsBuilder(
48        String sku, String name, double price, String brand) {
49      super(sku, name, price, ProductCategory.ELECTRONICS);
50      this.brand = brand;
51    }
52
53    // Methods to set optional electronics-specific parameters
54    public ElectronicsBuilder warrantyPeriod(int warrantyPeriod) {
55      this.warrantyPeriod = warrantyPeriod;
56      return this;
57    }
58
59    public ElectronicsBuilder modelNumber(String modelNumber) {
60      this.modelNumber = modelNumber;
61      return this;
62    }
63
64    public ElectronicsBuilder wirelessConnectivity(
65        boolean wirelessConnectivity) {
66      this.wirelessConnectivity = wirelessConnectivity;
67      return this;
68    }
69
70    public ElectronicsBuilder powerConsumption(int powerConsumption) {
71      this.powerConsumption = powerConsumption;
72      return this;
73    }
74
75    @Override
76    protected ElectronicsBuilder self() {
77      return this;
78    }
79
80    @Override
81    public ElectronicsProduct build() {
82      return new ElectronicsProduct(this);
83    }
84  }
85}
```

‍

NOTE :

> Similarly, other classes can be modified to accommodate the Builder Design Pattern, which will help in managing complex objects. This pattern allows for the step-by-step construction of complex objects, making the code more readable and maintainable.

‍

## Conclusion : 

This low-level design for the Inventory Management System showcases a well-structured, scalable, and modular architecture. It emphasizes the use of key design patterns like Factory, Strategy, Observer, and Builder, which enhance the flexibility, maintainability, and extensibility of the system. By supporting various enhancements such as the addition of new product categories, replenishment strategies, and complex product configurations, this design ensures that the system can grow and adapt with the business needs.

‍

In an interview setting, presenting this design would demonstrate your ability to create robust and adaptable solutions, highlighting your proficiency in applying design patterns and best practices\n\n---\n\n## Source: https://codewitharyan.com/tech-blogs/design-car-rental-system\n\n# Design Car Rental System

## ‍

## Low-Level Design: Car Rental System 🚗

A car rental system is designed to efficiently manage vehicle inventory, handle reservations, process payments, and track rental operations. The system needs to support multiple vehicle types, manage availability across different locations, handle user reservations, and provide a seamless rental experience. The system should be scalable, reliable, and capable of handling concurrent operations.

## Rules of the System:

Setup:

• The business operates multiple rental stores across different locations.

• Each store has its own inventory of vehicles of various types (economy, luxury, SUV, etc.).

• Vehicles have attributes like registration number, model, make, year, condition, and rental price.

• The system tracks vehicle availability and manages reservations.

‍

Operation:

• Users can search for available vehicles based on location, date range, and vehicle preferences.

• Users can filter and sort vehicles based on various criteria (price, type, features).

• Reservations can be created, modified, or canceled by users.

• The system generates billing based on rental duration and additional services.

• Payment processing handles various payment methods.

‍

Safety Features:

• Reservation conflicts are prevented through proper availability tracking.

• User authentication ensures secure access to the system.

• Audit trails track all rental transactions and vehicle status changes.

• Damage reports and vehicle condition monitoring ensure fleet maintenance.

## Interview Setting 🤝

**Point 1: Introduction and Vague Problem Statement:**

Interviewer: Let's start with a basic problem statement. Design a Car Rental System.

‍

Candidate: Certainly! Here's my understanding of the Car Rental System:

• The system will manage multiple vehicles across different rental locations.

• Users can search, filter, and reserve vehicles based on their preferences.

• The system tracks vehicle availability and prevents booking conflicts.

• Billing and payment processing are integrated for a complete rental cycle.

• The system should be scalable to handle operations across multiple cities.

Is this the expected flow?

‍

Interviewer: Yes, you are aligned with the flow. Please continue ahead.

‍

Candidate: Great! Before diving into the design, I'd like to clarify a few requirements:

• What types of vehicles should the system support? Is the System that we are designing only for cars or can it support multiple Vehicle Types as well ? 

• How should the system handle reservation modifications and cancellations?

• Are there specific pricing strategies to implement for different vehicle types?

‍

**Point 2: Clarifying Requirements:**

‍

Interviewer: We want a system that:

• Supports multiple vehicle types across different rental locations.

• Handles reservations, modifications, and cancellations efficiently.

• Processes payments and generates appropriate billing.

‍

Candidate: To summarize, the key requirements are:

• A system with multiple rental stores and vehicle categories.

• Reservation management with conflict prevention.

• Dynamic pricing implementation based on vehicle type and duration.

• Ability to handle edge cases like late returns, damages, or early pickups.

‍

Interviewer: Perfect, let's proceed.

‍

**Point 3: Identifying Key Components:**

Candidate: Now that we have the requirements, let's identify the key components of our Car Rental System:

‍

Vehicle: Represents individual vehicles in the rental fleet.

Class:

Vehicle : This class represents individual vehicles with their attributes.

‍

Java

```
1public class Vehicle {
2    private String registrationNumber;
3    private String make;
4    private String model;
5    private int year;
6    private VehicleType type;
7    private VehicleStatus status;
8    private double baseRentalPrice;
9}
```

‍

RentalStore: Contains vehicles and manages operations at a location.

‍

Class: 

RentalStore Description: This class represents physical rental locations.

‍

Java

```
1public class RentalStore {
2    private int id;
3    private String name;
4    private Location location;
5    private Map<String, Vehicle> vehicles; // Registration -> Vehicle
6    
7    public List<Vehicle> getAvailableVehicles(Date startDate, Date endDate);
8    public void addVehicle(Vehicle vehicle);
9    public void removeVehicle(String registrationNumber);
10    public boolean isVehicleAvailable(String registrationNumber, Date startDate, Date endDate);
11}
```

‍

RentalSystem: Manages the overall rental operations.

‍

Class: RentalSystem Description: This class handles rental operations and uses the Singleton pattern.

‍

Java

```
1public class RentalSystem {
2    private List<RentalStore> stores; // list of stores
3    private VehicleFactory vehicleFactory; // Vehicle Factory for creation of different Vehicles
4    private ReservationManager reservationManager; // to manage User reservations
5    private PaymentProcessor paymentProcessor; // to manage payments 
6    private List<SystemObserver> observers; // to notify users and systems about the changes
7}
```

Interviewer: That sounds good. Let's proceed with the design details.

‍

**Point 4: Design Challenges:**

Interviewer: What design challenges do you anticipate?

‍

Candidate: The key challenges for the Car Rental System include:

• Concurrency: Managing multiple simultaneous reservation requests.

• Availability Tracking: Ensuring accurate vehicle availability across time slots.

• Pricing Complexity: Handling various pricing models and discounts.

• Payment Processing: Securely handling different payment methods.

• Edge Cases: Managing late returns, damages, maintenance schedules, and location transfers.

‍

**Point 5: Approach:**

Interviewer: How would you approach these challenges?

‍

Candidate: I propose using design patterns effectively. Here are my strategies:

‍

1\. Factory Pattern for Vehicle Creation:

○ Encapsulates vehicle creation logic.

○ Allows for easy addition of new vehicle types.

‍

2\. Singleton Pattern for RentalSystem:

○ Ensures a single point of control for the entire rental operation.

○ Maintains consistency across all stores and operations.

‍

3\. Strategy Pattern for Payment :

○ Enables different Payment strategies based on different payment types like Credit Card , Debit Card, Cash etc.

○ Can switch between Different payment strategies dynamically.

‍

4\. Enumerations for Reservation Status:

○ Manages the lifecycle of reservations.

○ Handles transitions between different states (pending, confirmed, in-progress, completed, canceled).

‍

5\. State Management with Enums:

To effectively manage vehicle types and statuses, we'll use enums.

‍

Java

```
1public enum VehicleType { ECONOMY, COMPACT, SEDAN, SUV, LUXURY, VAN, TRUCK }
2
3public enum VehicleStatus {
4    AVAILABLE,
5    RESERVED,
6    RENTED,
7    MAINTENANCE,
8    OUT_OF_SERVICE
9}
10
11public enum ReservationStatus {
12    PENDING,
13    CONFIRMED,
14    IN_PROGRESS,
15    COMPLETED,
16    CANCELED
17}
```

‍

Interviewer: That sounds like a solid approach. Let's delve into the implementation details.

‍

**Point 6: Implementation:**

Interviewer: Ready to discuss implementation?

‍

Candidate: Yes. I'll focus on implementing the design patterns we discussed and show how they work together in the Car Rental System:

‍

Car Rental System Design With Design Patterns : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743757600023-Frame-245-\(7\).png)

‍

1. Factory Pattern for Vehicle Creation : 

‍

> NOTE : The term "Vehicle" is being used instead of "Car" to emphasize that our design is not limited to cars alone. It is extensible and adaptable to various vehicle types, including bikes, cycles, and auto-rickshaws, among others.

‍

This section implements the Factory pattern for creating different types of vehicles. It allows for easy addition of new vehicle types without modifying client code.

‍

Base Vehicle Abstract Class : 

Java

```
1// Base Vehicle abstract class
2public abstract class Vehicle {
3    private String registrationNumber;
4    private String model;
5    private VehicleType type;
6    private VehicleStatus status;
7    private double baseRentalPrice;
8
9    // Constructor
10    public Vehicle(String registrationNumber, String model, VehicleType type,
11            double baseRentalPrice) {
12        this.registrationNumber = registrationNumber;
13        this.model = model;
14        this.type = type;
15        this.status = VehicleStatus.AVAILABLE;
16        this.baseRentalPrice = baseRentalPrice;
17    }
18
19    // Abstract method for calculating rental fee
20    public abstract double calculateRentalFee(int days);
21
22    // Getters and setters
23    public String getRegistrationNumber() {
24        return registrationNumber;
25    }
26
27    public String getModel() {
28        return model;
29    }
30
31    public VehicleType getType() {
32        return type;
33    }
34
35    public VehicleStatus getStatus() {
36        return status;
37    }
38
39    public void setStatus(VehicleStatus status) {
40        this.status = status;
41    }
42
43    public double getBaseRentalPrice() {
44        return baseRentalPrice;
45    }
46}
```

‍

• Concrete Car Vehicle Types : 

EconomyVehicle : 

‍

Java

```
1package VehicleFactoryPattern.ConcreteVehicles;
2import CommonEnums.VehicleEnums.VehicleType;
3import VehicleFactoryPattern.Vehicle;
4
5public class EconomyVehicle extends Vehicle {
6    private static final double RATE_MULTIPLIER = 1.0;
7    public EconomyVehicle(String registrationNumber, String model, VehicleType type, double baseRentalPrice) {
8        super(registrationNumber, model, type, baseRentalPrice);
9    }
10
11    @Override
12    public double calculateRentalFee(int days) {
13        return getBaseRentalPrice() * days * RATE_MULTIPLIER;
14    }
15}
```

‍

○ LuxuryVehicle : 

‍

Java

```
1package VehicleFactoryPattern.ConcreteVehicles;
2import CommonEnums.VehicleEnums.VehicleType;
3import VehicleFactoryPattern.Vehicle;
4
5public class LuxuryVehicle extends Vehicle {
6    private static final double RATE_MULTIPLIER = 2.5;
7    private static final double PREMIUM_FEE = 50.0;
8    public LuxuryVehicle(String registrationNumber, String model, VehicleType type, double baseRentalPrice) {
9        super(registrationNumber, model, type, baseRentalPrice);
10    }
11
12    @Override
13    public double calculateRentalFee(int days) {
14        return (getBaseRentalPrice() * days * RATE_MULTIPLIER) + PREMIUM_FEE;
15    }
16}
```

‍

○ SUVVehicle : 

‍

Java

```
1package VehicleFactoryPattern.ConcreteVehicles;
2
3import CommonEnums.VehicleEnums.VehicleType;
4import VehicleFactoryPattern.Vehicle;
5
6public class SUVVehicle extends Vehicle {
7    private static final double RATE_MULTIPLIER = 1.5;
8    public SUVVehicle(String registrationNumber, String model, VehicleType type, double baseRentalPrice) {
9        super(registrationNumber, model, type, baseRentalPrice);
10    }
11    @Override
12    public double calculateRentalFee(int days) {
13        return getBaseRentalPrice() * days * RATE_MULTIPLIER;
14    }
15}
```

‍

• VehicleFactory class : 

‍

Java

```
1public class VehicleFactory {
2    public static Vehicle createVehicle(VehicleType vehicleType, String registrationNumber, String model, double baseRentalPrice) {
3        switch (vehicleType) {
4            case ECONOMY:
5                return new EconomyVehicle(registrationNumber, model, vehicleType,  baseRentalPrice);
6            case LUXURY:
7                return new LuxuryVehicle(registrationNumber, model, vehicleType, baseRentalPrice);
8            case SUV:
9                return new SUVVehicle(registrationNumber, model,vehicleType,  baseRentalPrice);
10            default:
11                throw new IllegalArgumentException("Unsupported vehicle type: " + vehicleType);
12        }
13    }
14}
```

‍

NOTE : 

‍

> The Car Rental System can be extended to support new vehicle types as well. By adding BIKE and AUTO as valid vehicle types, we make the system more versatile and capable of managing a wider variety of vehicles in our rental platform.

‍

○ Update Vehicle Type Enum to Accommodate New Vehicles : 

‍

The VehicleType enum will now include the new types BIKE and AUTO alongside the existing types. This ensures that the new vehicle types are formally recognized in our system and can be used consistently throughout the codebase.

‍

Java

```
1public enum VehicleType {
2			    ECONOMY,
3			    LUXURY,
4			    SUV,
5			    BIKE,
6			    AUTO
7}
```

‍

○ Create New Concrete Vehicle Classes : 

For each new vehicle type, a corresponding concrete class is created. These classes inherit from the Vehicle abstract class and implement the calculateRentalFee method uniquely for each type.

‍

BikeVehicle.java : 

‍

Java

```
1// Bike Vehicle class
2public class BikeVehicle extends Vehicle {
3    private static final double RATE_MULTIPLIER = 0.5;
4
5    public BikeVehicle(
6        String registrationNumber, String model, vehicleType type, double baseRentalPrice) {
7        super(registrationNumber, model, type, baseRentalPrice);
8    }
9
10    @Override
11    public double calculateRentalFee(int days) {
12        return getBaseRentalPrice() * days * RATE_MULTIPLIER;
13    }
14}
```

‍

▪ AutoVehicle.java : 

‍

Java

```
1// Auto Vehicle class
2public class AutoVehicle extends Vehicle {
3    private static final double RATE_MULTIPLIER = 1.2;
4
5    public AutoVehicle(
6        String registrationNumber, String model, vehicleType type, double baseRentalPrice) {
7        super(registrationNumber, model, type, baseRentalPrice);
8    }
9
10    @Override
11    public double calculateRentalFee(int days) {
12        return getBaseRentalPrice() * days * RATE_MULTIPLIER;
13    }
14}
```

‍

○ Update VehicleFactory Class : 

The VehicleFactory class is updated to accommodate the new vehicle types. Now, when a VehicleType of BIKE or AUTO is provided, the factory will return a BikeVehicle or AutoVehicle instance, respectively. This ensures that the new vehicle types are seamlessly integrated into the existing vehicle creation process.

‍

Java

```
1public class VehicleFactory {
2    public static Vehicle createVehicle(VehicleType type,
3        String registrationNumber, String model, double baseRentalPrice) {
4        switch (type) {
5            case ECONOMY:
6                return new EconomyVehicle(
7                    type, registrationNumber, model, baseRentalPrice);
8            case LUXURY:
9                return new LuxuryVehicle(
10                    type, registrationNumber, model, baseRentalPrice);
11            case SUV:
12                return new SUVVehicle(
13                    type, registrationNumber, model, baseRentalPrice);
14            case VAN:
15                return new VanVehicle(
16                    type, registrationNumber, model, baseRentalPrice);
17            case BIKE:
18                return new BikeVehicle(
19                    type, registrationNumber, model, baseRentalPrice);
20            case AUTO:
21                return new AutoVehicle(
22                    type, registrationNumber, model, baseRentalPrice);
23            default:
24                throw new IllegalArgumentException(
25                    "Unsupported vehicle type: " + type);
26        }
27    }‍ 
28}
```

‍

2\. Core Classes Implementation : 

With the Vehicle Design in place, we can now proceed to implement the coding for the following classes: RentalStore, User, Reservation, ReservationManager and RentalSystem.

‍

○ Rental Store Class : 

Since a rental store will always be tied to a specific location, we should begin by designing the Location class as the foundation.

‍

▪ Location.java : 

This class represents the geographical location of a rental store, containing attributes like address, city, state, and zip code.

‍

Java

```
1// Location class
2public class Location {
3    private String address;
4    private String city;
5    private String state;
6    private String zipCode;
7
8    public Location(String address, String city, String state, String zipCode) {
9        this.address = address;
10        this.city = city;
11        this.state = state;
12        this.zipCode = zipCode;
13    }
14    // Getters and setters can be defined here
15}
```

‍

▪ RentalStore.java :

The RentalStore class represents a physical rental location. It maintains a list of vehicles and provides methods to add, remove, and check availability of vehicles. 

‍

Java

```
1// Rental Store class
2public class RentalStore {
3    private int id;
4    private String name;
5    private Location location;
6    private Map<String, Vehicle> vehicles; // Registration Number (Key) -> Vehicle (Value)
7
8    public RentalStore(int id, String name, Location location) {
9        this.id = id;
10        this.name = name;
11        this.location = location;
12        this.vehicles = new HashMap<>();
13    }
14
15    public List<Vehicle> getAvailableVehicles(Date startDate, Date endDate) {
16        List<Vehicle> availableVehicles = new ArrayList<>();
17        for (Vehicle vehicle : vehicles.values()) {
18            if (vehicle.getStatus() == VehicleStatus.AVAILABLE) {
19                availableVehicles.add(vehicle);
20            }
21        }
22        return availableVehicles;
23    }
24
25    public void addVehicle(Vehicle vehicle) {
26        vehicles.put(vehicle.getRegistrationNumber(), vehicle);
27    }
28
29    public void removeVehicle(String registrationNumber) {
30        vehicles.remove(registrationNumber);
31    }
32
33    public boolean isVehicleAvailable(
34        String registrationNumber, Date startDate, Date endDate) {
35        Vehicle vehicle = vehicles.get(registrationNumber);
36        return vehicle != null
37            && vehicle.getStatus() == VehicleStatus.AVAILABLE;
38    }
39
40    public Vehicle getVehicle(String registrationNumber) {
41        return vehicles.get(registrationNumber);
42    }
43    // Getters and setters can be defined here
44}
```

‍

```
1public class User {

14    public void addReservation(Reservation reservation) {

16    }

20    }
21    public int getId() {

23    }
24
```

○ Reservation Class : 

The Reservation class manages vehicle bookings, containing details about the user, vehicle, store locations, and rental period.

‍

Java

```
1// Reservation class
2public class Reservation {
3    private int id;
4    private User user;
5    private Vehicle vehicle;
6    private RentalStore pickupStore;
7    private RentalStore returnStore;
8    private Date startDate;
9    private Date endDate;
10    private ReservationStatus status;
11    private double totalAmount;
12
13    public Reservation(int id, User user, Vehicle vehicle,
14        RentalStore pickupStore, RentalStore returnStore, Date startDate,
15        Date endDate) {
16        this.id = id;
17        this.user = user;
18        this.vehicle = vehicle;
19        this.pickupStore = pickupStore;
20        this.returnStore = returnStore;
21        this.startDate = startDate;
22        this.endDate = endDate;
23        this.status = ReservationStatus.PENDING;
24
25        // Calculate days between start and end dates
26        long diffInMillies = endDate.getTime() - startDate.getTime();
27        int days = (int) (diffInMillies / (1000 * 60 * 60 * 24)) + 1;
28        this.totalAmount = vehicle.calculateRentalFee(days);
29    }
30
31    public void confirmReservation() {
32        if (status == ReservationStatus.PENDING) {
33            status = ReservationStatus.CONFIRMED;
34            vehicle.setStatus(VehicleStatus.RESERVED);
35        }
36    }
37
38    public void startRental() {
39        if (status == ReservationStatus.CONFIRMED) {
40            status = ReservationStatus.IN_PROGRESS;
41            vehicle.setStatus(VehicleStatus.RENTED);
42        }
43    }
44
45    public void completeRental() {
46        if (status == ReservationStatus.IN_PROGRESS) {
47            status = ReservationStatus.COMPLETED;
48            vehicle.setStatus(VehicleStatus.AVAILABLE);
49        }
50    }
51
52    public void cancelReservation() {
53        if (status == ReservationStatus.PENDING
54            || status == ReservationStatus.CONFIRMED) {
55            status = ReservationStatus.CANCELED;
56            vehicle.setStatus(VehicleStatus.AVAILABLE);
57        }
58    }
59     public Integer getId() {
60        return id;
61    }
62
63
64    public double getTotalAmount() {
65        return totalAmount;
66    }
67      // Getters and setters can be defined here
68  }
```

‍

○ ReservationManager.java :

This class manages all reservations, providing methods to create, confirm, start, complete, and cancel reservations.

‍

Java

```
1// Reservation Manager class
2public class ReservationManager {
3    private Map<Integer, Reservation> reservations;
4    private int nextReservationId;
5
6    public ReservationManager() {
7        this.reservations = new HashMap<>();
8        this.nextReservationId = 1;
9    }
10
11    public Reservation createReservation(User user, Vehicle vehicle,
12        RentalStore pickupStore, RentalStore returnStore, Date startDate,
13        Date endDate) {
14        Reservation reservation = new Reservation(nextReservationId++, user,
15            vehicle, pickupStore, returnStore, startDate, endDate);
16        reservations.put(reservation.getId(), reservation);
17        user.addReservation(reservation);
18        return reservation;
19    }
20
21    public void confirmReservation(int reservationId) {
22        Reservation reservation = reservations.get(reservationId);
23        if (reservation != null) {
24            reservation.confirmReservation();
25        }
26    }
27
28    public void startRental(int reservationId) {
29        Reservation reservation = reservations.get(reservationId);
30        if (reservation != null) {
31            reservation.startRental();
32        }
33    }
34
35    public void completeRental(int reservationId) {
36        Reservation reservation = reservations.get(reservationId);
37        if (reservation != null) {
38            reservation.completeRental();
39        }
40    }
41
42    public void cancelReservation(int reservationId) {
43        Reservation reservation = reservations.get(reservationId);
44        if (reservation != null) {
45            reservation.cancelReservation();
46        }
47    }
48
49    public Reservation getReservation(int reservationId) {
50        return reservations.get(reservationId);
51    }
52}
```

‍

○ RentalSystem.java : 

This is the main class managing the entire rental service. It is implemented as a singleton and handles users, stores, reservations, and payments.

‍

Java

```
1// Singleton Rental System class
2public class RentalSystem {
3    private static RentalSystem instance;
4    private List<RentalStore> stores;
5    private VehicleFactory vehicleFactory;
6    private ReservationManager reservationManager;
7    private PaymentProcessor paymentProcessor;
8    private Map<Integer, User> users;
9    private int nextUserId;
10
11    private RentalSystem() {
12        this.stores = new ArrayList<>();
13        this.vehicleFactory = new VehicleFactory();
14        this.reservationManager = new ReservationManager();
15        this.paymentProcessor = new PaymentProcessor();
16        this.users = new HashMap<>();
17        this.nextUserId = 1;
18    }
19
20    public static synchronized RentalSystem getInstance() {
21        if (instance == null) {
22            instance = new RentalSystem();
23        }
24        return instance;
25    }
26
27    public void addStore(RentalStore store) {
28        stores.add(store);
29    }
30
31    public RentalStore getStore(int storeId) {
32        for (RentalStore store : stores) {
33            if (store.getId() == storeId) {
34                return store;
35            }
36        }
37        return null;
38    }
39
40    public List<RentalStore> getStores() {
41        return stores;
42    }
43
44    public User getUser(int userId) {
45        return users.get(userId);
46    }
47
48    public Reservation createReservation(int userId, String vehicleRegistration,
49        int pickupStoreId, int returnStoreId, Date startDate, Date endDate) {
50        User user = users.get(userId);
51        RentalStore pickupStore = getStore(pickupStoreId);
52        RentalStore returnStore = getStore(returnStoreId);
53        Vehicle vehicle = (pickupStore != null) ? pickupStore.getVehicle(vehicleRegistration): null;
54
55        if (user != null && pickupStore != null && returnStore != null && vehicle != null) {
56            return reservationManager.createReservation(
57                user, vehicle, pickupStore, returnStore, startDate, endDate);
58        }
59        return null;
60    }
61
62    public boolean processPayment(
63        int reservationId, PaymentStrategy paymentStrategy) {
64        Reservation reservation =
65            reservationManager.getReservation(reservationId);
66        if (reservation != null) {
67            boolean result = paymentProcessor.processPayment(
68                reservation.getTotalAmount(), paymentStrategy);
69            if (result) {
70                reservationManager.confirmReservation(reservationId);
71                return true;
72            }
73        }
74        return false;
75    }
76
77    public void startRental(int reservationId) {
78        reservationManager.startRental(reservationId);
79    }
80
81    public void completeRental(int reservationId) {
82        reservationManager.completeRental(reservationId);
83    }
84
85    public void cancelReservation(int reservationId) {
86        reservationManager.cancelReservation(reservationId);
87    }
88
89    public void registerUser(User user){
90        int userID = user.getId();
91        if(users.containsKey(userID)){
92            System.out.println("User with id : " + userID + "Already exists in the system");
93            return;
94        }
95        users.put(userID , user);
96    }
97}
```

‍

3\. Strategy Pattern for Payments :

To efficiently manage parking payments for different Vehicles, we will utilize the Strategy pattern for User Payments. let's define the Strategy interfaces and their corresponding concrete strategies.

PaymentStrategy.java (Common Interface for Different Payment Strategies) : 

‍

Java

```
1// Payment Strategy interface
2	public interface PaymentStrategy {
3	    void processPayment(double amount);
4	}
```

‍

• Concrete Payment Strategies : 

CreditCardPayment.java :

‍

Java

```
1// Credit Card Payment class
2public class CreditCardPayment implements PaymentStrategy {
3    @Override
4    public void processPayment(double amount) {
5        System.out.println("Processing credit card payment of $" + amount);
6        System.out.println("Card details: " + maskCardNumber(cardNumber) + ", "
7            + name + ", expires: " + expiryDate);
8        // Logic for credit card payment processing would go here
9    }
10}
```

‍

• CashPayment.java : 

‍

Java

```
1// Cash Payment class
2	public class CashPayment implements PaymentStrategy {
3	    @Override
4	    public void processPayment(double amount) {
5	        System.out.println("Processing cash payment of $" + amount);
6	        // Logic for cash payment processing would go here
7	    }
8	}
```

‍

• PaypalPayment.java : 

‍

Java

```
1// PayPal Payment class
2	public class PayPalPayment implements PaymentStrategy {
3	    @Override
4	    public void processPayment(double amount) {
5	        System.out.println("Processing PayPal payment of $" + amount);
6	        System.out.println("PayPal Account: " + email);
7	        // Logic for PayPal payment processing would go here
8	    }
9}
```

‍

• PaymentProcessor.java for Handling Payments : 

‍

Java

```
1// Payment Processor
2	public class PaymentProcessor {
3	    public boolean processPayment(double amount, PaymentStrategy paymentStrategy) {
4	        paymentStrategy.processPayment(amount);
5	        return true; // Assume payment is successful for simplicity
6	    }
7}
```

‍

NOTE : 

Just like we can add a new Vehicle type directly, we can also add new payment strategies easily by incorporating new Concrete Payment Strategy and use It whenever we need.

‍

To extend our payment strategy system to support Debit Card payments as well, we can easily incorporate a new Debit Card payment Strategy by just adding a new Payment Strategy Debit Card Strategy : 

‍

Java

```
1public class DebitCardPayment implements PaymentStrategy { 
2	    @Override 
3	    public void processPayment(double amount) { 
4	        System.out.println("Processing debit card payment of $" + amount); 
5	        // Logic for debit card payment processing 
6	    } 
7	}
```

‍

4\. Main Method to Run the Car Rental System : 

 This section demonstrates how all the components work together in the Car Rental System.

‍

Java

```
1public class CarRentalMain {
2    public static void main(String[] args) throws ParseException {
3        // Get the Rental System instance (Singleton)
4        RentalSystem rentalSystem = RentalSystem.getInstance();
5
6        // Create rental stores

9        RentalStore store2 = new RentalStore(

11        rentalSystem.addStore(store1);
12        rentalSystem.addStore(store2);
13
14        // Create vehicles using Factory Pattern

16            VehicleType.ECONOMY, "EC001", "Toyota", 50.0);
17        Vehicle luxuryCar = VehicleFactory.createVehicle(
18            VehicleType.LUXURY, "LX001", "Mercedes", 200.0);
19        Vehicle suvCar =
20            VehicleFactory.createVehicle(VehicleType.SUV, "SV001", "Honda", 75.0);
21
22        // Add vehicles to stores
23        store1.addVehicle(economyCar);
24        store1.addVehicle(luxuryCar);
25        store2.addVehicle(suvCar);
26
27         // Register user
28        User user1 = new User(123, "ABC" , "abc@gmail.com");
29        User user2 = new User(345 , "BCD" , "bcd@yahoo.com");
30
31
32        rentalSystem.registerUser(user1);
33        rentalSystem.registerUser(user2);
34
35        // Create reservations
36        Reservation reservation1 = rentalSystem.createReservation(user1.getId(), economyCar.getRegistrationNumber(),
37                        store1.getId(), store1.getId(), new Date(2025 - 1900, 4, 1),
38                new Date(2025 - 1900, 5, 15));
39
40        // Process payment using different strategies (Strategy Pattern)
41        Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
42        System.out.println("
43Processing payment for reservation #" + reservation1.getId());
44        System.out.println("Total amount: $" + reservation1.getTotalAmount());
45        System.out.println("Select payment method:");
46        System.out.println("1. Credit Card");
47        System.out.println("2. Cash");
48        System.out.println("3. PayPal");
49
50        int choice = 1; // Default to credit card for this example
51        // In a real application, you would get user input:
52        // int choice = scanner.nextInt();
53        PaymentStrategy paymentStrategy;
54        switch (choice) {
55            case 1:
56                paymentStrategy = new CreditCardPayment();
57                break;
58            case 2:
59                paymentStrategy = new CashPayment();
60                break;
61            case 3:
62                paymentStrategy = new PayPalPayment();
63                break;
64            default:
65                System.out.println("Invalid choice! Defaulting to credit card payment.");
66                paymentStrategy = new CreditCardPayment();
67                break;
68        }
69        boolean paymentSuccess = rentalSystem.processPayment(reservation1.getId(), paymentStrategy);
70        if (paymentSuccess) {
71            System.out.println("Payment successful!");
72
73            // Start the rental
74            rentalSystem.startRental(reservation1.getId());
75
76            // Simulate rental period
77            System.out.println("
78Simulating rental period...");
79
80            // Complete the rental
81            rentalSystem.completeRental(reservation1.getId());
82        } else {
83            System.out.println("Payment failed!");
84        }
85    }
86}
```

‍

Interviewer: Looks good. What makes your approach effective? 

‍

Candidate: Here are the key strengths of my approach for the car rental application:

• Scalability: The design supports seamless expansion to accommodate more vehicle types, rental locations, and additional features like loyalty programs and subscription models.

‍

• Modularity: Each component, such as vehicle management, reservation handling, and payment processing, is implemented separately, ensuring a clean, maintainable, and testable architecture.

‍

• Flexibility: The use of design patterns like Factory (for vehicle creation), Singleton (for rental system management), and Strategy (for payment processing) enables easy modifications and enhancements without impacting existing functionality.

‍

• Clarity: The well-structured architecture ensures that developers can easily understand, implement, and extend the system when needed, making it adaptable for future business requirements.

‍

Extensibility : 

1.) Strategy Pattern for Pricing : ‍

‍

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743764155901-image.png)

• Define the Pricing Strategy Interface : 

The PricingStrategy interface defines a method to calculate rental prices, allowing for flexible pricing models.

‍

Java

```
1// Pricing Strategy Interface
2	public interface PricingStrategy {
3	    double calculateRentalPrice(Vehicle vehicle, int rentalPeriod);
4	}
```

‍

• Implement Concrete Payment Strategies : 

‍

Three concrete strategies are implemented: 

• HourlyPricingStrategy: Calculates price as a fraction of the daily rate

• DailyPricingStrategy: Standard per-day pricing

WeeklyPricingStrategy: Applies a discount for weekly rentals and handles partial weeks

‍

Java

```
1// Concrete Hourly Pricing Strategy
2public class HourlyPricingStrategy implements PricingStrategy {
3    private static final double HOURLY_RATE_MULTIPLIER = 0.2; // 20% of daily rate per hour
4
5    @Override
6    public double calculateRentalPrice(Vehicle vehicle, int rentalPeriod) {
7        double dailyRate = vehicle.getBaseRentalPrice();
8        return dailyRate * HOURLY_RATE_MULTIPLIER * rentalPeriod;
9    }
10}
11
12// Concrete Daily Pricing Strategy
13public class DailyPricingStrategy implements PricingStrategy {
14    @Override
15    public double calculateRentalPrice(Vehicle vehicle, int rentalPeriod) {
16        return vehicle.getBaseRentalPrice() * rentalPeriod;
17    }
18}
19
20// Concrete Weekly Pricing Strategy
21public class WeeklyPricingStrategy implements PricingStrategy {
22    private static final double WEEKLY_DISCOUNT = 0.8; // 20% discount for weekly rentals
23
24    @Override
25    public double calculateRentalPrice(Vehicle vehicle, int rentalPeriod) {
26        double dailyRate = vehicle.getBaseRentalPrice();
27        int weeks = rentalPeriod / 7;
28        int remainingDays = rentalPeriod % 7;
29        double weeklyPrice = dailyRate * 7 * WEEKLY_DISCOUNT * weeks;
30        double remainingDaysPrice = dailyRate * remainingDays;
31        return weeklyPrice + remainingDaysPrice;
32    }
33}
```

‍

• Modify Reservation and RentalSystem Class to accept the PricingStrategy : 

‍

Java

```
1// Modified Reservation Class to Support Pricing Strategy
2public class Reservation {
3    private PricingStrategy pricingStrategy;
4    public Reservation(int id, User user, Vehicle vehicle, RentalStore pickupStore,
5        RentalStore returnStore, Date startDate, Date endDate, PricingStrategy pricingStrategy) {
6        // ... existing constructor code ...
7        this.pricingStrategy = pricingStrategy;
8        // Calculate total amount using the selected pricing strategy
9        long diffInMillies = endDate.getTime() - startDate.getTime();
10        int days = (int) (diffInMillies / (1000 * 60 * 60 * 24)) + 1;
11        this.totalAmount = pricingStrategy.calculateRentalPrice(vehicle, days);
12    }
13}
14
15// Usage Example in RentalSystem
16public class RentalSystem {
17    public Reservation createReservation(int userId, String vehicleRegistration, int pickupStoreId,
18        int returnStoreId, Date startDate, Date endDate, PricingStrategy pricingStrategy) {
19        // ... existing code ...
20
21        return reservationManager.createReservation(
22            user, vehicle, pickupStore, returnStore, startDate, endDate, pricingStrategy);
23    }
24}
```

‍

• Incorporate Pricing Strategy in the Main Method as well : 

‍

Java

```
1public class CarRentalMain {
2    public static void main(String[] args) throws ParseException {
3        // ...existing code
4        // Create reservations with different pricing strategies
5        Reservation dailyReservation =
6            rentalSystem.createReservation(user1.getId(), economyCar.getRegistrationNumber(),
7                store1.getId(), store1.getId(), sdf.parse("03/01/2025"), sdf.parse("03/05/2025"),
8                new DailyPricingStrategy() // Using Daily Pricing Strategy
9            );
10        Reservation weeklyReservation =
11            rentalSystem.createReservation(user1.getId(), luxuryCar.getRegistrationNumber(),
12                store1.getId(), store1.getId(), sdf.parse("03/01/2025"), sdf.parse("03/22/2025"),
13                new WeeklyPricingStrategy() // Using Weekly Pricing Strategy
14            );
15
16        // the Pricing strategies can be taken as user input as well while making the reservation
17        //...existing code
18    }
19}
```
