## ✨ Conclusion : 

This low-level design for the Car Rental System demonstrates a well-structured, scalable, and extensible architecture. It emphasizes modularity by clearly defining components such as vehicles, reservations, and payment processing. The design supports a range of enhancements, including the addition of new vehicle types, diverse payment options, ensuring the system remains maintainable and flexible. These extensibility points make the system adaptable to future requirements and evolving business needs.  

In an interview setting, presenting this design showcases your ability to craft robust and versatile solutions, underscoring your expertise in applying design patterns, best practices, and thoughtful architecture to real-world scenarios.\n\n---\n\n## Source: https://codewitharyan.com/tech-blogs/design-vending-machine\n\n# Design Vending Machine

Topic Tags:

System DesignLLD

### 🐈‍⬛ Github Codes Link: [https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode](https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode)

## ‍

## Low-Level Design: Vending Machine System 🥤

A vending machine system is designed to efficiently manage product inventory, handle customer selections, process payments, and dispense products. The system needs to support multiple product types, manage inventory availability, handle various payment methods, and provide a seamless purchase experience. The system should be reliable and capable of handling different machine states and payment strategies.

‍

## Rules of the System:

Setup:

• The vending machine has an inventory of products of various types (beverages, snacks, etc.).

• Products have attributes like product ID, name, price, category, and quantity available.

• The system tracks product availability and manages inventory.

‍

Operation:

• Users can browse available products and select items they wish to purchase.

• The vending machine has several states: ready, item selected, payment pending, dispensing, and maintenance.

• The system accepts various payment methods (cash, credit card, mobile payment).

• Once payment is confirmed, the machine dispenses the selected product.

‍

Safety Features:

• The system prevents dispensing when products are out of stock.

• Payment validation ensures secure transactions.

• Audit trails track all purchases and inventory changes.

• Maintenance mode prevents user interaction during servicing.

## Interview Setting 🤝

**Point 1: Introduction and Vague Problem Statement:**

Interviewer: Let's start with a basic problem statement. Design a Vending Machine System.

‍

Candidate: Certainly! Here's my understanding of the Vending Machine System:

• The system will manage products within a single vending machine.

• Users can browse, select, and purchase products based on their preferences.

• The system tracks product availability and prevents dispensing unavailable items.

• Payment processing is integrated for coin-based payments only.

• The system transitions through various states during the purchase cycle.

Is this the expected flow?

‍

Interviewer: Yes, you are aligned with the flow. Please continue ahead.

Candidate: Great! Before diving into the design, I'd like to clarify a few requirements:

• What types of products should the system support?

• How should the system handle coin-based payments?

• Are there specific states the vending machine should manage?

‍

**Point 2: Clarifying Requirements:**

Interviewer: We want a system that:

• Supports multiple product types within a single vending machine.

• Handles coin-based payments methods efficiently.

• Manages the state transitions of the vending machine during operations.

‍

Candidate: To summarize, the key requirements are:

• A system with a vending machine containing various product categories.

• State management to handle the flow from product selection to dispensing.

• Coin-based payment implementation to support various payment methods.

• Ability to handle edge cases like out-of-stock items, payment failures, or machine maintenance.

‍

Interviewer: Perfect, let's proceed.

‍

**Point 3: Identifying Key Components:**

Candidate: Now that we have the requirements, let's identify the key components of our Vending Machine System:

• Item: Represents individual items in the vending machine.

• ItemType Enum : Represents Different Types of Product.

• Item Shelf: Contains a Product

• Coin : Represents Different Denominations of Coins which the Vending Machine can accept.

• Inventory : Manages the Inventory of the Items in the Vending Machine.

‍

Interviewer: That sounds good. Let's proceed with the design details.

‍

**Point 4: Design Challenges:**

Interviewer: What design challenges do you anticipate?

‍

Candidate: The key challenges for the Vending Machine System include:

• State Management: Properly transitioning between different machine states.

• Payment Processing: Supporting multiple payment methods securely. (Currently Coin, but extensible in future)

• Inventory Management: Ensuring accurate product tracking and availability.

• Error Handling: Managing scenarios like payment failures or product jams.

• Maintenance Operations: Supporting restocking and machine servicing.

‍

**Point 5: Approach:**

Interviewer: How would you approach these challenges?

‍

Candidate: I propose using design patterns effectively. Here are my strategies:

1\. State Pattern for Machine States:

○ Encapsulates state-specific behavior.

○ Manages transitions between states (ready, item selected, payment pending, dispensing, maintenance).

○ Prevents invalid operations based on current state.

‍

2\. Strategy Pattern for Payment Methods:

○ Enables different payment strategies (cash, credit card, mobile payment).

○ Can switch between payment methods dynamically.

○ Encapsulates payment processing logic.

‍

Interviewer: That sounds like a solid approach. Let's delve into the implementation details.

‍

**Point 6: Implementation:**

Interviewer: Ready to discuss implementation?

‍

Candidate: Yes. I'll focus on implementing the design patterns we discussed and show how they work together in the Vending Machine Problem :

‍

## Vending Machine Design with Design Patterns : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743830302760-Frame-245-\(8\).png)

‍

State Diagram : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743830348526-Frame-245-\(9\).png)

‍

1\. Implementing Core Classes : 

• Item: Represents individual items in the vending machine.

• ItemType Enum : Represents Different Types of Products

‍

Enum : 

Java

```
1// Enum representing different types of items in the vending machine
2	public enum ItemType {
3	    COKE,
4	    PEPSI,
5	    JUICE,
6	    SODA
7}
```

‍

Class:

Java

```
1// Class representing an item in the vending machine
2	public class Item {
3	    // Type of the item (e.g., COKE, PEPSI)
4	    private ItemType type;
5	    
6	    // Price of the item
7	    private int price;
8	    // Getters and Setters
9	    public ItemType getType() {
10	        return type;
11	    }
12	    public void setType(ItemType type) {
13	        this.type = type;
14	    }
15
16	    public int getPrice() {
17	        return price;
18	    }
19
20	    public void setPrice(int price) {
21	        this.price = price;
22	    }
23	}
```

‍

• ItemShelf: Contains a Product 

Class : 

‍

Java

```
1// Class representing a slot in the vending machine that holds multiple items
2	public class ItemShelf {
3	    // Code to identify the slot
4	    private int code;
5	    // List of items stored in the slot
6	    private List<Item> items;
7	    // Flag to indicate if the shelf is sold out
8	    private boolean isSoldOut;
9
10	    // Constructor to initialize the item shelf
11	    public ItemShelf(int code) {
12	        this.code = code;
13	        this.items = new ArrayList<>(); // Initialize the list of items
14	        this.isSoldOut = false;
15	    }
16
17	    // Getters and Setters
18	    public int getCode() {
19	        return code;
20	    }
21
22	    public void setCode(int code) {
23	        this.code = code;
24	    }
25
26	    public List<Item> getItems() {
27	        return items;
28	    }
29
30	    public boolean checkIsSoldOut() {
31	        return isSoldOut;
32	    }
33
34	    public void setIsSoldOut(boolean isSoldOut) {
35	        this.isSoldOut = isSoldOut;
36	    }
37
38	    public void setItems(List<Item> items) {
39	        this.items = items;
40	        if(isSoldOut) setIsSoldOut(false); // Update the sold-out status when items are set
41	    }
42
43	    // Add an item to the shelf
44	    public void addItem(Item item) {
45	        items.add(item);
46	        if(isSoldOut) setIsSoldOut(false); // Update sold-out status after adding an item
47	    }
48
49	    // Remove an item from the shelf
50	    public void removeItem(Item item) {
51	        items.remove(item);
52	        if(items.isEmpty()) setIsSoldOut(true); // Update sold-out status after removing an item
53	    }
54
55       public boolean hasItems() { 
56		        for(ItemShelf itemShelf : inventory){ 
57		            if(!itemShelf.checkIsSoldOut()) return true; 
58		        } 
59		        return false;  
60		   } 
61  	}
```

‍

• Coin : Represents Different Denominations of Coins which the Vending Machine can accept 

‍

Java

```
1// Enum representing different types of Indian coins in the vending machine
2	public enum Coin {
3	    ONE_RUPEE(1),
4	    TWO_RUPEES(2),
5	    FIVE_RUPEES(5),
6	    TEN_RUPEES(10);
7	    // Value of the coin in Indian rupees
8	    public int value;
9	    // Constructor to initialize the coin's value
10	    Coin(int value) {
11	        this.value = value;
12	    }
13}
```

‍

• Inventory : Manages the Inventory of the Items in the Vending Machine

Class : 

‍

Java

```
1// Class representing the inventory of the vending machine
2public class Inventory {
3  // Array to hold item shelves in the inventory
4  ItemShelf[] inventory = null;
5
6  // Constructor for Inventory
7  public Inventory(int itemCount) {
8    inventory = new ItemShelf[itemCount]; // Initialize the array with the given item count
9    initialEmptyInventory(); // Initialize the inventory with empty shelves
10  }
11
12  // Getter for the inventory array
13  public ItemShelf[] getInventory() {
14    return inventory;
15  }
16
17  // Setter for the inventory array
18  public void setInventory(ItemShelf[] inventory) {
19    this.inventory = inventory;
20  }
21
22  // Method to initialize the inventory with empty shelves
23  public void initialEmptyInventory() {
24    int startCode = 101; // Starting code for item shelves
25    for (int i = 0; i < inventory.length; i++) {
26      ItemShelf space = new ItemShelf(startCode); // Create a new item shelf with a code
27      inventory[i] = space; // Add the shelf to the inventory
28      startCode++; // Increment the code for the next shelf
29    }
30  }
31
32  // Method to add an item to the inventory at a specific code number
33  public void addItem(Item item, int codeNumber) throws Exception {
34    for (ItemShelf itemShelf : inventory) {
35      if (itemShelf.getCode() == codeNumber) {
36        // Add the item to the shelf
37        itemShelf.addItem(item);
38        return;
39      }
40    }
41    throw new Exception("Invalid Code");
42  }
43
44  // Method to get and remove an item from the inventory by its code number
45  public Item getItem(int codeNumber) throws Exception {
46    for (ItemShelf itemShelf : inventory) {
47      if (itemShelf.getCode() == codeNumber) {
48        if (itemShelf.checkIsSoldOut()) {
49          throw new Exception("Item already sold out");
50        } else {
51          // Get and remove the first item from the shelf
52          Item item = itemShelf.getItems().get(0); // Get the first item
53          return item;
54        }
55      }
56    }
57    throw new Exception("Invalid Code");
58  }
59
60  // Method to update an item shelf as sold out by its code number
61  public void updateSoldOutItem(int codeNumber) {
62    for (ItemShelf itemShelf : inventory) {
63      if (itemShelf.getCode() == codeNumber) {
64        if (itemShelf.getItems().isEmpty())
65          itemShelf.setIsSoldOut(true); // Mark the shelf as sold out
66      }
67    }
68  }
69
70  // Method to remove a specific item from the inventory by its code number
71  public void removeItem(int codeNumber) throws Exception {
72    for (ItemShelf itemShelf : inventory) {
73      if (itemShelf.getCode() == codeNumber) {
74        itemShelf.removeItem(
75            itemShelf.getItems().get(0)); // Remove the specific item from the shelf
76        return;
77      }
78    }
79    throw new Exception("Invalid Code");
80  }
81}
```

‍‍

2\. State Pattern for Vending Machine States : 

1.) Define the Vending Machine State Interface : 

‍

Java

```
1// Interface defining the common methods for all states
2	public interface VendingMachineState {
3	    // Get the name of the current state
4	    String getStateName();
5	    
6	    // Method to handle state transitions
7	    VendingMachineState next(VendingMachineContext context);
8	}
```

‍

     2.) Define the Context Handler : 

‍

Java

```
1// Context class that maintains state and handles transitions in the vending machine
2public class VendingMachineContext {
3  private VendingMachineState currentState; // Current state of the vending machine
4  private Inventory inventory; // Inventory to store items
5  private List<Coin> coinList; // List to keep track of inserted coins
6  private int selectedItemCode; // Code of the selected item
7
8
9  // Constructor to initialize the vending machine with an idle state
10  public VendingMachineContext() {
11    inventory = new Inventory(10); // Initialize inventory with 10 slots
12    coinList = new ArrayList<>(); // Initialize the coin list
13    currentState = new IdleState(); // Set initial state to idle
14    System.out.println("Initialized: " + currentState.getStateName());
15  }
16
17  // Gets the current state of the vending machine
18  public VendingMachineState getCurrentState() {
19    return currentState;
20  }
21
22  // Advances the vending machine to the next state
23  public void advanceState() {
24    VendingMachineState nextState = currentState.next(this); // Get the next state
25    currentState = nextState; // Transition to the next state
26    System.out.println("Current state: " + currentState.getStateName());
27  }
28
29  // Handles the insertion of a coin
30  public void clickOnInsertCoinButton(Coin coin) {
31    if (currentState instanceof IdleState || currentState instanceof HasMoneyState) {
32      System.out.println("Inserted " + coin.name() + " worth " + coin.value);
33      coinList.add(coin); // Add the coin to the list
34      advanceState(); // Move to the next state
35    } else {
36      System.out.println("Cannot insert coin in " + currentState.getStateName());
37    }
38  }
39
40  // Handles the product selection process
41  public void clickOnStartProductSelectionButton(int codeNumber) {
42    if (currentState instanceof HasMoneyState) {
43      advanceState(); // Move to selection state
44      selectProduct(codeNumber); // Select the product
45    } else {
46      System.out.println("Product selection button can only be clicked in HasMoney state");
47    }
48  }
49
50  // Selects a product based on its code
51  public void selectProduct(int codeNumber) {
52    if (currentState instanceof SelectionState) {
53      try {
54        Item item = inventory.getItem(codeNumber); // Fetch the item from inventory
55
56        int balance = getBalance(); // Calculate the total balance
57        if (balance < item.getPrice()) { // Check for sufficient funds
58          System.out.println(
59              "Insufficient amount. Product price: " + item.getPrice() + ", paid: " + balance);
60          return;
61        }
62        setSelectedItemCode(codeNumber); // Set the selected item code
63        advanceState(); // Move to dispense state
64        dispenseItem(codeNumber); // Dispense the item
65
66        if (balance >= item.getPrice()) { // Return change if applicable
67          int change = balance - item.getPrice();
68          System.out.println("Returning change: " + change);
69        }
70      } catch (Exception e) {
71        System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage());
72      }
73    } else {
74      System.out.println("Products can only be selected in Selection state");
75    }
76  }
77
78  // Dispenses the selected item
79  public void dispenseItem(int codeNumber) {
80    if (currentState instanceof DispenseState) {
81      try {
82        Item item = inventory.getItem(codeNumber);
83        System.out.println("Dispensing: " + item.getType());
84        inventory.removeItem(codeNumber); // Remove item from inventory
85        // Update inventory
86        inventory.updateSoldOutItem(codeNumber);
87        // Reset machine state
88        resetBalance();
89        resetSelection();
90        advanceState(); // Move to the next state
91      } catch (Exception e) {
92        System.out.println("Failed to Dispense the Product with code : " + codeNumber);
93      }
94    } else {
95      System.out.println("System cannot dispense in : " + currentState);
96    }
97  }
98
99  // Updates the inventory by adding a new item
100  public void updateInventory(Item item, int codeNumber) {
101    if (currentState instanceof IdleState) { // Only update inventory in Idle state
102      try {
103        inventory.addItem(item, codeNumber); // Add the item to inventory
104        System.out.println("Added " + item.getType() + " to slot " + codeNumber);
105      } catch (Exception e) {
106        System.out.println("Error updating inventory: " + e.getMessage());
107      }
108    } else {
109      System.out.println("Inventory can only be updated in Idle state");
110    }
111  }
112
113  // Getters and setters for context properties
114  public Inventory getInventory() {
115    return inventory;
116  }
117
118  public void setInventory(Inventory inventory) {
119    this.inventory = inventory;
120  }
121
122  public List<Coin> getCoinList() {
123    return coinList;
124  }
125
126  public void setCoinList(List<Coin> coinList) {
127    this.coinList = coinList;
128  }
129
130  public int getSelectedItemCode() {
131    return selectedItemCode;
132  }
133
134  public void setSelectedItemCode(int codeNumber) {
135    this.selectedItemCode = codeNumber;
136  }
137
138  // Resets the product selection
139  public void resetSelection() {
140    this.selectedItemCode = 0;
141  }
142
143  // Calculates the total balance from inserted coins
144  public int getBalance() {
145    int balance = 0;
146    for (Coin coin : coinList) {
147      balance += coin.value; // Sum up the coin values
148    }
149    return balance;
150  }
151
152  // Resets the balance by clearing all coins
153  public void resetBalance() {
154    coinList.clear();
155  }
156}
```

‍

3.) Concrete States : 

 Idle State : Initial state where the only possible option is to insert coins into the machine  

‍

Java

```
1// Implementation of the Idle state
2public class IdleState implements VendingMachineState {
3  public IdleState() {
4    System.out.println("Vending machine is now in Idle State");
5  }
6
7  @Override
8  public String getStateName() {
9    return "IdleState";
10  }
11
12  @Override
13  public VendingMachineState next(VendingMachineContext context) {
14    // Check if inventory has items
15    if (!context.getInventory().hasItems()) {
16      return new OutOfStockState();
17    }
18    // If money has been inserted, transition to HasMoneyState
19    if (!context.getCoinList().isEmpty()) {
20      return new HasMoneyState();
21    }
22    // Otherwise, remain in idle state
23    return this;
24  }
25}
```

‍

• Has Money State : The Possible actions which can be performed in the Has Money State can be : 

a. Insert Coins.

b. Start the Product Selection. 

c. Cancel Product Selection. 

d. Get Refund. 

‍

Java

```
1package VendingMachineStates.ConcreteStates;
2
3import VendingMachineStates.VendingMachineContext;
4import VendingMachineStates.VendingMachineState;
5
6public class HasMoneyState implements VendingMachineState {
7    public HasMoneyState() {
8        System.out.println("Vending machine is now in HasMoney State");
9    }
10
11    @Override
12    public String getStateName() {
13        return "HasMoneyState";
14    }
15
16    @Override
17    public VendingMachineState next(VendingMachineContext context) {
18        if (!context.getInventory().hasItems()) {
19            return new OutOfStockState();
20        }
21        if (context.getCoinList().isEmpty()) {
22            return new IdleState();
23        }
24        // Transition to SelectionState if user starts product selection
25        if (context.getCurrentState() instanceof HasMoneyState) {
26            return new SelectionState();
27        }
28        return this;
29    }
30}
```

‍

• Selection State : The Possible actions which can be performed in the Selection State can be : 

a. Choose the Product. 

b. Cancel Selected Product. 

c. Get Refund. 

Get Change after purchasing the product

‍

Java

```
1// Implementation of the Selection state
2	public class SelectionState implements VendingMachineState {
3	    public SelectionState() {
4	        System.out.println("Vending machine is now in Selection State");
5	    }
6	    
7	    @Override
8	    public String getStateName() {
9	        return "SelectionState";
10	    }
11	    
12	    @Override
13	    public VendingMachineState next(VendingMachineContext context) {
14	        // If inventory has no items, transition to OutOfStock
15	        if (!context.getInventory().hasItems()) {
16	            return new OutOfStockState();
17	        }	        
18	        // If no money left, go back to idle
19	        if (context.getCoinList().isEmpty()) {
20	            return new IdleState();
21	        }	        
22	        // If an item has been selected, transition to dispense state
23	        if (context.getSelectedItemCode() > 0) {
24	            return new DispenseState();
25	        }	       
26	        // Otherwise, remain in Selection state
27	        return this;
28	    }
29	}
```

‍

• Dispense State : The Possible actions which can be performed in the Dispense State can only be of dispensing the product.

‍

Java

```
1// Implementation of the Dispense state
2	public class DispenseState implements VendingMachineState {
3	    public DispenseState() {
4	        System.out.println("Vending machine is now in Dispense State");
5	    }
6	    
7	    @Override
8	    public String getStateName() {
9	        return "DispenseState";
10	    }
11	    
12	    @Override
13	    public VendingMachineState next(VendingMachineContext context) {
14	        // Dispense the selected product
15	        return new IdleState();
16     	}
17}
```

‍

• OutOfStockState : The Possible actions which can be performed in the Out of Stock State can only be going to the idle state 

If the current selected item is out of stock.

‍

Java

```
1// Implementation of the Out of Stock state
2	public class OutOfStockState implements VendingMachineState {
3	    public OutOfStockState() {
4	        System.out.println("Vending machine is now in Out of Stock State");
5	    }
6	    
7	    @Override
8	    public String getStateName() {
9	        return "OutOfStockState";
10	    }
11	    
12	    @Override
13	    public VendingMachineState next(VendingMachineContext context) {
14	        // If inventory has items again, return to idle state
15	        if (context.getInventory().hasItems()) {
16	            return new IdleState();
17	        }
18	        
19	        // Otherwise, remain in out of stock state
20	        return this;
21	    }
22	}
```

‍

3\. Main Method to Run the Vending Machine : 

‍

Java

```
1public class Main {
2  // Main method to execute the vending machine operations
3  public static void main(String args[]) {
4    // Create a new vending machine context
5    VendingMachineContext vendingMachine = new VendingMachineContext();
6    try {
7      System.out.println("|");
8      System.out.println("Filling up the inventory");
9      System.out.println("|");
10      fillUpInventory(vendingMachine); // Fill up the inventory with items
11      displayInventory(vendingMachine); // Display the current inventory
12      System.out.println("|");
13      System.out.println("Inserting coins");
14      System.out.println("|");
15      // Insert coins using the context methods
16      vendingMachine.clickOnInsertCoinButton(Coin.TEN_RUPEES);
17      vendingMachine.clickOnInsertCoinButton(Coin.FIVE_RUPEES);
18      System.out.println("|");
19      System.out.println("Clicking on ProductSelectionButton");
20      System.out.println("|");
21      // Start product selection and choose a product
22      vendingMachine.clickOnStartProductSelectionButton(102);
23      // Display the updated inventory
24      displayInventory(vendingMachine);
25    } catch (Exception e) {
26      System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage());
27      displayInventory(vendingMachine);
28    }
29  }
30
31  // Method to fill up the inventory of the vending machine
32  private static void fillUpInventory(VendingMachineContext vendingMachine) {
33    for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
34      Item newItem = new Item();
35      int codeNumber = 101 + i; // Shelf code
36      // Set item type and price based on the index range
37      if (i >= 0 && i < 3) {
38        newItem.setType(ItemType.COKE);
39        newItem.setPrice(12);
40      } else if (i >= 3 && i < 5) {
41        newItem.setType(ItemType.PEPSI);
42        newItem.setPrice(9);
43      } else if (i >= 5 && i < 7) {
44        newItem.setType(ItemType.JUICE);
45        newItem.setPrice(13);
46      } else if (i >= 7 && i < 10) {
47        newItem.setType(ItemType.SODA);
48        newItem.setPrice(7);
49      }
50      // Update the inventory with multiple same items per shelf
51      for (int j = 0; j < 5; j++) {
52        // Add 5 items to each shelf
53        vendingMachine.updateInventory(newItem, codeNumber);
54      }
55    }
56  }
57
58  // Method to display the current inventory of the vending machine
59  private static void displayInventory(VendingMachineContext vendingMachine) {
60    ItemShelf[] slots = vendingMachine.getInventory().getInventory();
61    for (ItemShelf slot : slots) {
62      List<Item> items = slot.getItems(); // Get the list of items in the shelf
63      if (!items.isEmpty()) {
64        System.out.println("CodeNumber: " + slot.getCode() + " Items: ");
65        for (Item item : items) { // Display all items in the shelf
66          System.out.println(
67              "    - Item: " + item.getType().name() + ", Price: " + item.getPrice());
68        }
69        System.out.println("SoldOut: " + slot.checkIsSoldOut());
70      } else {
71        // Display empty shelf information
72        System.out.println("CodeNumber: " + slot.getCode() + " Items: EMPTY"
73            + " SoldOut: " + slot.checkIsSoldOut());
74      }
75    }
76  }
77}
```

‍

Interviewer: Looks good. What makes your approach effective?

‍

Candidate: Here are the key strengths of my approach for the Vending Machine System:

‍

• State Pattern Implementation: I've implemented the State pattern to model the vending machine's different states (Idle, HasMoney, Selection, Dispense). This allows the machine to behave differently based on its current state while encapsulating state-specific behavior in separate classes.

‍

• Single Responsibility Principle: Each class has a clear, focused responsibility. The Item class represents products, ItemShelf manages individual slots, Inventory handles the collection of items, and the VendingMachine orchestrates the overall operation.

‍

• Extensibility: The design easily accommodates new product types through the ItemType enum, and the state pattern allows for introducing new states or behaviors without modifying existing code.

‍

• Maintainability: The clean separation of concerns makes the code easy to maintain and modify. For example, changing the payment processing logic would only affect specific methods in relevant state classes.

‍

• Real-world Modeling: The implementation accurately models the behavior of an actual vending machine with its natural workflow from idle to money collection, product selection, and dispensing.

‍

Extensibility : 

1.) Addition of New Payment Methods Using Strategy pattern : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743830476162-Frame-245-\(10\).png)

‍

To efficiently incorporate different payment methods for Vending Machine, we can utilize the Strategy pattern for User Payments. let's define the Strategy interfaces and their corresponding concrete strategies.

‍

Here's how we can achieve this extension in our current code : 

‍

• Define the Payment Strategy interface : 

PaymentStrategy.java :

‍

Java

```
1// Payment strategy interface
2	public interface PaymentStrategy {
3	    boolean processPayment(double amount);
4}
```

‍

• Implement Concrete Payment Strategies : 

CoinPaymentStrategy.java : 

‍

Java

```
1// Coin payment strategy implementation
2	public class CoinPaymentStrategy implements PaymentStrategy {
3	    private List<Coin> coins;
4	    public CoinPaymentStrategy(List<Coin> coins) {
5	        this.coins = coins;
6	    }
7
8	    @Override
9	    public boolean processPayment(double amount) {
10	        int total = 0;
11	        for (Coin coin : coins) {
12	            total += coin.value;
13	        }
14	        return total >= amount;
15	    }
16}
```

‍

• CardPaymentStrategy.java : 

‍

Java

```
1// Card payment strategy implementation
2	public class CardPaymentStrategy implements PaymentStrategy {
3	    private String cardNumber;
4	    private String expiryDate;
5	    private String cvv;
6	    public CardPaymentStrategy(String cardNumber, String expiryDate, String cvv) {
7	        this.cardNumber = cardNumber;
8	        this.expiryDate = expiryDate;
9	        this.cvv = cvv;
10	    }
11
12	    @Override
13	    public boolean processPayment(double amount) {
14	        // Simulate card payment processing (replace with actual logic)
15	        System.out.println("Processing card payment of " + amount + " using card " + cardNumber);
16	        // In real-world, integrate with a payment gateway here.
17	        // For simulation, always return true for simplicity.
18	        return true;
19	    }
20	}
```

‍

• Modify Vending Machine Context Class to Accommodate Payment Strategy : 

VendingMachineContext.java : 

‍

Java

```
1public class VendingMachineContext {
2  // ... other fields ...
3  private PaymentStrategy paymentStrategy; // Added payment strategy
4
5  // ... other methods ...
6
7  public void setPaymentStrategy(PaymentStrategy paymentStrategy) {
8    this.paymentStrategy = paymentStrategy;
9  }
10
11  public boolean processPayment(double amount) {
12    if (paymentStrategy == null) {
13      System.out.println("No payment strategy set.");
14      return false;
15    }
16    return paymentStrategy.processPayment(amount);
17  }
18
19  public void clickOnInsertCoinButton(Coin coin) {
20    if (currentState instanceof IdleState || currentState instanceof HasMoneyState) {
21      System.out.println("Inserted " + coin.name() + " worth " + coin.value);
22      coinList.add(coin);
23      advanceState();
24    } else {
25      System.out.println("Cannot insert coin in " + currentState.getStateName());
26    }
27  }
28
29  public void clickOnCardPaymentButton(String cardNumber, String expiryDate, String cvv) {
30    if (currentState instanceof IdleState || currentState instanceof HasMoneyState) {
31      System.out.println("Starting card payment process.");
32      setPaymentStrategy(
33          new CardPaymentStrategy(cardNumber, expiryDate, cvv)); // Set the card payment strategy
34      advanceState();
35    } else {
36      System.out.println("Cannot start card payment in " + currentState.getStateName());
37    }
38  }
39
40  // Dispenses the selected item
41  public void dispenseItem(int codeNumber, double amount) {
42    try {
43      // can also be taken as user input
44      setPaymentStrategy(new CoinPaymentStrategy(coinList)); // Set the coin payment strategy
45      processPayment(amount) inventory.removeItem(codeNumber); // Remove item from inventory
46      advanceState(); // Move to the next state
47    } catch (Exception e) {
48      System.out.println("Failed to Dispense the Product with code : " + codeNumber);
49    }
50  }
51
52  public int getBalance() {
53    if (paymentStrategy instanceof CoinPaymentStrategy) {
54      int balance = 0;
55      for (Coin coin : coinList) {
56        balance += coin.value;
57      }
58      return balance;
59    }
60    return 0;
61  }
62
63  public void resetBalance() {
64    coinList.clear();
65    paymentStrategy = null;
66  }
67
68  // ... other methods ...
69}
```

‍

• Update the Dispense State Class:

Modify the Dispense State Class to use the Payment Strategy.

‍

Java

```
1// modified dispense state next method including the paymentStrategy
2	public VendingMachineState next(VendingMachineContext context) {
3	    // ...
4	    try {
5	        // ...
6	        context.resetBalance();
7	        context.resetSelection();
8	        context.setPaymentStrategy(null); // Reset payment strategy
9	        return new IdleState();
10	    } catch (Exception e) {
11	        // ...
12	        context.refundMoney();
13	        context.setPaymentStrategy(null); // Reset payment strategy
14	        return new IdleState();
15	    }
16}
```

‍

• Modify The Client Code to include the Payment Strategies : 

‍

Java

```
1import java.util.Scanner;
2
3public class Main {
4  public static void main(String args[]) {
5    VendingMachineContext vendingMachine = new VendingMachineContext();
6    try {
7      System.out.println("|");
8      System.out.println("Filling up the inventory");
9      System.out.println("|");
10      fillUpInventory(vendingMachine);
11      displayInventory(vendingMachine);
12      System.out.println("|");
13      System.out.println("Select Payment Method:");
14      System.out.println("1. Coin Payment");
15      System.out.println("2. Card Payment");
16      System.out.print("Enter your choice: ");
17      Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
18      int paymentChoice = scanner.nextInt();
19      scanner.nextLine(); // Consume the newline character
20      switch (paymentChoice) {
21        case 1:
22          System.out.println("Inserting coins");
23          vendingMachine.clickOnInsertCoinButton(Coin.TEN_RUPEES);
24          vendingMachine.clickOnInsertCoinButton(Coin.FIVE_RUPEES);
25          break;
26        case 2:
27          System.out.println("Making card payment");
28          System.out.print("Enter card number: ");
29          String cardNumber = scanner.nextLine();
30          System.out.print("Enter expiry date (MM/YY): ");
31          String expiryDate = scanner.nextLine();
32          System.out.print("Enter CVV: ");
33          String cvv = scanner.nextLine();
34          vendingMachine.clickOnCardPaymentButton(cardNumber, expiryDate, cvv);
35          break;
36        default:
37          System.out.println("Invalid payment choice.");
38          return;
39      }
40      System.out.println("|");
41      System.out.println("Clicking on ProductSelectionButton");
42      System.out.println("|");
43      vendingMachine.clickOnStartProductSelectionButton(102);
44      displayInventory(vendingMachine);
45      scanner.close();
46    } catch (Exception e) {
47      System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage());
48      displayInventory(vendingMachine);
49    }
50  }
51
52  // ... (fillUpInventory and displayInventory methods remain the same) ...
53}
```

‍

This demonstrates how the Strategy Pattern can be implemented to extend the design for multiple payment strategies.

‍

## ✨Conclusion : 

This low-level design for the Vending Machine System demonstrates a well-structured, scalable, and extensible architecture. It emphasizes modularity by clearly defining components such as Item, Inventory, and VendingMachine. The design supports a range of enhancements, including the addition of new payment options, ensuring the system remains maintainable and flexible. These extensibility points make the system adaptable to future requirements and evolving business needs.  

‍

In an interview setting, presenting this design showcases your ability to craft robust and versatile solutions, underscoring your expertise in applying design patterns, best practices, and thoughtful architecture to real-world scenarios.\n\n---\n\n## Source: https://codewitharyan.com/tech-blogs/design-file-system\n\n# Design File System

Topic Tags:

System DesignLLD

### 🐈‍⬛ Github Codes Link: [https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode](https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode)

# ‍

# Low-Level Design: File System 📂

## Introduction : 

A File System is a fundamental component of any operating system, responsible for organizing, storing, and managing files and directories on storage devices. It provides a structured way to manage data, ensuring efficient storage, retrieval, and manipulation of digital information.

‍

## Rules and Core Concepts : 

**Basic File System Principles:**

1\. Hierarchical Structure: Files and directories are organized in a tree-like hierarchy.

2\. File Attributes: Each file has metadata like name, size, creation time, permissions.

3\. Directory Management: Directories can contain files and other directories.

4\. File Operations: Support for basic operations like create, read, update, delete (CRUD).

5\. Path-Based Access: Resources are accessed using string paths like

```
"/documents/report.txt"
```

‍

## Interview Setting 🤝

**Point 1:Introduction and Vague Problem Statement:**

Interviewer: Design a basic File System management system.

‍

Candidate: Let me outline the core requirements and key characteristics:

• Support hierarchical path structure using the "/" delimiter.

• Implement path creation and value association.

• Manage file metadata.

• Handle different file types

‍

**Point 2: Clarifying Requirements :** 

Interviewer: Specify the core requirements for our File System:

• Support creating files and directories

• Implement path navigation

• Manage file metadata.

• Support for different file types

‍

Candidate: To ensure clarity, I'll summarize the key requirements:

• Hierarchical path structure with "/" as delimiter

• CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete)

• Path validation including parent path existence

• Efficient storage and retrieval of files and directories. 

• Support for path-value associations.

‍

**Point 3: Identifying Key Components :** 

Key components of our File System design:

1\. File System Node (Trie Node) :

• Common properties and methods for both files and directories

• Represents the base entity in the file system

‍

2\. File Class:

• Represents individual files

• Stores file-specific metadata and content

‍

3\. Directory Class:

• Manages collection of files and subdirectories

• Supports hierarchical organization

‍

4\. File System Manager:

• Central manager for file system operations

• Handles path creation, validation, and retrieval

‍

**Point 4: Design Challenges :** 

Interviewer: What design challenges do you anticipate?

‍

Candidate: Key challenges include:

• Efficient path resolution and navigation.

• Handling nested path structures.

• Ensuring parent paths exist before creating child paths

• Managing path-value associations

• Supporting path traversal and retrieval

‍

**Point 5: Design Approach :** 

To address these challenges, I propose leveraging several design patterns:

1\. Composite Pattern for Hierarchical Structure :

• Treat files and directories uniformly

• Enable recursive operations on file system nodes

‍

2\. Singleton Pattern for File System Manager : 

• Centralized management of file system

• Ensure single point of control

‍

Interviewer: That sounds like a solid approach. Let's delve into the implementation details.

‍

**Point 6: Implementation:**

Interviewer: Ready to discuss implementation?

‍

Candidate: Yes. I'll focus on implementing the design patterns we discussed and show how they work together in the File System Problem :

‍

## Design File System with Design Patterns : 

### Path Representation : 

In our file system implementation, paths are represented using a trie data structure, which efficiently handles hierarchical data. This approach allows us to store and navigate the file system with optimal time complexity for operations like path creation, deletion, and retrieval.

‍

The trie representation maps each component of a path to a node in the tree, where:

• The root node represents the root directory "/"

• Each subsequent path component becomes a child node

• Directory nodes can have multiple children

• File nodes are always leaf nodes in the tree

• Each node contains metadata such as creation time and modification time.

‍

### Path-to-Trie Mapping Example : 

Let's examine how a specific path is represented as a trie structure in our system. For example, the path:

```
/document/cwa_lld/solid_principles.java : Is broken down and represented in the trie as follows:
```

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743916939982-Frame-246.png)

2\. Now Suppose we add another file in the cwa\_lld folder, let's say we add a new file "design\_file\_system.java", then it's corresponding  

     Path and trieNode structure will look like :  

```
/document/cwa_lld/design_file_system.java
```

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743916987651-Frame-246-\(1\).png)

‍

3\. Now Suppose we add another folder in document folder, let's say we add a new folder "cwa\_hld", and we create two files namely "horizontal\_scaling.txt" and "consistent\_hashing.java", then it's corresponding Path and trieNode structure will look like :  

```
/document/cwa_hld/horizontal_scaling.txt
/document/cwa_hld/consistent_hashing.java
```

‍

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743917051655-Frame-246-\(2\).png)

‍

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1743917104583-Frame-247.png)

‍

1\. Composite Pattern for Managing File and Directory Hierarchical Structure :

‍

a. Define the Common Class / Interface : 

• FileSystemNode.java : 

• Abstract base class for file and directory nodes.

• Contains properties: name, createdAt, modifiedAt, and size.

Abstract methods: display(int depth), calculateSize(), and isDirectory().

‍

Java

```
1// Base class for File System Node (Composite Pattern)
2public abstract class FileSystemNode {
3  // Name of the node
4  private String name;
5  // Map of child nodes
6  private Map<String, FileSystemNode> children;
7  // Timestamp for node creation
8  private LocalDateTime createdAt;
9  // Timestamp for the last modification
10  private LocalDateTime modifiedAt;
11  // Constructor to initialize the node with a name
12  public FileSystemNode(String name) {
13    this.name = name;
14    this.children = new HashMap<>();
15    this.createdAt = LocalDateTime.now();
16    this.modifiedAt = LocalDateTime.now();
17  }
18
19  // Add child node
20  public void addChild(String name, FileSystemNode child) {
21    this.children.put(name, child);
22    this.modifiedAt = LocalDateTime.now();
23  }
24
25  // Check if child exists
26  public boolean hasChild(String name) {
27    return this.children.containsKey(name);
28  }
29
30  // Get child node by name
31  public FileSystemNode getChild(String name) {
32    return this.children.get(name);
33  }
34
35  // Remove child node
36  public boolean removeChild(String name) {
37    if (hasChild(name)) {
38      children.remove(name);
39      return true;
40    }
41    return false;
42  }
43
44  // Abstract methods for node operations
45  public abstract boolean isFile();
46  public abstract void display(int depth);
47
48  // Getters and Setters
49  public String getName() {
50    return name;
51  }
52
53  public Collection<FileSystemNode> getChildren() {
54    return children.values();
55  }
56
57  public LocalDateTime getCreatedAt() {
58    return createdAt;
59  }
60
61  public LocalDateTime getModifiedAt() {
62    return modifiedAt;
63  }
64
65  // Update the modification timestamp
66  protected void updateModifiedTime() {
67    this.modifiedAt = LocalDateTime.now();
68  }
69}
```

‍

b. Create Concrete File and Directory Classes : 

‍

File.java : 

• Represents individual files.

• Properties: content, type, and extension.

Methods: writeContent(String content), readContent(), display(int depth), calculateSize(), and isDirectory().

‍

Java

```
1// File class representing individual files (Leaf)
2public class File extends FileSystemNode {
3  // Content of the file
4  private String content;
5  // File extension
6  private String extension;
7
8  // Constructor for file with name
9  public File(String name) {
10    super(name);
11    this.extension = extractExtension(name);
12  }
13
14  // Extract file extension from name
15  private String extractExtension(String name) {
16    int dotIndex = name.lastIndexOf('.');
17    return (dotIndex > 0) ? name.substring(dotIndex + 1) : "";
18  }
19
20  // Set content of the file
21  public void setContent(String content) {
22    this.content = content;
23    updateModifiedTime();
24  }
25
26  // Get content of the file
27  public String getContent() {
28    return content;
29  }
30
31  @Override
32  public boolean isFile() {
33    return true;
34  }
35
36  @Override
37  public void display(int depth) {
38    // Example: For a file at path "/document/cwa_lld/requirements.txt" at depth 3
39    // indent = "      " (6 spaces: depth 3 * 2 spaces per depth)
40    // Output would be: "      📄 requirements.txt"
41    // For our example, if depth is 3 (meaning this file is at the 3rd level)
42    // Generate indent string of 6 spaces (3*2)
43    String indent = " ".repeat(depth * 2);
44    // Print the file with appropriate indentation and emoji
45    // e.g., "      📄 requirements.txt"
46    System.out.println(indent + "📄 " + getName());
47  }
48}
```

‍

**Directory.java :** 

• Represents directories.

• Contains a list of child nodes (children).

• Methods: addChild(FileSystemNode node), removeChild(FileSystemNode node), getChild(String name), display(int depth), and calculateSize().

‍

Java

```
1// Directory class representing directories (Composite)
2public class Directory extends FileSystemNode {
3  // Constructor for directory with name
4  public Directory(String name) {
5    super(name);
6  }
7
8  @Override
9  public boolean isFile() {
10    return false;
11  }
12
13  @Override
14  public void display(int depth) {
15    // Example: For a directory at path "/document/cwa_lld" at depth 2
16    // indent = "    " (4 spaces: depth 2 * 2 spaces per depth)
17    // Let's say it has 3 child items
18    // Output would be: "    📁 cwa_lld (3 items)"
19    // Then it will recursively display each child with depth 3
20
21
22    // For our example, if depth is 2 (meaning this directory is at the 2nd level)
23    // Generate indent string of 4 spaces (2*2)
24    String indent = " ".repeat(depth * 2);
25
26    // Print the directory name with appropriate indentation, emoji and number of children
27    // e.g., "    📁 cwa_lld (3 items)"
28    System.out.println(indent + "📁 " + getName() + " (" + getChildren().size() + " items)");
29    // Then for each child (let's say we have "design_file_system" directory,
30    // "requirements.txt" file, and "notes.md" file)
31    // We recursively call display with depth+1 (3 in this case)
32    // This will produce:
33    //     "      📁 design_file_system (0 items)" (if empty directory)
34    //     "      📄 requirements.txt"
35    //     "      📄 notes.md"
36    for (FileSystemNode child : getChildren()) {
37      child.display(depth + 1);
38    }
39  }
40}
```

‍

2\. Implement The Core File System Class : 

‍

Java

```
1// Main File System class implementing the trie structure
2public class FileSystem {
3  // Root directory
4  private FileSystemNode root;
5
6  // Constructor to initialize the file system with a root directory
7  public FileSystem() {
8    this.root = new Directory("/");
9  }
10  // path = "/document/cwa_lld/design_file_system"
11  // Checking if path is not null, not empty, and starts with
12  // Validate file path to be non-empty and properly formatted
13  public boolean isValidFilePath(String path) {
14    // Returns true because path meets all criteria
15    return path != null && !path.isEmpty() && path.startsWith("/");
16  }
17
18  // Create a new path
19  // path = "/document/cwa_lld/design_file_system"
20  public boolean createPath(String path) {
21    // Validate path
22    // path is valid, so continue
23    if (!isValidFilePath(path))
24      return false;
25    // Split path into components
26    // pathComponents = ["", "document", "cwa_lld", "design_file_system"]
27    String[] pathComponents = path.split("/");
28    // Start from root
29    // current = root directory "/"
30    FileSystemNode current = root;
31    // Traverse to the parent directory
32    // We need to process: "document" and "cwa_lld" (stopping before the last component)
33    for (int i = 1; i < pathComponents.length - 1; i++) {
34      String component = pathComponents[i];
35      if (component.isEmpty())
36        continue; // Skip empty components
37      // First iteration: component = "document"
38      // Second iteration: component = "cwa_lld"
39      if (!current.hasChild(component)) {
40        // If "document" doesn't exist, create it
41        // If "cwa_lld" doesn't exist, create it
42        FileSystemNode newDir = new Directory(component);
43        current.addChild(component, newDir);
44      }
45      FileSystemNode child = current.getChild(component);
46      if (child.isFile()) {
47        // If "document" or "cwa_lld" is a file, we cannot navigate through it
48        // Return false in that case
49        return false;
50      }
51      // Move to the next level
52      // First iteration: current = "document" directory
53      // Second iteration: current = "cwa_lld" directory
54      current = child;
55    }
56    // Get the last component (file or directory name)
57    // lastComponent = "design_file_system"
58    String lastComponent = pathComponents[pathComponents.length - 1];
59    if (lastComponent.isEmpty())
60      return false;
61    // Check if the component already exists
62    // If "design_file_system" already exists under "cwa_lld", return false
63    if (current.hasChild(lastComponent)) {
64      return false;
65    }
66    // Create new node based on whether it's a file (has extension) or directory
67    // "design_file_system" has no dot, so create as directory
68    FileSystemNode newNode;
69    if (lastComponent.contains(".")) {
70      newNode = new File(lastComponent);
71    } else {
72      newNode = new Directory(lastComponent);
73    }
74    // Add the new node to the parent
75    // Add "design_file_system" directory to "cwa_lld"
76    current.addChild(lastComponent, newNode);
77    return true;
78  }
79
80  // Helper method to get node at path
81  // path = "/document/cwa_lld/design_file_system"
82  private FileSystemNode getNode(String path) {
83    // Check if path is valid
84    // Path is valid, so continue
85    if (!isValidFilePath(path))
86      return null;
87    // For root path
88    // Path is not "/", so skip this
89    if (path.equals("/"))
90      return root;
91    // Split path into components
92    // pathComponents = ["", "document", "cwa_lld", "design_file_system"]
93    String[] pathComponents = path.split("/");
94    // Start from root
95    // current = root directory "/"
96    FileSystemNode current = root;
97    // Traverse through the path
98    // We need to process: "document", "cwa_lld", and "design_file_system"
99    for (int i = 1; i < pathComponents.length; i++) {
100      String component = pathComponents[i];
101      if (component.isEmpty())
102        continue; // Skip empty components
103      // First iteration: component = "document"
104      // Second iteration: component = "cwa_lld"
105      // Third iteration: component = "design_file_system
106      if (!current.hasChild(component)) {
107        // If any component doesn't exist at its level, return null
108        return null;
109      }
110      // Move to the next level
111      // First iteration: current = "document" directory
112      // Second iteration: current = "cwa_lld" directory
113      // Third iteration: current = "design_file_system" directory
114      current = current.getChild(component);
115    }
116    // Return the node found at the path
117    // Returns the "design_file_system" directory node
118    return current;
119  }
120
121  // Delete path
122  public boolean deletePath(String path) {
123    // path = "/document/cwa_lld/design_file_system"
124    // Check if path is valid
125    // Path is valid, so continue
126    if (!isValidFilePath(path))
127      return false;
128    // Can't delete root
129    // Path is not "/", so continue
130    if (path.equals("/"))
131      return false;
132    // Get parent path
133    // parentPath = "/document/cwa_lld"
134    String parentPath = getParentPath(path);
135    // Get the parent node
136    // parent = "cwa_lld" directory node
137    FileSystemNode parent = getNode(parentPath);
138    // If parent doesn't exist or is a file, can't delete
139    // Assuming parent exists and is a directory, continue
140    if (parent == null || parent.isFile())
141      return false;
142    // Get the last component of the path
143    // lastComponent = "design_file_system"
144    String lastComponent = path.substring(path.lastIndexOf('/') + 1);
145    // Check if the component exists
146    // If "design_file_system" doesn't exist under "cwa_lld", return false
147    if (!parent.hasChild(lastComponent)) {
148      return false;
149    }
150    // Remove the child from the parent
151    // Remove "design_file_system" from "cwa_lld"
152    return parent.removeChild(lastComponent);
153  }
154
155  // Helper method to get parent path
156  private String getParentPath(String path) {
157    // path = "/document/cwa_lld/design_file_system"
158    // Find the last slash
159    // lastSlash = 19 (position of last slash before "design_file_system")
160    int lastSlash = path.lastIndexOf('/');
161    // If there's no slash or only the root slash, return root
162    // lastSlash is 19, which is > 0, so continue
163    if (lastSlash <= 0) {
164      return "/";
165    }
166    // Return the substring up to the last slash
167    // Returns "/document/cwa_lld"
168    return path.substring(0, lastSlash);
169  }
170
171  // Display the entire file system structure
172  public void display() {
173    root.display(0);
174  }
175
176  // Set content for file
177  public boolean setFileContent(String path, String content) {
178    FileSystemNode node = getNode(path);
179    if (node == null || !node.isFile())
180      return false;
181    File file = (File) node;
182    file.setContent(content);
183    return true;
184  }
185
186  // Get content from file
187  public String getFileContent(String path) {
188    FileSystemNode node = getNode(path);
189    if (node == null || !node.isFile())
190      return null;
191    File file = (File) node;
192    return file.getContent();
193  }
194}
```

‍

3\. Write the Main Method / Client Code to run the File System : 

‍

Java

```
1// Client code to test the file system
2public class FileSystemClient {
3  public static void main(String[] args) {
4    // Create a new file system instance
5    FileSystem fs = new FileSystem();
6    // Create a scanner to handle user input
7    Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
8    boolean isRunning = true; // Flag to control the program loop
9    // Display instructions for the user
10    System.out.println("File System Manager - Commands:");
11    System.out.println("1. create <path> - Create a new path");
12    System.out.println("2. write <path> <content> - Write content to a file");
13    System.out.println("3. read <path> - Read content from a file");
14    System.out.println("4. delete <path> - Delete a path");
15    System.out.println("5. display - Show the entire file system structure");
16    System.out.println("6. exit - Exit the program");
17    // Main program loop to process commands
18    while (isRunning) {
19      System.out.print("
20Enter command: ");
21      String input = scanner.nextLine().trim(); // Read and trim user input
22      String[] parts =
23          input.split("\\s+", 3); // Split input into command, path, and possibly content
24      if (parts.length == 0)
25        continue; // Skip empty input
26      String command = parts[0].toLowerCase(); // Get the command
27      try {
28        switch (command) {
29          case "create":
30            // Create a new path
31            if (parts.length >= 2) {
32              String path = parts[1]; // Extract path
33              boolean isCreated = fs.createPath(path); // Attempt to create the path
34              System.out.println(isCreated ? "Path created successfully" : "Failed to create path");
35            } else {
36              System.out.println("Usage: create <path>");
37            }
38            break;
39          case "write":
40            // Write content to a file
41            if (parts.length >= 3) {
42              String path = parts[1]; // Extract path
43              String content = parts[2]; // Extract content
44              boolean isWritten = fs.setFileContent(path, content); // Attempt to write content
45              System.out.println(
46                  isWritten ? "Content written successfully" : "Failed to write content");
47            } else {
48              System.out.println("Usage: write <path> <content>");
49            }
50            break;
51          case "read":
52            // Read content from a file
53            if (parts.length >= 2) {
54              String path = parts[1]; // Extract path
55              String content = fs.getFileContent(path); // Attempt to read content
56              if (content != null) {
57                System.out.println("Content: " + content);
58              } else {
59                System.out.println("Failed to read content");
60              }
61            } else {
62              System.out.println("Usage: read <path>");
63            }
64            break;
65          case "delete":
66            // Delete a specific path from the file system
67            if (parts.length >= 2) {
68              String path = parts[1]; // Extract path
69              boolean isDeleted = fs.deletePath(path); // Attempt to delete the path
70              System.out.println(isDeleted ? "Path deleted successfully" : "Failed to delete path");
71            } else {
72              System.out.println("Usage: delete <path>");
73            }
74            break;
75          case "display":
76            // Display the entire file system structure
77            System.out.println("
78File System Structure:");
79            fs.display();
80            break;
81          case "exit":
82            // Exit the program
83            isRunning = false;
84            System.out.println("Exiting...");
85            break;
86          default:
87            // Handle unknown commands
88            System.out.println(
89                "Unknown command. Available commands: create, write, read, delete, display, exit");
90        }
91      } catch (Exception e) {
92        // Handle general exceptions
93        System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage());
94      }
95    }
96    // Close the scanner to release system resources
97    scanner.close();
98  }
99}
```

‍

Interviewer: Looks good. What makes your approach effective?

‍

Candidate: Here are the key strengths of my approach for the File System:

1.  Composite Pattern Implementation:

I've implemented the Composite pattern to represent the file system hierarchy, where both files and directories share a common interface. This allows uniform treatment of individual files and directories while supporting recursive operations.

‍

2\. Single Responsibility Principle:

Each class has a distinct responsibility. The File class represents individual files, Directory manages collections of files and subdirectories, and the overall file system operations are handled cohesively without violating SRP.

‍

3\. Extensibility:

The design allows easy addition of new file types or directory behaviors without modifying existing code. The common interface ensures that new components integrate seamlessly into the existing structure.

‍

4\. Maintainability:

The separation of concerns makes the system easy to manage and extend. Operations like adding, removing, or searching files and directories are encapsulated within relevant classes, reducing code complexity.

‍

5\. Real-world Modeling:

The implementation closely resembles an actual file system, where directories can contain files or other directories, and operations like traversal, searching, and organization follow a natural hierarchical structure.

‍

## Conclusion : 

This low-level design for the File System demonstrates a well-structured, scalable, and extensible architecture. It emphasizes modularity by clearly defining components such as File, Directory, and the overall file system structure. The design leverages the Composite pattern to enable seamless management of files and directories, ensuring a flexible and hierarchical organization.

‍

In an interview setting, presenting this design highlights your ability to build robust and adaptable solutions, showcasing your expertise in applying design patterns, best practices, and architectural principles to real-world scenarios.\n\n---\n\n## Source: https://codewitharyan.com/tech-blogs/design-logging-system\n\n# Design Logging System
