2.) Factory Pattern for Different Split Types : 

Split Interface : 

‍

Java

```
1// Interface defining the behavior for all types of splits
2	interface Split {
3	    /**
4	     - Calculates the split for the given amount among participants based on specific split details.
5	     - 
6	     - @param amount        The total amount to split.
7	     - @param participants  The list of users participating in the split.
8	     - @param splitDetails  Additional details required for the specific split type.
9	     - @return A map where the key is the User and the value is the amount they owe.
10	     */
11	    Map<User, Double> calculateSplit(double amount, List<User> participants, Map<String, Object> splitDetails);
12}
```

‍

• Concrete Split Classes : 

Equal Split : 

‍

Java

```
1// Implementation of the Split interface for equal split
2	public class EqualSplit implements Split {
3	    @Override
4	    public Map<User, Double> calculateSplit(double amount, List<User> participants, Map<String, Object> splitDetails) {
5	        double amountPerPerson = amount / participants.size(); // Divide the amount equally among all participants
6	        Map<User, Double> splits = new HashMap<>(); // Map to hold the calculated split
7	        for (User user : participants) {
8	            splits.put(user, amountPerPerson); // Assign each participant the equal amount
9	        }
10	        return splits;
11	    }
12}
```

‍

• Percentage Split : 

‍

Java

```
1// Implementation of the Split interface for percentage-based split
2	public class PercentageSplit implements Split {
3	    @Override
4	    public Map<User, Double> calculateSplit(double amount, List<User> participants, Map<String, Object> splitDetails) {
5	        // Retrieve the percentage allocation for each participant from the split details
6	        Map<User, Double> percentages = (Map<User, Double>) splitDetails.get("percentages");
7	        Map<User, Double> splits = new HashMap<>(); // Map to hold the calculated split
8
9
10	        for (User user : participants) {
11	            double percentage = percentages.getOrDefault(user, 0.0); // Get the percentage for the user
12	            splits.put(user, amount * percentage / 100.0); // Calculate the share based on the percentage
13	        }
14	        return splits;
15	    }
16	}
```

‍

• Split Factory :

‍

Java

```
1// Factory class for creating instances of different Split types
2	public class SplitFactory {
3	    /**
4	     - Factory method to create a Split instance based on the specified split type.
5	     - 	     - @param splitType The type of split to create ("EQUAL", "PERCENTAGE", "EXACT").
6	     - @return An instance of the corresponding Split implementation.
7	     - @throws IllegalArgumentException if the split type is unknown.
8	     */
9	    public static Split createSplit(String splitType) {
10	        switch (splitType) {
11	            case "EQUAL":
12	                return new EqualSplit(); // Return an EqualSplit instance
13	            case "PERCENTAGE":
14	                return new PercentageSplit(); // Return a PercentageSplit instance
15	             default:
16	                // Throw an exception if the split type is invalid
17	            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Unknown split type: " + splitType);
18	        }
19	    }
20}
```

‍

3.) Observer Pattern for Balance Updates (Not a core part of the Problem): 

Observer Interface : 

‍

Java

```
1// Interface for observers that need to be notified of expense updates.
2	interface ExpenseObserver {
3	    // Called when a new expense is added to the system.
4	    void onExpenseAdded(Expense expense);
5	    
6	    // Called when an expense is updated in the system.
7	    void onExpenseUpdated(Expense expense);
8}
```

‍

• Interface for Observable Objects : 

‍

Java

```
1// Interface for objects that can be observed for expense updates.
2	interface ExpenseSubject {
3	    // Adds an observer to the notification list.
4	    void addObserver(ExpenseObserver observer);
5	    
6	    // Removes an observer from the notification list.
7	    void removeObserver(ExpenseObserver observer);
8	    
9	    // Notifies all observers about a new expense.
10	    void notifyExpenseAdded(Expense expense);
11	    
12	    // Notifies all observers about an updated expense.
13	    void notifyExpenseUpdated(Expense expense);
14}
```

‍

• Concrete Implementation of Expense Subject : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1744093312100-Frame-246-\(7\).png)

‍

Java

```
1// Concrete implementation of ExpenseSubject that manages expense notifications.
2	public class ExpenseManager implements ExpenseSubject {
3	    private List<ExpenseObserver> observers = new ArrayList<>();
4	    private List<Expense> expenses = new ArrayList<>();
5	    
6	    @Override
7	    public void addObserver(ExpenseObserver observer) {
8	        observers.add(observer);
9	    }
10	    
11	    @Override
12	    public void removeObserver(ExpenseObserver observer) {
13	        observers.remove(observer);
14	    }
15	    
16	    @Override
17	    public void notifyExpenseAdded(Expense expense) {
18	        for (ExpenseObserver observer : observers) observer.onExpenseAdded(expense);
19	    }
20	    
21	    @Override
22	    public void notifyExpenseUpdated(Expense expense) {
23	        for (ExpenseObserver observer : observers) observer.onExpenseUpdated(expense);
24	    }
25	    
26	    // Adds a new expense to the system and notifies observers.
27	    public void addExpense(Expense expense) {
28	        expenses.add(expense);
29	        notifyExpenseAdded(expense);
30	    }
31	    
32	    // Updates an existing expense and notifies observers.
33	    public void updateExpense(Expense expense) {
34	    // Find and replace the expense with the same ID in the list
35	    for (int i = 0; i < expenses.size(); i++) {
36	        if (expenses.get(i).getId().equals(expense.getId())) { // Check if the IDs match
37	            expenses.set(i, expense); // Replace the old expense with the updated one
38	            notifyExpenseUpdated(expense); // Notify all observers about the update
39	            return; // Exit the method after updating
40	        }
41	    }
42	    // Throw an exception if the expense with the given ID is not found
43	    throw new IllegalArgumentException("Expense with ID " + expense.getId() + " not found.");
44	}
45	    
46	    // Retrieves all expenses in the system.
47	    public List<Expense> getAllExpenses() {
48	        return new ArrayList<>(expenses);
49	    }
50	}
```

‍

4.) Balance Sheet Algorithm :

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1744093519101-Frame-246-\(8\).png)

‍

Java

```
1/**
2 - Balance sheet that observes expenses and calculates balances.
3 */
4public class BalanceSheet implements ExpenseObserver {
5  // Stores the net balance between pairs of users
6  private Map<UserPair, Double> balances = new HashMap<>();
7
8  @Override
9  public void onExpenseAdded(Expense expense) {
10    // Update balances when a new expense is added
11    updateBalances(expense);
12  }
13
14  @Override
15  public void onExpenseUpdated(Expense expense) {
16    // To simplify logic, just update balances without reversing the previous state
17    updateBalances(expense);
18  }
19
20  /**
21   - Updates the balances based on a new or updated expense.
22   - Each participant's share is added to their balance with the payer.
23   -    - @param expense The expense to process.
24   */
25  private void updateBalances(Expense expense) {
26    User payer = expense.getPayer(); // User who paid for the expense
27    Map<User, Double> shares = expense.getShares(); // Participants and their shares
28    for (Map.Entry<User, Double> entry : shares.entrySet()) {
29      User participant = entry.getKey(); // A participant in the expense
30      Double amount = entry.getValue(); // The amount owed by the participant
31      if (!participant.equals(payer)) {
32        // Create a unique pair for the payer and participant
33        UserPair userPair = new UserPair(participant, payer);
34        // Update the balance (add the amount owed by the participant)
35        Double currentBalance = balances.getOrDefault(userPair, 0.0);
36        balances.put(userPair, currentBalance + amount);
37      }
38    }
39  }
40
41  /**
42   - Gets the net balance between two users.
43   -    - @param user1 First user.
44   - @param user2 Second user.
45   - @return The amount user1 owes user2 (negative if user2 owes user1).
46   */
47  public double getBalance(User user1, User user2) {
48    // Represent the balance both ways (user1 -> user2 and user2 -> user1)
49    UserPair pair1 = new UserPair(user1, user2);
50    UserPair pair2 = new UserPair(user2, user1);
51    // Retrieve balances in both directions and calculate the net
52    double balance1 = balances.getOrDefault(pair1, 0.0);
53    double balance2 = balances.getOrDefault(pair2, 0.0);
54    return balance1 - balance2;
55  }
56
57  /**
58   - Calculates the total balance for a single user.
59   - The balance is negative if the user owes money and positive if they are owed money.
60   -    - @param user The user to calculate the balance for.
61   - @return The total balance for the user.
62   */
63  public double getTotalBalance(User user) {
64    double total = 0.0
65        // Iterate through all user pairs and calculate the total
66        for (Map.Entry<UserPair, Double> entry : balances.entrySet()) {
67      UserPair pair = entry.getKey();
68      double amount = entry.getValue();
69      if (pair.getUser1().equals(user)) {
70        total -= amount; // Money owed by the user (He has to give money)
71      } else if (pair.getUser2().equals(user)) {
72        total += amount; // Money owed to the user (He has to take money)
73      }
74    }
75    return total;
76  }
77
78  /**
79   - Simplifies the balances into a list of transactions to settle all debts.
80   - Simple and Straightforward implementation of the problem
81   - @return List of transactions needed to settle all debts.
82   */
83  public List<Transaction> getSimplifiedSettlements() {
84    // Step 1: Calculate net balances for each user
85    Map<User, Double> netBalances = new HashMap<>();
86    for (Map.Entry<UserPair, Double> entry : balances.entrySet()) {
87      UserPair pair = entry.getKey();
88      double amount = entry.getValue();
89
90      User debtor = pair.getUser1(); // User who owes money (Who has to pay)
91      User creditor = pair.getUser2(); // User who is owed money (Who gets the money)
92      // Update the net balances for debtor and creditor
93      netBalances.put(debtor, netBalances.getOrDefault(debtor, 0.0) - amount);
94      netBalances.put(creditor, netBalances.getOrDefault(creditor, 0.0) + amount);
95    }
96
97    // Step 2: Separate users into debtors and creditors
98    List<User> debtors = new ArrayList<>();
99    List<User> creditors = new ArrayList<>();
100    for (Map.Entry<User, Double> entry : netBalances.entrySet()) {
101      User user = entry.getKey();
102      double balance = entry.getValue();
103      if (balance < 0) {
104        debtors.add(user); // Users who owe money (Who has to pay)
105      } else if (balance > 0) {
106        creditors.add(user); // Users who are owed money (Who gets the money back)
107      }
108    }
109
110    // Step 3: Match debtors and creditors to create transactions
111    List<Transaction> transactions = new ArrayList<>();
112    int debtorIndex = 0;
113    int creditorIndex = 0;
114
115    while (debtorIndex < debtors.size() && creditorIndex < creditors.size()) {
116      User debtor = debtors.get(debtorIndex);
117      User creditor = creditors.get(creditorIndex);
118      double debtorBalance = netBalances.get(debtor);
119      double creditorBalance = netBalances.get(creditor);
120
121      // Determine the transfer amount as the smaller of the two balances
122      double transferAmount = Math.min(Math.abs(debtorBalance), creditorBalance);
123
124      // Create a transaction for the transfer amount
125      transactions.add(new Transaction(debtor, creditor, transferAmount));
126
127      // Update net balances after the transaction
128      netBalances.put(debtor, debtorBalance + transferAmount);
129      netBalances.put(creditor, creditorBalance - transferAmount);
130
131      // Move to the next debtor or creditor if their balance is settled
132      if (Math.abs(netBalances.get(debtor)) < 0.001) {
133        debtorIndex++;
134      }
135      if (Math.abs(netBalances.get(creditor)) < 0.001) {
136        creditorIndex++;
137      }
138    }
139    return transactions;
140  }
141
142  /**
143   - Calculates the minimum number of transactions needed to settle all balances.
144   - Uses backtracking approach to find the solution.
145   -    - @return minimum count needed to settle all debts.
146   */
147  public int getSubOptimalMinimumSettlements() {
148    // Step 1: Calculate net balances for each user
149    Map<User, Double> netBalances = new HashMap<>();
150    for (Map.Entry<UserPair, Double> entry : balances.entrySet()) {
151      UserPair pair = entry.getKey();
152      double amount = entry.getValue();
153      User debtor = pair.getUser1(); // The user who owes money
154      User creditor = pair.getUser2(); // The user who is owed money
155
156      // Update the net balance of each user
157      netBalances.put(debtor, netBalances.getOrDefault(debtor, 0.0) - amount);
158      netBalances.put(creditor, netBalances.getOrDefault(creditor, 0.0) + amount);
159    }
160    List<Double> creditList = new ArrayList<>();
161    for (Map.Entry<User, Double> entry : netBalances.entrySet()) {
162      if (Math.abs(entry.getValue()) > 0.001) { // Ignore near-zero balances
163        allUsers.add(entry.getKey()); // Store the user
164        creditList.add(entry.getValue()); // Store the net balance
165      }
166    }
167    int n = creditList.size(); // Total number of users with non-zero balance
168    return subOptimalDfs(0, creditList, n); // Call DFS to compute the minimum transactions
169  }
170
171  /**
172   - Recursively finds the minimum number of transactions required to settle debts.
173   - Uses a greedy approach by settling the current user's balance with future users.
174   -    - @param currentUserIndex Index of the user whose balance needs to be settled.
175   - @param creditList List of net balances for all users.
176   - @param n Number of users with non-zero balances.
177   - @return Minimum transactions required to settle all debts.
178   */
179  private int subOptimalDfs(int currentUserIndex, List<Double> creditList, int n) {
180    // Skip already settled users (those with zero balance)
181    while (currentUserIndex < n && creditList.get(currentUserIndex) == 0) {
182      currentUserIndex++;
183    }
184
185    // Base case: If all users have zero balance, no further transactions are needed
186    if (currentUserIndex == n)
187      return 0;
188    int cost = Integer.MAX_VALUE; // Variable to track the minimum number of transactions
189    // Try to settle currentUserBalance with a future user having an opposite balance
190    for (int nextIndex = currentUserIndex + 1; nextIndex < n; nextIndex++) {
191      // Ensure we only settle debts between users with opposite balances
192      if (creditList.get(nextIndex) * creditList.get(currentUserIndex) < 0) {
193        // Transfer current user's balance to the next valid user
194        creditList.set(nextIndex, creditList.get(nextIndex) + creditList.get(currentUserIndex));
195        // Recursively settle the remaining balances
196        cost = Math.min(cost, 1 + subOptimalDfs(currentUserIndex + 1, creditList, n));
197
198        // Backtrack: Undo the transaction to explore other possibilities
199        creditList.set(nextIndex, creditList.get(nextIndex) - creditList.get(currentUserIndex));
200      }
201    }
202    return cost; // Return the minimum transactions required
203  }
```

‍

### Complexity Analysis of Backtracking Approach : 

Let n be the length of transactions.

‍

> Time complexity: O((n−1)!) :

‍

• In dfs(0), there exists a maximum of n−1 persons as possible nxt, each of which leads to a recursive call to dfs(1). Therefore, we have

```
dfs(0)=(n−1)⋅dfs(1)=(n−1)⋅((n−2)⋅dfs(2))=(n−1)⋅(n−2)⋅((n−3)⋅dfs(3))=...=(n−1)!⋅dfs(n−1)
```

• dfs(n - 1) can be determined in O(1) time.

‍

> Space complexity: O(n) :

‍

• Both balance\_map and balance\_list possess at most n net balances.

‍

• The space complexity of a recursive call relies on the maximum depth of the recursive call stack, which is equal to n. As each recursive call increments cur by 1, and each level consumes a constant amount of space.

‍

### Dynamic Programming Approach : 

Imagine there are 8 people in which we can make 4 groups of size 2 each, which will have a sum of 0. I.e Each group will have a 

Sum of 0, then we only need 4 transactions thus what it ultimately means is that In General, If we divide n people into m groups 

Whose balance sum is 0, then it only takes n – m transactions. Each group we create saves us one transaction.

‍

1.) Let's take an example of 8 People divided in 4 groups each of size 2 : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1744170072751-Frame-248-\(1\).png)

‍

For the above Example, Since the Number of people are 8 and the number of groups is 4, based on our algorithm, 

The Minimum number of transactions required to complete the settlement will be 8 – 4 which is 4 transactions.

‍

2.) Let's take another example of 8 People divided in 3 groups of size 3,3,2 :

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1744170086671-Frame-249.png)

‍

In this case, Since the Number of people are 8 and the number of groups is 3, based on our algorithm, 

The Minimum number of transactions required to complete the settlement will be 8 – 3 which is 5 transactions.

```
/**
	     - Calculates the minimum number of transactions needed to settle all balances.
	     - Uses dynamic programming to find the optimal solution.
	     - 
	     - @return minimum count needed to settle all debts.
	     */
```

‍

Let's Showcase the example of 8 People and 3 Groups here for better understanding : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1744094021940-Frame-246-\(9\).png)

‍

Consider a scenario where we have N = 8 ndividuals divided into M = 3 groups. Let the sizes of these groups be Z₁, Z₂, and Z₃, representing the number of people in each group. Thus, the total number of people can be expressed as:

```
Z₁ + Z₂ + Z₃ = N
```

To settle transactions within each group efficiently, the number of required transactions per group follows the pattern Z(group\_number) - 1. This is because each individual can settle with another person, who then continues the process until all members in the group are accounted for.

‍

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1744094094147-Frame-246-\(10\).png)

Total Number of Transactions : 

The total number of transactions required across all groups is calculated as:

```
(Z1−1)+(Z2−1)+(Z3−1)
```

Rearranging the equation:

```
(Z1+Z2+Z3)−(1+1+1)=N−M
```

Thus, the total number of transactions required for complete settlement among N individuals divided into M groups is:

```
N−M
```

‍

1.) Map Balances to Net Balances : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1744170146985-Frame-249-\(1\).png)

‍

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1744170200092-Frame-248-\(2\).png)

‍

2.) Credit List and bitmask Representation : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1744170275935-Frame-249-\(2\).png)

‍

3.) Finding the Optimal Number of Transactions :  

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1744170404385-Frame-249-\(3\).png)

‍

Java

```
1public int getOptimalMinimumSettlements() {
2	        // Step 1: Calculate net balances for each user
3	        Map<User, Double> netBalances = new HashMap<>();
4	        for (Map.Entry<UserPair, Double> entry : balances.entrySet()) {
5	            UserPair pair = entry.getKey();
6	            double amount = entry.getValue();
7	            User debtor = pair.getUser1(); // The user who owes money
8	            User creditor = pair.getUser2(); // The user who is owed money
9	            // Update the net balance of each user
10	            netBalances.put(debtor, netBalances.getOrDefault(debtor, 0.0) - amount);
11	            netBalances.put(creditor, netBalances.getOrDefault(creditor, 0.0) + amount);
12	        }
13	        List<Double> creditList = new ArrayList<>();
14
15
16	        for (Map.Entry<User, Double> entry : netBalances.entrySet()) {
17	            if (Math.abs(entry.getValue()) > 0.001) { // Ignore near-zero balances
18	                creditList.add(entry.getValue()); // Store the net balance
19	            }
20	        }
21	        // Step 3: Apply Dynamic Programming to find the minimum transactions required
22	        int n = creditList.size(); // Number of users with non-zero balance
23	        int[] dp = new int[1 << n]; // DP array for memoization
24	        Arrays.fill(dp, -1);
25	        dp[0] = 0; // Base case: No users left means zero transactions
26
27
28	        // Find the maximum number of fully settled subgroups using DFS + DP
29	        int maxSubGroups = dfs((1 << n) - 1, dp, creditList);
30	        
31	        // Minimum transactions needed = Total users - Maximum fully settled groups
32	        return n - maxSubGroups;
33	    }
34	    /**
35	     - Helper method to calculate the sum of balances in a subset, given by a bitmask.
36	     - 
37	     - @param values The list of credit balances.
38	     - @param mask The bitmask representing a subset of users.
39	     - @return The sum of balances in the subset.
40	     */
41	    private double sumOfMask(List<Double> values, int mask) {
42	        double sum = 0;
43	        for (int i = 0; i < values.size(); i++) {
44	            if ((mask & (1 << i)) != 0) { // Check if the i-th bit is set in the mask
45	                sum += values.get(i); // Add the corresponding balance to the sum
46	            }
47	        }
48	        return sum;
49	    }
50	    /**
51	     - DFS with memoization to determine the maximum number of balanced subgroups.
52	     - 
53	     - @param mask Bitmask representing the remaining users.
54	     - @param dp Memoization array for storing computed results.
55	     - @param creditList List of net balances for each user.
56	     - @return The maximum number of fully settled subgroups.
57	     */
58	    private int dfs(int mask, int[] dp, List<Double> creditList) {
59	        if (mask == 0) // Base case: No users left to process
60	            return 0;
61	        if (dp[mask] != -1) // Return cached result if already computed
62	            return dp[mask];
63	        int maxSubGroups = 0;
64	        int n = creditList.size();
65	        // Try all possible subsets (submasks) of the current mask
66	        for (int submask = 1; submask < (1 << n); submask++) {
67	            // Check if submask is a subset of mask and sums to zero (i.e., can be settled)
68	            if ((submask & mask) == submask && Math.abs(sumOfMask(creditList, submask)) < 0.001) {
69	                // If a subset can be settled, find the remaining subgroups recursively
70	                maxSubGroups = Math.max(maxSubGroups, 1 + dfs(mask ^ submask, dp, creditList));
71	            }
72	        }
73	        dp[mask] = maxSubGroups; // Store result in memoization table
74	        return maxSubGroups;
75	    }
76	}
```

‍

### Complexity Analysis of the DP Approach : 

Let n be the length of transactions.

‍

> Time complexity: O(n⋅2^n) :

‍

• We build memo, an array of size O(2^n) as memory, equal to the number of possible states. Each state is computed with a traverse through balance\_list, which takes O(n) time.

‍

> Space complexity: O(2^n) :

‍

• The length of memo is 2^n.

‍

• The space complexity of a recursive call depends on the maximum depth of the recursive call stack, which is n. As each recursive call removes one set bit from total\_mask. Therefore, at most O(n) levels of recursion will be created, and each level consumes a constant amount of space.

‍

 5.) Client Code to run the Algorithm :

‍

Java

```
1// Main class to demonstrate the system
2public class SplitwiseSystem {
3    public static void main(String[] args) {
4        // Create users
5        User alice = new User("u1", "Alice", "alice@example.com");
6        User bob = new User("u2", "Bob", "bob@example.com");
7        User charlie = new User("u3", "Charlie", "charlie@example.com");
8       
9        // Create expense manager and balance sheet
10        ExpenseManager expenseManager = new ExpenseManager();
11        BalanceSheet balanceSheet = new BalanceSheet();
12       
13        // Register the balance sheet as an observer
14        expenseManager.addObserver(balanceSheet);
15       
16        // Create and add users to a list for expenses
17        List<User> participants = new ArrayList<>();
18        participants.add(alice);
19        participants.add(bob);
20        participants.add(charlie);
21       
22        // Alice pays for dinner - Create an equal split expense
23        Split equalSplit = SplitFactory.createSplit("EQUAL");
24        Map<String, Object> splitDetails = new HashMap<>();
25        Map<User, Double> dinnerShares = equalSplit.calculateSplit(60.0, participants, splitDetails);
26       
27        Expense dinnerExpense = new Expense("e1", "Dinner", 60.0, alice, participants, dinnerShares);
28        
29        // Add the expense to the expense manager which will notify observers
30        expenseManager.addExpense(dinnerExpense);
31       
32        // Bob pays for movie tickets - Create a percentage split expense
33        Map<String, Object> percentageSplitDetails = new HashMap<>();
34        Map<User, Double> percentages = new HashMap<>();
35        percentages.put(alice, 40.0);
36        percentages.put(bob, 30.0);
37        percentages.put(charlie, 30.0);
38        percentageSplitDetails.put("percentages", percentages);
39       
40        Split percentageSplit = SplitFactory.createSplit("PERCENTAGE");
41        Map<User, Double> movieShares = percentageSplit.calculateSplit(45.0, participants, percentageSplitDetails);
42       
43        Expense movieExpense = new Expense("e2", "Movie", 45.0, bob, participants, movieShares);
44        
45        // Add the movie expense to the expense manager
46        expenseManager.addExpense(movieExpense);
47       
48        // Get individual balances
49        System.out.println("Individual balances:");
50        System.out.println("Alice's total balance: $" + balanceSheet.getTotalBalance(alice));
51        System.out.println("Bob's total balance: $" + balanceSheet.getTotalBalance(bob));
52        System.out.println("Charlie's total balance: $" + balanceSheet.getTotalBalance(charlie));
53        
54        // Print specific balances between users
55        System.out.println("
56Pairwise balances:");
57        System.out.println("Alice and Bob: $" + balanceSheet.getBalance(alice, bob));
58        System.out.println("Alice and Charlie: $" + balanceSheet.getBalance(alice, charlie));
59        System.out.println("Bob and Charlie: $" + balanceSheet.getBalance(bob, charlie));
60       
61        // Get the simplified settlements
62        List<Transaction> settlements = balanceSheet.getSimplifiedSettlements();
63        
64        // Display optimal minimum settlements (DP algorithm)
65        System.out.println("
66=== OPTIMAL MINIMUM SETTLEMENTS ===");
67        int optimalSettlements = balanceSheet.getOptimalMinimumSettlements();
68        System.out.println(optimalSettlements);
69     
70        // Print the settlements
71        System.out.println("
72Simplified settlements:");
73        for (Transaction transaction : settlements) {
74            System.out.println(transaction.getFrom().getName() + " pays " + 
75                              transaction.getTo().getName() + " $" + 
76                              transaction.getAmount());
77        }
78    }
79}
```

‍

Interviewer: Looks good. What makes your approach effective?

‍

Candidate: Here are the key strengths of my approach to the Splitwise solution:

• Extensibility: The design patterns I've used, particularly the Factory Pattern for split types, make it easy to add new ways of splitting expenses (like equal, percentage, and potentially custom splits) without modifying existing code.

‍

• Maintainability: By separating concerns into distinct components (users, expenses, splits, balances), the codebase remains organized and easy to understand, reducing technical debt over time.

‍

• Decoupling: The Observer Pattern ensures that components like the BalanceSheet stay updated without tight coupling to the ExpenseManager, making the system more robust to changes.

‍

• Optimized Settlements: The BalanceSheet algorithm efficiently calculates the minimum number of transactions needed to settle debts, simplifying the user experience considerably.

‍

Extensibility : 

1.) Addition of Group based Interfaces : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1744094356105-image.png)

‍

We can extend our solution to include support for Group based functionalities as well, where multiple users can create a group and share the expenses on to the group which can be split between the users of the group based on the type of split the group users want to achieve 

‍

Here are the steps to achieve the Same : 

‍

Create a New group Class : 

‍

Java

```
1public class Group {
2	    private String id;                 // Unique identifier for the group
3	    private String name;               // Name of the group
4	    private String description;        // Description of the group
5	    private Set<User> members;         // Members of the group
6	    private List<Expense> expenses;    // Expenses associated with the group
7	    
8	    // Constructor to initialize Group attributes
9	    public Group(String id, String name, String description) {
10	        this.id = id;
11	        this.name = name;
12	        this.description = description;
13	        this.members = new HashSet<>();
14	        this.expenses = new ArrayList<>();
15	    }
16	    
17	    // Getters and setters
18	    public String getId() { return id; }
19	    public String getName() { return name; }
20	    public String getDescription() { return description; }
21	    public Set<User> getMembers() { return Collections.unmodifiableSet(members); }
22	    public List<Expense> getExpenses() { return Collections.unmodifiableList(expenses); }
23	    
24	    // Method to add a member to the group
25	    public void addMember(User user) {
26	        members.add(user);
27	    }
28	    
29	    // Method to remove a member from the group
30	    public void removeMember(User user) {
31	        members.remove(user);
32	    }
33	    
34	    // Method to add an expense to the group
35	    public void addExpense(Expense expense) {
36	        expenses.add(expense);
37	    }
38	    
39	    // Method to check if a user is a member of the group
40	    public boolean isMember(User user) {
41	        return members.contains(user);
42	    }
43	    
44	    // Override equals() to compare groups by ID
45	    @Override
46	    public boolean equals(Object o) {
47	        if (this == o) return true;
48	        if (!(o instanceof Group)) return false;
49	        Group group = (Group) o;
50	        return id.equals(group.id);
51	    }
52	    
53	    // Override hashCode() to generate hash based on group ID
54	    @Override
55	    public int hashCode() {
56	        return Objects.hash(id);
57	    }
58	}
59‍
60
612.) Modify The Expense Class : 
62
63/**
64	 - Modified Expense class to include group information
65	 */
66	public class Expense {
67	    private String id;                     // id of the expense
68	    private String description;           // Description of the expense
69	    private double amount;                // Total amount of the expense
70	    private User payer;                   // User who paid the expense
71	    private List<User> participants;      // List of users sharing the expense
72	    private Map<User, Double> shares;     // Split amounts owed by each participant
73	    private Group group;                 // Group associated with the expense (can be null for non-group expenses)
74	    
75	    // Constructor for group expenses
76	    public Expense(String id, String description, double amount, User payer, 
77	                   List<User> participants, Map<User, Double> shares, Group group) {
78	        this.id = id;
79	        this.description = description;
80	        this.amount = amount;
81	        this.payer = payer;
82	        this.participants = participants;
83	        this.shares = shares;
84	        this.group = group;
85	    }
86	    
87	    // Constructor for non-group expenses (compatibility with existing code)
88	    public Expense(String id, String description, double amount, User payer, 
89	                  List<User> participants, Map<User, Double> shares) {
90	        this(id, description, amount, payer, participants, shares, null);
91	    }
92	    
93	    // Getters for the expense attributes
94	    public Group getGroup() { return group; }
95	    public boolean isGroupExpense() { return group != null; }
96
97
98	    // Rest code remains the same 
99	}
100‍
101
1023\. Create a New Group Manager Class : 
103
104public class GroupManager {
105	    private Map<String, Group> groups;
106	    private ExpenseManager expenseManager;
107	    private BalanceSheet balanceSheet;
108	    
109	    public GroupManager(ExpenseManager expenseManager, BalanceSheet balanceSheet) {
110	        this.groups = new HashMap<>();
111	        this.expenseManager = expenseManager;
112	        this.balanceSheet = balanceSheet;
113	    }
114	    
115	    // Create a new group
116	    public Group createGroup(String id, String name, String description) {
117	        Group group = new Group(id, name, description);
118	        groups.put(id, group);
119	        return group;
120	    }
121	    
122	    // Add an expense to a group
123	    public void addExpenseToGroup(Expense expense, Group group) {
124	        // Validate that all participants are group members
125	        for (User participant : expense.getParticipants()) {
126	            if (!group.isMember(participant)) {
127	                throw new IllegalArgumentException("User " + participant.getName() + " is not a member of group " + group.getName());
128	            }
129	        }
130	        
131	        // Validate that the payer is a group member
132	        if (!group.isMember(expense.getPayer())) {
133	            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Payer " + expense.getPayer().getName() + " is not a member of group " + group.getName());
134	        }
135	        
136	        // Add the expense to both the group and the expense manager
137	        group.addExpense(expense);
138	        expenseManager.addExpense(expense);
139	    }
140	    
141	      
142	    // Getters and Setters
143	    public Group getGroup(String id) {
144	        return groups.get(id);
145	    }
146	    
147	    public Collection<Group> getAllGroups() {
148	        return Collections.unmodifiableCollection(groups.values());
149	    }
150
151
152	    public List<Expense> getGroupExpenses(Group group) {
153	        return group.getExpenses();
154	    }
155	    
156	    // Get balance between two users within a group
157	    public double getGroupBalance(User user1, User user2, Group group) {
158	        return balanceSheet.getGroupBalance(user1, user2, group.getId());
159	    }
160	    
161	    // Get total balance for a user within a group
162	    public double getGroupTotalBalance(User user, Group group) {
163	        return balanceSheet.getGroupTotalBalance(user, group.getId());
164	    }
165	    
166	    // Get all balances within a group
167	    public Map<UserPair, Double> getGroupBalances(Group group) {
168	        return balanceSheet.getGroupBalances(group.getId());
169	    }
170	    
171	    // Get simplified settlements for a group
172	    public List<Transaction> getGroupSettlements(Group group) {
173	        return balanceSheet.getGroupSimplifiedSettlements(group.getId());
174	    }
175	}
```

‍

4\. Modify Balance Sheet Class to Accommodate group Logic : 

‍

Java

```
1public class BalanceSheet implements ExpenseObserver {
2	    // Stores the net balance between pairs of users
3	    private Map<UserPair, Double> balances = new HashMap<>();
4	    
5	    // Store group-specific balances
6	    private Map<String, Map<UserPair, Double>> groupBalances = new HashMap<>();
7
8
9	    @Override
10	    public void onExpenseAdded(Expense expense) {
11	        // Update balances when a new expense is added
12	        updateBalances(expense);
13	    }
14
15	    @Override
16	    public void onExpenseUpdated(Expense expense) {
17	        // For simplicity, we just update the balances without trying to undo the previous state
18	        updateBalances(expense);
19	    }
20
21	    /**
22	     - Updates the balances based on a new or updated expense.
23	     - If the expense belongs to a group, it also updates group-specific balances.
24	     - 
25	     - @param expense The expense to process.
26	     */
27	    public void updateBalances(Expense expense) {
28	        User payer = expense.getPayer();
29	        Map<User, Double> shares = expense.getShares();
30	        
31	        // Determine if this is a group expense
32	        Group group = expense.getGroup();
33	        String groupId = (group != null) ? group.getId() : null;
34	        
35	        // Ensure the group's balance map exists if this is a group expense
36	        if (groupId != null && !groupBalances.containsKey(groupId)) {
37	            groupBalances.put(groupId, new HashMap<>());
38	        }
39	        
40	        // Get the appropriate balance map (either group-specific or global)
41	        Map<UserPair, Double> targetBalances = balances; // Default to global balances
42	        
43	        for (Map.Entry<User, Double> entry : shares.entrySet()) {
44	            User participant = entry.getKey();
45	            Double amount = entry.getValue();
46	            
47	            if (!participant.equals(payer)) {
48	                // Create a unique pair for the payer and participant
49	                UserPair userPair = new UserPair(participant, payer);
50	                
51	                // Update global balances
52	                Double currentGlobalBalance = balances.getOrDefault(userPair, 0.0);
53	                balances.put(userPair, currentGlobalBalance + amount);
54	                
55	                // If this is a group expense, also update group-specific balances
56	                if (groupId != null) {
57	                    Map<UserPair, Double> groupBalanceMap = groupBalances.get(groupId);
58	                    Double currentGroupBalance = groupBalanceMap.getOrDefault(userPair, 0.0);
59	                    groupBalanceMap.put(userPair, currentGroupBalance + amount);
60	                }
61	            }
62	        }
63	    }
64
65	    /**
66	     - Gets the net balance between two users.
67	     - 
68	     - @param user1 First user.
69	     - @param user2 Second user.
70	     - @return The amount user1 owes user2 (negative if user2 owes user1).
71	     */
72	    public double getBalance(User user1, User user2) {
73	        UserPair pair1 = new UserPair(user1, user2);
74	        UserPair pair2 = new UserPair(user2, user1);
75	        
76	        double balance1 = balances.getOrDefault(pair1, 0.0);
77	        double balance2 = balances.getOrDefault(pair2, 0.0);
78	        
79	        return balance1 - balance2;
80	    }
81	    
82	    /**
83	     - Gets the net balance between two users within a specific group.
84	     - 
85	     - @param user1 First user.
86	     - @param user2 Second user.
87	     - @param groupId The ID of the group.
88	     - @return The amount user1 owes user2 in the group (negative if user2 owes user1).
89	     */
90	    public double getGroupBalance(User user1, User user2, String groupId) {
91	        // Return 0 if the group doesn't exist in our records
92	        if (!groupBalances.containsKey(groupId)) {
93	            return 0.0;
94	        }
95	        
96	        Map<UserPair, Double> groupBalanceMap = groupBalances.get(groupId);
97	        UserPair pair1 = new UserPair(user1, user2);
98	        UserPair pair2 = new UserPair(user2, user1);
99	        
100	        double balance1 = groupBalanceMap.getOrDefault(pair1, 0.0);
101	        double balance2 = groupBalanceMap.getOrDefault(pair2, 0.0);
102	        
103	        return balance1 - balance2;
104	    }
105
106	    /**
107	     - Calculates the total balance for a single user.
108	     - 
109	     - @param user The user to calculate the balance for.
110	     - @return The total balance for the user.
111	     */
112	    public double getTotalBalance(User user) {
113	        double total = 0.0;
114	        
115	        for (Map.Entry<UserPair, Double> entry : balances.entrySet()) {
116	            UserPair pair = entry.getKey();
117	            double amount = entry.getValue();
118	            
119	            if (pair.getUser1().equals(user)) {
120	                total -= amount; // Money owed by the user
121	            } else if (pair.getUser2().equals(user)) {
122	                total += amount; // Money owed to the user
123	            }
124	        }
125	        
126	        return total;
127	    }
128	    
129	    /**
130	     - Calculates the total balance for a user within a specific group.
131	     - 
132	     - @param user The user to calculate the balance for.
133	     - @param groupId The ID of the group.
134	     - @return The total balance for the user within the group.
135	     */
136	    public double getGroupTotalBalance(User user, String groupId) {
137	        // Return 0 if the group doesn't exist in our records
138	        if (!groupBalances.containsKey(groupId)) {
139	            return 0.0;
140	        }
141	        
142	        double total = 0.0;
143	        Map<UserPair, Double> groupBalanceMap = groupBalances.get(groupId);
144	        
145	        for (Map.Entry<UserPair, Double> entry : groupBalanceMap.entrySet()) {
146	            UserPair pair = entry.getKey();
147	            double amount = entry.getValue();
148	            
149	            if (pair.getUser1().equals(user)) {
150	                total -= amount; // Money owed by the user
151	            } else if (pair.getUser2().equals(user)) {
152	                total += amount; // Money owed to the user
153	            }
154	        }
155	        
156	        return total;
157	    }
158	    
159	    /**
160	     - Returns a copy of all balances.
161	     - 
162	     - @return A copy of the balances map
163	     */
164	    public Map<UserPair, Double> getAllBalances() {
165	        return new HashMap<>(balances);
166	    }
167	    
168	    /**
169	     - Returns a copy of group-specific balances.
170	     - 
171	     - @param groupId The ID of the group
172	     - @return A copy of the group's balances map or an empty map if the group doesn't exist
173	     */
174	    public Map<UserPair, Double> getGroupBalances(String groupId) {
175	        if (!groupBalances.containsKey(groupId)) {
176	            return new HashMap<>();
177	        }
178	        return new HashMap<>(groupBalances.get(groupId));
179	    }
180
181
182	    /**
183	     - Simplifies the balances into a list of transactions to settle all debts.
184	     - 
185	     - @return List of transactions needed to settle all debts.
186	     */
187	    public List<Transaction> getSimplifiedSettlements() {
188	        return calculateSettlements(balances);
189	    }
190	    
191	    /**
192	     - Simplifies the group-specific balances into a list of transactions to settle all debts within a group.
193	     - 
194	     - @param groupId The ID of the group.
195	     - @return List of transactions needed to settle all debts within the group.
196	     */
197	    public List<Transaction> getGroupSimplifiedSettlements(String groupId) {
198	        if (!groupBalances.containsKey(groupId)) {
199	            return new ArrayList<>();
200	        }
201	        return calculateSettlements(groupBalances.get(groupId));
202	    }
203	    
204	    /**
205	     - Helper method to calculate settlements from a balance map
206	     - 
207	     - @param balanceMap The map of balances between user pairs
208	     - @return List of transactions to settle all debts
209	     */
210	    private List<Transaction> calculateSettlements(Map<UserPair, Double> balanceMap) {
211	        // Step 1: Calculate net balances for each user
212	        Map<User, Double> netBalances = new HashMap<>();
213	        
214	        for (Map.Entry<UserPair, Double> entry : balanceMap.entrySet()) {
215	            UserPair pair = entry.getKey();
216	            double amount = entry.getValue();
217	            
218	            User debtor = pair.getUser1();
219	            User creditor = pair.getUser2();
220	            
221	            netBalances.put(debtor, netBalances.getOrDefault(debtor, 0.0) - amount);
222	            netBalances.put(creditor, netBalances.getOrDefault(creditor, 0.0) + amount);
223	        }
224	        
225	        // Step 2: Separate users into debtors and creditors
226	        List<Map.Entry<User, Double>> debtors = new ArrayList<>();
227	        List<Map.Entry<User, Double>> creditors = new ArrayList<>();
228	        
229	        for (Map.Entry<User, Double> entry : netBalances.entrySet()) {
230	            if (Math.abs(entry.getValue()) < 0.001) continue; // Skip users with zero balance
231	            
232	            if (entry.getValue() < 0) debtors.add(entry);
233	            else creditors.add(entry);
234	        }
235	        
236	        // Sort debtors and creditors by the absolute amount (largest first)
237	        /* 
238	          Matching the largest debtor to the largest creditor first ensures that large balances are settled early.
239	          This minimizes the number of transactions because fewer people remain in debt.The approach is greedy, 
240	          meaning at every step, the algorithm minimizes the remaining total debt in the most optimal way.
241	        */
242	        
243	        debtors.sort((a, b) -> Double.compare(Math.abs(b.getValue()), Math.abs(a.getValue())));
244	        creditors.sort((a, b) -> Double.compare(Math.abs(b.getValue()), Math.abs(a.getValue())));
245	        
246	        // Step 3: Match debtors and creditors to create transactions
247	        List<Transaction> transactions = new ArrayList<>();
248	        int debtorIndex = 0;
249	        int creditorIndex = 0;
250	        
251	        while (debtorIndex < debtors.size() && creditorIndex < creditors.size()) {
252	            Map.Entry<User, Double> debtor = debtors.get(debtorIndex);
253	            Map.Entry<User, Double> creditor = creditors.get(creditorIndex);
254	            
255	            double debtorBalance = debtor.getValue(); // Negative value
256	            double creditorBalance = creditor.getValue(); // Positive value
257	            
258	            // Determine the transfer amount as the smaller of the two balances
259	            double transferAmount = Math.min(Math.abs(debtorBalance), creditorBalance);
260	            
261	            if (transferAmount > 0.001) { // Only create transactions for significant amounts
262	                transactions.add(new Transaction(debtor.getKey(), creditor.getKey(), transferAmount));
263	            }
264	            
265	            // Update balances after the transaction
266	            debtor.setValue(debtorBalance + transferAmount);
267	            creditor.setValue(creditorBalance - transferAmount);
268	            
269	            // Move to the next debtor or creditor if their balance is settled
270	            if (Math.abs(debtor.getValue()) < 0.001) {
271	                debtorIndex++;
272	            }
273	            if (Math.abs(creditor.getValue()) < 0.001) {
274	                creditorIndex++;
275	            }
276	        }
277	        
278	        return transactions;
279	    }
280	}
```

‍

5\. Modify The Client code to Handle the Grouping Logic : 

‍

Java

```
1/**
2	 - Modified client code to demonstrate group functionality with proper balance handling
3	 */
4	public class SplitwiseSystem {
5	    public static void main(String[] args) {
6	        // Create users
7	        User alice = new User("u1", "Alice", "alice@example.com");
8	        User bob = new User("u2", "Bob", "bob@example.com");
9	        User charlie = new User("u3", "Charlie", "charlie@example.com");
10	        User david = new User("u4", "David", "david@example.com");
11	        
12	        // Create expense manager and balance sheet
13	        ExpenseManager expenseManager = new ExpenseManager();
14	        BalanceSheet balanceSheet = new BalanceSheet();
15	        
16	        // Register the balance sheet as an observer
17	        expenseManager.addObserver(balanceSheet);
18	        
19	        // Create group manager with the balance sheet
20	        GroupManager groupManager = new GroupManager(expenseManager, balanceSheet);
21	        
22	        // Create a new group
23	        Group roommates = groupManager.createGroup("g1", "Roommates", "Shared apartment expenses");
24	        
25	        // Add users to the group
26	        roommates.addMember(alice);
27	        roommates.addMember(bob);
28	        roommates.addMember(charlie);
29	        
30	        // Create a different group
31	        Group tripGroup = groupManager.createGroup("g2", "Weekend Trip", "Weekend trip expenses");
32	        tripGroup.addMember(alice);
33	        tripGroup.addMember(bob);
34	        tripGroup.addMember(david);
35	        
36	        // Create a list of participants for a group expense
37	        List<User> roommatesParticipants = new ArrayList<>(roommates.getMembers());
38	        
39	        // Alice pays for groceries - Create an equal split expense for the roommates group
40	        Split equalSplit = SplitFactory.createSplit("EQUAL");
41	        Map<String, Object> splitDetails = new HashMap<>();
42	        Map<User, Double> groceryShares = equalSplit.calculateSplit(90.0, roommatesParticipants, splitDetails);
43	        
44	        Expense groceryExpense = new Expense("e1", "Groceries", 90.0, alice, roommatesParticipants, groceryShares, roommates);
45	        
46	        // Add the group expense
47	        groupManager.addExpenseToGroup(groceryExpense, roommates);
48	        
49	        // Bob pays for utilities - Create a percentage split for the roommates group
50	        Map<String, Object> percentageSplitDetails = new HashMap<>();
51	        Map<User, Double> percentages = new HashMap<>();
52	        percentages.put(alice, 33.3);
53	        percentages.put(bob, 33.3);
54	        percentages.put(charlie, 33.4);
55	        percentageSplitDetails.put("percentages", percentages);
56	        
57	        Split percentageSplit = SplitFactory.createSplit("PERCENTAGE");
58	        Map<User, Double> utilityShares = percentageSplit.calculateSplit(120.0, roommatesParticipants, percentageSplitDetails);
59	        
60	        Expense utilityExpense = new Expense("e2", "Utilities", 120.0, bob, roommatesParticipants, utilityShares, roommates);
61	        
62	        // Add the utility expense to the roommates group
63	        groupManager.addExpenseToGroup(utilityExpense, roommates);
64	        
65	        // Create an expense for the trip group
66	        List<User> tripParticipants = new ArrayList<>(tripGroup.getMembers());
67	        Map<User, Double> hotelShares = equalSplit.calculateSplit(300.0, tripParticipants, splitDetails);
68	        
69	        Expense hotelExpense = new Expense("e3", "Hotel", 300.0, alice, tripParticipants, hotelShares, tripGroup);
70	        
71	        // Add the hotel expense to the trip group
72	        groupManager.addExpenseToGroup(hotelExpense, tripGroup);
73	        
74	        // Create a non-group expense between Alice and David
75	        List<User> nonGroupParticipants = new ArrayList<>();
76	        nonGroupParticipants.add(alice);
77	        nonGroupParticipants.add(david);
78	        
79	        Map<User, Double> lunchShares = equalSplit.calculateSplit(30.0, nonGroupParticipants, splitDetails);
80	        Expense lunchExpense = new Expense("e4", "Lunch", 30.0, david, nonGroupParticipants, lunchShares);
81	        
82	        // Add the non-group expense
83	        expenseManager.addExpense(lunchExpense);
84	        
85	        // Print roommates group balances
86	        System.out.println("Roommates Group Balances:");
87	        List<Transaction> roommateSettlements = groupManager.getGroupSettlements(roommates);
88	        for (Transaction transaction : roommateSettlements) {
89	            System.out.println(transaction.getFrom().getName() + " pays " + 
90	                              transaction.getTo().getName() + " $" + 
91	                              transaction.getAmount());
92	        }
93	        
94	        // Print trip group balances
95	        System.out.println("
96Trip Group Balances:");
97	        List<Transaction> tripSettlements = groupManager.getGroupSettlements(tripGroup);
98	        for (Transaction transaction : tripSettlements) {
99	            System.out.println(transaction.getFrom().getName() + " pays " + 
100	                              transaction.getTo().getName() + " $" + 
101	                              transaction.getAmount());
102	        }
103	        
104	        // Print overall balances (including both group and non-group expenses)
105	        System.out.println("
106Overall Balances for All Users:");
107	        List<Transaction> allSettlements = balanceSheet.getSimplifiedSettlements();
108	        for (Transaction transaction : allSettlements) {
109	            System.out.println(transaction.getFrom().getName() + " pays " + 
110	                              transaction.getTo().getName() + " $" + 
111	                              transaction.getAmount());
112	        }
113	        
114	        // Print individual user balances within specific groups
115	        System.out.println("
116User Balances Within Groups:");
117	        System.out.println("Alice's balance in Roommates group: $" + 
118	                           groupManager.getGroupTotalBalance(alice, roommates));
119	        System.out.println("Bob's balance in Roommates group: $" + 
120	                           groupManager.getGroupTotalBalance(bob, roommates));
121	        System.out.println("Alice's balance in Trip group: $" + 
122	                           groupManager.getGroupTotalBalance(alice, tripGroup));
123	        System.out.println("David's balance in Trip group: $" + 
124	                           groupManager.getGroupTotalBalance(david, tripGroup));
125	        
126	        // Print overall user balances
127	        System.out.println("
128Overall User Balances:");
129	        System.out.println("Alice's total balance: $" + balanceSheet.getTotalBalance(alice));
130	        System.out.println("Bob's total balance: $" + balanceSheet.getTotalBalance(bob));
131	        System.out.println("Charlie's total balance: $" + balanceSheet.getTotalBalance(charlie));
132	        System.out.println("David's total balance: $" + balanceSheet.getTotalBalance(david));
133	    }
134	}
```

## ✨ Conclusion : 

This low-level design for Splitwise showcases a well-structured and scalable architecture, emphasizing modularity and extensibility. By supporting various enhancements such as new Split Types and Grouping logic, this design ensures maintainability and flexibility. In an interview setting, presenting this design would demonstrate your ability to create robust and adaptable solutions, highlighting your proficiency in applying design patterns and best practices.\n\n---\n\n## Source: https://codewitharyan.com/tech-blogs/design-atm-machine\n\n# Design ATM Machine

System DesignLLD

### 🐈‍⬛ Github Codes Link: [https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode](https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode)

## ‍

## Low-Level Design: ATM Machine System 💰

An ATM (Automated Teller Machine) system is designed to efficiently handle customer banking operations, authenticate users, process transactions, and manage cash inventory. The system needs to support multiple transaction types, maintain security, handle various states of operation, and provide a seamless user experience. The system should be reliable and capable of handling different machine states and transaction processing.

‍

## Rules of the System:

**Setup:**

• The ATM has a cash inventory with different denominations.

• Cards have attributes like card number, PIN, and associated account details.

• The system authenticates users before allowing access to their accounts.

‍

**Operation:**

• Users insert their cards and enter their PIN for authentication.

• The ATM has several states: idle, has card, select operation, cash withdrawal, check balance.

• Once authenticated, users can select from available operations (withdraw cash, check balance, etc.).

• After completing operations, the card is returned to the user.

‍

**Safety Features:**

• The system validates PIN for secure authentication.

• Transaction validation ensures secure and accurate processing.

• The system maintains transaction logs for auditing.

• Maintenance mode prevents user interaction during servicing.

‍

## Interview Setting 🤝

### Point 1: Introduction and Vague Problem Statement:

Interviewer: Let's start with a basic problem statement. Design an ATM Machine System.

‍

Candidate: Certainly! Here's my understanding of the ATM Machine System:

• The system will process customer transactions on a single ATM machine.

• Users can insert cards, authenticate, and perform various banking operations.

• The system authenticates users via PIN verification before allowing transactions.

• The ATM maintains cash inventory and prevents dispensing when insufficient.

• The system transitions through various states during the transaction cycle.

Is this the expected flow?

‍

Interviewer: Yes, you are aligned with the flow. Please continue ahead.

‍

Candidate: Great! Before diving into the design, I'd like to clarify a few requirements:

• What types of operations should the system support?

• How should the system handle user authentication?

• Are there specific states the ATM should manage?

### Point 2: Clarifying Requirements:

Interviewer: We want a system that:

• Supports basic operations like cash withdrawal and balance checking.

• Handles card insertion and PIN-based authentication.

• Manages the state transitions of the ATM during operations.

‍

Candidate: To summarize, the key requirements are:

• A system that authenticates users via card and PIN.

• State management to handle the flow from card insertion to transaction completion.

• Support for basic banking operations (withdraw cash, check balance).

• Ability to handle edge cases like incorrect PIN, insufficient funds, or machine maintenance.

‍

Interviewer: Perfect, let's proceed.

‍

### Point 3: Identifying Key Components:

Candidate: Now that we have the requirements, let's identify the key components of our ATM

Machine System:

• Card: Represents user's bank card with card number and PIN.

• Account: Contains user account details including balance.

• Cash: Represents different denominations of cash in the ATM.

• ATM Inventory: Manages the cash inventory in the ATM.

• ATM Machine: Contains the core functionality and manages operations.

• Transaction Type: Represents different types of transactions.

‍

Interviewer: That sounds good. Let's proceed with the design details.

‍

### Point 4: Design Challenges:

Interviewer: What design challenges do you anticipate?

‍

Candidate: The key challenges for the ATM Machine System include:

• State Management: Properly transitioning between different machine states.

• Authentication: Securely validating user credentials.

• Transaction Processing: Handling different transaction types correctly.

• Error Handling: Managing scenarios like incorrect PIN, insufficient funds, or hardware failures.

• Inventory Management: Ensuring accurate cash tracking and availability.

### Point 5: Approach:

Interviewer: How would you approach these challenges?

‍

Candidate: I propose using design patterns effectively. Here are my strategies:

State Pattern for ATM States: 

• Encapsulates state-specific behavior.

• Manages transitions between states (idle, has card, select operation, cash withdrawal, check balance).

• Prevents invalid operations based on current state.

‍

Factory Pattern for Creation of ATM States : 

• Centralizes creation of state objects

• Decouples client code from concrete state implementations

• Allows for easy extension with new states

‍

Interviewer: That sounds like a solid approach. Let's delve into the implementation details.

‍

### Point 6: Implementation:

Interviewer: Ready to discuss implementation?

‍

Candidate: Yes. I'll focus on implementing the design patterns we discussed and show how they work together in the ATM Machine Problem:

‍

ATM Machine Design with Design Patterns :

1.) Implementing the Core Classes : 

This section outlines the fundamental components needed for the ATM machine design, such as the card, account, cash denominations, and ATM inventory management.

‍

• Card.java : 

This class represents a user's bank card.

‍

Java

```
1// Class representing a user's bank card
2	public class Card {
3	    private String cardNumber;
4	    private int pin;
5	    private String accountNumber;
6	    // Constructor
7	   public Card(String cardNumber, int pin, String accountNumber) {
8	        this.cardNumber = cardNumber;
9	        this.pin = pin;
10	        this.accountNumber = accountNumber;
11	    }
12	    // Getters and Setters
13	    public String getCardNumber() {
14	        return cardNumber;
15	    }
16
17	    public boolean validatePin(int enteredPin) {
18	        return this.pin == enteredPin;
19	    }
20
21	    public String getAccountNumber() {
22	        return accountNumber;
23	    }
24	}
```

‍

• Account.java : 

This class represents a user's bank account.

‍

Java

```
1// Account class
2	public class Account {
3	    private String accountNumber;
4	    private double balance;
5	    
6	    public Account(String accountNumber, double initialBalance) {
7	        this.accountNumber = accountNumber;
8	        this.balance = initialBalance;
9	    }
10	    
11	    public boolean withdraw(double amount) {
12	        if (balance >= amount) {
13	            balance -= amount;
14	            return true;
15	        }
16	        return false;
17	    }
18	    
19	    public void deposit(double amount) {
20	        balance += amount;
21	    }
22	    
23	    public double getBalance() {
24	        return balance;
25	    }
26	    
27	    public String getAccountNumber() {
28	        return accountNumber;
29	    }
30	}
```

‍

‍

Java

```
7	    BILL_10(10),
8	    BILL_5(5),
9	    BILL_1(1);
```
