‍

_Client code to run on system_

Java

```
1public class Main {
2	    public static void main(String[] args) {
3	        try {
4	            // Initialize services
5	            MovieService movieService = new MovieService();
6	            TheatreService theatreService = new TheatreService();
7	            ShowService showService = new ShowService();
8	            // Create a seat lock provider with a 10-minute timeout (600 seconds)
9	            ISeatLockProvider seatLockProvider = new SeatLockProvider(600);
10	            // Initialize booking service with the seat lock provider
11	            BookingService bookingService = new BookingService(seatLockProvider);
12	            // Initialize seat availability service
13	            SeatAvailabilityService seatAvailabilityService = new SeatAvailabilityService(bookingService, seatLockProvider);
14
15	            // Initialize payment service with a simple payment strategy
16	            PaymentService paymentService = new PaymentService(new DebitCardStrategy(), bookingService);
17
18	            // Initialize controllers
19	            MovieController movieController = new MovieController(movieService);
20	            TheatreController theatreController = new TheatreController(theatreService);
21	            ShowController showController = new ShowController(seatAvailabilityService, showService, theatreService, movieService);
22	            BookingController bookingController = new BookingController(showService, bookingService, theatreService);
23	            PaymentController paymentController = new PaymentController(paymentService);
24
25	            // Step 1: Create a theatre
26	            System.out.println("Creating a new theatre...");
27	            int theatreId = theatreController.createTheatre("PVR Cinemas");
28	            System.out.println("Theatre created with ID: " + theatreId);
29	            // Step 2: Create a screen in the theatre
30	            System.out.println("Creating a new screen...");
31	            int screenId = theatreController.createScreenInTheatre("Screen 1", theatreId);
32	            System.out.println("Screen created with ID: " + screenId);
33
34	            // Step 3: Create seats in the screen
35	            System.out.println("Creating seats...");
36	            // Create 5 rows with 10 seats each
37	            for (int row = 1; row <= 5; row++) {
38	                SeatCategory category;
39	                if (row == 1) {
40	                    category = SeatCategory.PLATINUM; // First row is premium
41	                } else if (row <= 3) {
42	                    category = SeatCategory.GOLD;     // Next two rows are gold
43	                } else {
44	                    category = SeatCategory.SILVER;   // Rest are silver
45	                }
46	                for (int seatNum = 1; seatNum <= 10; seatNum++) {
47	                    int seatId = theatreController.createSeatInScreen(row, category, screenId);
48	                    System.out.println("Created seat at row " + row + " with ID: " + seatId + " and category: " + category);
49	                }
50	            }
51
52	            // Step 4: Create a movie
53	            System.out.println("
54Creating a new movie...");
55	            int movieId = movieController.createMovie("Inception", 150);
56	            System.out.println("Movie created with ID: " + movieId);
57
58	            // Step 5: Create a show
59	            System.out.println("
60Creating a new show...");
61	            Date showTime = new Date(); // Current time
62	            int showId = showController.createShow(movieId, screenId, showTime, 150);
63	            System.out.println("Show created with ID: " + showId);
64
65	            // Step 6: Get available seats for the show
66	            System.out.println("
67Checking available seats...");
68	            List<Integer> availableSeats = showController.getAvailableSeats(showId);
69	            System.out.println("Available seats: " + availableSeats);
70
71	            // Step 7: Create a user
72	            System.out.println("
73Creating a user...");
74	            User user = new User("John Doe", "john.doe@example.com");
75	            System.out.println("User created: " + user.getUserName() + " with email: " + user.getUserEmail());
76
77	            // Step 8: Book tickets sequentially
78	            System.out.println("
79Sequential booking of seats 1, 2, 3...");
80	            String bookingId = bookingController.createBooking(user, showId, Arrays.asList(1, 2, 3));
81	            System.out.println("Booking created with ID: " + bookingId);
82
83	            // Step 9: Process payment for the booking
84	            System.out.println("
85Processing payment...");
86	            paymentController.processPayment(bookingId, user);
87	            System.out.println("Payment processed successfully!");
88
89	            // Step 10: Verify booking status
90	            Booking booking = bookingService.getBooking(bookingId);
91	            System.out.println("
92Booking status: " + booking.getBookingStatus());
93	            System.out.println("Is booking confirmed? " + booking.isConfirmed());
94
95	            // Step 11: Check available seats again after booking
96	            System.out.println("
97Checking available seats after booking...");
98	            availableSeats = showController.getAvailableSeats(showId);
99	            System.out.println("Available seats: " + availableSeats);
100
101	            // ------------------------------
102	            // CONCURRENT BOOKING SIMULATION
103	            // ------------------------------
104	            System.out.println("
105Simulating concurrent booking attempts...");
106	            Thread t1 = new Thread(() -> {
107	                try {
108	                    // User 1 (John Doe) trying to book seats 5, 6, 7
109	                    String bookingIdT1 = bookingController.createBooking(user, showId, Arrays.asList(5, 6, 7));
110	                    System.out.println("User1 booking (seats 5,6,7) succeeded with Booking ID: " + bookingIdT1);
111	                } catch (Exception e) {
112	                    System.err.println("User1 booking (seats 5,6,7) failed: " + e.getMessage());
113	                }
114	            });
115	            Thread t2 = new Thread(() -> {
116	                try {
117	                    // User 2 trying to book seats 7, 8, 9 (seat 7 overlaps with User1’s attempt)
118	                    User user2 = new User("Jane Doe", "jane.doe@example.com");
119	                    String bookingIdT2 = bookingController.createBooking(user2, showId, Arrays.asList(7, 8, 9));
120	                    System.out.println("User2 booking (seats 7,8,9) succeeded with Booking ID: " + bookingIdT2);
121	                } catch (Exception e) {
122	                    System.err.println("User2 booking (seats 7,8,9) failed: " + e.getMessage());
123	                }
124	            });
125	            t1.start();
126	            t2.start();
127	            t1.join();
128	            t2.join();
129	            // Final available seats after concurrent attempts
130	            System.out.println("
131Final available seats after concurrent booking attempts: " + showController.getAvailableSeats(showId));
132	        } catch (Exception e) {
133	            System.err.println("Error occurred: " + e.getMessage());
134	            e.printStackTrace();
135	        }
136	    }
137	}
```

Created seat at row 1 with ID: 6 and category: PLATINUM

Created seat at row 1 with ID: 10 and category: PLATINUM

‍

Interviewer: Looks good. What makes your approach effective?

‍

Candidate: Here are the key strengths of my approach are:

• **Separation of Concerns** : 

Each layer (Controller → Service → Domain) in the architecture has a clearly defined responsibility:

• Controllers are responsible for handling client requests and delegating logic.

• Services encapsulate business logic and coordination between components.

• Domain classes like Movie, Theatre, Show, and Seat represent the core data model.

‍

• **Single Responsibility** : 

Each class in the system has one and only one responsibility:

▪ TheatreService manages theatre-related operations.

▪ BookingService is responsible for bookings.

▪ PaymentsService handles payment retries and unlocking.

▪ SeatLockProvider abstracts seat locking strategy.

Following SRP (Single Responsibility Principle) results in smaller, focused classes that are easier to understand and extend.

‍

• **Immutability** : 

• Core domain models (like Screen, Movie, Show, etc.) declare their fields as final, ensuring that once initialized, their core identity doesn’t change.

• Services are injected as final fields in the controllers and are stateless, promoting thread safety in a multithreaded environment.

• Using final where appropriate enforces immutability, making code safer and less error-prone.

‍

## ✨Conclusion : 

This low-level design for the Movie Ticket Booking System demonstrates a well-structured, scalable, and extensible architecture. It leverages several key design patterns to achieve modularity, flexibility, and clear separation of concerns.\n\n---\n\n## Source: https://codewitharyan.com/tech-blogs/design-cache\n\n# Design cache

Topic Tags:

System DesignLLD

### 🐈‍⬛ Github Codes Link: [https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode](https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode)

# **Low-Level Design: Cache System 💾** 

A cache system is designed to efficiently store and retrieve data while managing limited memory resources. The system needs to handle multiple operations including insertion, retrieval, and eviction of data based on specific policies to maintain optimal performance and memory utilization. 

 ‍

### **Setup:** 

*   The cache has a configurable maximum capacity. 
*   Each cache entry consists of a unique key and its corresponding value. 
*   The cache supports different eviction policies for when it reaches capacity. 
*   The system provides operations for putting, getting, and removing data. 

 ‍

### **Operation:** 

*   Users can store data in the cache using a key-value pair. 
*   Users can retrieve previously stored data using its key. 
*   When the cache reaches capacity, an eviction policy determines which entry to remove. 
*   The system handles concurrent access to the cache efficiently. 

 ‍

### **Safety Features:** 

*   Thread safety mechanisms to handle concurrent operations. 
*   Automatic eviction to prevent memory overflow. 
*   Cache hit/miss tracking for performance monitoring. 
*   Optional time-based expiration for cache entries. 

 ‍

## **Interview Setting 🤝:** 

### **Point 1: Introduction and Vague Problem Statement:** 

🧑‍💼 **Interviewer**: Let's start with a basic problem statement. Design a Cache System. 

‍

🧑‍💻 **Candidate**: Certainly! Here's my understanding of the Cache System: 

*   The system will store and retrieve data using key-value pairs. 
*   It needs to manage limited memory by employing eviction policies. 
*   The cache supports two basic operations: accessData (get) and updateData (write). 
*   We'll use Java generics for keys and values. 
*   The system should be thread-safe and handle concurrent operations. 
*   It should be extensible to support different eviction and write strategies. 
*   We need to ensure "read your own writes" consistency. 
*   All operations for the same key should be handled by the same thread to maintain order. (All reads & write of same key, go to same thread to ensure, "read your own write". (Because request is handled by single thread thus Ordering of request within a thread is maintained, as thread internally runs sequentially, it will not affect ordering) 

Does this align with what you're looking for? 

 ‍

🧑‍💼 **Interviewer**: Yes, you are aligned with the direction. Please continue ahead. 

‍

🧑‍💻 **Candidate**: Great! Before diving into the design, I'd like to clarify a few requirements: 

*   What write policies should we implement? I'm thinking write-through at minimum. (we have write-back, write-around also) 
*   For eviction policies, should we focus on implementing LRU, or do we need multiple policies? (we have LFU & mix of LRU & LFU etc) 
*   How should we handle concurrent access to ensure thread safety? 
*   Is thread affinity for key-based operations acceptable for ensuring "read your own writes"? 
*   Are there any performance requirements we need to consider? 

 ‍

### **Point 2: Clarifying Requirements:** 

🧑‍💼 **Interviewer**: We want a system that: 

*   Supports different write policies, but only implements write-through for now. 
*   Supports different eviction algorithms, but only implements LRU for now. 
*   Handles concurrent operations safely without data corruption. 
*   Ensures "read your own writes" consistency. 
*   Uses key-based hashing to route operations to specific threads. (Due to limited threads) 

 ‍

🧑‍💻 **Candidate**: To summarize, the key requirements are: 

*   A cache with configurable capacity. 
*   Implementation of write-through policy (sync DB in parallel on write action to the cache). 
*   Implementation of LRU (Least Recently Used) eviction. 
*   Thread affinity for operations on the same key to maintain ordering & "read your own writes" 
*   "Read your own writes" consistency guarantees. 
*   Use of Java generics for flexible key-value types. 
*   Extensible design for future write policies and eviction algorithms. 
*   We'll ignore request collapsing, hot loading, event logging, and head-of-line blocking. (We'll discuss these concepts in HLD course) 

 ‍

🧑‍💼 **Interviewer**: Perfect, let's proceed. 

‍

### **Point 3: Identifying Key Components:** 

🧑‍💻 **Candidate**: Now that we have the requirements, let's identify the key components of our Cache system: 

**Storage Interfaces**:  

*   CacheStorage<K, V>: Interface for in-memory cache with operations like put, get, remove, containsKey, size, and getCapacity. 
*   DBStorage<K, V>: Interface for persistent storage with operations like write, read, and delete. 

‍

**Strategy Interfaces**:  

*   WritePolicy<K, V>: Interface for different write policies with a write method. 
*   EvictionAlgorithm<K>: Interface for eviction algorithms with keyAccessed and evictKey methods. 

‍‍

**Concrete Implementations**:  

*   InMemoryCacheStorage<K, V>: Concrete implementation using ConcurrentHashMap. 
*   SimpleDBStorage<K, V>: Simple database storage implementation. 
*   WriteThroughPolicy<K, V>: Implementation of write-through policy. 
*   LRUEvictionAlgorithm<K>: Implementation of LRU eviction algorithm. 

‍

**Utility Classes**:  

*   DoublyLinkedList<K> and DoublyLinkedListNode<K>: For LRU implementation. 
*   KeyBasedExecutor: For key-based thread affinity using hashing. 

‍

**Core Component**:  

*   Cache<K, V>: Main cache class that integrates all components with accessData and updateData operations. 

 ‍

**Interviewer**: That sounds good. Let's proceed with the design details. 

‍

### **Point 4: Design Challenges:** 

🧑‍💼 **Interviewer**: What design challenges do you anticipate? 

‍

🧑‍💻 **Candidate**: The key challenges for the Cache system include: 

1.  **Thread Affinity Implementation**: Ensuring all operations for a given key go to the same thread without creating bottlenecks. 
2.  **Eviction Policy Efficiency**: Implementing LRU efficiently while maintaining thread safety. 
3.  **Write-Through Performance**: Managing synchronization between cache and database in the write-through policy. 
4.  **Cache Consistency**: Ensuring "read your own writes" without compromising performance. 
5.  **Key-Based Hashing**: Creating a good distribution of keys across executor threads. 

 ‍

### **Point 5: Approach:** 

🧑‍💼 **Interviewer**: How would you approach these challenges? 

‍

🧑‍💻 **Candidate**: I propose the following approach: 

1.  **Strategy Pattern for Policies**: Using interfaces for WritePolicy and EvictionAlgorithm allows us to swap implementations without changing core code. 
2.  **Thread Affinity via KeyBasedExecutor**: We'll implement a KeyBasedExecutor that maps keys to specific single-threaded executors using a simple modulo hash to ensure operations on the same key run in the same thread. 
3.  **Completable Futures**: We'll use CompletableFuture for asynchronous operations, particularly for updateData, to make the system responsive. 
4.  **Concurrent Data Structures**: We'll use thread-safe collections like ConcurrentHashMap for our cache storage implementation. 
5.  **Future Extensibility**: Our design will allow easy addition of new write policies (like write-back) and eviction algorithms (like LFU) in the future. 

 ‍

🧑‍💼 **Interviewer**: That sounds like a solid approach. Let's delve into the implementation details. 

 ‍

### **Point 6: Implementation:** 

🧑‍💼 **Interviewer**: Ready to discuss implementation? 

‍

🧑‍💻 **Candidate**: Yes, I'll outline the implementation for our key components: 

 ‍

### **Cache System Design with Design Patterns :**

Java

```
1public interface CacheStorage<K, V> { 
2    void put(K key, V value) throws Exception; 
3    V get(K key) throws Exception; 
4    void remove(K key) throws Exception; 
5    boolean containsKey(K key) throws Exception; 
6    int size() throws Exception; 
7    int getCapacity(); 
8}
```

‍

*   **DB Storage Interface :**  
    

Java

```
1public interface DBStorage<K, V> { 
2    void write(K key, V value) throws Exception; 
3    V read(K key) throws Exception; 
4    void delete(K key) throws Exception; 
5}
```

‍

*   **Concrete Data Storage Classes :**  
    
*   **In Memory Cache Storage :**  
    

Java

```
1public class InMemoryCacheStorage<K, V> implements CacheStorage<K, V> { 
2    private final Map<K, V> cache; 
3    private final int capacity; 
4  
5    public InMemoryCacheStorage(int capacity) { 
6        this.capacity = capacity; 
7        this.cache = new ConcurrentHashMap<>(); 
8    } 
9  
10    @Override 
11    public void put(K key, V value) throws Exception { 
12        cache.put(key, value); 
13    } 
14  
15    @Override 
16    public V get(K key) throws Exception { 
17        if (!cache.containsKey(key)) { 
18            throw new Exception("Key not in cache: " + key); 
19        } 
20        return cache.get(key); 
21    } 
22 
23    @Override 
24    public void remove(K key) throws Exception { 
25        if (!cache.containsKey(key)) { 
26            throw new Exception("Key not in cache: " + key); 
27        } 
28        cache.remove(key); 
29    } 
30  
31    @Override 
32    public boolean containsKey(K key) throws Exception { 
33        return cache.containsKey(key); 
34    } 
35 
36    @Override 
37    public int size() throws Exception { 
38        return cache.size(); 
39    } 
40 
41    @Override 
42    public int getCapacity() { 
43        return capacity; 
44    } 
45}
```

‍

*   **Simple DB Storage :**  
    

Java

```
1public class SimpleDBStorage<K, V> implements DBStorage<K, V> { 
2    // This is a simple mock database using a ConcurrentHashMap. 
3    private final Map<K, V> database = new ConcurrentHashMap<>(); 
4  
5    @Override 
6    public void write(K key, V value) throws Exception { 
7        database.put(key, value); 
8    } 
9 
10    @Override 
11    public V read(K key) throws Exception { 
12        if (!database.containsKey(key)) { 
13            throw new Exception("Key not found in DB: " + key); 
14        } 
15        return database.get(key); 
16    } 
17  
18    @Override 
19    public void delete(K key) throws Exception { 
20        if (!database.containsKey(key)) { 
21            throw new Exception("Key not found in DB: " + key); 
22        } 
23        database.remove(key); 
24    } 
25}
```

‍

1.  **Implementing Write Policies :**  

*   **Write Policy Interface :**  
    

Java

```
1public interface WritePolicy<K, V> { 
2    /** 
3     - Write a key/value pair to both cache storage and DB storage concurrently. 
4     - This is the write‑through policy. 
5     */ 
6    void write(K key, V value, CacheStorage<K, V> cacheStorage, DBStorage<K, V> dbStorage) throws Exception; 
7}
```

‍

*   **Concrete Write Policies :**  
    
*   **Write Through Policy :**  
    

Java

```
1public class WriteThroughPolicy<K, V> implements WritePolicy<K, V> { 
2    @Override 
3    public void write(K key, V value, CacheStorage<K, V> cacheStorage, DBStorage<K, V> dbStorage) throws Exception { 
4        // Write to both cache and db concurrently and wait for both to complete. 
5        CompletableFuture<Void> cacheFuture = CompletableFuture.runAsync(() -> { 
6            try { 
7                cacheStorage.put(key, value); 
8            } catch (Exception e) { 
9                throw new CompletionException(e); 
10            } 
11        }); 
12  
13        CompletableFuture<Void> dbFuture = CompletableFuture.runAsync(() -> { 
14            try { 
15                dbStorage.write(key, value); 
16            } catch (Exception e) { 
17                throw new CompletionException(e); 
18            } 
19        });  
20        CompletableFuture.allOf(cacheFuture, dbFuture).join(); 
21    } 
22}
```

‍‍

**2.Startegy Pattern for Eviction Algorithms :**  

*   **Eviction Algorithm interface :**  
    

Java

```
1public interface EvictionAlgorithm<K> { 
2    /** 
3     - Notifies the eviction algorithm that the given key was accessed. 
4     */ 
5    void keyAccessed(K key) throws Exception; 
6 
7    /** 
8     - Selects and removes one key to be evicted (from the cache). 
9     */ 
10    K evictKey() throws Exception; 
11}
```

‍

*   **Utility Classes :**  
    
*   **Doubly Linked List Node :**  
    

Java

```
1public class DoublyLinkedListNode<K> { 
2    private final K value; 
3    DoublyLinkedListNode<K> prev; 
4    DoublyLinkedListNode<K> next; 
5  
6    public DoublyLinkedListNode(K value) { 
7        this.value = value; 
8        this.prev = null; 
9        this.next = null; 
10    } 
11 
12 
13    public K getValue() { 
14        return value; 
15    } 
16}
```

‍

*   **Doubly Linked List :**  
    

Java

```
1public class DoublyLinkedList<K> { 
2    private DoublyLinkedListNode<K> head; 
3    private DoublyLinkedListNode<K> tail; 
4  
5    public DoublyLinkedList() { 
6        this.head = null; 
7        this.tail = null; 
8    } 
9  
10    /** 
11     - Adds a node to the tail of the list. 
12     */ 
13    public void addNodeAtTail(DoublyLinkedListNode<K> node) { 
14        if (tail == null) { 
15            head = node; 
16            tail = node; 
17        } else { 
18            tail.next = node; 
19            node.prev = tail; 
20            tail = node; 
21        } 
22        node.next = null; 
23    } 
24  
25    /** 
26     - Detaches a node from the list. 
27     */ 
28    public void detachNode(DoublyLinkedListNode<K> node) { 
29        if (node == null) return; 
30        if (node.prev != null) { 
31            node.prev.next = node.next; 
32        } else { 
33            // Node is head. 
34            head = node.next; 
35        } 
36        if (node.next != null) { 
37            node.next.prev = node.prev; 
38        } else { 
39            // Node is tail. 
40            tail = node.prev; 
41        } 
42        node.prev = null; 
43        node.next = null; 
44    } 
45  
46    /** 
47     - Returns the head node (the least recently used). 
48     */ 
49    public DoublyLinkedListNode<K> getHead() { 
50        return head; 
51    } 
52  
53    /** 
54     - Removes the head node from the list. 
55     */ 
56    public void removeHead() { 
57        if (head != null) { 
58            if (head.next != null) { 
59                head = head.next; 
60                head.prev = null; 
61            } else { 
62                head = null; 
63                tail = null; 
64            } 
65        } 
66    } 
67}
```

‍

*   **Concrete Eviction Algorithm Strategies :**  
    
*   **LRU Eviction Algorithm :**  
    

Java

```
1public class LRUEvictionAlgorithm<K> implements EvictionAlgorithm<K> { 
2    // A custom doubly linked list to track the LRU order. 
3    private final DoublyLinkedList<K> dll; 
4    // Map of key to its node in the linked list. 
5    private final Map<K, DoublyLinkedListNode<K>> keyToNodeMap; 
6 
7    public LRUEvictionAlgorithm() { 
8        this.dll = new DoublyLinkedList<>(); 
9        this.keyToNodeMap = new HashMap<>(); 
10    } 
11  
12    @Override 
13    public synchronized void keyAccessed(K key) throws Exception { 
14        if (keyToNodeMap.containsKey(key)) { 
15            // Move the node to the tail (most recently used). 
16            DoublyLinkedListNode<K> node = keyToNodeMap.get(key); 
17            dll.detachNode(node); 
18            dll.addNodeAtTail(node); 
19        } else { 
20            // New key: add it to the tail. 
21            DoublyLinkedListNode<K> newNode = new DoublyLinkedListNode<>(key); 
22            dll.addNodeAtTail(newNode); 
23            keyToNodeMap.put(key, newNode); 
24        } 
25    } 
26  
27    @Override 
28    public synchronized K evictKey() throws Exception { 
29        // Evict the least recently used key (from the head). 
30        DoublyLinkedListNode<K> nodeToEvict = dll.getHead(); 
31        if (nodeToEvict == null) { 
32            return null; 
33        } 
34        K evictKey = nodeToEvict.getValue(); 
35        dll.removeHead(); 
36        keyToNodeMap.remove(evictKey); 
37        return evictKey; 
38    } 
39}
```

‍

> **NOTE :**  Similarly We can Implement the LFU etc.. Algorithms as well in the form of Different concrete Strategies.

 ‍

**3.Implementing Executor :**  

Java

```
1public class KeyBasedExecutor { 
2    private final ExecutorService[] executors; 
3    private final int numExecutors; 
4  
5    public KeyBasedExecutor(int numExecutors) { 
6        this.numExecutors = numExecutors; 
7        this.executors = new ExecutorService[numExecutors]; 
8        for (int i = 0; i < numExecutors; i++) { 
9            executors[i] = Executors.newSingleThreadExecutor(); 
10        } 
11    } 
12  
13    /** 
14     - Dispatch a task for a given key so that all tasks for that key run on the same single-thread executor. 
15     */ 
16    public <T> CompletableFuture<T> submitTask(Object key, Supplier<T> task) { 
17        int index = getExecutorIndexForKey(key); 
18        ExecutorService executor = executors[index]; 
19        return CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(task, executor); 
20    } 
21  
22    /** 
23     - Determines the executor index for a key using a basic mod hash. 
24     */ 
25    public int getExecutorIndexForKey(Object key) { 
26        return Math.abs(key.hashCode() % numExecutors); 
27    } 
28 
29    /** 
30     - Shuts down all executors. 
31     */ 
32    public void shutdown() { 
33        for (ExecutorService executor : executors) { 
34            executor.shutdown(); 
35        } 
36    } 
37}
```

‍‍

**4.Implementing the Core Cache Class :**  

*   **Cache Class :**  
    

Java

```
1public class Cache<K, V> { 
2    private final CacheStorage<K, V> cacheStorage; 
3    private final DBStorage<K, V> dbStorage; 
4    private final WritePolicy<K, V> writePolicy; 
5    private final EvictionAlgorithm<K> evictionAlgorithm; 
6    private final KeyBasedExecutor keyBasedExecutor; 
7 
8    /** 
9     - Constructs the cache. 
10     - 
11     - @param cacheStorage  The in-memory cache (with limited capacity). 
12     - @param dbStorage     The underlying persistent storage (database). 
13     - @param writePolicy   The write-through policy. 
14     - @param evictionAlgorithm  The eviction strategy (custom LRU implementation). 
15     - @param numExecutors  Number of single-thread executors for key-based dispatch. 
16     */ 
17    public Cache(CacheStorage<K, V> cacheStorage, DBStorage<K, V> dbStorage, 
18                 WritePolicy<K, V> writePolicy, EvictionAlgorithm<K> evictionAlgorithm, 
19                 int numExecutors) { 
20        this.cacheStorage = cacheStorage; 
21        this.dbStorage = dbStorage; 
22        this.writePolicy = writePolicy; 
23        this.evictionAlgorithm = evictionAlgorithm; 
24        this.keyBasedExecutor = new KeyBasedExecutor(numExecutors); 
25    } 
26  
27    /** 
28     - Reads data from the cache for the given key. 
29     - Updates the eviction algorithm as the key is accessed. 
30     */ 
31    public CompletableFuture<V> accessData(K key) { 
32        return keyBasedExecutor.submitTask(key, () -> { 
33            try { 
34                if (!cacheStorage.containsKey(key)) { 
35                    throw new Exception("Key not found in cache: " + key); 
36                } 
37                evictionAlgorithm.keyAccessed(key); 
38                return cacheStorage.get(key); 
39            } catch (Exception e) { 
40                throw new CompletionException(e); 
41            } 
42        }); 
43    } 
44  
45    /** 
46     - Writes (or updates) data in both the cache and DB storage using the write-through policy. 
47     - If the key is new and the cache is at capacity, evicts the least recently used key from the cache. 
48     */ 
49    public CompletableFuture<Void> updateData(K key, V value) { 
50        return keyBasedExecutor.submitTask(key, () -> { 
51            try { 
52                if (cacheStorage.containsKey(key)) { 
53                    // Update case: perform concurrent write. 
54                    writePolicy.write(key, value, cacheStorage, dbStorage); 
55                    evictionAlgorithm.keyAccessed(key); 
56                } else { 
57                    // New key: If the cache is full, evict one key. 
58                    if (cacheStorage.size() >= cacheStorage.getCapacity()) { 
59                        K evictedKey = evictionAlgorithm.evictKey(); 
60                        if (evictedKey != null) { 
61                            // Removal on the evicted key's executor to maintain ordering. 
62                            int currentIndex = keyBasedExecutor.getExecutorIndexForKey(key); 
63                            int evictedIndex = keyBasedExecutor.getExecutorIndexForKey(evictedKey); 
64                            if (currentIndex == evictedIndex) { 
65                                cacheStorage.remove(evictedKey); 
66                            } else { 
67                                CompletableFuture<Void> removalFuture = keyBasedExecutor.submitTask(evictedKey, () -> { 
68                                    try { 
69                                        cacheStorage.remove(evictedKey); 
70                                        return null; 
71                                    } catch (Exception ex) { 
72                                        throw new CompletionException(ex); 
73                                    } 
74                                }); 
75                                removalFuture.join(); 
76                            } 
77                        } 
78                    } 
79                    // Write the new key/value concurrently to both storages. 
80                    writePolicy.write(key, value, cacheStorage, dbStorage); 
81                    evictionAlgorithm.keyAccessed(key); 
82                } 
83                return null; 
84            } catch (Exception e) { 
85                throw new CompletionException(e); 
86            } 
87        }); 
88    } 
89  
90    /** 
91     - Shuts down all executors. 
92     */ 
93    public void shutdown() { 
94        keyBasedExecutor.shutdown(); 
95    } 
96}
```

‍

**4.) Client Code to Run the System :**  

Java

```
1public class Main { 
2    public static void main(String[] args) { 
3        try { 
4            // Set a small capacity for the in-memory cache (e.g., 5 items) 
5            CacheStorage<String, String> cacheStorage = new InMemoryCacheStorage<>(5); 
6            // The underlying persistent store (DB storage) can be assumed to have large or unlimited capacity. 
7            DBStorage<String, String> dbStorage = new SimpleDBStorage<>(); 
8            // Create the write-through policy (writes concurrently to both storages). 
9            WritePolicy<String, String> writePolicy = new WriteThroughPolicy<>(); 
10            // Create the LRU eviction algorithm. 
11            EvictionAlgorithm<String> evictionAlg = new LRUEvictionAlgorithm<>(); 
12            // Create the cache with 4 executor threads to guarantee per-key ordering. 
13            Cache<String, String> cache = new Cache<>(cacheStorage, dbStorage, writePolicy, evictionAlg, 4); 
14 
15            // Demonstrate write operations. 
16            cache.updateData("A", "Apple").join(); 
17            cache.updateData("B", "Banana").join(); 
18            cache.updateData("C", "Cherry").join(); 
19            cache.updateData("D", "Durian").join(); 
20            cache.updateData("E", "Elderberry").join(); 
21 
22            // At this point, the in-memory cache is at capacity. 
23            // The next write will trigger eviction (of the least recently used key) from the cache. 
24            cache.updateData("F", "Fig").join(); 
25 
26            // Demonstrate read operations. 
27            try { 
28                String valueA = cache.accessData("A").join(); 
29                System.out.println("A: " + valueA); 
30            } catch(Exception e) { 
31                System.out.println("A is evicted or not found in cache."); 
32            } 
33 
34            String valueF = cache.accessData("F").join(); 
35            System.out.println("F: " + valueF); 
36 
37            // Update an existing key and then read it to demonstrate read-your-own-writes. 
38            cache.updateData("B", "Blueberry").join(); 
39            String valueB = cache.accessData("B").join(); 
40            System.out.println("B: " + valueB); 
41
42            // Shut down executors when finished. 
43            cache.shutdown();  
44        } catch (Exception e) { 
45            e.printStackTrace(); 
46        } 
47    } 
48}
```

‍

**Output :**  

```
A is evicted or not found in cache. 
F: Fig 
B: Blueberry
```

‍ 

🧑‍💼 **Interviewer**: Looks good. What makes your approach effective? 

‍

🧑‍💻 **Candidate**: Here are the key strengths of my approach to the caching system: 

**Separation of Concerns:** 

Each component in the architecture has a clearly defined responsibility: 

*   DataStorage interface (IProducer equivalent) defines the contract for interacting with the underlying data source. 
*   HashMapDataSource (SimpleProducer equivalent) provides a concrete implementation of DataStorage using a ConcurrentHashMap. 
*   EvictionAlgorithm interface defines the contract for different eviction strategies. 
*   LRUEvictionAlgorithm (SleepingConsumer or ConsolePrintConsumer equivalent in terms of specific behavior) provides a concrete implementation of the LRU eviction policy. 
*   Cache (KafkaQueue equivalent) serves as the central component, orchestrating data access, storage, and eviction. 

‍

**Single Responsibility:** 

Each class adheres to the single responsibility principle: 

*   HashMapDataSource is solely responsible for managing data within an in-memory hash map. 
*   LRUEvictionAlgorithm is solely responsible for determining which key to evict based on the Least Recently Used policy. 
*   Cache manages the interaction between the DataStorage and the EvictionAlgorithm, providing the main caching functionalities (put, get). This results in focused classes that are easier to understand, test, and maintain. 

 ‍

## **✨ Conclusion:** 

The caching system we've designed effectively combines interfaces and concrete implementations to create a flexible and robust solution. By defining clear contracts with DataStorage and EvictionAlgorithm, we can easily swap out different data storage mechanisms (like a database connector) or eviction policies (like LFU) without modifying the core Cache logic. 

‍

Our approach excels through its separation of concerns, adherence to the single responsibility principle, and the provision for asynchronous operations. The use of the Strategy pattern (via the EvictionAlgorithm interface) allows for easy extension with new eviction strategies.\n\n---\n\n## Source: https://codewitharyan.com/tech-blogs/design-pub-sub-model-like-kafka\n\n# Design Pub-Sub Model like Kafka

Topic Tags:

system designlld

### 🐈‍⬛ Github Codes Link: [https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode](https://github.com/aryan-0077/CWA-LowLevelDesignCode)

# ‍

# Low-Level Design: Pub-Sub Message Queue System (Kafka) 📝

A Pub-Sub Message Queue system is designed to facilitate asynchronous communication between publishers and subscribers through topics. The system needs to support multiple topics, efficient message publishing, reliable message delivery to subscribers, and parallel subscriber execution while maintaining high throughput and low latency.

‍

## Kafka Key Concepts : 

### Topic : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1745292252963-image.png)

‍

A topic is a category or feed name to which messages are published. Publishers send messages to specific topics, and subscribers consume messages from the topics they're interested in. Topics act as logical channels that separate different streams of data.

‍

### Partition : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1745292259944-image.png)

‍

Each topic is split into partitions, which are the fundamental unit of parallelism in Kafka:

• Partitions allow horizontal scaling of message processing

• Each partition is an ordered, immutable sequence of messages

• Messages within a partition are assigned a sequential ID called an offset

• Partitions are distributed across the brokers in a Kafka cluster

‍

### Offset : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1745292267666-image.png)

‍

An offset is a unique identifier for each message within a partition:

• It's a sequential integer assigned when a message is published to a partition

• Consumers track their position in each partition using offsets

• This allows consumers to resume from where they left off after a failure

• Offsets enable at-least-once, at-most-once and exactly-once delivery semantics

‍

### Consumer : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1745292276254-image.png)

‍

### Producers : 

• Multiple producers (Producer1, Producer2, Producer3) generate data streams like order, access\_log, and user\_behavior, which are then consumed by corresponding consumers. 🧾➡️📦

‍

### Consumers read messages from topics:

• Consumers can be part of consumer groups for parallel processing

• When multiple consumers belong to the same group, each consumer receives messages from a subset of partitions

• This enables horizontal scaling of message consumption

• Consumers track the latest offset they've processed for each partition

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1745292302872-image.png)

‍

### Consumer Lag :

Consumer lag is the difference between the producer offset and the consumer offset, indicating how many messages the consumer is behind—here, a lag of 4 (7 - 3). 🕒📉

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1745292312492-image.png)

‍

### Consumer Pulling Mechanism : 

In a messaging system, there are two modes for data consumption:

• Push Model: The topic pushes data directly into the consumer.

• Pull Model: The consumer actively pulls data from the topic. (Mostly used)

‍

As illustrated in the image, the Pull model involves the consumer requesting data from the topic queue, typically tracking message offsets (like g, f, e, etc.) to know which messages to consume next.

‍

## System Design Considerations for Topics and Consumers : 

• ✅ Multiple Topic Support:

 The system allows the creation of multiple topics where messages can be published.

• ✅ Publisher Flexibility:

 Publishers can send messages to specific topics of interest.

• ✅ Subscriber Flexibility:

 Subscribers have the option to subscribe to one or more topics based on their needs.

• ✅ Broadcast to Subscribers:

 Messages published to a topic are delivered to all subscribers of that topic.

• ✅ Parallel Consumption:

 Subscribers (consumers) should be designed to run in parallel, improving throughput and scalability.

‍

Operation:

• Publishers create and send messages to specific topics

• The system stores messages and manages their distribution

• Subscribers receive messages from topics they've subscribed to

• The system handles concurrent message processing by multiple subscribers

‍

Features:

• Topic-based message routing

• Parallel processing of messages by subscribers

• Message persistence for reliability

• Support for multiple publishers and subscribers

‍

## Interview Setting 🤝

### Point 1: Introduction and Vague Problem Statement : 

Interviewer: Let's start with a basic problem statement. Design a Message Queue supporting the publisher-subscriber model like Kafka.

‍

Candidate: Sure! Here's my understanding of the requirements for a Pub-Sub Message Queue:

• The system will allow messages to be published to specific topics

• Multiple consumers/subscribers can subscribe to these topics

• When a message is published, all consumers/subscribers of that topic should receive it in parallel

• Subscribers should process messages independently and in parallel

Is this the expected direction for the design?

‍

Interviewer: Yes, you are on the right track. Please continue.

‍

Candidate: Great! Before diving into the design, I'd like to clarify a few requirements:

• Do we need to support message persistence if subscribers are offline?

• Are there any ordering guarantees for messages?

• Do we need to support message acknowledgment or retry mechanisms?

### Point 2: Clarifying Requirements : 

Interviewer: We want a system that:

• Supports multiple topics where publishers can send messages

• Allows subscribers to subscribe to one or more topics

• Delivers each message to all subscribers of the topic

• Supports parallel execution/consumption by subscribers

• Maintains message ordering within a topic (partition in more granular terms)

• Provides basic persistence to handle temporary subscriber unavailability

‍

Candidate: To summarize, the key requirements are:

• A flexible pub-sub system with topic-based message routing

• Support for parallel subscriber consumption & processing

• Message persistence and ordering within topics

• Ability to scale with multiple publishers and subscribers

‍

Interviewer: Perfect, let's proceed.

‍

### Point 3: Identifying Key Components : 

Candidate: Now that we have the requirements, let's identify the key components of our Pub-Sub Message Queue system:

• Message: Class representing the message payload

• Topic: Entity that categorizes messages and maintains a list of messages

• IPublisher: Interface for publishing messages to topics

• ISubscriber: Interface for receiving and processing messages from subscribed topics

• TopicSubscriber: Associates a subscriber with a topic and tracks message consumption offset

• TopicPublisher: Associates a publisher with a topic

• KafkaController: Central component that manages topics, publishers, subscribers and message delivery

‍

Interviewer: That sounds good. Let's proceed with the design details.

‍

### Point 4: Design Challenges : 

Interviewer: What design challenges do you anticipate?

‍‍

Candidate: The key challenges for the Pub-Sub Message Queue system include:

• Concurrency Control: Managing concurrent access to topics and messages

• Message Delivery: Ensuring all subscribers receive messages reliably & concurrently

• Offset Management: Tracking which messages each subscriber has processed

• Parallelism: Supporting parallel message processing by subscribers

• State Management: Maintaining topic state and subscriber positions in message streams

‍

### Point 5: Implementation : 

Interviewer: Ready to discuss implementation?

‍

Candidate: Yes. I'll implement a system with the above discussed key components:

‍

### Kafka Design with Design Patterns : 

![Article image](https://cwa-prod.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/1745292328186-image.png)

‍

1.) Implement Core Publisher and Subscriber Interfaces : 

_• IPublisher Interface (for Publisher):_

Interface representing a message publisher in the pub-sub system. Publishers will implement this interface to publish messages to topics.

Java

```
1public interface IPublisher {
2	    String getId();
3	    void publish(String topicId, Message message) throws IllegalArgumentException;
4}
```

‍

_• ISubscriber Interface (for Subscriber ) :_  

Interface representing a message subscriber in the pub-sub system.

Subscribers implement this interface to receive and process messages from topics.

Java

```
1public interface ISubscriber {
2	    String getId();
3	    void onMessage(Message message) throws InterruptedException;
4}
```

‍‍

2.) Implement Concrete Publishers and Subscribers :

Concrete Subscribers : 

Simple Subscriber :

Java

```
1public class SimpleSubscriber implements ISubscriber {
2	    private final String id;
3	    public SimpleSubscriber(String id) {
4	        this.id = id;
5	    }
6	    @Override
7	    public String getId() {
8	        return id;
9	    }
10
11	    @Override
12	    public void onMessage(Message message) throws InterruptedException {
13	        // Processing the received message.
14	        System.out.println("Subscriber " + id + " received: " + message.getMessage());
15	        // Simulate processing delay if desired
16	        Thread.sleep(500);
17	    }
18	}
```

Concrete Publishers : 

Simple Publisher : 

Java

```
1public class SimplePublisher implements IPublisher {
2	    private final String id;
3	    private final KafkaController kafkaController;
4
5	    public SimplePublisher(String id, KafkaController kafkaController) {
6	        this.id = id;
7	        this.kafkaController = kafkaController;
8	    }
9
10	    @Override
11	    public String getId() {
12	        return id;
13	    }
14	    @Override
15	    public void publish(String topicId, Message message) throws IllegalArgumentException {
16	        kafkaController.publish(this, topicId, message);
17	        System.out.println("Publisher " + id + " published: " + message.getMessage() + " to topic " + topicId);
18	    }
19	}

These are the core entities of our system.

Message : 

Java

_• TopicSubscriber :_

_• Topic Publisher :_ 

Java

• _Topic_ : 

A topic maintains a list of messages.

Java

```
1public class Topic {
2	    private final String topicName; // Name of the topic, used for identification/display purposes.
3	    private final String topicId; // Unique identifier for the topic.
4	    // List to store all messages published to this topic.
5	    // This list is exposed to the outside using an immutable getter.
6	    private final List<Message> messages;
7	    public Topic(final String topicName, final String topicId) {
8	        this.topicName = topicName;
9	        this.topicId = topicId;
10	        this.messages = new ArrayList<>();
11	    }
12
13	    public String getTopicName() {
14	        return topicName;
15	    }
16	    public String getTopicId() {
17	        return topicId;
18	    }
19	    public synchronized void addMessage(Message message) {
20	        messages.add(message);
21	     }
22
23	    public synchronized List<Message> getMessages() {
24	        return Collections.unmodifiableList(messages);
25	    }
26	    // Getters Section End
27	}
```

‍

3.) Implement Corresponding Controllers : 

_Topic Publisher Controller :_ 

Java

```
1public class TopicPublisherController {
2	    private final Topic topic; //We could have used TopicPublisher also directly 
3	    private final IPublisher publisher;
4	    public TopicPublisherController(Topic topic, IPublisher publisher) {
5	        this.topic = topic;
6	        this.publisher = publisher;
7	    }
8	    // Synchronized publish method ensures thread-safe publishing for this topic.
9	    public synchronized void publish(Message message, KafkaController controller) {
10	        controller.publish(publisher, topic.getTopicId(), message);
11	        System.out.println("Publisher " + publisher.getId() + " published to topic " + topic.getTopicName());
12	    }
13	}
```

‍

• _Topic Subscriber Controller :_ 

Java

```
1public class TopicSubscriberController implements Runnable {
2	    private final TopicSubscriber topicSubscriber;
3	    public TopicSubscriberController(TopicSubscriber topicSubscriber) {
4	        this.topicSubscriber = topicSubscriber;
5	    }
6
7	    @Override
8	    public void run() {
9	        Topic topic = topicSubscriber.getTopic();
10	        ISubscriber subscriber = topicSubscriber.getSubscriber();
11	        while (true) {
12	            Message messageToProcess = null;
13	            
14	            synchronized (topicSubscriber) {
15	                // Wait until there is a new message (offset is less than the number of messages)
16	                while (topicSubscriber.getOffset().get() >= topic.getMessages().size()) {
17	                    try {
18	                        topicSubscriber.wait();
19	                    } catch (InterruptedException e) {
20	                        Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
21	                        return;
22	                    }
23	                }
24	                // Retrieve the next message and increment the offset
25	                int currentOffset = topicSubscriber.getOffset().getAndIncrement();
26	                messageToProcess = topic.getMessages().get(currentOffset);
27	            }
28	            // Process the message outside of the synchronized block & this processing can also be done in parallel in some other threads
29	            try {
30	                subscriber.onMessage(messageToProcess);
31	            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
32	                Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
33	                return;
34	            }
35	        }
36	    }
37	}
```

‍

• Kafka Controller : 

Java

```
1public class KafkaController {
2  // Map of topic IDs to Topic objects.
3  private final Map<String, Topic> topics;
4  // Map of topic IDs to their list of TopicSubscriber associations.
5  private final Map<String, List<TopicSubscriber>> topicSubscribers;
6  // ExecutorService to run subscriber tasks concurrently.
7  private final ExecutorService subscriberExecutor;
8  private final AtomicInteger topicIdCounter;
9
10  public KafkaController() {
11    topics = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
12    topicSubscribers = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
13    // Using a cached thread pool to dynamically manage threads.
14    subscriberExecutor = Executors.newCachedThreadPool();
15    topicIdCounter = new AtomicInteger(0);
16  }
17
18  public Topic createTopic(String topicName) {
19    String topicId = String.valueOf(topicIdCounter.incrementAndGet());
20    Topic topic = new Topic(topicName, topicId);
21    topics.put(topicId, topic);
22    topicSubscribers.put(topicId, new CopyOnWriteArrayList<>());
23    System.out.println("Created topic: " + topicName + " with id: " + topicId);
24    return topic;
25  }
26
27  public void subscribe(ISubscriber subscriber, String topicId) {
28    Topic topic = topics.get(topicId);
29    if (topic == null) {
30      System.err.println("Topic with id " + topicId + " does not exist");
31      return;
32    }
33    TopicSubscriber ts = new TopicSubscriber(topic, subscriber);
34    topicSubscribers.get(topicId).add(ts);
35    // Submit the subscriber task to the executor.
36    subscriberExecutor.submit(new TopicSubscriberController(ts));
37    System.out.println(
38        "Subscriber " + subscriber.getId() + " subscribed to topic: " + topic.getTopicName());
39  }
40
41  public void publish(IPublisher publisher, String topicId, Message message) {
42    Topic topic = topics.get(topicId);
43    if (topic == null) {
44      throw new IllegalArgumentException("Topic with id " + topicId + " does not exist");
45    }
46    topic.addMessage(message);
47    // wake up each subscriber on its own monitor
48    List<TopicSubscriber> subs = topicSubscribers.get(topicId);
49    for (TopicSubscriber topicSubscriber : subs) {
50      synchronized (topicSubscriber) {
51        topicSubscriber.notify();
52      }
53    }
54    System.out.println(
55        "Message \"" + message.getMessage() + "\" published to topic: " + topic.getTopicName());
56  }
57
58  // Resets the offset for the given subscriber on the specified topic.
59  public void resetOffset(String topicId, ISubscriber subscriber, int newOffset) {
60    List<TopicSubscriber> subscribers = topicSubscribers.get(topicId);
61    if (subscribers == null) {
62      System.err.println("Topic with id " + topicId + " does not exist");
63      return;
64    }
65    for (TopicSubscriber ts : subscribers) {
66      if (ts.getSubscriber().getId().equals(subscriber.getId())) {
67        ts.getOffset().set(newOffset);
68        // Notify in case the subscriber thread is waiting.
69        synchronized (ts) {
70          ts.notify();
71        }
72        System.out.println("Offset for subscriber " + subscriber.getId() + " on topic "
73            + ts.getTopic().getTopicName() + " reset to " + newOffset);
74        break;
75      }
76    }
77  }
78
79  // Shutdown the ExecutorService gracefully.
80  public void shutdown() {
81    subscriberExecutor.shutdown();
82    try {
83      if (!subscriberExecutor.awaitTermination(5, TimeUnit.SECONDS)) {
84        subscriberExecutor.shutdownNow();
85      }
86    } catch (InterruptedException e) {
87      subscriberExecutor.shutdownNow();
88    }
89  }
90}
```

‍

4.) Client Code to Run the System : 

Java

```
1public class Main {
2	    public static void main(String[] args) {
3	        KafkaController kafkaController = new KafkaController();
4	        // Create topics.
5	        Topic topic1 = kafkaController.createTopic("Topic1");
6	        Topic topic2 = kafkaController.createTopic("Topic2");
7
8	        // Create subscribers.
9	        SimpleSubscriber subscriber1 = new SimpleSubscriber("Subscriber1");
10	        SimpleSubscriber subscriber2 = new SimpleSubscriber("Subscriber2");
11	        SimpleSubscriber subscriber3 = new SimpleSubscriber("Subscriber3");
12	        // Subscribe: subscriber1 subscribes to both topics,
13	        // subscriber2 subscribes to topic1, and subscriber3 subscribes to topic2.
14	        kafkaController.subscribe(subscriber1, topic1.getTopicId());
15	        kafkaController.subscribe(subscriber1, topic2.getTopicId());
16	        kafkaController.subscribe(subscriber2, topic1.getTopicId());
17	        kafkaController.subscribe(subscriber3, topic2.getTopicId());
18	        // Create publishers.
19	        SimplePublisher publisher1 = new SimplePublisher("Publisher1", kafkaController);
20	        SimplePublisher publisher2 = new SimplePublisher("Publisher2", kafkaController);
21	        // Publish some messages.
22	        publisher1.publish(topic1.getTopicId(), new Message("Message m1"));
23	        publisher1.publish(topic1.getTopicId(), new Message("Message m2"));
24	        publisher2.publish(topic2.getTopicId(), new Message("Message m3"));
25
26	        // Allow time for subscribers to process messages.
27	        try {
28	            Thread.sleep(5000);
29	        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
30	            e.printStackTrace();
31	        }
32	        publisher2.publish(topic2.getTopicId(), new Message("Message m4"));
33	        publisher1.publish(topic1.getTopicId(), new Message("Message m5"));
34	        // Reset offset for subscriber1 on topic1 (for example, to re-process messages).
35	        kafkaController.resetOffset(topic1.getTopicId(), subscriber1, 0);
36	        // Allow some time before shutting down.
37	        try {
38	            Thread.sleep(5000);
39	        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
40	            e.printStackTrace();
41	        }
42	        kafkaController.shutdown();
43	    }
44	}
```

‍

Output : 

```

	Created topic: Topic2 with id: 2
	Subscriber Subscriber1 subscribed to topic: Topic1
	Subscriber Subscriber1 subscribed to topic: Topic2
	Subscriber Subscriber2 subscribed to topic: Topic1
	Subscriber Subscriber3 subscribed to topic: Topic2
	Message "Message m1" published to topic: Topic1
	Subscriber Subscriber1 received: Message m1
	Subscriber Subscriber2 received: Message m1
	Publisher Publisher1 published: Message m1 to topic 1
	Message "Message m2" published to topic: Topic1
	Publisher Publisher1 published: Message m2 to topic 1
	Message "Message m3" published to topic: Topic2

	Publisher Publisher2 published: Message m3 to topic 2
